name: MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS

topics.title: Definition of Marine Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Marine Pollution # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Marine Pollution** is formally defined under **Article 1(1)(4) of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982** as: > *"The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing and other legitimate uses of the sea, impairment of quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities."* A more concise operational definition adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **MARPOL Convention (1973, as amended)** is: > *"The introduction by man of harmful substances, wastes, or energy into the marine environment from ships, offshore platforms, or other sources, which results in damage to the marine ecosystem, human health, or legitimate sea-use."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Examinations:** > Every formal definition of marine pollution has **THREE mandatory elements** that examiners test: > 1. **Source** → Must be *anthropogenic* (man-made); natural phenomena are NOT pollution. > 2. **Medium** → Substance OR energy (heat, noise, radioactive) entering the marine environment. > 3. **Effect** → Must cause *deleterious* (harmful) effects on biota, human health, or amenities. ### 1.2 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Marine Pollution | Metric | Symbol | Typical Threshold / Unit | |---|---|---| | Concentration of pollutant in water | C | **µg/L** or **ppb** (parts per billion) | | Mass loading rate | L | **kg/day** or **tonnes/year** | | Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) | BOD₅ | **mg/L** (5-day test at 20°C) | | Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | **mg/L** | | Bioaccumulation Factor | BAF | Dimensionless ratio | | Half-life (persistence) | t₁/₂ | **days**, **years** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ocean as a Giant Bathtub 🛁 Imagine the world's ocean as a **single, enormous bathtub** filled with water. Every coastal city, every ship, every factory, and every farm is essentially "pouring" something into this bathtub. Now, the bathtub has three special features: - The water **does not drain quickly** — ocean turnover takes **~1,000 years**. - The bathtub is **shared by everyone** on Earth. - Whatever you pour in **does not stay where you poured it** — ocean currents (like the **Great Ocean Conveyor Belt**) spread pollutants worldwide. So if a factory in Kerala pours a toxin into the bathtub, the toxin may eventually reach the coast of Antarctica via thermohaline circulation. This is why marine pollution is uniquely **transboundary** and **persistent**. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Distinction (often asked in KTU 3-mark questions):** > - **Contamination** = Presence of a foreign substance; concentration is *measurable* but may not yet cause harm. > - **Pollution** = Contamination + *measurable deleterious effect* on the ecosystem or human health. > > All pollution begins as contamination, but not all contamination becomes pollution. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the UNCLOS Definition The UNCLOS definition can be decomposed into **four logical pillars**. Mastering these four pillars guarantees full marks on any definition-based KTU question. ### Pillar 1 — The Source Criterion (Anthropogenic Origin) - The substance must be introduced **directly OR indirectly** by humans. - **Direct** = dumping waste from a ship, oil spill from a tanker. - **Indirect** = agricultural runoff carried by rivers, atmospheric deposition of vehicle emissions, plastic debris blown by wind from landfills. - Natural events (volcanic eruptions, algal blooms) are **excluded** from this legal definition. ### Pillar 2 — The Pathway Criterion (Marine Environment) - Includes **open ocean, coastal waters, estuaries, semi-enclosed seas, and seabed**. - The substance must enter this environment — not merely exist in isolation. ### Pillar 3 — The Form Criterion (Substance OR Energy) Pollution can be in **two distinct forms**: - **Material pollution** → chemical, biological, or physical substances (e.g., crude oil, plastic, sewage). - **Energy pollution** → thermal discharge (cooling water from power plants), noise pollution (sonar from naval vessels), radioactive discharge. ### Pillar 4 — The Effect Criterion (Deleterious Impact) At least **one** of the following must occur: - Harm to **living resources** (fish kills, coral bleaching). - **Hazard to human health** (contaminated seafood, beach closures). - **Hindrance to marine activities** (fouled fishing nets, navigation hazards). - **Impairment of water quality** for legitimate use. - **Reduction of amenities** (loss of recreational value, foul smell). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Pattern:** > If a student writes ONLY "dumping of waste into the sea" — they LOSE marks. The complete answer MUST include **man**, **substance OR energy**, **marine environment**, and **deleterious effect**. ## 2.2 Classification of Marine Pollutants Marine pollutants are broadly classified into **four categories** based on the UNEP/MARPOL framework: | Category | Examples | Primary Concern | |---|---|---| | **Chemical** | Oil & hydrocarbons, heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd), pesticides (DDT, PCBs), plastics | Toxicity, bioaccumulation, persistence | | **Biological** | Sewage, pathogens (*E. coli*, *Vibrio*), invasive species (ballast water) | Disease outbreaks, ecosystem disruption | | **Physical** | Plastic debris, suspended sediments, thermal discharge | Entanglement, habitat smothering, oxygen depletion | | **Radioactive** | Nuclear waste, fallout from accidents (Fukushima, Chernobyl) | Long-term genetic damage, bioaccumulation | ## 2.3 Source-Based Classification Marine pollution sources are categorized as: 1. **Land-based sources (≈ 80% of marine pollution globally)** — rivers, coastal cities, industrial outfalls, agricultural runoff, atmospheric deposition. 2. **Ship-based sources (MARPOL-regulated)** — operational discharges (bilge water, sewage, garbage) and accidental spills. 3. **Offshore installations** — oil & gas platforms, drilling muds. 4. **Dumping at sea** — deliberate disposal of dredged material, sewage sludge, industrial waste. 5. **Atmospheric input** — airborne contaminants precipitated by rain. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Conceptual Foundations) > [!NOTE] > Although "Definition of Marine Pollution" is conceptual, the following foundational formulas are essential for problem-solving questions in **later modules** and are frequently referenced in Part B questions. | Formula | LaTeX | Application in Marine Pollution | |---|---|---| | **Concentration** | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass $m$ of pollutant in water volume $V$ | | **Mass Loading Rate** | $L = \dfrac{M}{T}$ | Mass $M$ discharged per unit time $T$ | | **Dilution Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{C_0}{C_d}$ | Initial concentration $C_0$ vs. diluted concentration $C_d$ | | **Bioaccumulation Factor** | $BAF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Tendency of chemical to accumulate in tissue | | **Biomagnification Factor** | $BMF = \dfrac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$ | Concentration increase along food chain | | **Persistence Half-life** | $N = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}}$ for radioactive/pollutant decay | | **BOD₅ Reaction** | $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f) \times \dfrac{V_b}{V_s}$ | Standard 5-day biochemical oxygen demand test | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance The definition of marine pollution is the **foundational legal-engineering principle** behind: - **Design of wastewater treatment plants** for coastal cities. - **Sizing of oil-water separators (OWS)** on ships (MARPOL Annex I). - **Setting of effluent discharge standards** (e.g., EPA, CPCB, IMO). - **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** for port and offshore construction. - **Liability assessment** under conventions like the **Bunker Oil Convention 2001** and **Civil Liability Convention 1969**. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of "When is Something Considered a Marine Pollutant?" A substance is classified as a marine pollutant ONLY when **all four logical conditions** are simultaneously satisfied. We can express this as a Boolean logic function: $$ P = S \land M \land E \land H $$ Where: - $P$ = Pollution event (TRUE = pollution occurred) - $S$ = Source condition (anthropogenic introduction) - $M$ = Medium condition (enters marine environment) - $E$ = Effect condition (deleterious impact occurs) - $H$ = Human/anthropogenic attribution ### Step 1 — Verification of Source Condition ($S$) We must establish that the substance was **introduced by humans**. The classification rule is: $$ S = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if origin is anthropogenic} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{if origin is natural (volcanic, biogenic)} \end{cases} $$ **Example:** Crude oil entering the sea from a ruptured tanker → $S = \text{TRUE}$. **Counter-example:** Natural oil seepage from the seabed (e.g., Coal Oil Point, California) → $S = \text{FALSE}$. ### Step 2 — Verification of Medium Condition ($M$) $$ M = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if substance or energy reaches marine environment} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{if confined to terrestrial environment} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 3 — Verification of Effect Condition ($E$) A deleterious effect is established when the **measured concentration** exceeds the **threshold limit**: $$ E = \text{TRUE} \iff C_{measured} > C_{threshold} $$ Where $C_{threshold}$ is the regulatory limit (e.g., for mercury in seawater: **0.05 µg/L** as per EPA, for oil: **15 ppm** instantaneous discharge per MARPOL Annex I). ### Step 4 — Final Pollutant Status $$ \text{Status} = \begin{cases} \text{POLLUTANT} & \text{if } P = \text{TRUE} \\ \text{CONTAMINANT ONLY} & \text{if } P = \text{FALSE} \text{ but at least one of } S, M \text{ is TRUE} \\ \text{NOT POLLUTANT} & \text{if all conditions FALSE} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Concentration Threshold Calculation **Problem:** A coastal industry discharges 500 kg/day of a pollutant into a receiving water body with a flow rate of 2.5 × 10⁶ L/day. The regulatory threshold is 0.2 mg/L. Determine whether this constitutes pollution. **Solution:** Step 1 — Calculate the discharge concentration: $$ C = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{6} \text{ mg}}{2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ L}} $$ $$ C = 200 \text{ mg/L} $$ Step 2 — Compare with regulatory threshold: $$ C_{measured} = 200 \text{ mg/L} \quad \text{vs.} \quad C_{threshold} = 0.2 \text{ mg/L} $$ Step 3 — Compute the exceedance ratio: $$ R = \dfrac{C_{measured}}{C_{threshold}} = \dfrac{200}{0.2} = 1000 $$ Step 4 — Conclusion: $$ R = 1000 \gg 1 \implies E = \text{TRUE} $$ $$ P = S \land M \land E \land H = \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE} $$ **→ The discharge constitutes marine pollution under UNCLOS Article 1.** ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix The following matrix is used by environmental engineers and KTU examiners to **classify a marine contamination event**: | Anthropogenic Source? | Reaches Marine Environment? | Deleterious Effect? | Classification | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | **POLLUTION** | | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Contamination only | | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | Not marine pollution | | ❌ No (natural) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Natural disturbance (NOT pollution legally) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The fourth row is a classic KTU trick question: students often mistakenly classify a natural oil seep or volcanic ash as "pollution." Under UNCLOS, **natural events are excluded** from the legal definition of pollution. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Logical Framework of Marine Pollution Definition ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Anthropogenic Source
Man-Made Origin"] nodeB["Substance OR Energy
Enters Marine Environment"] nodeC["Deleterious Effect
Harm to Biota, Health, Amenities"] nodeD{"Marine Pollution
Established?
UNCLOS Article 1"} nodeE["LAND-BASED
Rivers, Sewage, Industry"] nodeF["SHIP-BASED
MARPOL Regulated"] nodeG["OFFSHORE
Platforms, Drilling"] nodeH["DUMPING
Deliberate Disposal"] nodeI["ATMOSPHERIC
Airborne Deposition"] nodeJ["CHEMICAL
Oil, Metals, Plastics"] nodeK["BIOLOGICAL
Pathogens, Invasive Species"] nodeL["PHYSICAL
Sediment, Thermal"] nodeM["RADIOACTIVE
Nuclear Waste"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeA --> nodeH nodeA --> nodeI nodeB --> nodeJ nodeB --> nodeK nodeB --> nodeL nodeB --> nodeM nodeD -->|YES| nodeN["Apply MARPOL
EPA Standards
EIA Required"] nodeD -->|NO| nodeO["Contamination Only
Monitor But No
Regulatory Action"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Marine Pollution Impact Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR src1["Industrial
Effluent"] src2["Domestic
Sewage"] src3["Oil Spill
Tanker"] src4["Agricultural
Runoff"] src5["Atmospheric
Deposition"] med["Marine
Environment"] phys["Physical
Impact"] chem["Chemical
Impact"] bio["Biological
Impact"] eco["Ecosystem
Damage"] hum["Human
Health Risk"] econ["Economic
Loss"] reg["Regulatory
Response"] src1 --> med src2 --> med src3 --> med src4 --> med src5 --> med med --> phys med --> chem med --> bio phys --> eco chem --> eco bio --> eco eco --> hum eco --> econ hum --> reg econ --> reg ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - **Top half** = Five major sources feeding into the marine environment. > - **Middle band** = Three pathways of impact. > - **Bottom cascade** = Ecological → Human/Economic → Regulatory response loop. > This is the standard flow engineers follow in **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** reports. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]` **Q: Define marine pollution as per the UNCLOS Convention. List the four essential elements of this definition.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** As per **Article 1(1)(4) of UNCLOS, 1982**, marine pollution is the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment which results in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, impairment of water quality, and reduction of amenities. **Four Essential Elements (2 Marks):** 1. **Anthropogenic Source** — Substance must originate from human activity. 2. **Substance OR Energy** — Both material and non-material pollutants are covered. 3. **Marine Environment** — Includes open seas, coastal waters, estuaries, and seabed. 4. **Deleterious Effect** — Must cause measurable harm to biota, humans, or amenities. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` **Q: Differentiate between marine pollution and marine contamination with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Marine Contamination | Marine Pollution | |---|---|---| | Definition (1 Mark) | Presence of a foreign substance in the marine environment | Contamination that causes measurable deleterious effects | | Harmful Effect | May or may not be harmful | Always harmful | | Legal Status | Not always regulated under law | Regulated under UNCLOS, MARPOL, etc. | | **Example** (2 Marks) | Trace levels of iron in seawater from natural weathering | Oil spill from a tanker killing marine birds and fish | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]` **Q: (a)** Explain the UNCLOS definition of marine pollution in detail. Discuss the four essential pillars with examples. **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** Classify the various sources of marine pollution. Discuss land-based and ship-based sources with specific examples. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution for Question A — Part (a) — 7 Marks The UNCLOS definition is the most comprehensive legal definition of marine pollution. The four pillars are: **Pillar 1 — Anthropogenic Source (1.5 Marks):** The pollution must originate from human activity. Example: Industrial effluent from a coastal factory. **Pillar 2 — Substance or Energy Form (1.5 Marks):** Both physical matter (oil, plastic) and forms of energy (heat from power plants, noise from ships, radioactive emissions) qualify. **Pillar 3 — Marine Environment as Medium (1.5 Marks):** The introduction must reach any part of the marine environment — open ocean, coast, estuary, or seabed. **Pillar 4 — Deleterious Effects (2.5 Marks):** At least one of the following must occur: - Harm to living resources (e.g., coral bleaching from thermal discharge). - Hazard to human health (e.g., *Vibrio cholerae* from sewage contamination). - Hindrance to marine activities (e.g., ghost nets entangling fishing boats). - Impairment of water quality (e.g., eutrophication from fertilizer runoff). - Reduction of amenities (e.g., plastic-littered beaches deterring tourism). --- #### Solution for Question A — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Classification of Marine Pollution Sources (2 Marks):** Marine pollution sources are classified into: 1. Land-based sources 2. Ship-based sources (MARPOL) 3. Offshore installations 4. Dumping at sea 5. Atmospheric input **Land-based Sources (2.5 Marks):** These constitute **≈ 80%** of total marine pollution globally. Examples include: - **Municipal sewage** from coastal cities (pathogens, nutrients). - **Industrial discharges** (heavy metals, PCBs, dyes). - **Agricultural runoff** (fertilizers, pesticides like DDT). - **River-borne sediment** from deforestation. - **Plastic waste** from coastal landfills. **Ship-based Sources — MARPOL Regulated (2.5 Marks):** Governed by the **MARPOL Convention** with six annexes: - **Annex I** — Oil (operational discharges from machinery spaces). - **Annex II** — Noxious liquid substances in bulk. - **Annex III** — Harmful packaged goods. - **Annex IV** — Sewage from ships. - **Annex V** — Garbage (plastics strictly prohibited). - **Annex VI** — Air pollution (SOx, NOx, ODS). --- ### 📗 QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` **Q: (a)** "Contamination is not the same as pollution." Critically evaluate this statement with examples and logical reasoning. **(7 Marks)** **Q: (b)** Discuss the criteria for classifying a substance as a marine pollutant. Use the Boolean logic framework to justify your answer. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution for Question B — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Critical Evaluation (3 Marks):** The statement is **TRUE** under UNCLOS definitions. Contamination refers to the mere **presence** of a foreign substance in the marine environment. Pollution, however, requires the additional criterion of a **deleterious effect**. All pollution begins as contamination, but not all contamination becomes pollution. **Examples (2 Marks):** - **Contamination only:** Trace amounts of copper in seawater from natural weathering of rocks — present but below toxic thresholds, no harm observed. - **Pollution:** Industrial copper discharge exceeding 0.05 mg/L causes liver damage in fish — this is pollution. **Logical Reasoning (2 Marks):** The distinction is critical in **environmental law** because: - Regulatory action is triggered only upon **pollution**, not mere contamination. - The **threshold concentration** is the key parameter that separates the two. - Continuous monitoring is required to upgrade contamination events to pollution status. --- #### Solution for Question B — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Boolean Logic Framework (4 Marks):** A substance qualifies as a marine pollutant only when: $$ P = S \land M \land E \land H = \text{TRUE} $$ Where: - $S$ = Source is anthropogenic - $M$ = Substance reaches marine environment - $E$ = Effect concentration exceeds threshold ($C_{measured} > C_{threshold}$) - $H$ = Harm to biota, health, or amenities is documented **Criteria for Classification (3 Marks):** 1. **Anthropogenic origin** — natural events excluded. 2. **Concentration above threshold** — e.g., mercury > 0.05 µg/L. 3. **Bioaccumulative potential** — BAF > 1 indicates accumulation. 4. **Persistence** — half-life greater than acceptable duration. 5. **Documented ecological or health impact** — through scientific studies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - ⚠️ **Do NOT** write a definition without mentioning **ALL FOUR elements** (source, medium, form, effect). Examiners deduct up to 1.5 marks for incomplete definitions. > - ⚠️ **Do NOT** confuse natural oil seepage or volcanic events with pollution — they are legally **excluded**. > - ⚠️ **Do NOT** write only "dumping of waste" — UNCLOS explicitly includes **energy pollution** (thermal, noise, radioactive). > - ⚠️ For 14-mark questions, **always write sub-part answers separately** with clear headings. Mixing (a) and (b) loses marks. > - ⚠️ In the Boolean logic question, **show the equation** explicitly. Partial marks are awarded for correct notation even if final evaluation is wrong. --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Definition of Marine Pollution":** - ✅ **UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4)** is the universally accepted legal definition. - ✅ Four **mandatory pillars**: **Anthropogenic Source → Substance/Energy → Marine Environment → Deleterious Effect**. - ✅ **MARPOL Convention (1973/78)** has **six annexes** regulating ship-based pollution. - ✅ **Contamination ≠ Pollution** — pollution requires demonstrated harmful effect. - ✅ Land-based sources contribute **≈ 80%** of total marine pollution. - ✅ Pollution can be **material** (chemical, biological, physical) OR **energetic** (thermal, noise, radioactive). - ✅ Natural phenomena (volcanic, biogenic) are **legally excluded** from the definition. - ✅ Key metrics: **Concentration (µg/L)**, **Mass Loading (kg/day)**, **BAF**, **BMF**, **Half-life**. - ✅ Boolean logic: **$P = S \land M \land E \land H$** must all be TRUE for pollution status. - ✅ Three definitions commonly tested: **UNCLOS**, **IMO/MARPOL**, and **EPA**. - ✅ Marine pollution is **transboundary** — ocean currents can carry pollutants globally. - ✅ Ocean turnover time is **~1,000 years** — pollution accumulates persistently. - ✅ Always quote **specific articles and conventions** (UNCLOS, MARPOL Annex I-VI) for full marks.

topics.title: Types of Marine Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Marine Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Pollution (UNCLOS / IMO Definition):** The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries and coastal waters) which results in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities (including fishing), impairment of quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities.

According to the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), Article 1(1)(4)**, the marine environment encompasses all waters from the high-water line of ordinary coastlines out to the limits of national jurisdiction, and beyond into the high seas. The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** classifies marine pollutants into the following primary categories based on source, chemical nature, and impact pathway.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 1 emphasizes the *taxonomy* of marine pollutants — students must be able to classify a given pollutant (e.g., crude oil, tributyltin, microbeads) into its correct category and explain its mechanism of damage to marine ecosystems.

### 1.2 Classification Master Framework

Marine pollution is broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Source-based Classification** (Point, Non-point, and Atmospheric)
2. **Nature-based Classification** (Physical, Chemical, Biological, Thermal, Radioactive)
3. **Activity-based Classification** (Land-based, Sea-based, and Air-based)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the 8 main types of marine pollution:** **"O-CHANTS-N"**
> **O**il, **C**hemicals, **H**eavy metals, **A**gricultural runoff, **N**utrients, **T**hermal, **S**ewage/Plastic, **N**oise (acoustical).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Ocean as a Giant Sink

Imagine the ocean as a **vast glass bathtub** in a busy city. Every day, residents (humans) toss different things into the tub — cooking oil (oil pollution), shampoo residues (chemical pollution), plastic bottles (plastic pollution), warm bathwater (thermal pollution), and even loud music (noise pollution). The tub has **one drain (natural recovery)**, but it is much smaller than the rate at which pollutants enter. Over decades, the bathtub becomes murky, toxic, and uninhabitable. The 8 major types of marine pollution are exactly these 8 "categories of things" that humanity dumps into the marine bathtub.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** are used throughout KTU evaluations and global marine monitoring:

- **Petroleum Hydrocarbon Concentration:** Expressed in **µg/L** (micrograms per litre) or **ppb** (parts per billion). Regulatory threshold for chronic exposure: **≤ 5 µg/L**.
- **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD₅):** Measured in **mg/L**. Unpolluted seawater: **< 2 mg/L**; polluted: **> 5 mg/L**.
- **Plastic Density:** Measured in **items/km²** or **g/km²**. Garbage Patch density: **> 1 million particles/km²**.
- **Sound Intensity:** Expressed in **decibels (dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m)**. Ambient ocean noise: **~ 70–95 dB**; ship sonar: **> 200 dB**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant:** Speed of sound in seawater (used in acoustic pollution calculations) — **c ≈ 1500 m/s** at 25 °C, 35 ppt salinity, and 1 atm pressure.

### 1.5 The Eight Major Types — Quick Reference Table

| # | Type | Primary Source | Key Pollutant(s) |
|---|------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Oil Pollution | Tankers, offshore rigs | Crude oil, bunker fuel |
| 2 | Chemical Pollution | Industry, agriculture | PCBs, DDT, pesticides |
| 3 | Heavy Metal Pollution | Mining, effluents | Hg, Pb, Cd, As |
| 4 | Plastic Pollution | Urban waste, fishing | Microplastics, nets |
| 5 | Nutrient Pollution | Fertilizers, sewage | Nitrates, phosphates |
| 6 | Sewage / Organic Pollution | Municipal discharge | Coliforms, BOD load |
| 7 | Thermal Pollution | Power plants | Heated coolant water |
| 8 | Noise Pollution | Shipping, sonar | Low/High freq. sound |
| 9 | Radioactive Pollution | Nuclear plants, accidents | Cs-137, Sr-90, I-131 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Type 1 — Oil Pollution

**Definition:** Release of liquid petroleum hydrocarbons into the marine environment, either by accident (tanker spills, blowouts) or routinely (bilge pumping, offshore operations).

**Operational Sub-Types:**
- **Acute Spills** — sudden, large-volume releases (e.g., Torrey Canyon 1967, 119,000 tonnes; MV Wakashio 2020, 1,000 tonnes; Deepwater Horizon 2010, **~ 4.9 million barrels**).
- **Chronic Operational Discharges** — small but continuous releases from tank cleaning and bilge water.
- **Natural Seeps** — submarine natural oil seeps (e.g., Santa Barbara Channel releases ~ 20–25 tonnes/day naturally; ironically these ecosystems are *adapted*).

**Mechanism of Damage (Why and How):**

1. **Physical Smothering** — Why? Oil film blocks **UV penetration** and **air–sea gas exchange**. How? A 1 µm oil film reduces oxygen diffusion by **~ 30%**.
2. **Chemical Toxicity** — Why? Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) are **lipophilic** and **bioaccumulative**. How? They dissolve into cell membranes of gills, causing narcotic toxicity.
3. **Long-term Sediment Incorporation** — Why? Heavy oil fractions (asphaltenes) sink and persist in sediments for **> 30 years**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Governing Equation for Oil Spill Dispersion:** The **Fay–Houk–Schmidt (FAS) Model** divides slick spreading into three regimes — **gravity–inertia**, **gravity–viscosity**, and **surface tension–viscosity**. Critical area at any time *t*:

$$A(t) = k_2 \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta \rho \, g \, V^{2} \, t^{3/2}}{\nu^{1/2}} \right)$$

where *V* = spill volume, *ν* = kinematic viscosity, *Δρ* = density difference, *g* = gravity, *k₂* ≈ 0.025 (calibrated constant for the gravity–viscous regime).

### 2.2 Type 2 — Chemical Pollution (Xenobiotics & Persistent Organic Pollutants)

**Definition:** Discharge of synthetic organic compounds that are **non-biodegradable**, **lipophilic**, and **bioaccumulative** — the so-called **POPs (Persistent Organic Pollutants)**.

**Operational Sub-Types:**
- **Organochlorines** — DDT, Dieldrin, Endosulfan
- **Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs)** — used in transformers/capacitors
- **Polyaromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)** — from incomplete combustion
- **Tributyltin (TBT)** — anti-fouling paint biocide (now banned under IMO AFS Convention 2001/2008)

**Mechanism of Damage:** Bioaccumulation factor (BAF) increases up the food chain — a process called **biomagnification**. The classic illustration is DDT: seawater **0.003 µg/L** → zooplankton **0.04 µg/L** → small fish **0.5 µg/L** → large fish **2.0 µg/L** → fish-eating birds **25 µg/L**.

### 2.3 Type 3 — Heavy Metal Pollution

**Definition:** Discharge of **trace metals with atomic density > 5 g/cm³** that exhibit toxicity at low concentrations. The "Big Four" hazardous marine heavy metals are **Mercury (Hg), Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), and Arsenic (As)**.

**Mechanism of Damage:** Unlike organic pollutants, heavy metals are **not biodegradable** — they undergo **speciation changes** (e.g., inorganic Hg → methylmercury, CH₃Hg⁺) that *increase* toxicity. Methylmercury causes **Minamata disease** in humans (Japan, 1956).

### 2.4 Type 4 — Plastic Pollution

**Definition:** Solid waste of synthetic polymer origin. Subdivided by size:
- **Macroplastics** — > 5 mm (bottles, bags, fishing nets — *"ghost nets"*)
- **Microplastics** — 1 µm to 5 mm (microbeads, fragments)
- **Nanoplastics** — < 1 µm (recent research focus)

**Mechanism of Damage:** Physical ingestion → gut blockage, false-saturation → starvation; chemical leaching of plasticizers (BPA, phthalates) → endocrine disruption; **vector effect** — plastics adsorb POPs and act as transport vehicles.

### 2.5 Type 5 — Nutrient Pollution (Eutrophication)

**Definition:** Excess introduction of **biostimulatory nutrients**, primarily **nitrogen (N)** and **phosphorus (P)**, triggering uncontrolled algal growth.

**Mechanism of Damage — The Eutrophication Cascade:**

1. Nutrient enrichment → 2. Phytoplankton bloom → 3. Light attenuation blocks benthic photosynthesis → 4. Algal die-off sinks → 5. Microbial decomposition → 6. Hypoxia / Anoxia (O₂ < 2 mg/L) → 7. **Dead Zone** formation (e.g., Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone ~ 16,000 km² annually).

### 2.6 Type 6 — Sewage and Organic Pollution

**Definition:** Discharge of biodegradable organic matter, primarily from **municipal wastewater outfalls** and **vessel blackwater**.

**Key Indicator:** **Faecal Coliform Count (FCC)**. WHO guideline for recreational waters: **≤ 200 CFU/100 mL**.

### 2.7 Type 7 — Thermal Pollution

**Definition:** Discharge of heated effluent (typically from coastal **power plants** or **LNG regasification terminals**) that raises ambient seawater temperature above the natural seasonal range.

**Mechanism of Damage:** Reduced dissolved oxygen (DO) capacity — warm water holds less O₂; thermal shock to stenothermic species; coral bleaching threshold exceeded at **+1 to +2 °C** above summer maximum.

### 2.8 Type 8 — Noise (Acoustic) Pollution

**Definition:** Anthropogenic sound in the marine acoustic environment. Two sub-types:
- **Low-frequency** — shipping propellers (10–1000 Hz)
- **High-frequency** — military sonar, seismic airguns (1–200 kHz)

**Mechanism of Damage:** Marine mammals (cetaceans) rely on **biosonar** and acoustic communication. Anthropogenic noise causes:
- **Masking** — overlap of signal and noise bands
- **Behavioural disturbance** — displacement, dive-pattern changes
- **Physiological trauma** — gas-bubble disease from rapid ascent during sonar exposure

### 2.9 Type 9 — Radioactive Pollution

**Definition:** Discharge of **radionuclides** (Cs-137, Sr-90, I-131, Pu-239) from nuclear power plants, reprocessing facilities (e.g., Sellafield, La Hague), weapons testing fallout, and accidents (Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011).

**Mechanism of Damage:** Ionizing radiation damages DNA directly (single/double-strand breaks) and indirectly (via reactive oxygen species, ROS). Bioaccumulation of **Cs-137** mimics potassium in muscle tissue; **Sr-90** mimics calcium in bone.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Sound Speed in Seawater | $c = 1449 + 4.6T - 0.055T^2 + 0.00029T^3 + (1.34 - 0.01T)(S - 35) + 0.016D$ | m/s | UNESCO (Chen-Millero) eqn. T in °C, S in ppt, D in m |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $SIL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$ | dB re 1 µPa | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 3 | Source Level (Spherical) | $SL = 20 \log_{10}(r) + TL$ | dB | $r$ = range, $TL$ = transmission loss |
| 4 | Transmission Loss (Spherical) | $TL_{sph} = 20 \log_{10}(r)$ | dB | Geometric spreading only |
| 5 | Transmission Loss (Cylindrical) | $TL_{cyl} = 10 \log_{10}(r) + \alpha r$ | dB | $\alpha$ = absorption coeff. (dB/km) |
| 6 | Bioconcentration Factor | $BCF = \frac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | dimensionless | Threshold for "bioaccumulative" |
| 7 | First-Order Decay | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | mg/L | $k$ = decay constant (1/day) |
| 8 | DO Saturation (mg/L) | $DO_{sat} = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$ | mg/L | Benson-Krause, $T$ in °C, fresh water |
| 9 | BOD₅ to BODₗₐₜₑ | $BOD_L = \frac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | mg/L | $k$ = deoxygenation rate |
| 10 | Dilution (Streeter-Phelps) | $D_O = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | mg/L | Classic dissolved-oxygen sag |
| 11 | Plastic Settling Velocity | $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Stokes' law (laminar, spherical) |
| 12 | Oil Slick Area (FAS) | $A(t) = k_2 \left( \frac{\Delta \rho g V^2 t^{3/2}}{\nu^{1/2}} \right)$ | m² | Gravity-viscous regime |
| 13 | Half-life of Pollutant | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$ | days | First-order kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:** The **Streeter-Phelps equation** is the cornerstone of wastewater outfall design — it predicts the **dissolved-oxygen sag curve** downstream of a discharge, allowing engineers to size the dilution required to maintain DO > 4 mg/L (the standard for healthy aquatic life). The **FAS model** drives oil spill response — the time-evolution of slick area determines boom-deployment strategy.

### 2.11 Real-World Engineering Application

In designing a **marine outfall diffuser** for a coastal city (e.g., the **Bandra-Worli Sea Link outfall, Mumbai** or **Boston Outfall, Massachusetts**), the environmental engineer must:
1. Calculate the **initial dilution ratio (S)** using the **density current / Froude number** equations.
2. Use the **Streeter-Phelps DO sag model** to confirm downstream DO never falls below regulatory limits.
3. Predict the **plume rise height** and **near-field mixing zone** extent.
4. Determine whether the **benthic impact zone** falls within permissible limits for benthic community recovery.

This same workflow applies whether the pollutant is sewage (Type 6), thermal effluent (Type 7), or nutrient-laden (Type 5).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sound Intensity Level in Marine Acoustics (Worked Example)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A coastal cargo ship emits broadband noise with a measured acoustic intensity of **I = 1 × 10⁻⁴ W/m²** at a reference distance of 1 m from the hull. The reference intensity in seawater is **I₀ = 0.67 × 10⁻¹⁸ W/m²** (the standard reference for underwater acoustics, normalized to 1 µPa pressure). Calculate the **Source Level (SL)** in dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m.

**Step 1 — State the fundamental intensity-level relationship.**

The intensity level in decibels is defined as ten times the base-10 logarithm of the intensity ratio:

$$IL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

Substituting $I = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, W/m^2$ and $I_0 = 0.67 \times 10^{-18} \, W/m^2$:

$$IL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.67 \times 10^{-18}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the argument of the logarithm.**

Divide the numerator by the denominator, paying careful attention to the powers of 10:

$$\frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.67 \times 10^{-18}} = \frac{1}{0.67} \times 10^{-4-(-18)} = \frac{1}{0.67} \times 10^{14}$$

Compute the numeric factor: $\frac{1}{0.67} \approx 1.4925$.

Therefore the argument becomes $1.4925 \times 10^{14}$.

**Step 4 — Take the base-10 logarithm.**

Apply $\log_{10}(a \cdot 10^b) = \log_{10}(a) + b$:

$$\log_{10}(1.4925 \times 10^{14}) = \log_{10}(1.4925) + 14 = 0.1741 + 14 = 14.1741$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 10 to convert to decibels.**

$$IL = 10 \times 14.1741 = 141.74 \, dB$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{SL \approx 141.7 \text{ dB re } 1 \, \mu Pa @ 1 m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A typical large cargo vessel has a source level of **140–190 dB re 1 µPa @ 1 m**, which is comparable to a jet engine in air. This is why chronic low-frequency shipping noise is considered one of the most pervasive forms of marine noise pollution.

### 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Decay of an Oil Spill in the Water Column

**Problem Statement:** An oil tanker releases **1000 kg** of a soluble PAH (e.g., naphthalene) into a well-mixed estuarine volume of **V = 10⁶ m³**. The first-order decay constant is **k = 0.05 day⁻¹** (typical for naphthalene in warm seawater). Calculate the **concentration after 10 days** and the **half-life**.

**Step 1 — Convert mass and volume to concentration units.**

Initial concentration:

$$C_0 = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{1000 \, kg}{10^6 \, m^3} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, kg/m^3 = 1.0 \, mg/L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order decay law.**

The governing equation is:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

With $C_0 = 1.0 \, mg/L$, $k = 0.05 \, day^{-1}$, and $t = 10 \, days$:

$$C(10) = 1.0 \cdot e^{-(0.05)(10)} = 1.0 \cdot e^{-0.5}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential term.**

$$e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

**Step 5 — Final concentration.**

$$C(10) = 1.0 \times 0.6065 = 0.607 \, mg/L$$

**Step 6 — Compute the half-life.**

For first-order kinetics, the half-life is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{0.05} = 13.86 \, days$$

$$\boxed{C(10) \approx 0.61 \, mg/L, \quad t_{1/2} \approx 13.9 \text{ days}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Bioconcentration Factor for a Heavy Metal

**Problem Statement:** Seawater Cadmium (Cd) concentration is measured at **0.05 µg/L** in a coastal bay. Tissue concentration in a sampled mussel (*Mytilus edulis*) is **0.75 µg/g (wet weight)**. Calculate the Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) and comment on the regulatory classification.

**Step 1 — Define BCF.**

$$BCF = \frac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$$

**Step 2 — Unit conversion (essential!).**

$C_{organism} = 0.75 \, \mu g/g = 0.75 \times 10^{-3} \, mg/g = 0.75 \, mg/kg$
$C_{water} = 0.05 \, \mu g/L = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \, mg/L = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, mg/L$

**Step 3 — Convert to consistent L/kg basis.**

For BCF in L/kg, multiply $C_{water}$ (in mg/L) by 1000 to get µg/L equivalent, OR more cleanly, use:

$$BCF = \frac{0.75 \, \mu g/g}{0.05 \, \mu g/L} = \frac{0.75}{0.05} \cdot \frac{L}{g} = 15 \, L/g$$

Convert to standard L/kg:

$$BCF = 15 \times 1000 = 15{,}000 \, L/kg$$

**Step 4 — Regulatory interpretation.**

A BCF ≥ 5000 L/kg classifies a substance as **"very bioaccumulative"** under the EU REACH regulation and as a **CMR/PMT/vPvB** category concern.

$$\boxed{BCF = 15{,}000 \, L/kg \quad \Rightarrow \text{Cd is "very bioaccumulative" in mussels.}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Type vs. Impact Pathway

| Pollution Type | Persistence | Bioaccumulation | Spatial Scale | Time to Recovery |
|----------------|-------------|------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Oil (acute) | Months–decades | Low–Medium | 10–1000 km | 2–10 years |
| POPs (PCBs, DDT) | Decades–centuries | Very high | Global | 30–100 years |
| Heavy Metals | Indefinite | High | Local–Regional | 50+ years |
| Macroplastics | 100s of years | Low (physical only) | Global gyres | Centuries |
| Microplastics | Indefinite (fragment) | Medium (vector) | Global | Permanent |
| Nutrients → Eutrophication | Days–weeks | Low | 10–100 km | Months–years |
| Sewage (organic) | Days | Low | 1–10 km | Days–weeks |
| Thermal | Hours (transient) | None | 1–5 km | Hours–days |
| Noise | Continuous | None | 10–1000 km | Immediate (when source stops) |
| Radioactive | Decades–millennia (isotope-dependent) | High | Local–Ocean basin | 30 years to > 10,000 years (Pu-239) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define marine pollution as per UNCLOS, Article 1(1)(4). List any four major types of marine pollution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** As per UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4), marine pollution is the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries and coastal waters) which results in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, impairment of quality for use of seawater, and reduction of amenities.

> **Four Major Types (0.375 marks each = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Oil Pollution** — from tankers and offshore platforms.
> 2. **Chemical Pollution** — Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) like PCBs, DDT.
> 3. **Heavy Metal Pollution** — Mercury, Lead, Cadmium, Arsenic from industrial effluents.
> 4. **Plastic Pollution** — macro- and microplastics from urban and fishing sources.

*(Any 4 of the 8–9 standard types accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between point source and non-point source marine pollution. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Point Source (1.5 marks):** A pollution source with a *single, identifiable, and locatable discharge point*. Discharges can be monitored and quantified at a single outfall.
> **Example:** Municipal sewage outfall pipe discharging at a specific coastal coordinate; an industrial effluent pipe from a fertilizer plant.

> **Non-Point Source (1.5 marks):** A diffuse pollution source spread over a wide geographic area, originating from multiple unspecified locations. Difficult to attribute to a single outfall; requires watershed-scale management.
> **Example:** Agricultural runoff carrying fertilizers and pesticides into a river delta; urban stormwater runoff carrying oil and litter from roads; atmospheric deposition of pollutants.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the major sources and effects of oil pollution in the marine environment. Describe the Fay–Houk–Schmidt (FAS) model of oil slick spreading. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of eutrophication in coastal waters. With the help of a flow diagram, describe the cascade of events leading to the formation of hypoxic dead zones. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 marks

**1. Major Sources of Oil Pollution (3 marks):**

| Source Category | Examples | Volume Characteristics |
|-----------------|----------|------------------------|
| **Tanker accidents** | Exxon Valdez (1989, 37,000 t), Prestige (2002) | Large, acute |
| **Offshore drilling** | Deepwater Horizon (2010, 4.9 M bbl) | Largest single spill |
| **Routine operations** | Bilge pumping, tank cleaning | Chronic, small per event |
| **Natural seeps** | Santa Barbara, Gulf of Mexico | ~ 0.1–0.2 Mt/year global |

**2. Effects of Oil Pollution (2 marks):**

- **Physical Smothering** — oil film blocks O₂ diffusion (~ 30% reduction at 1 µm) and UV penetration, killing plankton and intertidal organisms.
- **Chemical Toxicity** — PAHs dissolve in gill membranes → narcotic toxicity; long-term carcinogenic and mutagenic effects.
- **Bioaccumulation** — lipophilic PAHs concentrate in fatty tissues.
- **Long-term Sediment Incorporation** — heavy fractions persist in sediments for decades.

**3. FAS Model of Oil Slick Spreading (2 marks):**

The Fay–Houk–Schmidt model describes slick area evolution in **three successive regimes**:

**Regime I — Gravity–Inertia (early, t < ~ 1 hour):**

$$A(t) = k_1 \left( \frac{\Delta \rho \, g \, V}{t^2} \right)^{1/3} t^2 = k_1 (\Delta \rho \, g \, V)^{1/3} t^{4/3}$$

**Regime II — Gravity–Viscosity (intermediate):**

$$A(t) = k_2 \left( \frac{\Delta \rho \, g \, V^2}{\nu^{1/2}} \right)^{1/3} t^{3/4}$$

**Regime III — Surface Tension–Viscosity (late):**

$$A(t) = k_3 \left( \frac{\sigma^2 \, t^3}{\rho_w^2 \, \nu} \right)^{1/4}$$

where $k_1 \approx 1.5$, $k_2 \approx 0.025$, $k_3 \approx 2.0$ (calibrated), $V$ = spill volume, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity, $\Delta\rho$ = density difference, $\sigma$ = surface tension.

The transition time between Regime I and II is approximately:

$$t_1 = \left( \frac{V}{g' \nu} \right)^{1/2}$$

> **Engineering use:** The model determines optimum **booming strategy** and the time window for in-situ burning or chemical dispersant application.

---

### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 marks

**1. Definition of Eutrophication (1 mark):**

Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of a water body with **biostimulatory nutrients** (primarily nitrogen N and phosphorus P), leading to accelerated biological productivity and consequent ecological disturbance.

**2. Major Sources (1 mark):**
- Agricultural fertilizer runoff (NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻)
- Municipal sewage discharge
- Aquaculture waste
- Atmospheric N deposition (NOₓ from combustion)

**3. Cascade of Events (Flow Diagram, 3 marks):**

```
Nutrient Enrichment (N, P load ↑)
        ↓
Phytoplankton Bloom (chlorophyll-a ↑)
        ↓
Light Attenuation (Secchi depth ↓)
        ↓
Benthic Macrophyte Death (seagrass)
        ↓
Algal Senescence & Sinking
        ↓
Microbial Decomposition (BOD ↑)
        ↓
Oxygen Depletion (DO < 2 mg/L → Hypoxia)
        ↓
Benthic & Pelagic Faunal Mortality
        ↓
Dead Zone Formation
```

**4. Real-world Example (2 marks):**

The **Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone** is the largest in the Western Atlantic, with an area of **~ 16,000 km²** (varying 5,000–22,000 km²), fed by the **Mississippi River nutrient load** (~ 1.5 million tonnes N/year and ~ 100,000 tonnes P/year). The **Baltic Sea dead zone** spans ~ 70,000 km² due to nutrient over-enrichment from nine surrounding countries.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- Nutrient input caps via the **Nitrates Directive (EU, 1991)**.
- Constructed wetlands for N removal.
- Precision agriculture to reduce fertilizer runoff.
- Tertiary sewage treatment (P precipitation with FeCl₃ or alum).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Describe the mechanism of biomagnification in marine food chains, with a specific example using DDT. Calculate the approximate bioconcentration factor at each trophic level given the initial seawater concentration is 0.003 µg/L. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major sources, environmental behaviour, and ecological effects of plastic pollution in the ocean. Differentiate between macroplastics, microplastics, and nanoplastics. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition of Biomagnification (1 mark):**

Biomagnification is the progressive increase in the concentration of a persistent, lipophilic, non-biodegradable substance at successively higher trophic levels of a food chain.

**2. Why Biomagnification Occurs — Mechanism (2 marks):**

- The pollutant is **lipophilic** (fat-soluble) — stored in adipose tissue rather than excreted.
- The pollutant is **non-biodegradable** — accumulates over an organism's lifetime.
- At each predation step, the consumer assimilates ~ 80–90% of the prey's body burden while gaining only ~ 10% of the prey's mass (10% ecological efficiency).
- Net effect: BCF increases ~ 10× per trophic step.

**3. DDT Biomagnification Worked Example (3 marks):**

Starting from $C_{seawater} = 0.003 \, \mu g/L$:

| Trophic Level | Organism | Concentration (µg/L or µg/kg) | BCF |
|---------------|----------|-------------------------------|------|
| 0 | Seawater | 0.003 µg/L | 1 |
| 1 | Phytoplankton | 0.04 µg/kg | ~ 13,000 |
| 2 | Zooplankton | 0.5 µg/kg | ~ 167,000 |
| 3 | Small fish | 2.0 µg/kg | ~ 667,000 |
| 4 | Large fish | 10 µg/kg | ~ 3.3 million |
| 5 | Fish-eating bird (e.g., Bald eagle) | 25 µg/kg | ~ 8.3 million |

**Concentration amplification factor (top to base):** $\frac{25}{0.003} = 8333 \times$.

**4. Effect on Bird Population (1 mark):** DDT at 25 µg/kg in tissues caused **eggshell thinning** in raptors (Bald Eagle, Peregrine Falcon) — the population crashed in the 1950s–1970s, prompting the 1972 ban in the US.

---

### Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 marks

**1. Sources of Marine Plastic (1.5 marks):**
- **Land-based (80%):** urban litter, single-use packaging, mismanaged municipal solid waste.
- **Sea-based (20%):** abandoned/lost/discarded fishing gear ("ghost nets"), shipping waste, aquaculture.
- **Atmospheric:** microfibres from textile washing transported by wind.

**2. Classification by Size (1.5 marks):**

| Class | Size | Examples |
|-------|------|----------|
| **Macroplastic** | > 5 mm | Bags, bottles, fishing nets |
| **Mesoplastic** | 1–5 mm | Pellet "nurdles", pre-production resin |
| **Microplastic** | 1 µm – 5 mm | Microbeads, fragmentation debris |
| **Nanoplastic** | < 1 µm | Degradation fragments, weathering |

**3. Environmental Behaviour (Block Diagram, 2 marks):**

```
Plastic enters ocean
       ↓
Buoyant plastics drift via gyres (Great Pacific Garbage Patch ~ 1.6 M km²)
       ↓
UV + Wave action → Fragmentation (macro → micro → nano)
       ↓
Sinking (biofouled or dense polymers like PET, PVC) → benthic deposition
       ↓
Ingestion by zooplankton, fish, seabirds, turtles
       ↓
Chemical leaching (BPA, phthalates) + adsorbed POPs (vector effect)
       ↓
Endocrine disruption, gut blockage, false-saturation starvation
       ↓
Trophic transfer up food chain (microplastic in seafood → human)
```

**4. Ecological Effects (2 marks):**
- **Physical:** Entanglement of seals, turtles, cetaceans; gut impaction in seabirds.
- **Chemical:** Endocrine disruption via plasticizers; vector transport of POPs.
- **Habitat:** Ghost nets continue fishing for decades ("ghost fishing").
- **Human:** Microplastics detected in drinking water, salt, seafood, and human blood/placenta.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Definition marks are FREE — never skip them.** Examiners allocate 1–1.5 marks for a clean, citation-style definition (UNCLOS, IMO, EPA) before any elaboration. Students who jump straight to examples lose easy marks.
> 2. **Always state numerical values** in formulas — write down $k_2 \approx 0.025$, not just the symbol. KTU valuation gives credit for *substituted* formulas.
> 3. **Diagrams in Part B must be drawn (or described) — without one, you lose 1–2 marks.** Even a textual flow chart on the answer script suffices.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason for losing marks** in numerical sub-questions (e.g., confusing µg/L with mg/L). Show the conversion step explicitly.
> 5. **For biomagnification questions, present a TABLE** — it is the cleanest way to show 5 trophic levels and earn full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Types of Marine Pollution"**

- ☐ **UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4) definition** must be quoted verbatim for full definition marks.
- ☐ Remember the **8 main types** (Oil, Chemical, Heavy Metals, Plastic, Nutrient, Sewage, Thermal, Noise) + **Radioactive** as the 9th.
- ☐ **Three classification axes:** Source, Nature, Activity — know at least one example per axis.
- ☐ **Fay–Houk–Schmidt model** — three regimes, names and the power-law exponent for $A(t)$.
- ☐ **Streeter-Phelps equation** for DO sag — applies to organic/sewage pollution.
- ☐ **Biomagnification** is *not* the same as **bioaccumulation** — bioaccumulation is in one organism; biomagnification is across trophic levels.
- ☐ **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** threshold for "bioaccumulative" = **5000 L/kg** (REACH).
- ☐ **Sound intensity in water uses reference $I_0 = 0.67 \times 10^{-18}$ W/m²** — not the in-air reference.
- ☐ Speed of sound in seawater **c ≈ 1500 m/s** (use 1500 in all noise-propagation problems unless temperature/salinity/depth is given).
- ☐ **Microplastics < 5 mm; macroplastics > 5 mm; nanoplastics < 1 µm**.
- ☐ Eutrophication cascade ends in a **hypoxic dead zone (DO < 2 mg/L)**.
- ☐ **Minamata disease** = methylmercury; **Itai-itai disease** = cadmium.
- ☐ **FAS model constants:** $k_1 \approx 1.5$ (gravity–inertia), $k_2 \approx 0.025$ (gravity–viscosity), $k_3 \approx 2.0$ (surface-tension).
- ☐ **Stokes' law** for microplastic settling: $v_s = \frac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w) d_p^2}{18\mu}$.
- ☐ **First-order decay** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ and **half-life** $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$.
- ☐ **Great Pacific Garbage Patch** area ≈ 1.6 million km² (3× France); not a visible "island" but a dispersed gyre.
- ☐ **PCBs, DDT, dioxins** are the "dirty dozen" Stockholm Convention POPs (2001).
- ☐ **Tributyltin (TBT)** — banned globally as anti-fouling biocide under the **AFS Convention (2001)** effective 2008.
- ☐ For **14-mark Part B questions**, allocate: 1–2 marks for definition, 2–3 marks for sources, 2–3 marks for mechanism/effects, 1–2 marks for diagram, 2–3 marks for example/case study.
- ☐ Always show **units and significant figures** in numerical sub-parts.

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Types of Marine Pollution**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Sources of Marine Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various Sources of Marine Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Pollution (UNCLOS / IMO Definition)**
> *"The introduction by humans, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) which results in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine ecosystems, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing, impairment of quality for use of seawater, and reduction of amenities."* — United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), Article 1(1)(4).

A **Source of Marine Pollution** is therefore any *point*, *line*, *area*, or *diffuse* anthropogenic or natural pathway through which a contaminant (mass, energy, or biological agent) is introduced into the marine environment with the potential to alter its physical, chemical, or biological integrity.

### 1.2 Classification Hierarchy (As Per GESAMP & KTU Module 1)

The **Joint Group of Experts on the Scientific Aspects of Marine Pollution (GESAMP)** categorizes marine pollution sources into the following nine primary classes adopted by KTU Module 1:

| S.No | Source Category | Nature of Entry | Example Pollutants |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Land-Based Sources (LBS) | Rivers, canals, runoff, outfalls | Sewage, plastics, nutrients, heavy metals |
| 2 | Sea-Based Sources (SBS) | Ships, vessels, offshore platforms | Oil, ballast water, antifouling biocides |
| 3 | Dumping at Sea | Dredged material, sewage sludge, industrial waste | Heavy metals, organic matter, radionuclides |
| 4 | Offshore Mining & Drilling | Oil/gas platforms, seabed mining | Hydrocarbons, drill cuttings, suspended solids |
| 5 | Atmospheric Deposition | Airborne particulates, wet/dry fallout | CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ, POPs, mercury |
| 6 | Aquaculture | Fish/prawn farm discharges | Antibiotics, uneaten feed, feces, nutrients |
| 7 | Tourism & Recreation | Coastal resorts, cruise ships | Sewage, litter, sunscreens, fuel |
| 8 | Natural Sources | Erosion, volcanic, algal blooms | Sediments, hydrocarbons, biotoxins |
| 9 | Electromagnetic / Energy | Sonar, cables, thermal plumes | Heat, noise, EMF |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sink" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think of the Ocean as a Giant Kitchen Sink 🪠**
> Imagine a kitchen sink with **one faucet (the source)** dripping contaminants, but **one drain (dilution + dispersion)** that can only handle so much. When the *input rate* of contaminants exceeds the ocean's natural **assimilative capacity**, the sink overflows — and that overflow is **marine pollution**.
>
> - **Point source** = Single tap pouring dirty water (e.g., a factory pipe).
> - **Non-point (diffuse) source** = Water seeping in from many cracks (e.g., agricultural runoff from farms).
> - The "sink capacity" depends on **tidal flushing, current speed, depth, and biological recycling**.

This analogy helps clarify why some coastal bays (low flushing) are far more vulnerable than open oceans, even when receiving the same pollutant load.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Assimilative Capacity (AC):** The maximum amount of pollutant a water body can absorb without violating water quality standards. Units typically in **kg/day** or **mg/L**.
> - **Pollutant Load (L):** Total mass of contaminant discharged per unit time. Standard unit: **kg/day** or **tonnes/year**.
> - **Critical Concentration (C_crit):** The threshold above which ecological/human harm begins.
> - **Fate and Transport:** Describes where a pollutant goes (advection, diffusion, sedimentation, bioaccumulation) once released.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Land-Based Sources (LBS) — The Dominant Contributor (~80%)

**Operational Mechanism:**
- Industrial effluents → discharged via coastal outfalls.
- Domestic sewage → treated/untreated municipal waste via rivers.
- Agricultural runoff → fertilizers (N, P), pesticides (organochlorines), soil sediments.

**Key 'Why' & 'How':**
- *Why dominant?* Coastal urbanization drives 70–80% of marine litter and nutrient loading globally (UNEP, 2021).
- *How does it reach the sea?* Via **riverine transport**, **sub-surface groundwater seepage**, and **overland stormwater flow** during monsoon events.

**Pollutants Released:**
- **BOD/COD load**, **nutrients (N, P)** → eutrophication & hypoxia.
- **Heavy metals:** Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd) — bioaccumulative, toxic.
- **Plastics & microplastics** — persistent, sorb hydrophobic contaminants.

---

### 2.1.2 Sea-Based Sources (SBS)

**Components:**
1. **Operational discharges** — bilge water, sewage, gray water from vessels.
2. **Accidental discharges** — oil spills (e.g., MV Wakashio, 2020; MV X-Press Pearl, 2021).
3. **Ballast water** — invasive species translocation (IMO BWM Convention).
4. **Antifouling paints** — tributyltin (TBT), now banned; copper-based replacements.

**Mechanism of Oil Spill Fate (ITOPF Model):**

$$ \text{Spill Fate} \rightarrow \text{Spreading} \rightarrow \text{Drift} \rightarrow \text{Dispersion} \rightarrow \text{Emulsification} \rightarrow \text{Evaporation} \rightarrow \text{Photo-oxidation} \rightarrow \text{Sedimentation} \rightarrow \text{Biological degradation} $$

---

### 2.1.3 Dumping at Sea (Convention: London Protocol 1996)

- Permitted only for **dredged material, sewage sludge, fish waste, inert geological material**.
- **Banned substances:** organohalogens, mercury, cadmium, oils, high-level radioactive waste, plastics.

---

### 2.1.4 Atmospheric Deposition

- Accounts for **~30% of global trace metal input** to oceans (Duce et al.).
- Long-range transport via **aerosols** of industrial origin, **biomass burning**, and **volcanic ash**.

---

### 2.1.5 Offshore Installations

- **Produced water** (water co-extracted with oil) — contains dispersed oil, heavy metals, radionuclides (naturally occurring radioactive material — NORM).
- **Drill cuttings** — coated with drilling muds (barite, bentonite, hydrocarbons).

---

### 2.1.6 Aquaculture

- Discharge of **uneaten feed (~20–30% loss)**, fish feces, antibiotics (e.g., oxytetracycline), parasiticides.
- Causes localized **organic enrichment, anoxia, and drug resistance gene proliferation**.

---

### 2.1.7 Natural Sources

- **Hydrocarbon seeps** — natural underwater oil/gas seeps (e.g., Gulf of Mexico).
- **Coastal erosion & riverine sediment transport.**
- **Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs)** — biotoxins (saxitoxin, domoic acid).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $$ L = Q \times C $$ | Pollutant Load = Flow × Concentration | kg/day |
| 2 | $$ C_{\text{final}} = \frac{L}{Q_{\text{total}}} $$ | Dilution equation | mg/L |
| 3 | $$ C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt} $$ | First-order decay of pollutant | mg/L |
| 4 | $$ \text{BOD}_t = \text{BOD}_u \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right) $$ | Streeter-Phelps BOD equation | mg/L |
| 5 | $$ D(t) = \frac{k_1 \cdot L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t} $$ | Dissolved Oxygen Sag (Streeter-Phelps) | mg/L |
| 6 | $$ t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) $$ | Time to critical DO (minimum) | days |
| 7 | $$ C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{Q_s C_s + Q_d C_d}{Q_s + Q_d} $$ | Mixing concentration of two streams | mg/L |
| 8 | $$ \text{FM} = \frac{C}{C_{\text{sat}}} $$ | Fraction of saturation (for DO) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $$ \eta = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100 $$ | Removal efficiency of a treatment unit | % |
| 10 | $$ \tau = \frac{V}{Q} $$ | Hydraulic retention / flushing time | days |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s or MLD)
- $C$ = pollutant concentration (mg/L)
- $k, k_1, k_2$ = reaction rate constants (day⁻¹)
- $L_0$ = ultimate BOD (mg/L)
- $D_0$ = initial DO deficit (mg/L)
- $V$ = volume of receiving water body

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These source-characterization equations are foundational for:

- **Designing Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)** for coastal industries.
- **Preparing Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** under India's EIA Notification 2006.
- **Modeling oil spill trajectories** (e.g., GNOME, MOTHY models) for coast guard response.
- **Establishing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)** based on assimilative capacity zoning.
- **Designing ballast water management systems** per IMO D-2 Standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass Balance for a Coastal Discharge (Pollutant Load)

> **Problem Statement:** A coastal industry discharges effluent at rate **$Q_d = 5$ m³/min** with pollutant concentration **$C_d = 250$ mg/L**. The receiving seawater has flow **$Q_s = 500$ m³/min** with background concentration **$C_s = 2$ mg/L**. Calculate the (a) pollutant load discharged per day and (b) the mixed downstream concentration. Assume complete and instantaneous mixing.

### Step 1: Unit Conversion (Critical Step)

Convert effluent flow rate from m³/min to m³/day.

$$Q_d = 5 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{min}} \times 1440 \, \frac{\text{min}}{\text{day}} = 7200 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}}$$

Convert seawater flow rate from m³/min to m³/day.

$$Q_s = 500 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{min}} \times 1440 \, \frac{\text{min}}{\text{day}} = 7.2 \times 10^5 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}}$$

### Step 2: Compute Pollutant Load (Part a)

Apply the fundamental load equation:

$$L = Q_d \times C_d$$

Substitute values (with unit conversion: $C_d = 250 \, \text{mg/L} = 250 \, \text{g/m}^3 = 0.25 \, \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$L = 7200 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{day}} \times 0.25 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$$

$$L = 1800 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}}$$

$$\boxed{L = 1800 \, \text{kg/day} = 1.8 \, \text{tonnes/day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** $1 \, \text{mg/L} = 1 \, \text{g/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/m}^3$. This is essential to ensure dimensional consistency.

### Step 3: Compute Mixed Downstream Concentration (Part b)

Apply the steady-state mixing equation:

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{Q_s C_s + Q_d C_d}{Q_s + Q_d}$$

Substitute in consistent units (mg/L):

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{(7.2 \times 10^5)(2) + (7200)(250)}{(7.2 \times 10^5) + (7200)}$$

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^6 + 1.8 \times 10^6}{7.272 \times 10^5}$$

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{3.24 \times 10^6}{7.272 \times 10^5}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{mix}} \approx 4.456 \, \text{mg/L}}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

- The effluent contributes **$\approx 55.5\%$** of the total downstream load despite being only **$\approx 0.99\%$** of the total flow.
- This shows the **disproportionate impact** of even small-volume, high-concentration discharges — a recurring KTU valuation point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Flushing Time for a Coastal Bay

> **Problem Statement:** A semi-enclosed coastal bay has volume **$V = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m³** and receives a tidal inflow of **$Q = 3 \times 10^6$ m³/day**. A pollutant with first-order decay constant **$k = 0.05$ day⁻¹** is released. Determine (a) the hydraulic flushing time, (b) the fraction of pollutant remaining after one flushing time.

### Step 1: Flushing Time

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q}$$

$$\tau = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8 \, \text{m}^3}{3 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 50 \, \text{days}}$$

### Step 2: Fraction Remaining After Time t

Apply first-order decay:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

At $t = \tau = 50$ days:

$$\frac{C(\tau)}{C_0} = e^{-(0.05)(50)} = e^{-2.5}$$

$$e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(\tau)}{C_0} \approx 8.21\%}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

After **one flushing time (50 days)**, only **~8.2%** of the original pollutant mass remains. Thus, **$1 - 0.0821 = 91.8\%$** of pollutant has either flushed out or been degraded.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dilution Factor for Ocean Outfall Discharge

> **Problem Statement:** A submarine outfall discharges at depth **$h = 25$ m**. The diffuser has port diameter **$d = 0.1$ m**, **$n = 20$ ports**, and total flow **$Q = 0.5$ m³/s**. Seawater density **$\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³**, effluent density **$\rho_e = 998$ kg/m³**. Compute initial dilution **$S_i$** using the simplified Cederwall formula:

$$S_i = \frac{0.27 \, (0.1 n)^{1/3} \cdot h}{Q^{1/3}}$$

### Step 1: Compute Composite Parameter

$$(0.1 n) = (0.1)(20) = 2$$

### Step 2: Compute Numerator

$$0.27 \cdot (2)^{1/3} \cdot 25 = 0.27 \cdot 1.26 \cdot 25 = 8.505$$

### Step 3: Compute Denominator

$$Q^{1/3} = (0.5)^{1/3} = 0.7937$$

### Step 4: Final Calculation

$$S_i = \frac{8.505}{0.7937}$$

$$\boxed{S_i \approx 10.72}$$

This means at the water surface (or trapping level), pollutant concentration is reduced by **~10.72×** relative to the port discharge.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source Contribution Assessment

| Source Type | % Global Marine Pollution | Persistency | Treatability | KTU Exam Weightage |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Land-based (Sewage, Nutrients) | ~70–80% | Variable | High (Tertiary) | ★★★★★ |
| Shipping / Oil Spills | ~12% | High | Low | ★★★★★ |
| Atmospheric | ~5–10% | High (POPs) | Difficult | ★★★ |
| Dumping | ~2–5% | Variable | Medium | ★★ |
| Offshore Oil & Gas | ~1–2% | High | Medium | ★★★ |
| Aquaculture | ~1% | Low–Medium | High | ★★ |
| Natural | Negligible | Low | N/A | ★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
