<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Classify the various sources of marine pollution. Briefly explain land-based and sea-based sources with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Marine pollution sources are broadly classified into **nine categories** as per GESAMP guidelines adopted by KTU Module 1:

**(i) Land-Based Sources (LBS):** Constitute ~80% of marine pollution globally. These include:
- *Domestic sewage* discharged via coastal outfalls (carries pathogens, nutrients, BOD load).
- *Industrial effluents* containing heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd), dyes, and toxic chemicals.
- *Agricultural runoff* with fertilizers, pesticides, and soil sediment.
- *Plastic litter* via rivers and stormwater drainage.

**(ii) Sea-Based Sources (SBS):** Originate from maritime activities:
- *Operational discharges* (bilge water, gray water, sewage from ships).
- *Accidental oil spills* (e.g., MV Wakashio, 2020 in Mauritius).
- *Ballast water* — introduces invasive aquatic species.
- *Antifouling paints* (TBT, copper biocides).

> KTU Key Point: LBS dominates quantitatively; SBS dominates in acute/high-profile incidents.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by ocean dumping? List the materials prohibited under the London Protocol, 1996.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ocean Dumping** is the deliberate disposal of wastes into the marine environment from vessels, aircraft, platforms, or other man-made structures at sea. It is regulated internationally by the **London Convention (1972)** and its stricter successor, the **London Protocol (1996)**.

**Prohibited substances under the London Protocol:**
1. Organohalogen compounds (e.g., DDT, PCBs).
2. Mercury and mercury compounds.
3. Cadmium and cadmium compounds.
4. Persistent plastics and other synthetic materials.
5. Crude oil, fuel oil, oily wastes, and refined products.
6. High-level radioactive wastes.
7. Chemical and biological warfare agents.
8. Industrial waste containing the above materials.

**Permitted (with assessment):** Dredged material, sewage sludge, fish-processing waste, inert geological material, vessels/platforms.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major sources of marine pollution with a focus on **land-based, atmospheric, and offshore sources**. Add notes on the typical pollutants released by each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coastal industry releases **$Q_d = 8$ m³/min** of effluent with pollutant concentration **$C_d = 400$ mg/L** into a tidal creek carrying **$Q_s = 600$ m³/min** of seawater with background **$C_s = 3$ mg/L**. Calculate **(i)** the daily pollutant load discharged, and **(ii)** the mixed downstream concentration after complete mixing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) Land-Based Sources (LBS):**
Land-based sources dominate marine pollution, contributing ~70–80% by mass.
- **Domestic sewage:** BOD, COD, pathogens (coliforms), nutrients (N, P) → coastal eutrophication.
- **Industrial discharges:** Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd), dyes, PCBs, phenols → bioaccumulation, toxicity.
- **Agricultural runoff:** Fertilizers (urea, DAP) and pesticides (organochlorines, organophosphates) → nutrient over-enrichment and biomagnification.
- **Plastic litter & microplastics:** Persistent, adsorb hydrophobic contaminants, ingested by marine fauna.

**(ii) Atmospheric Sources:**
Atmospheric deposition accounts for ~30% of trace metals and a major fraction of POPs entering oceans.
- **Aerosol transport** of industrial SO₂, NOₓ, CO₂, fly ash, mercury vapor.
- **Biomass burning** releases PAHs and dioxins.
- **Long-range transport** delivers pollutants thousands of km from source (e.g., Saharan dust → Atlantic).
- *Key pollutants:* Mercury (Hg), lead (Pb), PCBs, DDT, dioxins, furans.

**(iii) Offshore Sources:**
- **Produced water:** Saline water co-extracted with oil → contains dispersed oil, BTEX, heavy metals, NORM (Naturally Occurring Radioactive Material).
- **Drill cuttings** coated with drilling muds (barite, bentonite, hydrocarbons).
- **Accidental spills** during blowouts (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, 2010 — ~4.9 million barrels).
- **Routine operational discharges** of deck drainage, sanitary waste, and ballast.

> [!NOTE]
> Each sub-source should be paired with **two specific pollutants** for full marks in KTU valuation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $Q_d = 8 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$
- $C_d = 400 \, \text{mg/L}$
- $Q_s = 600 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}$
- $C_s = 3 \, \text{mg/L}$

**Step 1: Convert flows to m³/day.**

$$Q_d = 8 \times 1440 = 11{,}520 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

$$Q_s = 600 \times 1440 = 864{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Convert concentration for load calculation.**

$$C_d = 400 \, \text{mg/L} = 0.4 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate Daily Pollutant Load.**

$$L = Q_d \times C_d = 11{,}520 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 0.4 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{L = 4608 \, \text{kg/day} = 4.608 \, \text{tonnes/day}}$$

**Step 4: Apply Mixing Equation.**

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{Q_s C_s + Q_d C_d}{Q_s + Q_d} = \frac{(864{,}000)(3) + (11{,}520)(400)}{864{,}000 + 11{,}520}$$

$$C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{2{,}592{,}000 + 4{,}608{,}000}{875{,}520} = \frac{7{,}200{,}000}{875{,}520}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{mix}} \approx 8.224 \, \text{mg/L}}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

The effluent, although only **1.32%** of total flow, raises the pollutant concentration from 3 mg/L to **~8.22 mg/L** — a **174% increase**. This demonstrates the disproportionate impact of concentrated point-source discharges.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **major sources of marine pollution** and explain how each contributes to **bioaccumulation and biomagnification** in the marine food chain. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bay of volume **$V = 2 \times 10^8$ m³** receives freshwater inflow **$Q = 5 \times 10^6$ m³/day**. A pollutant with first-order decay constant **$k = 0.04$ day⁻¹** is discharged continuously. Determine **(i)** the hydraulic flushing time, and **(ii)** the time required for the pollutant concentration to reduce to **5%** of its initial value. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sources of Marine Pollution and Bioaccumulation Pathways:**

**(i) Industrial Discharges — Heavy Metals:**
- Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd) discharged via rivers and outfalls.
- **Bioaccumulation:** Algae and phytoplankton absorb dissolved metals → concentrated 100×–1000× in tissues.
- **Biomagnification:** As small fish eat algae, and larger fish eat small fish, metal concentration escalates up the trophic ladder — culminating in **high levels in top predators (tuna, sharks, humans)**.

**(ii) Agricultural Runoff — Pesticides:**
- Persistent organic pollutants (POPs) like **DDT, dieldrin, endrin** enter via rivers.
- Highly lipophilic → accumulate in fatty tissues of fish, marine mammals, and seabirds.
- Classic example: DDT caused eggshell thinning in the **Bald Eagle** leading to near-extinction.

**(iii) Plastic Debris and Microplastics:**
- Ingested by zooplankton, fish, turtles, seabirds.
- Microplastics (<5 mm) accumulate in gut tissues and translocate to muscle.
- They also **adsorb hydrophobic POPs**, acting as vectors for chemical transfer up the food chain.

**(iv) Sewage Discharge — Pathogens & Nutrients:**
- Eutrophication → algal blooms → death → bacterial decomposition → **hypoxia (dead zones)** in bottom waters.
- Affects benthic organisms and disrupts the entire food web.

**Food Chain Amplification Diagram (Verbal):**
> Algae $(C_0)$ → Zooplankton $(10 C_0)$ → Small Fish $(100 C_0)$ → Large Fish $(1000 C_0)$ → Human $(10{,}000 C_0)$

This shows a **10,000× concentration** from primary producer to apex consumer — a typical KTU descriptive answer structure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V = 2 \times 10^8 \, \text{m}^3$
- $Q = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $k = 0.04 \, \text{day}^{-1}$
- Target: $C/C_0 = 0.05$

**(i) Hydraulic Flushing Time:**

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 40 \, \text{days}}$$

**(ii) Time to Reduce to 5% of Initial:**

Apply first-order decay:

$$\frac{C(t)}{C_0} = e^{-kt}$$

Take natural log on both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -kt$$

$$t = -\frac{1}{k}\ln\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$t = -\frac{1}{0.04}\ln(0.05) = -25 \times (-2.9957)$$

$$t = 25 \times 2.9957$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 74.89 \, \text{days} \approx 75 \, \text{days}}$$

**Interpretation:** It takes **~1.87 flushing times** (75/40) to achieve 95% removal of the pollutant. This highlights that **flushing alone is insufficient** for persistent pollutants — degradation kinetics must be considered.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Most students lose 2–3 marks for not converting m³/min to m³/day or mg/L to kg/m³.
> 2. **Confusing load with concentration:** Load ($L$) has units of mass/time (kg/day); concentration ($C$) is mass/volume (mg/L).
> 3. **Missing the decay constant $k$:** In flushing-time problems, students often forget to apply $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$.
> 4. **Inadequate examples:** For descriptive 7-mark questions, always list **at least two specific pollutants** per source. Generic answers like "industrial waste" fetch only partial marks.
> 5. **Diagrams:** A neat flowchart of sources fetches **1–2 grace marks** even if the text is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Yield Quick Recall — Marine Pollution Sources**

- ⭐ **LBS = ~80%** of total marine pollution globally — remember this statistic.
- ⭐ **GESAMP classification** has **9 source categories** — know all 9.
- ⭐ **UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4)** is the formal legal definition — quote verbatim in 3-mark questions.
- ⭐ **MARPOL 73/78** regulates shipping; **London Protocol 1996** regulates dumping — don't confuse them.
- ⭐ **Land-based vs. Sea-based:** LBS = chronic & voluminous; SBS = acute & high-impact (oil spills).
- ⭐ **Atmospheric deposition** is the major pathway for **mercury and POPs** to remote oceans.
- ⭐ **Offshore sources:** Produced water is the largest volume discharge from oil platforms.
- ⭐ **Aquaculture** discharge causes **localized anoxia** and **antibiotic resistance**.
- ⭐ **Bioaccumulation** = increase within an organism; **Biomagnification** = increase along trophic levels.
- ⭐ **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Pollutant Load: $L = Q \times C$
  - Mixing: $C_{\text{mix}} = \frac{Q_s C_s + Q_d C_d}{Q_s + Q_d}$
  - Flushing: $\tau = V/Q$
  - Decay: $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$
- ⭐ **Prohibited under London Protocol:** Plastics, mercury, cadmium, oils, organohalogens, high-level radioactive waste.
- ⭐ **Ballast water management** is governed by **IMO BWM Convention (2004, in force 2017)**.
- ⭐ **Antifouling biocide TBT** has caused **imposex in marine snails** — iconic ecotoxicology case study.
- ⭐ **Indian regulation:** CRZ Notification (2019) under Environment Protection Act, 1986.
- ⭐ **Numerical skill:** Always convert mg/L → kg/m³ (×10⁻³) before multiplying with m³/day for load in kg/day.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone** is defined (per UNEP, 1982 / GESAMP framework) as the **introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine (including coastal) environment which results in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing, impairment of the quality of sea water for use, and reduction of amenities.**

The **Coastal Zone** is the interface region extending from the landward limit of marine influence (highest astronomical tide line) seaward to the outer edge of the continental shelf, characteristically defined as a strip of land and adjacent sea of width **≈ 100 m vertical elevation contour to a depth of 200 m** or alternatively a horizontal belt of roughly **0–60 km from the shoreline**. It includes estuaries, mangroves, lagoons, coral reefs, tidal flats, deltas, and nearshore waters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea), the **Territorial Sea** extends **12 nautical miles** from the baseline, and the **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)** extends **200 nautical miles (≈ 370.4 km)**. Coastal pollution is most acute in the inner 12 nm belt because of concentrated anthropogenic discharges.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Sponge"

Imagine a city garbage bin (the ocean basin) connected to a half-open kitchen tap (the river inflow). Now imagine the bin is only **3 metres wide** (the narrow coastal strip) where the waste is dumped before it can spread. Because the bin's mouth is squeezed, **concentrations rise sharply**, smell builds up, and bacteria thrive — exactly what happens in a coastal zone. The deeper ocean has dilution capacity, but the coastal squeeze (rising population, ports, aquaculture, industry) means every gram of pollutant counts many times over.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Constant / Standard | Symbol | Value / Limit | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Salinity of typical coastal water | S | 30 – 35 ‰ (psu) | Open-ocean reference |
| Brackish water salinity | S | 0.5 – 30 ‰ | Venice System 1958 |
| Dissolved Oxygen saturation (15 °C, 35 ‰) | DO_sat | ≈ 7.85 mg/L | Benson & Krause |
| BOD₅ of domestic sewage (untreated) | BOD₅ | 200 – 400 mg/L | Metcalf & Eddy |
| COD/BOD₅ ratio (municipal) | — | ≈ 2.0 – 2.5 | Standard reference |
| WHO Coliform limit (recreational) | — | **< 200 faecal coliforms / 100 mL** | WHO 2003 |
| Heavy-metal (Pb) in seawater (chronic) | — | **8.5 µg/L** | US-EPA CMC |
| Tidal flushing time (semi-enclosed bays) | T_f | 1 – 30 days | Site-specific |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Coastal Marine Pollution

A clean analysis of the topic splits into four tightly coupled pillars, all directly aligned with KTU 2024 Module 1 outcomes (CO1: *Identify and classify marine pollutants in the coastal zone*).

**Pillar A — Sources (Where the pollution originates)**
- **Land-Based Sources (LBS):** ≈ **80%** of marine pollution (UNEP, GESAMP). Includes:
  - Municipal sewage & wastewater
  - Industrial effluents (textile, tannery, petrochemical, pharmaceutical)
  - Agricultural runoff (fertilizer-N, P, pesticides)
  - River-borne sediment (≈ 20 billion tonnes/year globally)
- **Sea-Based Sources:** shipping discharges, ballast water, bilge oil, antifouling paints (TBT, Cu), aquaculture wastes, offshore platforms.
- **Atmospheric Deposition:** combustion products, heavy metals (Hg, Pb), persistent organic pollutants (POPs).
- **Dumping & Dredging:** coastal landfills, mine tailings, seabed disposal.

**Pillar B — Pollutant Categories (What the pollution is)**
- **Nutrients (N, P, Si)** → Eutrophication, algal blooms, hypoxia
- **Heavy Metals** → Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr, As, Cu, Zn (bioaccumulative, toxic at trace levels)
- **Petroleum Hydrocarbons** → chronic and acute toxicity
- **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** → PCBs, DDT, dioxins, PAHs
- **Plastic & Microplastic Debris**
- **Pathogens** → faecal coliforms, *E. coli*, *Vibrio*
- **Thermal Discharge** → power-plant cooling water
- **Radionuclides** → coastal nuclear plants, reprocessing effluents
- **Suspended Sediments** → turbidity, light attenuation

**Pillar C — Pathways & Transport (How it moves)**
- Advection (tidal currents, long-shore drift, river plumes)
- Diffusion & turbulent dispersion
- Sedimentation & resuspension
- Bioaccumulation and trophic transfer
- Volatilisation and atmospheric re-deposition

**Pillar D — Impacts (What it does)**
- **Hypoxia / Anoxia** (DO < 2 mg/L → "dead zones")
- Loss of biodiversity, fishery collapse
- Coral bleaching (synergy with thermal stress)
- Public-health risk from contaminated seafood
- Economic loss to tourism and aquaculture

### 2.2 The Streeter–Phelps Framework — Self-Purification Capacity

The most-tested analytical tool in KTU exams for this topic is the **Streeter–Phelps dissolved-oxygen sag model** (1925). It quantifies the balance between deoxygenation (organic-load decay) and re-aeration (atmospheric oxygen uptake) in a receiving water body — typically a river, estuary, or coastal outfall.

**Governing differential equations:**

$$ \frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L $$

$$ \frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L - k_2 D $$

Where:
- $L$ = ultimate BOD remaining at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $D$ = dissolved-oxygen deficit, $D = DO_{sat} - DO$ (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typical **0.1 – 0.5 day⁻¹** at 20 °C
- $k_2$ = reaeration rate constant (day⁻¹), typical **0.2 – 5.0 day⁻¹** depending on turbulence

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield equations for Module 1 are consolidated below. Memorise the units and the typical ranges; examiners frequently award partial credit for correct units alone.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$ | First-order BOD decay | $L$ in mg/L; $t$ in days |
| 2 | $D_t = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \, e^{-k_2 t}$ | Streeter–Phelps DO deficit | $D$ in mg/L; $t$ in days |
| 3 | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - D_0 \dfrac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right]$ | Critical time (max deficit) | days |
| 4 | $D_c = \dfrac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \, e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | Critical (maximum) deficit | mg/L |
| 5 | $C_{mix} = \dfrac{Q_s C_s + Q_r C_r}{Q_s + Q_r}$ | Mixing-zone concentration (steady state) | mg/L |
| 6 | $T_f = \dfrac{V}{P}$ | Tidal flushing time (volume/ tidal prism) | days |
| 7 | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $LF = \dfrac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$ | Biomagnification / Lipid factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k_{1,T} = k_{1,20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ | Temperature correction (θ ≈ 1.047) | day⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ | Standard seawater density (35 ‰, 15 °C) | kg/m³ |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) engineers to design outfall diffusers in Kochi backwaters, to set industrial discharge consents, and to predict the impact of dredging on dissolved-oxygen profiles in estuaries such as the Vembanad Lake system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order BOD Decay

We start from the empirical observation that the **rate of oxidation of organic matter is proportional to the remaining organic matter**. This is the classical first-order kinetics assumption used in sanitary engineering.

**Step 1: Write the rate equation.**

$$ \frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L $$

The negative sign indicates that the remaining BOD ($L$) decreases as time ($t$) increases.

**Step 2: Separate variables.**

$$ \frac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \, dt $$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$ and $L_0$ to $L_t$.**

$$ \int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt $$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$ \big[\ln L\big]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k_1 \, t $$

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t $$

**Step 5: Exponentiate to obtain the closed-form solution.**

$$ L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t} \quad \text{...(Equation 1, Module 1)} $$

> **Engineering Meaning:** If a wastewater has an initial BOD $L_0$ of 250 mg/L and $k_1 = 0.23$ day⁻¹, then at $t = 5$ days, $L_5 = 250 \, e^{-0.23 \times 5} = 250 \times e^{-1.15} = 250 \times 0.3166 \approx 79.2$ mg/L. This is the classical BOD₅ measurement.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Streeter–Phelps Dissolved-Oxygen Deficit Equation

This is the most-asked derivation in KTU 2024 ESE for environmental engineering streams. We solve two coupled first-order ODEs.

**Step 1: Solution for $L(t)$.** Equation (1) is the solution: $L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$.

**Step 2: Substitute $L_t$ into the deficit equation.**

$$ \frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 D = k_1 L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t} $$

**Step 3: Identify the integrating factor.** For a linear first-order ODE of the form $D' + P(t)D = Q(t)$, the integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{\int P(t)dt}$.

$$ \mu(t) = e^{\int k_2 \, dt} = e^{k_2 t} $$

**Step 4: Multiply the equation by $\mu(t)$.**

$$ e^{k_2 t}\frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 e^{k_2 t} D = k_1 L_0 \, e^{(k_2 - k_1)t} $$

**Step 5: Recognise the left-hand side as the derivative of a product.**

$$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(D \, e^{k_2 t}\right) = k_1 L_0 \, e^{(k_2 - k_1)t} $$

**Step 6: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$ D(t)\,e^{k_2 t} - D(0) = k_1 L_0 \int_0^t e^{(k_2 - k_1)\tau} d\tau $$

**Step 7: Evaluate the integral on the right-hand side.**

$$ \int_0^t e^{(k_2 - k_1)\tau} d\tau = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\left[e^{(k_2 - k_1)\tau}\right]_0^t = \frac{e^{(k_2 - k_1)t} - 1}{k_2 - k_1} $$

**Step 8: Substitute back and solve for $D(t)$.**

$$ D(t) = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 \, e^{-k_2 t} \quad \text{...(Equation 2, Module 1)} $$

> **Engineering Meaning:** This equation tells the engineer how far the DO will dip below saturation at any distance downstream of a coastal outfall. The minimum DO determines whether fish survive — Kerala's backwater fisheries hinge on these calculations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Time and Critical Deficit

To find the point of maximum DO depletion, set $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = 0$ at $t = t_c$.

**Step 1: Differentiate the Streeter–Phelps equation with respect to $t$.**

$$ \frac{dD}{dt} = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) - k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t} $$

**Step 2: Set the derivative to zero.**

$$ \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t_c} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t_c}\right) = k_2 D_0 e^{-k_2 t_c} $$

**Step 3: Divide both sides by $e^{-k_2 t_c}$ and rearrange.**

$$ \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-(k_1 - k_2) t_c} + k_2\right) = k_2 D_0 $$

**Step 4: Solve for $e^{-(k_1 - k_2) t_c}$.**

$$ e^{-(k_1 - k_2) t_c} = \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0}\left(k_2 D_0 - \frac{k_1 k_2 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\right) \cdot (-1) $$

After algebraic simplification, the standard closed-form is:

$$ t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1}\ln\!\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\left(1 - D_0 \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right] \quad \text{...(Equation 3, Module 1)} $$

**Step 5: Substitute $t_c$ back into Equation (2) to obtain the critical (maximum) deficit.**

$$ D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} \, e^{-k_1 t_c} \quad \text{...(Equation 4, Module 1)} $$

> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A coastal outfall discharges wastewater with $L_0 = 50$ mg/L, $D_0 = 2$ mg/L, $k_1 = 0.3$ day⁻¹, $k_2 = 0.6$ day⁻¹. Then
> $t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.3}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.6}{0.3}\left(1 - 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.3}{0.3 \cdot 50}\right)\right] = \dfrac{1}{0.3}\ln(2 \cdot 0.96) = \dfrac{1}{0.3}\ln(1.92) = \dfrac{0.652}{0.3} \approx 2.17$ days.
> $D_c = \dfrac{0.3 \cdot 50}{0.6} e^{-0.3 \cdot 2.17} = 25 \cdot e^{-0.651} = 25 \cdot 0.521 \approx 13.0$ mg/L.
> Since $DO_{sat} \approx 7.85$ mg/L, the receiving water has $DO_{min} = 7.85 - 13.0 < 0$ → severe anoxia is predicted.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollution-Source-Impact Linkage

| Pollution Source | Primary Pollutant | Coastal Zone Impact | Sensitive Receptor in Kerala |
|---|---|---|---|
| Municipal sewage (Kochi, Calicut) | BOD, nutrients, faecal coliforms | Hypoxia, eutrophication, gastro-intestinal illness | Vembanad Lake, Ashtamudi Estuary |
| Industrial effluents (Eloor I.E.) | Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cr) | Bioaccumulation in fish, sediment contamination | Periyar River estuary |
| Agricultural runoff (Kuttanad) | N, P, pesticides | Algal blooms, paddy-field pesticide loading | Kuttanad paddy-belly below MSL |
| Shipping (Cochin Port) | Bilge oil, ballast, TBT | Chronic oil films, invasive species, imposex in gastropods | Approach channel, port waters |
| Aquaculture ponds | Nitrogen, antibiotics, feed wastes | Benthic anoxia, antibiotic resistance | Pokkali fields, Vypeen coast |
| Atmospheric (NE monsoon) | POPs, dust-bound metals | Long-range deposition on canopy, mangrove soils | Mangroves of Kannur |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Source → Pathway → Coastal Zone → Impact Flow Architecture

The following block-level functional flow diagram maps the complete chain of interactions, since a physical schematic of the coastal zone spans multiple processes. The diagram is constructed using purely alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Land Based Sources]:::src --> path1[River Discharge and Runoff]
    srcB[Sea Based Sources]:::src --> path2[Shipping Ballast Bilge]
    srcC[Atmospheric Deposition]:::src --> path3[Wet and Dry Fallout]
    srcD[Dumping and Dredging]:::src --> path4[Coastal Landfill Spoil]

    path1 --> zone1[Estuaries]:::zone
    path2 --> zone2[Ports and Harbours]:::zone
    path3 --> zone3[Mangroves and Lagoons]:::zone
    path4 --> zone4[Continental Shelf]:::zone

    zone1 --> pol1[Nutrients BOD Coliforms]:::pol
    zone2 --> pol2[Oil Hydrocarons TBT]:::pol
    zone3 --> pol3[POPs Heavy Metals]:::pol
    zone4 --> pol4[Suspended Sediments Nutrients]:::pol

    pol1 --> imp1[Hypoxia Fish Kills]:::imp
    pol2 --> imp2[Chronic Toxicity Imposex]:::imp
    pol3 --> imp3[Bioaccumulation Biomagnification]:::imp
    pol4 --> imp4[Turbidity Smothering of Benthos]:::imp

    imp1 --> mit1[Treatment Plants Outfall Diffusers]:::mit
    imp2 --> mit2[Ballast Water Exchange MARPOL]:::mit
    imp3 --> mit3[Source Control Mangrove Buffers]:::mit
    imp4 --> mit4[Dredge Silt Management BMP]:::mit

    classDef src fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef pol fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000
    classDef imp fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef mit fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Streeter–Phelps DO Sag Curve Schematic

The diagram below illustrates the conceptual dissolved-oxygen profile along a coastal receiving water downstream of a wastewater outfall.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outfall Point]:::start --> B[Initial High DO Deficit D0]:::drop
    B --> C[Deoxygenation Dominates]:::drop
    C --> D[Critical Point Minimum DO]:::critical
    D --> E[Reaeration Dominates]:::recover
    E --> F[Recovery to Background DO]:::recover

    classDef start fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#f39c12,stroke:#7e5109,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef recover fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Coastal Pollution Decision System

For a comprehensive engineering view, the coastal zone is treated as a multi-stage system. Each block represents a process module that the KTU examiner will test under CO2 (analyse) and CO3 (evaluate).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    block1[INPUT BLOCK: Source Inventory]:::in --> block2[POLLUTANT BLOCK: Speciation and Loading]:::in
    block2 --> block3[TRANSPORT BLOCK: Advection Diffusion Dispersion]:::mid
    block3 --> block4[FATE BLOCK: Degradation Sedimentation Volatilisation]:::mid
    block4 --> block5[IMPACT BLOCK: DO pH Toxicity Bioaccumulation]:::out
    block5 --> block6[MITIGATION BLOCK: Treatment BMP Policy]:::out
    block6 --> block7[MONITORING BLOCK: KSPCB CPCB Sensors]:::out

    classDef in fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#b7950b,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PENST412]**  
Define the term "Coastal Zone" as applied in marine pollution studies. List any four major land-based sources contributing to coastal marine pollution in Kerala.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Coastal Zone is the transitional interface between land and sea, defined as the belt extending from the landward limit of marine influence (mean higher high water line) seaward to the outer edge of the continental shelf. UNCLOS demarcates the territorial sea up to **12 nautical miles** and the EEZ up to **200 nautical miles** from the baseline.

> **Four major land-based sources in Kerala (2 marks, ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Municipal sewage discharge** from urban local bodies (e.g., Kochi Corporation outfalls into Vembanad Lake).
> 2. **Industrial effluents** from clustered industrial estates (Eloor, Edayar, FACT, BPCL).
> 3. **Agricultural runoff** from paddy and coconut fields carrying nutrients and pesticides.
> 4. **Aquaculture and pokkali-field discharges** carrying nitrogenous waste and antibiotics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PENST412]**  
What is meant by "Self-purification capacity" of a coastal water body? Name the two kinetic parameters of the Streeter–Phelps model that govern this capacity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Self-purification capacity (1.5 marks):** It is the inherent ability of a receiving water body (river, estuary, or coastal outfall zone) to assimilate organic and biodegradable pollutants through natural physical, chemical, and biological processes — chiefly microbial oxidation and surface re-aeration — such that the dissolved oxygen (DO) profile recovers downstream of a discharge point.

> **Two governing kinetic parameters (1.5 marks, ¾ each):**
> 1. **$k_1$ — Deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹):** rate of oxygen consumption by microbial decomposition of organic matter.
> 2. **$k_2$ — Reaeration rate constant (day⁻¹):** rate of oxygen transfer from the atmosphere back into the water column.

> The ratio $k_2/k_1$ is often called the **self-purification ratio**; a value > 1 indicates a healthy assimilative capacity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Streeter–Phelps Problems**
> - **Do NOT** confuse $L_0$ with BOD₅ unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. $L_0$ is the **ultimate BOD** ($L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5})$).
> - **Always** state the units of $k_1$, $k_2$, $t$, and answer in mg/L for $D$.
> - **Do NOT** stop at $t_c$ alone — examiners also expect $D_c$ and a comment on the **DO at the critical point relative to the saturation value** for full 14-mark credit.
> - **Do NOT** mix up the sign convention: a positive deficit $D$ means oxygen is *below* saturation. If your final $D_c > DO_{sat}$, the system has gone **anoxic** and you must say so.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Module 1 Full Question]*

**(a) Classify the major categories of pollutants in the coastal zone with two examples of each, and explain how *bioaccumulation* differs from *biomagnification*.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A coastal estuary receives a wastewater discharge with ultimate BOD $L_0 = 40$ mg/L. The deoxygenation constant is $k_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹ and reaeration constant is $k_2 = 0.55$ day⁻¹. The initial dissolved-oxygen deficit at the outfall is $D_0 = 1.5$ mg/L. Assume $DO_{sat} = 8.0$ mg/L. Determine (i) the critical time $t_c$, (ii) the critical deficit $D_c$, and (iii) the minimum dissolved-oxygen concentration at the critical point. Comment on whether fish survival is possible.** *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Classification of coastal pollutants (4 marks, ½ mark per category, ¼ mark per example):**

| Category | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Nutrients | Nitrate from fertilizer runoff | Phosphate from detergents |
| Heavy metals | Mercury from chlor-alkali plants | Lead from leaded petrol residue |
| Petroleum hydrocarbons | Diesel from bilge discharge | Crude oil from tanker accidents |
| Persistent Organic Pollutants | DDT (organochlorine) | PCBs (transformer oils) |
| Plastic & microplastic | Carrier bags | Microbeads from cosmetics |
| Pathogens | *E. coli* from sewage | *Vibrio cholerae* from coastal waters |
| Thermal discharge | Power-plant cooling water | Desalination brine |
| Suspended sediments | River silt | Dredge spoil |
| Radionuclides | Tritium from nuclear plants | Cs-137 fallout |

**Bioaccumulation vs Biomagnification (3 marks):**

- **Bioaccumulation (1.5 marks):** The progressive build-up of a substance in an individual organism's body over its lifetime because the rate of intake (via respiration, food, water) exceeds the rate of excretion or metabolic breakdown. Quantified by the **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** = $C_{organism} / C_{water}$ (dimensionless). Example: Hg accumulating in the muscle of a single mullet.
- **Biomagnification (1.5 marks):** The progressive increase in contaminant concentration as it passes up the food chain through predator–prey transfer. Quantified by the **Lipid Factor (LF)** = $C_{predator} / C_{prey}$. Example: Phytoplankton → zooplankton → small fish → large predatory fish → human; DDT amplifies ~10 million times in this chain.

**Key difference (½ mark, bonus):** Bioaccumulation occurs within *one* organism across time; biomagnification occurs *across trophic levels* in a food web.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $L_0 = 40$ mg/L; $k_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹; $k_2 = 0.55$ day⁻¹; $D_0 = 1.5$ mg/L; $DO_{sat} = 8.0$ mg/L.

**(i) Critical time $t_c$ (2.5 marks):**

Use Equation (3):
$$ t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\!\left[\frac{k_2}{k_1}\!\left(1 - D_0 \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0}\right)\right] $$

Compute the term in parentheses:
$$ \frac{k_2 - k_1}{k_1 L_0} = \frac{0.55 - 0.25}{0.25 \times 40} = \frac{0.30}{10.0} = 0.03 $$

$$ D_0 \times 0.03 = 1.5 \times 0.03 = 0.045 $$

$$ 1 - 0.045 = 0.955 $$

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} \times 0.955 = \frac{0.55}{0.25} \times 0.955 = 2.2 \times 0.955 = 2.101 $$

$$ \ln(2.101) = 0.7429 $$

$$ k_2 - k_1 = 0.55 - 0.25 = 0.30 \text{ day}^{-1} $$

$$ \boxed{t_c = \frac{0.7429}{0.30} \approx 2.476 \text{ days}} $$

**(ii) Critical deficit $D_c$ (2.5 marks):**

Use Equation (4):
$$ D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c} $$

$$ \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} = \frac{0.25 \times 40}{0.55} = \frac{10.0}{0.55} = 18.18 \text{ mg/L} $$

$$ k_1 t_c = 0.25 \times 2.476 = 0.619 $$

$$ e^{-0.619} = 0.5385 $$

$$ \boxed{D_c = 18.18 \times 0.5385 \approx 9.79 \text{ mg/L}} $$

**(iii) Minimum DO and fish survival (2 marks):**

$$ DO_{min} = DO_{sat} - D_c = 8.0 - 9.79 = -1.79 \text{ mg/L} $$

Since $DO_{min}$ is **negative**, the model predicts **complete anoxia** at the critical point — a so-called **"dead zone"**.

**Fish survival (½ mark):** Most coastal fish species require $DO \ge 4$ mg/L for sustained life and $DO \ge 2$ mg/L for short-term survival. With $DO_{min} = -1.79$ mg/L, **fish survival is not possible**; the estuary would experience massive fish kills and a hypoxic collapse of benthic fauna.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The discharge load is **not assimilable** by the estuary. Mitigation requires either pre-treatment to reduce $L_0$, or a longer outfall diffuser to enhance initial dilution and reduce $D_0$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Discuss the various land-based sources of marine pollution in the coastal zone of Kerala. What is the role of the National Green Tribunal (NGT) and the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) notification in controlling such pollution?** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A small fishing harbour discharges 2 ML/day of treated wastewater with BOD₅ = 30 mg/L into a tidal creek. The creek has a tidal prism volume $P = 5 \times 10^4$ m³. Compute the **tidal flushing time** $T_f$ and the **mass loading rate** of BOD₅ (kg/day) discharged. Comment on whether the receiving water meets the KSPCB standard of BOD₅ ≤ 5 mg/L for coastal bathing waters, assuming the harbour water volume is $V = 2 \times 10^5$ m³.** *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Land-based sources in Kerala (5 marks, 1 mark each for 5 categories):**

1. **Municipal sewage:** Cities like Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, Kozhikode discharge partially treated / untreated sewage (≈ 1,200 MLD combined) into rivers, canals, and backwaters.
2. **Industrial effluents:** Eloor-Edayar industrial belt contributes Hg, Pb, Cr, cyanide; distilleries contribute high-COD molasses waste.
3. **Agricultural runoff:** Kuttanad, Palakkad paddy belts contribute N, P, and pesticide residues (endosulfan legacy).
4. **Aquaculture discharges:** Effluents from shrimp/prawn farms containing nutrients, antibiotics, and uneaten feed.
5. **Tourism & religious wastes:** Pilgrim towns (Sabarimala, Guruvayur) generate seasonal sewage surges that overwhelm local treatment.

**NGT (½ mark):** The National Green Tribunal adjudicates environmental disputes and imposes penalties for violation of CRZ norms. Landmark cases include the **Sterlite Industries** closure and the **Ganga pollution** rulings.

**CRZ Notification 2019 (1 mark, ½ mark each):**
- **CRZ-I:** Ecologically sensitive areas (mangroves, coral reefs, national parks).
- **CRZ-II:** Developed urban areas within municipal limits.
- **CRZ-III:** Rural and undeveloped areas.
- **CRZ-IV (A & B):** Aquatic areas — water bodies up to 12 nm.
- Prohibits **new construction within 200 m of HTL** in many categories, restricts discharge of untreated effluents, and mandates environmental clearance for coastal projects.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** Discharge $Q_{ww} = 2$ ML/day = $2{,}000$ m³/day; BOD₅ in wastewater = 30 mg/L = 30 g/m³; $P = 5 \times 10^4$ m³; $V = 2 \times 10^5$ m³.

**(i) Tidal flushing time (2.5 marks):**

Use Equation (6):
$$ T_f = \frac{V}{P} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^4} = 4 \text{ days} $$

> **Interpretation:** The tidal creek fully exchanges its water with the open sea every 4 days — a moderate flushing system.

**(ii) BOD₅ mass loading rate (2.5 marks):**

$$ \dot{m}_{BOD_5} = Q_{ww} \times C_{BOD_5} = 2{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 30 \text{ g/m}^3 = 60{,}000 \text{ g/day} $$

$$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{BOD_5} = 60 \text{ kg/day}} $$

**(iii) BOD compliance check (2 marks):**

Assuming the worst-case complete mixing of the wastewater with the harbour water (a coarse conservative estimate):

$$ C_{BOD_5,\text{mix}} = \frac{Q_{ww} C_{ww} + (P - Q_{ww}) \times 0}{P} \approx \frac{2{,}000 \times 30}{5 \times 10^4} = \frac{60{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1.2 \text{ mg/L} $$

(Note: using the tidal prism as the effective dilution volume per tidal cycle is the standard engineering shortcut.)

Alternatively, using the larger volume $V$:

$$ C_{BOD_5} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ g/day}}{2 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}_{\text{eff}}} = 0.3 \text{ mg/L} \quad \text{(steady-state estimate)} $$

Both estimates fall **below 5 mg/L**, so the receiving water **meets the KSPCB coastal bathing standard of BOD₅ ≤ 5 mg/L**, although the **60 kg/day loading** is a concern for cumulative impact on sediments and should be monitored quarterly.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Discharge is compliant in dilution terms, but long-term monitoring of benthic BOD and refractory pollutants is recommended.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Marine Pollution in the Coastal Zone**

- **Definition recall:** Marine pollution = introduction of substances/energy causing harm; coastal zone = land–sea interface belt.
- **Numerical anchors:** **12 nm** (territorial sea), **200 nm** (EEZ), **≈ 80%** pollution is land-based, **BOD₅ ≈ 200–400 mg/L** in raw sewage.
- **Four Pillars to memorise:** Sources → Pollutants → Pathways → Impacts.
- **Two key kinetic constants:** $k_1$ (deoxygenation) and $k_2$ (reaeration); self-purification ratio $k_2/k_1 > 1$.
- **Streeter–Phelps essentials:** Equation (2) for $D(t)$, Equation (3) for $t_c$, Equation (4) for $D_c$. Always convert BOD₅ to $L_0$ if needed: $L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5})$.
- **Kerala-specific:** Vembanad Lake, Eloor, Pokkali fields, Sabarimala pilgrim loads, Cochin port ballast — quote these in answers for **Kerala context marks**.
- **Difference pairs (high-frequency exam):** Bioaccumulation vs Biomagnification, BOD vs COD, DO saturation vs DO deficit, Brackish vs Saline (Venice System: 0.5–30 ‰).
- **Standards to remember:** WHO coliform < 200/100 mL; KSPCB BOD₅ ≤ 5 mg/L for coastal bathing; EPA Pb ≤ 8.5 µg/L.
- **Streeter–Phelps pitfall:** If $D_c > DO_{sat}$, declare **anoxia/dead zone** explicitly.
- **Diagrams for full credit:** Always draw a **flow diagram (sources→impacts)** and the **DO sag curve** in 14-mark answers — examiners award up to 2 marks for neat labelled sketches.
- **Legislation:** UNCLOS, MARPOL, CRZ Notification 2019, Water Act 1974, NGT Act 2010 — quote section numbers where possible.
- **Units consistency:** mg/L for concentrations, day⁻¹ for rate constants, days for time, kg/day for mass loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollution from Ships and Offshore Assets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pollution from Ships and Offshore Assets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Marine Pollution from Ships and Offshore Assets

**Marine pollution from ships and offshore assets** refers to the introduction by humans, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) from vessels, fixed/floating offshore platforms, and ancillary support infrastructure, which results in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities (including fishing), impairment of seawater quality for legitimate uses, and reduction of amenities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), **Article 1(1)(4)** defines pollution of the marine environment as: *"the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) which results, or is likely to result, in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine ecosystems, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, including fishing and other legitimate uses of the sea, impairment of quality for use of sea water and reduction of amenities."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of the ocean as a giant, slow-flushing bathtub.** Every ship and offshore platform is like a faucet pouring different kinds of "dirt" into this shared tub. The "dirt" is not just obvious things like spilled oil — it also includes invisible things like:
- **Gases** from engine exhausts (CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ) that dissolve into water and acidify it.
- **Sewage** from toilets and grey water from sinks/showers.
- **Cargo residues** that stick to tank walls and wash out with seawater during cleaning.
- **Chemicals** from anti-fouling paints that prevent barnacles from growing on the hull.
- **Ballast water** that ships pump in at one port and release at another — carrying tiny stowaway organisms into a new ecosystem.

The key idea is that **oceans are not limitless sinks**. A bathtub can handle a small spill, but if you keep pouring and never drain, eventually the water becomes unusable. That is precisely what happens when pollution from shipping accumulates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why offshore assets matter separately:** Ships move. An oil platform is a *fixed factory in the sea*. When a platform has an accident, the pollution stays concentrated around the platform's location for a long time, with nowhere to drift except into the water column, seabed, or shoreline. So the engineering controls for offshore assets are far more demanding than for a moving ship.

### 1.3 Classification of Pollutants — KTU High-Yield Overview

Pollutants from ships and offshore assets are classified into **operational (routine)** and **accidental (incidental)** categories:

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Operational pollution** | Continuous, routine, legal/regulated discharges during normal vessel/platform operation | Bilge water, sewage, grey water, cargo hold washings, ballast water, engine exhaust, anti-fouling leachate |
| **Accidental pollution** | Unplanned, sudden releases due to equipment failure, human error, or extreme events | Oil spills from tank ruptures, collisions, groundings, blowouts, wellhead failures, pipeline leaks |
| **Deliberate (illegal) pollution** | Intentional bypassing of regulations | Magic pipe (sludge dumped overboard), falsified oil record books, prohibited cleaning agents |

> [!NOTE]
> The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** is the specialized United Nations agency responsible for the safety, security, and environmental performance of international shipping. The principal international treaty governing marine pollution from ships is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)**, which has six technical Annexes.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Field

The following metrics are used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus and must be memorized:

- **ppm (parts per million)**: Used for oil-in-water content. $1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L}$.
- **Emission Index (EI)**: Mass of pollutant per kg of fuel burned (g/kg fuel).
- **Discharge rate (Q)**: Litres per nautical mile or tonnes per day.
- **TBT (Tributyltin)**: A biocide measured in $\mu\text{g/cm}^2/\text{day}$ from anti-fouling paints.
- **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)**: mg O₂/L over 5 days — the standard for sewage strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL 73/78 — The Six Annexes (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Annex I**: Oil (oily discharges, oil record book)
> - **Annex II**: Noxious Liquid Substances in bulk (NLS)
> - **Annex III**: Harmful substances in packaged form
> - **Annex IV**: Sewage
> - **Annex V**: Garbage
> - **Annex VI**: Air pollution (SOₓ, NOₓ, ODS, CO₂ via IMO 2020)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Pollution from Ships — Detailed Breakdown

Ships contribute pollution through **multiple pathways simultaneously**. The KTU 2024 module requires a clear mapping of each pathway to its governing Annex.

#### 2.1.1 Oil Pollution (MARPOL Annex I)

**Origin of oil on a ship:**
- **Cargo oil tankers**: Crude oil, fuel oil, diesel as cargo or bunker.
- **Machinery spaces**: Lubricating oil, hydraulic oil, fuel oil sludge from purifiers.
- **Bilge water**: Water that accumulates in the lowest part of the hull, mixed with oil leaks.
- **Sludge tanks**: Residue from fuel oil purification, lube oil cleaning.
- **Ballast water in oil tankers**: When an oil tanker takes on ballast in empty cargo tanks, oily ballast water is generated.

**Why it matters:** A single VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) of 200,000 DWT can carry ~2 million barrels (~300,000 tonnes) of crude oil. A major spill can therefore exceed the volume of major historical incidents such as the *Exxon Valdez* (1989: ~37,000 tonnes) or *Prestige* (2002: ~63,000 tonnes).

#### 2.1.2 Sewage and Grey Water (MARPOL Annex IV)

- **Black water (sewage)**: From toilets and urinals. Contains pathogens, high BOD, nutrients.
- **Grey water**: From showers, sinks, laundry, galleys. Lower in pathogens but still high in BOD, detergents, oils.

**Standard:** Untreated sewage must not be discharged within **4 nautical miles** of the nearest land. With an approved sewage treatment plant (STP), discharge is allowed beyond 4 nm under regulated flow rates.

#### 2.1.3 Garbage (MARPOL Annex V)

Includes all kinds of food waste, domestic waste, operational waste, cargo residues, fishing gear, and plastics. **Discharge of plastics at sea is prohibited in all conditions.**

#### 2.1.4 Air Pollution (MARPOL Annex VI)

Ships burn **bunker fuel** (heavy fuel oil, HFO) in large marine diesel engines. Combustion releases:
- **Sulphur oxides (SOₓ)**: From sulphur in fuel. The global sulphur cap is **0.50% m/m** (down from 3.50% pre-2020).
- **Nitrogen oxides (NOₓ)**: From high-temperature combustion of nitrogen.
- **Particulate matter (PM)**: Soot, especially from HFO.
- **CO₂**: Greenhouse gas, governed by IMO's **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** and **EEXI** (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index).
- **ODS (Ozone Depleting Substances)**: Used historically in refrigeration, fire-fighting, air-conditioning.

#### 2.1.5 Ballast Water (IMO Ballast Water Management Convention, 2004)

- Ships pump ballast water in for stability when not fully loaded.
- This water — and the **organisms it carries** (bacteria, plankton, larvae) — is discharged at the next port, potentially introducing **invasive species**.
- The Convention enforces the **D-1 standard** (ballast water exchange) or the more stringent **D-2 standard** (treatment to limit organism concentration).

#### 2.1.6 Anti-fouling Paints (IMO AFS Convention, 2001)

- Anti-fouling paints release biocides to prevent hull fouling.
- **Tributyltin (TBT)**, the most effective biocide, was globally banned from 2008 because it causes **imposex** in marine snails and bioaccumulates.
- Modern alternatives: copper-based biocides, silicone foul-release coatings.

### 2.2 Sources of Pollution from Offshore Assets

Offshore assets include:
- **Fixed platforms** (jacket, compliant tower, gravity-based)
- **Floating production units**: FPSO (Floating Production, Storage, and Offloading), FSO, semi-submersibles
- **Drilling rigs**: Jack-up, semi-submersible, drillship
- **Subsea infrastructure**: Pipelines, manifolds, wellheads, umbilicals

> [!NOTE]
> **Offshore pollution pathways differ from ships** because platforms are stationary industrial sites in the marine environment. They are essentially *factories with no shore-based infrastructure*. Pollution sources include:
> - **Drilling fluids and cuttings** (mud discharge from drilling)
> - **Produced water** (water extracted along with oil/gas)
> - **Oil and gas leaks** from wellheads, separators, risers
> - **Operational discharges**: cooling water, deck drainage, sanitary waste
- **Atmospheric emissions**: gas flaring, venting, diesel generators
- **Pipeline failures**: Subsea pipelines carrying oil/gas can rupture

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities below are KTU high-yield. Memorize the units.**

| Formula | Description | Units | Reference / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Oil concentration (ppm)} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of oil (mg)}}{\text{Volume of water (L)}}$ | Oil-in-water content | ppm = mg/L | Bilge water discharge standard = **15 ppm** (MARPOL Annex I) |
| $\text{BOD}_5 \text{ loading} = Q \times C_{\text{BOD}}$ | BOD mass loading per day | kg/day | Sewage treatment plant sizing |
| $\text{SO}_x \text{ emission} = 2 \times \%S_{\text{fuel}} \times m_{\text{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric mass of SO₂ per kg fuel | g/kg fuel | For 0.5% S fuel: SO₂ = 10 g/kg fuel |
| $\text{NO}_x \text{ (Tier I EI)} \approx 17 \times n^{0.2} \times \dfrac{1}{\text{BMEP}}$ | NOₓ Emission Index (IMO formula) | g/kWh | Engine out NOₓ estimation |
| $\text{CO}_2 = 3.114 \times m_{\text{fuel}}$ | CO₂ emission from HFO per IMO | t CO₂/t fuel | EEDI/EEXI/CII calculations |
| $\text{EEXI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emission}}{\text{Transport work}} = \dfrac{C \times V_{\text{ref}} \times f_i}{f_c \times f_L \times \text{Capacity} \times V_{\text{ref}}}$ | Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index | g CO₂/tonne·nm | For vessels > 400 GT in service |
| $V_{\text{spill}} = Q_{\text{rupture}} \times t_{\text{detection}} \times f_{\text{recovery}}$ | Spill volume estimation | m³ | Where $f_{\text{recovery}}$ is fraction not recovered |
| $F_{\text{disp}} = \dfrac{M}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian plume (instantaneous) | g/m³ | Air dispersion at sea |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil spill response engineers** use oil-in-water concentration to determine if bilge water can be legally discharged.
- **Port State Control officers** use EEXI/CII ratings to decide whether to detain a ship.
- **Offshore HSE managers** use Gaussian dispersion to design safe flare booms and living quarter layouts.
- **Marine biologists** use BOD loadings to size STPs in cruise ships.
- **Naval architects** use SOₓ/CO₂ emission factors to select scrubbers, LNG, or methanol fuels.

> [!NOTE]
> **CO₂ emission factor for common marine fuels** (per IMO MEPC.364(79)):
> - HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil): **3.114** t CO₂/t fuel
> - Diesel/Gas Oil: **3.206** t CO₂/t fuel
> - LNG: **2.750** t CO₂/t fuel
> - LPG (Propane): **3.000** t CO₂/t fuel
> - Methanol: **1.375** t CO₂/t fuel
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CO₂ Emission from Bunker Fuel Burned by a Ship

A bulk carrier of 80,000 DWT burns heavy fuel oil (HFO) at a consumption rate of 50 tonnes/day on a trans-Pacific voyage. Estimate the CO₂ emitted per day and over a 20-day voyage.

**Given:**
- Daily fuel consumption $m_f = 50$ t/day
- Voyage duration $t = 20$ days
- Fuel type: HFO
- IMO emission factor for HFO: $E_{\text{CO}_2} = 3.114$ t CO₂/t fuel

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**

The IMO's "Fourth IMO GHG Study 2020" provides emission factors for each fuel type. The relationship is:

$$E_{\text{CO}_2} = f_{\text{CO}_2} \times m_f$$

where $f_{\text{CO}_2}$ is the CO₂ emission factor in t CO₂ per tonne of fuel, and $m_f$ is the mass of fuel consumed.

**Step 2: Substitute the known value of the emission factor.**

For HFO, the IMO factor is $f_{\text{CO}_2} = 3.114$. This factor is derived from the fuel's carbon content (~85% by mass for HFO) and the stoichiometry of combustion:

$$f_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{\%C}{100} \times \frac{44}{12}$$

where 44/12 is the molar mass ratio of CO₂ to C.

**Step 3: Calculate daily CO₂ emission.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{day}} &= 3.114 \;\frac{\text{t CO}_2}{\text{t fuel}} \times 50 \;\frac{\text{t fuel}}{\text{day}} \\
&= 155.7 \;\frac{\text{t CO}_2}{\text{day}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate total CO₂ over 20 days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{CO}_2, \text{total}} &= 155.7 \;\frac{\text{t CO}_2}{\text{day}} \times 20 \;\text{days} \\
&= 3{,}114 \;\text{t CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Translate to equivalent metrics for impact assessment.**

- Equivalent number of cars (1 car ≈ 4.6 t CO₂/year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{cars}} &= \frac{3{,}114 \text{ t}}{4.6 \text{ t/year}} \approx 677 \text{ car-years of CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A single bulk carrier voyage produces CO₂ equivalent to ~677 cars operating for a year. International shipping contributes roughly **2.9% of global anthropogenic CO₂** (IMO 4th GHG Study), which is why EEXI and CII were introduced in 2023.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Oil Spill Volume from a Pipeline Rupture

An offshore platform's loading pipeline of diameter 0.4 m ruptures at a depth of 80 m. Crude oil flows under a pressure differential of 8 bar. The leak is detected 25 minutes later and shutdown is achieved 10 minutes after that. Estimate the total spill volume.

**Given:**
- Pipe diameter $D = 0.4$ m
- Pressure differential $\Delta P = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Detection time $t_d = 25 \text{ min} = 1500$ s
- Shutdown time after detection $t_s = 10 \text{ min} = 600$ s
- Crude oil density $\rho \approx 850$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area of the pipe.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.16}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Use a Torricelli-like efflux equation for pressurized leak.**

For a pressurized release, the efflux velocity is governed by:

$$v = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient (assume $C_d = 0.62$ for sharp-edged rupture).

**Step 3: Compute efflux velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 0.62 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^5}{850}} \\
  &= 0.62 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^6}{850}} \\
  &= 0.62 \times \sqrt{1882.35} \\
  &= 0.62 \times 43.39 \\
  &= 26.90 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute volumetric flow rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \times v = 0.1257 \times 26.90 = 3.382 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute total time the leak is open.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{total}} &= t_d + t_s = 1500 + 600 = 2100 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute total spill volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{spill}} &= Q \times t_{\text{total}} = 3.382 \times 2100 = 7{,}102 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Convert to tonnes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{spill}} &= V_{\text{spill}} \times \rho = 7{,}102 \times 850 = 6{,}036{,}700 \text{ kg} \approx 6{,}037 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** This is roughly **1.5× the size of the *Exxon Valdez* spill**. It demonstrates why **emergency shutdown valves (ESDVs)**, **subsea isolation valves (SSIVs)**, and **leak detection systems** are mandatory on offshore platforms. Reducing $t_d$ and $t_s$ is the single biggest lever for limiting spill size.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollution Source → Pathway → Regulation → Control Technology

> [!NOTE]
> **The matrix below is one of the most KTU-high-yield constructs of this entire module.** Examiners routinely ask students to map pollution sources to MARPOL Annexes and to mitigation technologies.

| Pollution Source | Pathway (medium) | Governing Regulation | Typical Control Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo oil (tanker) | Sea surface | MARPOL Annex I | Segregated ballast tanks (SBT), double hull, OWS (Oily Water Separator), SBT, COW (Crude Oil Washing) |
| Bilge water | Sea surface | MARPOL Annex I | OWS (15 ppm), 100 ppm bilge alarm, sludge tank |
| Sewage (black water) | Water column | MARPOL Annex IV | Sewage Treatment Plant (STP), holding tank, comminuting |
| Garbage / plastics | Sea surface / shore | MARPOL Annex V | Garbage Management Plan, port reception facilities, incinerator |
| SOₓ exhaust | Atmosphere | MARPOL Annex VI | Low-sulphur fuel, Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS/scrubbers) |
| NOₓ exhaust | Atmosphere | MARPOL Annex VI | Tier I/II/III engines, SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction), EGR |
| CO₂ | Atmosphere | IMO EEXI + CII (2023) | Slow steaming, hull cleaning, route optimization, air lubrication, wind-assist, alternative fuels (LNG, methanol, ammonia) |
| Ballast water organisms | Water column | BWM Convention | Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS), exchange at sea (D-1), D-2 compliance |
| Anti-fouling biocides | Water column | AFS Convention | TBT-free paints, silicone foul-release, copper-free biocides |
| Drilling cuttings/mud | Water column & seabed | OSPAR / national | Cuttings re-injection, zero-discharge, skip-and-ship |
| Produced water | Water column | OSPAR / EPA | Hydrocyclone, flotation, compact flotation unit (CFU), media filtration |
| Subsea pipeline leak | Sea surface / seabed | Pipeline regulations | ESDV, SSIV, leak detection, PIG (Pipeline Inspection Gauge) |
| Flaring/venting | Atmosphere | MARPOL VI / local | Gas recovery, vapor recovery units, flare gas recovery |
| Accidental oil spill | Sea surface | OPA 90 / national | Booms, skimmers, sorbents, in-situ burning, dispersants |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Quantifying Sewage BOD Loading from a Cruise Ship

A cruise ship carries 5,000 passengers and crew. Each person generates 150 L/day of sewage with a BOD₅ of 250 mg/L. The ship has an STP with 95% BOD removal efficiency. Compute:
(a) Raw BOD load per day.
(b) Treated BOD concentration in effluent.
(c) Whether the treated effluent can be discharged at 3 nm from land under MARPOL.

**Step 1: Raw BOD load per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sewage}} &= 5{,}000 \text{ persons} \times 150 \text{ L/person/day} = 750{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 750 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD load} &= Q \times C = 750{,}000 \text{ L/day} \times 250 \text{ mg/L} \\
                &= 187{,}500{,}000 \text{ mg/day} = 187.5 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Treated BOD concentration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{treated}} &= C_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \eta) = 250 \times (1 - 0.95) = 12.5 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Check MARPOL Annex IV compliance.**

MARPOL Annex IV requires treated effluent to meet:
- **< 25 CFU/100 mL** (fecal coliform)
- **< 1 mg/L** PCBs in some jurisdictions

For BOD, the IMO standard is governed by the **resolution MEPC.227(64)** with limits varying by ship type and age. For untreated sewage: discharge is allowed only beyond **12 nm from nearest land** at speed > 4 knots with moderate rate. For treated sewage (Type II): discharge is allowed beyond **3 nm**.

The treated BOD of 12.5 mg/L satisfies the standard. Therefore **the effluent CAN be discharged at 3 nm from land**, provided the STP is certified and the discharge rate is moderated.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume that the 4 nm rule applies universally. The 4 nm rule is for **untreated sewage**; for treated sewage, the 3 nm rule applies with a properly certified STP. Always specify which Annex IV path is being used.
