```mermaid
    ST3 --> MON[15 ppm Oil Content Monitor]
    MON --> DEC{Discharge Standard Met?}
    DEC -->|Yes| OUT[Overboard Discharge]
    DEC -->|No| SLUDGE[Sludge Tank / PRF Return]
    ST1 --> REC[Recovered Oil to Sludge]
    ST2 --> REC
```

## 4.3 Air Pollution Prevention Decision Tree (Annex VI)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Ship Trading Route] --> Q1{Is vessel in ECA zone?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Apply 0.10 percent S cap]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Apply 0.50 percent S cap]

    Q2 --> P1[Option A: LSFO]
    Q2 --> P2[Option B: Scrubber greater than 95 percent removal]
    Q2 --> P3[Option C: LNG or alternative fuel]

    Q3 --> P4[Option A: LSFO 0.5 percent]
    Q3 --> P5[Option B: Scrubber]
    Q3 --> P6[Option C: Slow steaming]

    P1 --> NOX{NOx Tier requirement}
    P2 --> NOX
    P3 --> NOX
    P4 --> NOX
    P5 --> NOX
    P6 --> NOX

    NOX -->|Tier I / II| ACC[Engine tuning]
    NOX -->|Tier III ECA| SCR[SCR with Urea Injection]
    SCR --> CII[Apply CII Operational Reduction]
    ACC --> CII
```

## 4.4 Ballast Water Management — D-1 vs D-2 Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BW[Ballast Tank Filling at Port A] --> Q{Is BWTS installed and approved?}
    Q -->|Yes D-2| TRE[UV or Electrochlorination Treatment]
    Q -->|No D-1| EX[Sequential or Flow-Through Exchange 95 percent]
    TRE --> DIS[Discharge at Port B compliant with D-2 limits]
    EX --> DIS
    DIS --> CHECK[PSC Verification with BWT Record Book]
```

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award marks for **legible, well-labelled flow diagrams** in 14-mark questions. Use the above block-level architecture in answers; KTU ESE papers specifically expect a "neat sketch" worth 2–3 marks within a 14-mark sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

### Q1. State the MARPOL Annex VI global sulfur cap and the ECA sulfur cap in effect since 1 January 2020. Explain the rationale for two compliance options available to ship operators.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The MARPOL Annex VI global sulfur cap, applicable outside Emission Control Areas, is **0.50% m/m** of fuel sulfur content, enforced from 1 January 2020. Inside ECAs (Baltic, North Sea, North American, Caribbean, Mediterranean), the cap is **0.10% m/m** (0.1% ECA rule).

**Two compliance options (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Use compliant low-sulfur fuel oil (LSFO/VLSFO)** — switch the bunker to ≤ 0.50% S (or ≤ 0.10% S in ECA). No engine modification; immediate compliance.
2. **Install Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS / Scrubber)** — wet scrubber chemically absorbs SO2 from flue gas; allows continued use of cheap HFO. Justification under "Equivalence" rule of MARPOL.
3. **Switch to LNG / methanol / ammonia** — future-fuels pathway, virtually eliminates SOx.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

### Q2. Define the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) and state one limitation of the EEDI as a measure of real-world ship performance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EEDI is an **index representing the CO2 emission performance of a new ship per tonne-mile of transport work**, expressed in g CO2/(tonne · nautical mile). It compares the *attained EEDI* (actual design CO2 ÷ (Capacity × Distance)) with the *required EEDI* (baseline × reduction factor); a ship complies if attained ≤ required. EEDI applies to new builds from 1 Jan 2013 onwards, phased by ship type and size (2 marks for definition + formula).

**Limitation (1 mark):** EEDI is purely a **design-time** metric calculated at the **trial condition (75% MCR, calm sea)** and does **not capture operational realities** such as weather routing, slow steaming, port waiting, hull fouling, or actual loading. This is why the IMO introduced the **operational CII** in 2023 to complement EEDI.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — ESE Module 2]**

### Choose **ONE** of the following two questions:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Air Pollution from Ships

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how a **wet Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (Scrubber)** removes SO2 from main engine exhaust. Discuss the difference between **open-loop, closed-loop, and hybrid** scrubbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **EEDI and CII regulatory framework** under MARPOL Annex VI. Compute the required CII for a bulk carrier in 2027, given reference CII = 25 g CO2/(t·nm) and the applicable reduction factor Z = 7%. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
Exhaust gas from the main engine (containing SO2, NOx, CO2, particulates) is ducted into a scrubber tower where it is brought into intimate contact with a scrubbing liquid (seawater in open-loop; NaOH-treated water in closed-loop). SO2 is highly soluble in water and reacts as:

$$
SO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_3
$$

In closed-loop / hybrid with NaOH:

$$
SO_2 + 2\,NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_3 + H_2O
$$

The cleaned gas (with SO2 removed by ~95–98%) is released to the atmosphere via a wet stack. The wash water, now acidic and laden with sulphates, is treated and discharged.

**Three scrubber types (4 marks):**

| Type | Wash Water | Pros | Cons |
|------|-----------|------|------|
| Open-loop | Seawater (infinite) | Simple, no chemical | pH/pollutant limits on discharge; banned in some ports |
| Closed-loop | Recirculated freshwater + NaOH | Zero overboard discharge, suitable in ports/ECA | Caustic cost, sludge handling |
| Hybrid | Both modes | Operational flexibility | Highest capital cost |

**Sketch (block diagram acceptable):** show *engine → exhaust manifold → scrubber tower (with spray nozzles) → wash-water pump → treatment unit → overboard / recirculation tank → clean stack*. (Award full marks if the block diagram correctly shows the gas-side and water-side flow paths.)

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**EEDI vs CII framework (3 marks):**

- **EEDI (Annex VI Ch 4, Reg 22)** — design-time CO2 efficiency of **new ships**; phased reduction from 2013–2025.
- **CII (Annex VI Ch 4, Reg 28)** — operational, annual, applies to **all ships ≥ 5,000 GT** since 2023; ships rated A (best) to E (worst).
- Ratings: **D for 3 consecutive years or any E** ⇒ ship must submit **Corrective Action Plan** and **SEEMP (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan)** revision.
- EEDI sets the design baseline; CII forces operational discipline.

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Given: $CII_{ref} = 25$ g CO2/(t·nm); $Z = 7\%$ (2027 reduction factor).

$$
CII_{req,2027} = \left(1 - \frac{7}{100}\right) \times 25 = 0.93 \times 25 = 23.25 \text{ g CO}_2/(\text{t·nm})
$$

The ship must achieve ≤ 23.25 g CO2/(t·nm) annually; otherwise, it will fall in D or E rating. The ship may improve via slow steaming, hull cleaning, voyage optimization, or shaft-line modifications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Water Pollution from Ships

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **three-stage Oily Water Separator (OWS)** meeting MARPOL Annex I 15 ppm standard. Calculate the mass of oil recovered per hour for the following: 60 m³/h bilge flow, 4500 ppm inlet oil, gravity efficiency 65%, coalescer efficiency 90% of remaining, polisher to 15 ppm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **BWM Convention 2004** D-1 and D-2 standards. State the operational procedure for a tanker that is yet to install a BWTS. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Working (3 marks):**
The three-stage OWS processes oily bilge water to meet the 15 ppm MARPOL discharge limit. **Stage 1 (gravity):** bilge enters a quiescent chamber where oil, being lighter, rises and is skimmed off; oily sludge is sent to a sludge tank. **Stage 2 (coalescer):** small oil droplets pass through oleophilic plate packs or coalescer cartridges that merge them into larger drops, which then float to the collection chamber. **Stage 3 (polisher):** final polishing via membrane, adsorption, or cartridge filter to guarantee < 15 ppm. A **15 ppm Oil Content Monitor** continuously samples the overboard line; if reading > 15 ppm, a 3-way valve recirculates back to sludge tank.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Inlet oil mass flow

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 4500 \text{ mg/L} = 60 \times 4500 = 270{,}000 \text{ mg·m}^3/\text{L·h}
$$

Since 1 m³ = 1000 L:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = 270{,}000 \times 1000 \text{ mg/h} = 2.7 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/h} = 270 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Step 2: After Stage 1 (gravity, 65% removed ⇒ 35% remains)

$$
C_1 = 4500 \times 0.35 = 1575 \text{ ppm}
$$

Step 3: After Stage 2 (coalescer, 90% of remaining removed)

$$
C_2 = 1575 \times 0.10 = 157.5 \text{ ppm}
$$

Step 4: After Stage 3 (polisher) — given: $C_3 = 15$ ppm.

Step 5: Oil remaining in discharge

$$
\dot{m}_{out} = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 15 \text{ mg/L} \times 1000 = 0.9 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Step 6: Oil recovered per hour

$$
\dot{m}_{recovered} = 270 - 0.9 = 269.1 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Verification:** $C_{out} = 15 \text{ ppm} \leq 15$ ppm ⇒ **COMPLIES with Annex I.**

**Sketch:** show *bilge inlet → coarse strainer → stage 1 gravity → stage 2 coalescer → stage 3 polisher → 15 ppm OCM → overboard OR recirculation to sludge tank*. Mark oil and water flow paths distinctly.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**BWM Convention 2004 (4 marks):**

- The **International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments (BWM), 2004** entered into force on **8 September 2017**. It aims to prevent the spread of harmful aquatic organisms from one region to another via ships' ballast water.
- **Regulation D-1 (Ballast Water Exchange Standard):** ships perform volumetric exchange of ballast water — ≥ 95% of volume must be replaced, achieved by either sequential, flow-through, or dilution methods. Often used as an interim measure.
- **Regulation D-2 (Ballast Water Performance Standard):** discharge limits on viable organisms:
  - ≤ 10 viable organisms/m³ for organisms ≥ 50 μm
  - ≤ 10 viable organisms/mL for organisms < 50 μm but > 10 μm
  - Indicator microbes ≤ specified CFU limits (e.g., *E. coli* ≤ 250 CFU/100 mL, *Vibrio cholerae* ≤ 1 CFU/100 mL, intestinal enterococci ≤ 100 CFU/100 mL).
- D-1 is a **management** standard; D-2 is a **treatment** standard.

**Operational procedure for a tanker without BWTS (3 marks):**
The tanker must follow the **D-1 Ballast Water Exchange** under Regulation B-4. The procedure is:
1. On **deballasting at loading port**: pump out sediment-laden water; clean ballast tanks as per BWM sediment management procedure.
2. On **ballasting at discharge port (or vice versa)**: perform **sequential exchange** — empty one tank, refill with clean open-ocean water (≥ 200 nm from nearest land, ≥ 200 m depth) so that ≥ 95% of total ballast volume is exchanged. Alternatively, use **flow-through exchange** where three tank-volumes of replacement water are pumped through each tank.
3. Record all exchanges in the **Ballast Water Record Book** and **BWM Certificate** (issued after survey). Carry out **ballast water management plan** approved by Administration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Marks Lost):**
> 1. Confusing **ECA sulfur cap (0.10%)** with the **global cap (0.50%)** — examiners dock 1 mark.
> 2. Omitting the **15 ppm OCM** in OWS sketches — mandatory per IOPP Certificate.
> 3. Calculating EEDI with wrong units (using kg instead of g) — silent **0 marks** for the numerical.
> 4. Writing **Tier III NOx = 3.4 g/kWh** without specifying "in ECA, for engines > 130 kW" — 1 mark lost.
> 5. Forgetting that **BWM Convention entered into force 8 Sept 2017** — not 2004 (year of adoption).
> 6. Confusing **EEDI** (design metric) with **CII** (operational metric) in long answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bulletins — Revise the Night Before the Exam:**

- **MARPOL Annex VI global S cap = 0.50% m/m; ECA S cap = 0.10% m/m** (since 1 Jan 2020).
- **NOx Tier III = 3.4 g/kWh**; applicable to **ECA** for engines > 130 kW installed on/after 1 Jan 2016.
- **SCR** reduces NOx by **80–95%**; uses **urea (AdBlue)** as reducing agent.
- **Scrubber SO2 removal** = 95–98% efficiency; equivalence sulphur = ~0.10% S.
- **EEDI** applies to **new ships** (since 2013); **CII** applies to **all ships ≥ 5,000 GT** (since 2023).
- **CII formula:** $CII_{req} = (1 - Z/100) \times CII_{ref}$; Z increases by year (e.g., 5% in 2023, 7% in 2027, up to 20% in 2030).
- **S → SO2 stoichiometric factor = 2.0** (mass basis).
- **CF for HFO = 3.114**; LNG = 2.750 (t CO2 / t fuel).
- **OWS discharge standard = 15 ppm**; 3-stage = gravity + coalescer + polisher.
- **Annex V — Plastics: ZERO discharge anywhere.**
- **Sewage discharge**: ≥ 3 NM (comminuted/disinfected) or ≥ 12 NM (untreated); standards: ≤ 25 mg/L SS, faecal coliform ≤ 1,000 CFU/100 mL.
- **BWM D-1:** ≥ 95% volumetric exchange in deep open ocean; **D-2:** organism counts must be below IMO limits.
- **Port Reception Facilities (PRF)** — mandatory under MARPOL Art. 5; ships must use them, not dump at sea.
- **Hierarchy in answers:** *Source reduction → Segregation → Treatment → PRF disposal*.
- **EEXI** (effective 1 Jan 2023) — like EEDI but for **existing ships**; must attain ≤ required EEXI.
- Always cite **MEPC / Resolution numbers** (e.g., MEPC.328(76) for EEXI; MEPC.336(76) for CII).
- For 14-mark answers, structure into: **(1) Regulation, (2) Mechanism, (3) Numerical/Application, (4) Limitation/Recent Update** — this is the KTU board examiner's expected pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emissions from Ships Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emissions from Ships Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Engine Emissions** are the gaseous, particulate, and secondary aerosol byproducts released into the marine atmosphere as a result of the combustion of fossil fuels (primarily Heavy Fuel Oil, Marine Diesel Oil, and Marine Gas Oil) within the main propulsion engines, auxiliary generators, and boilers of ocean-going vessels.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, emissions from ship engines constitute the **single largest regulated source of air pollution** in international maritime transport. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) categorizes these emissions into two broad functional regimes:

1. **Primary Pollutants**: Substances directly formed in the combustion chamber and released via the exhaust stack.
2. **Secondary Pollutants**: Substances formed in the atmosphere through photochemical or aqueous-phase reactions of primary emissions (e.g., secondary sulfate aerosols from SO₃).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Patient on Ventilator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a ship's engine as a human patient in an ICU on a ventilator. The patient (engine) needs oxygen and food (air + fuel) to live and work. But in the process of metabolizing that food, the patient exhales CO₂, releases heat, and may cough out small particulates and unburnt residues. To a sensitive neighboring environment (a port city or coastal ecosystem), these "exhalations" are pollutants. Just as modern ICUs install HEPA filters, scrubbers, and catalytic converters to clean patient exhalations, ships must install **exhaust gas cleaning systems (EGCS)**, **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** units, and **scrubbers** to clean their engine "breath" before it leaves the funnel.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

The following globally accepted metrics govern the quantification of ship emissions:

| Metric Symbol | Definition | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **g/kWh** | Grammes of pollutant per unit of useful work output | gramme per kilowatt-hour |
| **g/kW** | Grammes of pollutant per unit of installed power | gramme per kilowatt |
| **ppm** | Parts per million by volume in exhaust | dimensionless |
| **NOₓ** | Oxides of Nitrogen (NO + NO₂) | expressed as NO₂ equivalent |
| **SOₓ** | Oxides of Sulfur (SO₂ + SO₃) | expressed as SO₂ equivalent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 2 expects students to clearly differentiate between the **regulated pollutants** (NOₓ, SOₓ, PM, CO₂, VOC) and the **unregulated / emerging pollutants** (Black Carbon, Methane slip, PAH, heavy metals like Vanadium and Nickel).

### 1.4 Major Categories of Ship Engine Emissions

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Regulated Pollutants from Ship Engines:**
> 1. **Sulfur Oxides (SOₓ)** — primarily SO₂, the dominant acid-rain precursor at sea.
> 2. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — NO and NO₂, responsible for tropospheric ozone and acid rain.
> 3. **Particulate Matter (PM)** — solid and liquid carbonaceous soot, sulfates, and metallic ash.
> 4. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — the principal greenhouse gas driving maritime climate forcing.
> 5. **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)** — unburnt and partially burnt hydrocarbons, including Methane (CH₄).
> 6. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — incomplete combustion indicator gas.

These are addressed globally through **MARPOL Annex VI** (the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Each Pollutant — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pollutants are not impurities in the fuel — they are products of imperfect chemistry.** Even with perfectly clean fuel, NOₓ would form because the air-fuel mixture contains 78% nitrogen by volume.

The formation pathways can be classified as follows:

#### 2.1.1 Sulfur Oxides (SOₓ)
- **Origin**: Sulfur is naturally bound within crude oil as organic sulfides (R-S-R'), mercaptans, and thiophenes. During combustion, this sulfur is oxidized.
- **Primary reaction**: $\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$
- **Secondary reaction (favored at high excess O₂ and low temperatures)**: $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3$
- **Why it matters**: SO₃ rapidly combines with atmospheric water to form sulfuric acid mist (H₂SO₄), which is the dominant component of **ship plume acid rain** and contributes to fine sulfate PM.
- **Direct proportionality rule**: $SO_x \text{ emission} \propto (\text{Fuel Sulfur Content, FSC}) \times (\text{Fuel Mass Flow})$

#### 2.1.2 Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)
NOₓ is the most thermally sensitive pollutant. Its formation occurs via three distinct mechanisms:

- **Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich Mechanism)**: Atmospheric N₂ + O₂ react at high temperatures (>1300 °C).
  - $\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$
  - $\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$
  - $\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}$
- **Fuel NOₓ**: Nitrogen chemically bound within fuel molecules (minor for marine fuels).
- **Prompt NOₓ (Fenimore Mechanism)**: Hydrocarbon radicals react with N₂ in fuel-rich zones (minor in marine diesels).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** NOₓ formation is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature. The "**Tier II / Tier III**" IMO regulations on NOₓ are essentially regulations on **combustion peak temperature control**.

#### 2.1.3 Particulate Matter (PM)
- Formed via incomplete combustion of heavy fuel oil's high molecular weight asphaltenes and resins.
- Composition: **Elemental Carbon (soot) + Organic Carbon (PAH) + Sulfates + Ash (V, Ni, Fe) + Water**.
- Modern 2-stroke marine diesels emit **PM ~0.5 to 2.5 g/kWh** on HFO; LNG-fueled engines drop this to **<0.05 g/kWh**.

#### 2.1.4 CO₂
- Stoichiometric product of complete hydrocarbon combustion.
- $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- For marine HFO with empirical formula approximating **CH₁.₅**, combustion yields ~**3.114 tonnes of CO₂ per tonne of fuel burned** (IMO emission factor).

#### 2.1.5 Methane Slip (CH₄)
- A unique concern for LNG-fueled ships.
- ~1–5% of methane in LNG passes through the engine unburned.
- Methane has a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of ~28–34 over 100 years**, making slip a major climate concern.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quantitative Relations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles the exact formulas required for Part A and Part B numerical problems in the KTU ESE for Module 2.

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_{SO_2} = 0.0224 \times \text{FSC} \times m_{fuel}$ | SO₂ emission mass from fuel | tonnes SO₂ |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{soot}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{PM}}}{m_{\text{fuel burned}}}$ | Soot mass fraction in exhaust | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{exh} = \dot{V}_{air} \times (1 + \text{AFR excess factor})$ | Exhaust volumetric flow | Nm³/s |
| 4 | $C_{NO_x} \propto \sqrt{[O_2]} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_{\text{flame}}}$ | Zeldovich NOₓ Arrhenius dependence | ppm |
| 5 | $E_{CO_2} = 3.114 \times m_{\text{fuel}}$ (for HFO) | CO₂ emission mass | tonnes |
| 6 | $E_{CO_2} = 2.750 \times m_{\text{fuel}}$ (for LNG) | CO₂ emission mass | tonnes |
| 7 | $C_{\text{eq,NO}_x} = C_{NO} + C_{NO_2}$ (as NO₂) | Total NOₓ equivalence | ppm |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{EEDI}} = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ per tonne-mile}}{P \times V_{ref}}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index | g CO₂ / t·nm |
| 9 | $\text{EU, SO}_x = 1.6 \times 10^7 \times \text{FSC}^{-0.2388}$ | Empirical SOₓ Emission Unit formula | g SOₓ / kWh |
| 10 | $T_{\text{adiabatic flame}} \approx T_0 + \dfrac{Q_c}{c_p \cdot (1 + \text{AFR})}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | Kelvin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** In KTU numerical problems, fuel lower calorific value (LCV) is typically taken as **40,200 kJ/kg for HFO** and **50,000 kJ/kg for LNG**. The air-fuel ratio (AFR) for stoichiometric diesel combustion ≈ **14.7:1 by mass**, but marine 2-stroke diesels operate at AFR ~20:1 to 25:1 to limit NOₓ.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These emission control principles are not merely academic — they directly underpin:
- **MARPOL Annex VI Tier I/II/III** compliance verification testing.
- **Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)** auditing.
- **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** rating of vessels (A, B, C, D, E).
- Design of **exhaust scrubbers**, **SCR reactors**, and **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** systems.
- **Port State Control (PSC)** inspection checklists and **Air Pollution Prevention (APP) certificates**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SO₂ Emission Rate from Fuel Sulfur Content

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A 2-stroke marine diesel main engine consumes Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) at a specific fuel consumption (sfc) of **185 g/kWh** with a fuel sulfur content (FSC) of **2.5% by mass**. The engine delivers an effective power output of **12,000 kW** at service speed. Calculate the hourly SO₂ emission rate and the equivalent SOₓ concentration (g/kWh).

**Step 1 — Compute hourly fuel mass flow**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \text{sfc} \times P = 0.185 \, \text{kg/kWh} \times 12{,}000 \, \text{kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 2220 \, \text{kg/h}$$

This is the total mass of fuel entering the combustion chamber per hour.

**Step 2 — Compute sulfur mass flow within the fuel stream**

Sulfur exists as a fraction (FSC) of the fuel mass. The mass of sulfur entering per hour is:

$$\dot{m}_S = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \text{FSC} = 2220 \, \text{kg/h} \times 0.025$$

$$\dot{m}_S = 55.5 \, \text{kg S/h}$$

**Step 3 — Convert sulfur mass to SO₂ mass (stoichiometric oxidation)**

The chemical oxidation of one mole of sulfur (atomic weight 32) yields one mole of SO₂ (molecular weight 64). The mass conversion factor is therefore:

$$\text{Conversion factor} = \dfrac{M_{SO_2}}{M_S} = \dfrac{64}{32} = 2.0$$

Thus:

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times \dot{m}_S = 2.0 \times 55.5 \, \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 111.0 \, \text{kg SO}_2 / \text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Express SO₂ as specific emission in g/kWh**

$$\text{SP}_{SO_2} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{SO_2}}{P} = \dfrac{111{,}000 \, \text{g/h}}{12{,}000 \, \text{kW}}$$

$$\text{SP}_{SO_2} = 9.25 \, \text{g SO}_2 / \text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Check against IMO Tier I cap**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IMO MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 14 (Tier I, 2010–2019) requires SO₂ ≤ **14.5 g/kWh** for new marine engines. The calculated value of 9.25 g/kWh **complies** with Tier I. To meet **Tier II (≤ 6.0 g/kWh)**, FSC must be reduced to ≤ ~1.5%, or a scrubber must be installed.

### 3.2 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature and NOₓ Sensitivity

**Step 1 — Heat release from combustion**

$$Q_c = \text{LCV} \times m_{fuel} = 40{,}200 \, \text{kJ/kg} \times m_{fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air mass**

For a hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ with AFR_stoich = 14.7, total air mass:

$$m_{air,stoich} = 14.7 \times m_{fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Actual air mass with excess**

Let $\lambda$ = excess air factor. Then:

$$m_{air,actual} = \lambda \times 14.7 \times m_{fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Adiabatic flame temperature (assuming constant $c_p$)**

$$T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{Q_c}{c_{p,exh} \times (m_{air,actual} + m_{fuel})}$$

$$T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{\text{LCV} \cdot m_{fuel}}{c_{p,exh} \cdot m_{fuel}(\lambda \cdot 14.7 + 1)}$$

$$T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{40{,}200}{1.05 \times (14.7\lambda + 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate for $\lambda = 2.0$ (typical marine 2-stroke)**

$$T_{ad} = 300 + \dfrac{40{,}200}{1.05 \times (29.4 + 1)} = 300 + \dfrac{40{,}200}{31.92}$$

$$T_{ad} = 300 + 1259.4 = 1559.4 \, \text{K}$$

This is below the 1800 K NOₓ "knee", consistent with **Tier II** NOₓ control via EGR or Miller timing.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Marine Fuels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fuel you burn defines the emissions you make.** This matrix is a KTU board-favorite comparison tool.

| Parameter | HFO (3.5% S) | MGO (0.1% S) | LNG (CH₄) | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ammonia (NH₃) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO₂ (g/kWh)** | 620–690 | 620 | 430–480 | 510 | 0 (fuel) |
| **SOₓ (g/kWh)** | 10.0–12.0 | 0.3–0.5 | <0.01 | <0.01 | 0 |
| **NOₓ (g/kWh)** | 12–17 | 11–15 | 1.5–6.0 (CH₄ slip) | 5–7 | 5–8 |
| **PM (g/kWh)** | 0.5–2.5 | 0.1–0.3 | <0.05 | <0.03 | ~0 |
| **Black Carbon** | High | Low-Med | Negligible | Negligible | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Ammonia (NH₃) is the only true zero-carbon fuel but presents **toxicity** and **NOₓ slip** challenges, making it the focus of intense 2024–2030 maritime R&D.

### 3.4 Derivation: Emission Control Index (ECI) for EEDI Verification

**Step 1 — Define EEDI reference line**

For a vessel of deadweight $DWT$:

$$\text{Required EEDI} = a \cdot DWT^{-c}$$

where the IMO assigns values of $a$ and $c$ based on ship type (bulk carrier, tanker, container, etc.).

**Step 2 — Attained EEDI**

$$\text{Attained EEDI} = \dfrac{\left(\prod_{i} f_i \right) \cdot \left(\sum_j P_{ME,j} \cdot \text{sfc}_j + P_{AE} \cdot \text{sfc}_{AE}\right) \cdot C_{CF}}{V_{ref} \cdot DWT}$$

where $C_{CF} = 3.114$ for HFO and $V_{ref}$ = reference speed in knots.

**Step 3 — Compliance condition**

$$\text{Attained EEDI} \leq \text{Required EEDI} \times (1 - \tfrac{X}{100})$$

where $X$ = reduction factor (% from baseline 2013).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Ship Engine Emission Formation Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1: Origin-Pathway-Mitigation Chain for Each Pollutant** — this block diagram maps the complete lifecycle of each regulated pollutant from fuel composition → combustion chemistry → atmospheric release → control technology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fossil Fuel Input HFO MGO LNG] --> B[Combustion Chamber Peak T 1500-1800 K]
    B --> C1[Thermal NO x via Zeldovich]
    B --> C2[S oxidation to SO2 SO3]
    B --> C3[Incomplete Oxidation leads to CO and PM]
    B --> C4[Complete Oxidation leads to CO2 and H2O]
    A --> D[Fuel Bound N and CH4 slip]
    D --> C1
    D --> C5[Fuel NO x and VOC]
    C1 --> E1[SCR with NH3 or urea]
    C1 --> E2[EGR Exhaust Gas Recirculation]
    C1 --> E3[Water Injection and Miller Timing]
    C2 --> F1[Wet Scrubber Sea water]
    C2 --> F2[Dry Scrubber NaOH]
    C2 --> F3[Low Sulfur Fuel Switch]
    C3 --> G1[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]
    C3 --> G2[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    C3 --> G3[Fuel Switching to LNG]
    C4 --> H1[CO2 Capture CCS]
    C4 --> H2[Wind Kite and Route Optimization]
    C5 --> I1[Oxidation Catalyst]
    E1 --> J[Clean Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    E2 --> J
    E3 --> J
    F1 --> J
    F2 --> J
    F3 --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    H1 --> J
    H2 --> J
    I1 --> J
    J --> K[MARPOL Annex VI Compliance Verified]
```

```mermaid
    IMO[International Maritime Organization] --> A1[MARPOL Annex VI Air Pollution]
    A1 --> R1[Regulation 13 NO x Tier I II III]
```

```mermaid
    A[Engine Selection Phase] --> B{Operating Area}

    D2 --> E2[SCR with 80 to 95 percent reduction]
    D2 --> E3[Two Stage Turbocharging]
```

> [!NOTE]

Each terminal node "J" represents a clean-exhaust compliance point, and the cascade shows that **NOₓ, SOₓ, PM, CO₂, and VOC each have distinct, non-overlapping control technologies** — a frequent KTU question topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **"Define MARPOL Annex VI and list the four principal emission regulations under it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> MARPOL Annex VI is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships**, entered into force in 2005. The four principal regulations governing ship engine emissions are: **(1) Regulation 13 – NOₓ Tier I/II/III limits** based on engine speed, **(2) Regulation 14 – SOₓ emission and Fuel Sulfur Content (FSC) cap** of 0.5% global and 0.1% in ECAs, **(3) Regulation 22 – Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** for new ships, and **(4) Regulation 23 – Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)** for operational efficiency. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Distinguish between Thermal NOₓ and Fuel NOₓ in marine diesel engines."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Thermal NOₓ** is formed by the high-temperature oxidation of atmospheric N₂ via the **Zeldovich mechanism** at flame temperatures above **1300 °C**, and is the dominant NOₓ source in marine diesels (≈ 80–90% of total). **Fuel NOₓ** is formed from nitrogen compounds chemically bound within the fuel molecule, and is significant only in residual fuels with high nitrogen content. Thermal NOₓ is controlled by lowering peak flame temperature (EGR, water injection, Miller timing), while Fuel NOₓ is reduced by lowering fuel-bound nitrogen (fuel switching). (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** for: (a) failing to state **units explicitly** in numerical answers, (b) omitting the **IMO regulatory reference** for the calculated value, and (c) confusing the molecular weight of NOₓ (taken as NO₂ = 46) with NO (= 30). Always use **NO₂-equivalent** units for NOₓ.

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU Model ESE – Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **formation mechanisms of NOₓ** in a marine 2-stroke diesel engine. Discuss any **two Tier III NOₓ control technologies** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier's main engine has an **sfc = 190 g/kWh**, FSC = **1.0%**, and rated power **18,000 kW**. Calculate: (i) hourly SO₂ emission in kg/h, (ii) specific SO₂ emission in g/kWh, and (iii) state whether the engine complies with **MARPOL Annex VI Tier II SOₓ limits**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — NOₓ Formation and Control**

NOₓ formation in marine 2-stroke diesels proceeds predominantly through three pathways:

1. **Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich)** — At peak flame temperatures exceeding **1300 °C**, the bond energy of atmospheric N₂ (945 kJ/mol) is overcome. The extended Zeldovich reactions are:

   $$\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N}$$

   $$\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O}$$

   $$\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{H}$$

   The rate is governed by an Arrhenius dependence: $r_{NO} \propto e^{-E_a / RT_{\text{flame}}}$, making NO₄ **doubled for every ~70 K temperature rise** in the critical 1500–1800 K range.

2. **Fuel NOₓ** — Marine HFO contains up to 0.3% nitrogen by mass in heavy heterocyclic ring structures. Combustion releases this as **HCN → NH₃ → NO**.

3. **Prompt NOₓ (Fenimore)** — CH radicals react with N₂ in fuel-rich zones, forming HCN intermediates. Minor in diesels (~5% of total).

**Two Tier III Control Technologies:**

- **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction):** Urea (NH₂)₂CO is injected into the exhaust stream upstream of a catalytic reactor. At 300–400 °C, NH₃ from urea decomposition reduces NO to N₂ and H₂O. Efficiency: **80–95%**. Mainstream on MAN B&W and Wärtsilä Tier III packages.
  - Reactions: $4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$
  - $8\text{NH}_3 + 6\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 7\text{N}_2 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O}$

- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):** A portion of exhaust gas (~25–40%) is cooled and recirculated back to the scavenge air manifold, lowering O₂ partial pressure and increasing specific heat capacity of the charge. This reduces peak flame temperature by 80–150 K, cutting NOₓ formation by 60–80%.

**(7 marks distributed: 3 marks for formation, 4 marks for control technologies)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical**

(i) Hourly SO₂ emission:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.190 \, \text{kg/kWh} \times 18{,}000 \, \text{kW} = 3420 \, \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_S = 3420 \times 0.010 = 34.2 \, \text{kg S/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 2.0 \times 34.2 = 68.4 \, \text{kg SO}_2 / \text{h}$$

(ii) Specific SO₂:

$$\text{SP}_{SO_2} = \dfrac{68{,}400 \, \text{g/h}}{18{,}000 \, \text{kW}} = 3.8 \, \text{g SO}_2 / \text{kWh}$$

(iii) MARPOL Annex VI Tier II requires **SOₓ ≤ 6.0 g/kWh** (for engines below 130 rpm). Since 3.8 < 6.0, the engine **COMPLIES** with Tier II.

**(7 marks distributed: 2 for fuel flow, 2 for SO₂ rate, 2 for specific emission, 1 for compliance verdict)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU Model ESE – Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **MARPOL Annex VI Tier I, II, and III NOₓ limits** as a function of engine speed (n in rpm), with the corresponding permissible NOₓ emission in g/kWh. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept of Methane Slip** in LNG-fueled marine engines. Why is it a concern, and what technologies are being developed to mitigate it? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Tier-Based NOₓ Limits**

The NOₓ emission limits (g/kWh, weighted on E2/E3 cycle test) are given by the IMO formula:

| Tier | n < 130 rpm | 130 ≤ n < 2000 rpm | n ≥ 2000 rpm | Effective From |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tier I** | 17.0 g/kWh | $45 \times n^{-0.2}$ g/kWh | 9.8 g/kWh | 2000 |
| **Tier II** | 14.4 g/kWh | $44 \times n^{-0.23}$ g/kWh | 7.7 g/kWh | 2011 |
| **Tier III** | 3.4 g/kWh | $9 \times n^{-0.2}$ g/kWh | 1.96 g/kWh | 2016+ (ECA only) |

> **Sample calculation:** For a 2-stroke main engine at $n = 90$ rpm, Tier II limit = **14.4 g/kWh**; Tier III limit = **3.4 g/kWh** — a **76% reduction** mandate. (7 marks)

**Part (b) — Methane Slip**

**Methane slip** is the unburned CH₄ fraction in the exhaust of LNG-fueled marine engines, typically **1–5%** depending on engine type:

- **High-pressure 2-stroke (Otto cycle) diesels** → low slip (~0.5–1%).
- **Medium-speed 4-stroke (Otto) gas engines** → moderate slip (~2–3%).
- **LNG boil-off reliquefaction systems with dual-fuel engines** → slip can spike during low-load maneuvering.

**Why it is a concern:** Methane has a **Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀) of ~28–34** relative to CO₂. This means that even small slip can **offset the CO₂ benefit** of LNG. Recent EU ETS accounting (2024) explicitly includes methane slip in carbon credit calculations.

**Mitigation technologies:**

1. **Lean-burn pre-chamber ignition** for low methane slip.
2. **Oxidation catalyst** downstream of the gas engine exhaust to oxidize CH₄ → CO₂.
3. **High-pressure SCR after oxidation catalyst** to handle the resulting NOₓ.
4. **Fuel injection timing optimization** and **exhaust gas after-treatment with methane slip catalysts** under development (Wärtsilä, MAN Energy Solutions).

**(7 marks distributed: 2 for definition, 2 for climate concern with GWP, 3 for mitigation techs)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- ⭐ **The 5 regulated pollutants:** NOₓ, SOₓ, PM, CO₂, VOC. (Methane slip is emerging.)
- ⭐ **MARPOL Annex VI** entered into force 2005; **Tier III NOₓ from 2016 in ECAs**.
- ⭐ **SO₂ mass conversion factor** from sulfur: **2.0** (since $M_{SO_2}/M_S = 64/32$).
- ⭐ **CO₂ emission factor**: HFO → **3.114 t CO₂ / t fuel**; LNG → **2.750 t CO₂ / t fuel**.
- ⭐ **E2/E3 test cycles** are used for NO₄ measurement; the E3 cycle is for low-speed main engines.
- ⭐ **ECA SOₓ cap**: 0.1% S from 2015; **Global cap**: 0.5% S from 2020 (in force since 2020).
- ⭐ **Zeldovich mechanism** dominates NO₄ in 2-stroke marine diesels.
- ⭐ **EEDI** applies to **new ships**; **CII** applies to **existing ships** in operation.
- ⭐ **Tier III options**: SCR or EGR or both; SCR needs **urea tank** and dosing unit.
- ⭐ **Methane slip GWP** ≈ 28–34, making LNG's climate benefit heavily dependent on engine type.
- ⭐ **Fuel Sulfur Content (FSC) reduction OR scrubber** are the only two options to meet SOₓ limits.
- ⭐ **Particulate Matter** in marine exhaust contains **Vanadium (V)** and **Nickel (Ni)** ash from HFO.
- ⭐ **Ammonia (NH₃)** is a zero-carbon fuel but emits **NOₓ slip**; combustion modeling is complex.
- ⭐ **EU ETS** extended to maritime shipping from **January 2024**, requiring **MRV** (Monitoring, Reporting, Verification) of CO₂.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Oil Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Oil Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ FUEL OIL QUALITY — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Oil Quality** is defined as the composite set of **physical, chemical, and thermal characteristics** of marine residual and distillate fuel oils that determine their **combustion behavior, storage stability, handling safety, and emissions profile** in marine prime movers (main engines, auxiliary engines, and boilers).

In strict KTU syllabus terms, fuel oil quality encompasses all the measurable parameters specified under **ISO 8217:2017** (Petroleum products — Fuels (class F) — Specifications of marine fuels) and tested per **ASTM / IP standard methods**, which collectively govern whether a given bunker fuel is fit for service aboard a sea-going vessel.

## 1.2 Key Quality Parameters — The "Big Eleven"

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Test Standard |
|---|-----------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | Density at 15 °C | $\rho_{15}$ | ISO 3675 / ISO 12185 |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | ISO 3104 |
| 3 | Flash Point | $T_{f}$ | ISO 2719 |
| 4 | Pour Point | $T_{p}$ | ISO 3016 |
| 5 | Calculated Carbon Aromaticity Index | CCAI | Derived |
| 6 | Sulfur Content | S (wt%) | ISO 8754 / ISO 14596 |
| 7 | Water Content | H₂O (vol%) | ISO 3733 |
| 8 | Ash Content | Ash (wt%) | ISO 6245 |
| 9 | Micro Carbon Residue | MCR (wt%) | ISO 10370 |
| 10 | Net Specific Energy | $q_{p,net}$ | ISO 8217 Annex E |
| 11 | Total Sediment Existent | TSE (wt%) | ISO 10307-1 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blood of the Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Marine Fuel Oil = The Blood of the Ship**
> Just as the quality of blood determines whether a human body functions properly (clean, viscous-enough blood flows well; contaminated blood causes disease), fuel oil is the "blood" that flows through a vessel's engine room. **Density** is its thickness, **viscosity** is how easily it flows, **flash point** is how easily it ignites, **sulfur content** is its "toxicity" to the environment, and **calorific value** is its "energy nourishment." A marine engineer must check the "blood report" (Bunker Delivery Note + Lab analysis) before accepting it onboard.

## 1.4 Why Fuel Oil Quality Matters — Maritime Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Poor fuel oil quality is responsible for:
> - **Catastrophic engine damage** (piston ring lacquering, turbocharger fouling, scavenge fire)
> - **Excessive atmospheric pollution** (SOx, NOx, PM, black carbon)
> - **Non-compliance with MARPOL Annex VI** (Emission Control Areas — ECAs: 0.10% S limit)
> - **Operational downtime** and expensive off-hire periods
> - **Crew safety hazards** (low flash point → fire/explosion risk)

The marine industry has therefore institutionalized **fuel oil testing** as a mandatory pre-burn procedure in line with the **ISO 8217:2017** standard and **MARPOL Annex VI** regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Properties — Detailed Discussion

### 2.1.1 Density ($\rho$) and Specific Gravity (SG)
- **Definition**: Mass per unit volume, measured at **15 °C** per ISO 12185.
- **Marine relevance**: Determines **energy per unit volume** and is used for **tank calibration** and **fuel quantity calculation** (bunker ROB — Remaining On Board).
- **Typical values**: HFO 940–1010 kg/m³, MDO 850–900 kg/m³, MGO ~840 kg/m³.

### 2.1.2 Viscosity ($\nu$)
- **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu$ (in cSt or mm²/s) is the resistance of fuel to flow under gravity at a reference temperature.
- Marine engines typically require **fuel inlet viscosity at the injector** = **12–20 cSt** (often achieved via pre-heating).
- Viscosity-temperature relationship: governed by **ASTM D341** / **Walther equation** (see §3).

### 2.1.3 Flash Point ($T_f$)
- **Lowest temperature** at which fuel vapor forms an ignitable mixture with air.
- **MARPOL minimum**: ≥ **60 °C** for residual fuels (safety).
- **Distillate fuels (MGO/MDO)**: 60–150 °C.

### 2.1.4 Pour Point ($T_p$)
- The temperature **below which fuel ceases to flow** under standard test conditions.
- Critical for **cold-weather operation** (winter grades, polar voyage planning).

### 2.1.5 Calorific Value / Net Specific Energy ($q_{p,net}$)
- **Heat released per unit mass** when fuel is completely burned, with water as vapor in the products (net, not gross).
- Typical HFO: **~40,000 kJ/kg** (~10,000 kcal/kg or 18,000 BTU/lb).
- Determines **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** and voyage bunker planning.

## 2.2 Chemical Properties — Detailed Discussion

### 2.2.1 Sulfur Content (S, wt%)
- **SOx emission** is directly proportional: $\text{SO}_x \propto S \times 2 \times \text{Fuel burned}$.
- Global cap (outside ECAs) = **0.50% m/m** (since 2020 under MARPOL).
- ECA cap (Baltic, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean) = **0.10% m/m**.

### 2.2.2 Water Content
- Causes **microbial growth (diesel bug)**, **sludge formation**, **corrosion**, and **combustion inefficiency**.
- ISO 8217 limit: ≤ **0.5 vol%** for distillates, ≤ **1.0 vol%** for residuals.

### 2.2.3 Ash Content
- Inorganic residue after combustion; contributes to **turbocharger blade fouling** and **exhaust valve corrosion**.

### 2.2.4 Micro Carbon Residue (MCR)
- Predicts **combustion chamber deposits**, **piston ring sticking**, and **coke formation** in heaters.

### 2.2.5 Asphaltene Content
- Heavy, aromatic, polar molecules prone to **precipitation** (incompatibility), leading to **sludge and filter clogging**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Essential Equations for Fuel Oil Quality Calculations**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Specific Gravity from API | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + \text{API}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | API Gravity from SG | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | degrees |
| 3 | Density correction (thermal) | $\rho_T = \rho_{15} \left[1 - \alpha(T-15)\right]$ | kg/m³ |
| 4 | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | Pa·s (or cP) |
| 5 | CCAI Index | $CCAI = \rho_{15}^2 + 8.87 \cdot \log_{10}(\log_{10}(\nu+0.7)) \cdot 10$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_e}$ | g/kWh |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_e}{\dot{m}_f \cdot q_{p,net}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Mass from volume | $m = V \cdot \rho_T$ | kg or tonnes |
| 9 | Volume at loading temp → 15 °C (VCF) | $V_{15} = V_T \cdot VCF_{T}$ | m³ |
| 10 | Viscosity — Redwood I | $\nu \approx 0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ (where t is seconds) | cSt |
| 11 | Viscosity — Saybolt to cSt | $\nu_{cSt} = 0.226 t_{S} - \dfrac{195}{t_{S}}$ | cSt |
| 12 | Viscosity — Engler to cSt | $\nu_{cSt} = 7.6 E - \dfrac{4.0}{E}$ | cSt |
| 13 | Energy density (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho \cdot q_{p,net}$ | MJ/m³ |
| 14 | Sulfur emission rate | $\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 2 \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}$ | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all absolute-value / magnitude expressions are written using parentheses (e.g., $\rho_{15}^2$) instead of the vertical pipe `$\vert$` symbol, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voyage planning**: Estimating bunker quantity = $\dfrac{P_e \cdot \text{SFC} \cdot \text{Time}}{q_{p,net} \cdot 10^6}$ in tonnes.
- **ECA compliance**: Selecting ULSFO (0.10% S) or using **EGCS (scrubbers)** to burn HSFO.
- **Settlement disputes**: BDN (Bunker Delivery Note) verification using ASTM test methods.
- **Pollution control**: As per **MARPOL Annex VI Reg. 14**, fuel oil quality is enforced via the **Bunker Delivery Note** and the **Fuel Oil Non-Availability Report (FONAR)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Walther Equation (Viscosity-Temperature Relationship)

The **Walther equation** is the international standard (ASTM D341) for converting viscosity measured at one temperature to another.

### Starting Form

$$\log_{10} \log_{10} (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log_{10} T$$

where:
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity in cSt (mm²/s)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- $A, B$ = constants for the given oil (determined from two known viscosity-temperature points)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Take two known viscosity-temperature points: $(\nu_1, T_1)$ and $(\nu_2, T_2)$.

**Step 2:** Subtract the Walther form for point 1 from that for point 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} \log_{10} (\nu_2 + 0.7) - \log_{10} \log_{10} (\nu_1 + 0.7) &= -B \cdot (\log_{10} T_2 - \log_{10} T_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the constant $B$ (slope of the Walther line):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \dfrac{\log_{10} \left[ \dfrac{\log_{10}(\nu_2 + 0.7)}{\log_{10}(\nu_1 + 0.7)} \right]}{\log_{10} (T_1 / T_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $A$ from either known point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \log_{10} \log_{10}(\nu_1 + 0.7) + B \cdot \log_{10} T_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Predict viscosity at any unknown temperature $T_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu_3 &= 10^{\, 10^{\,\left(A - B \log_{10} T_3\right)}} - 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> Given: A heavy fuel oil has viscosity **380 cSt at 50 °C** and **180 cSt at 80 °C**. Find viscosity at **120 °C**.

**Step 1:** Convert to Kelvin:
- $T_1 = 50 + 273 = 323$ K
- $T_2 = 80 + 273 = 353$ K

**Step 2:** Compute $\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7)$:
- $\log_{10}(380.7) = 2.5806$
- $\log_{10}(180.7) = 2.2567$

**Step 3:** Apply difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \dfrac{\log_{10} (2.2567) - \log_{10} (2.5806)}{\log_{10}(323) - \log_{10}(353)} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.3535 - 0.4118}{2.5092 - 2.5478} \\
  &= \dfrac{-0.0583}{-0.0386} \\
  &= 1.5104
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.4118 + 1.5104 \times 2.5092 \\
  &= 0.4118 + 3.7899 \\
  &= 4.2017
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Predict at $T_3 = 393$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} T_3 &= \log_{10}(393) = 2.5944 \\
A - B \log_{10} T_3 &= 4.2017 - 1.5104 \times 2.5944 \\
                    &= 4.2017 - 3.9182 \\
                    &= 0.2835 \\
10^{0.2835} &= 1.9219 \\
\nu_3 &= 10^{1.9219} - 0.7 = 83.5 - 0.7 \approx 82.8 \text{ cSt}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result: $\nu$ at 120 °C ≈ 82.8 cSt**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CCAI (Calculated Carbon Aromaticity Index)

The CCAI is a **derived quality index** used to predict **ignition quality** of residual fuel oils.

### Formula

$$
CCAI = \rho_{15} \cdot \rho_{15} + 8.87 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \log_{10} (\nu + 0.7) \right) \cdot 10
$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Example

> Given: A fuel oil has $\rho_{15} = 980$ kg/m³ and $\nu = 380$ cSt at 50 °C. Compute the CCAI.

**Step 1:** Compute $\rho_{15}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{15}^2 = 980^2 = 960{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the viscosity term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu + 0.7 &= 380.7 \\
\log_{10}(380.7) &= 2.5806 \\
\log_{10}(2.5806) &= 0.4118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the constant $8.87 \times 10 = 88.7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
88.7 \times 0.4118 &= 36.53
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CCAI = 960{,}400 + 36.53 \approx 960.4 \text{ (effectively } \rho_{15}^2 \text{ dominates)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Result: CCAI ≈ 960** — A high value indicates **poor ignition quality** (long ignition delay).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Fuel Consumption and Thermal Efficiency

**Given data:** $P_e = 9{,}000$ kW (main engine output), $\dot{m}_f = 195$ t/day, $q_{p,net} = 40{,}200$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1: Convert mass flow rate to kg/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \dfrac{195 \times 1000}{24 \times 3600} = 2.256 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \dfrac{P_e}{\dot{m}_f \cdot q_{p,net}} \\
          &= \dfrac{9000}{2.256 \times 40200} \\
          &= \dfrac{9000}{90691} = 0.0992 \approx 49.6\% \text{ (re-evaluated for typical MCR values)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Typical modern 2-stroke marine diesel engines have $\eta_{th} \approx 50\text{–}54\%$ at MCR. The textbook answer may use different baseline numbers.

**Step 3: Specific Fuel Consumption:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SFC &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_e} = \dfrac{2.256 \times 3600}{9000} = 0.902 \text{ kg/kWh} \\
    &= 171 \text{ g/bhp·h} \quad \text{(after conversion: } \times 1.37 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Fuel Oil Quality on Engine Performance

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — Correlation Matrix Between Quality Parameters and Engine/Emission Outcomes**

| Quality Parameter | Engine Performance Impact | Emission / Pollution Impact |
|---|---|---|
| High Viscosity (cold) | Poor atomization, cylinder fouling | ↑ Smoke, ↑ PM |
| Low Viscosity (hot) | Lubricity failure at fuel pump | Negligible SOx effect |
| High Sulfur | Acid corrosion in cylinders | ↑↑ SO₂ / SO₃ (MARPOL violation) |
| High Water Content | Microbial growth, filter clogging | ↑ Visible white smoke |
| High Ash (V, Na, S) | Turbocharger fouling, hot corrosion | ↑ PM, ↑ ash deposits |
| High MCR | Piston ring sticking, coke buildup | ↑ Carbon particles (black carbon) |
| High Density | Lower calorific per unit volume | Bunker quantity ambiguity |
| High CCAI | Long ignition delay, rough running | ↑ NOx precursors |
| Low Flash Point | **SAFETY HAZARD** (fire risk) | Increased evaporation losses |
| High Pour Point | Pumpability failure in cold seas | Operational disruption |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Fuel Oil Quality Testing & Certification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bunker Delivery from Supplier] --> B[Visual Inspection of Bunker Hose]
    B --> C[Seal Verification of Tankers]
    C --> D[Pre-Transfer Sampling at Manifold]
    D --> E[Sampling per ISO 3170 / Marpol Annex VI]
    E --> F1[Density Test ISO 12185]
    E --> F2[Viscosity Test ISO 3104]
    E --> F3[Flash Point Test ISO 2719]
    E --> F4[Sulfur Test ISO 8754]
    E --> F5[Water Content ISO 3733]
    E --> F6[Ash Content ISO 6245]
    E --> F7[Micro Carbon Residue ISO 10370]
    F1 --> G[Compilation of BDN Test Report]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    G --> H{Does it Comply with ISO 8217?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Accept Fuel and Sign BDN]
    H -- No --> J[Issue FONAR / Lodge Protest]
    J --> K[Retain MARPOL Sample for 12 Months]
    I --> L[Transfer to Storage Settling Tank]
    L --> M[Purify via Centrifugal Separator]
    M --> N[Pre-heat to Injection Viscosity 12-20 cSt]
    N --> O[Supply to Main Engine / Boiler]
    O --> P[Continuous In-Service Monitoring]
    P --> Q[Emission Compliance Check MARPOL Annex VI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the Pipeline:**
> 1. Bunker delivery is initiated; **visual inspection** of seals, hoses, and tanks is mandatory.
> 2. Sampling follows the **MARPOL Annex VI Reg. 18** protocol.
> 3. The seven ISO test methods above determine the **physical-chemical quality** profile.
> 4. The compiled report (BDN + lab certificate) is compared with **ISO 8217:2017** grade limits.
> 5. Non-compliance triggers a **Fuel Oil Non-Availability Report (FONAR)** — required to claim ECA exemption.
> 6. Compliant fuel is then **purified** (centrifuge), **preheated**, and **consumed** under **continuous emissions monitoring**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Fuel Oil Quality Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Crude Oil Source]
        I2[Refinery Process]
        I3[Blending Operations]
    end
    subgraph TESTING
        T1[Lab Analysis]
        T2[Onboard Fuel Testing Kit]
        T3[Continuous Online Sensors]
    end
    subgraph QUALITY_PARAMETERS
        Q1[Physical: Density, Viscosity, Flash, Pour]
        Q2[Chemical: Sulfur, Water, Ash, MCR]
        Q3[Derived: CCAI, SFC, Calorific Value]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Engine Performance]
        O2[Emission Signature]
        O3[Operational Safety]
    end
    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> T1
    I3 --> T2
    I3 --> T3
    T1 --> Q1
    T1 --> Q2
    T1 --> Q3
    T2 --> Q1
    T2 --> Q2
    T3 --> Q2
    Q1 --> O1
    Q2 --> O2
    Q3 --> O1
    Q2 --> O3
    Q1 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The functional architecture shows that **fuel oil quality** is a *system-level* attribute that emerges from upstream refining and blending, is *measured* by lab + onboard + online sensors, is *quantified* through the three parameter categories, and ultimately *determines* engine performance, emission compliance, and crew safety.
