# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition and Scope
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | CO1, Remember Level]**

**Q:** Define **Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT)** as per MARPOL 73/78, Annex I. State the **tonnage applicability** for crude oil tankers and product carriers.

**Model Answer:**

> A Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT) is a ballast tank **physically separated** from the cargo oil containment, fuel oil, and slop systems on an oil tanker, with its own dedicated pumps and piping, and having **no permanent cross-connection** to the cargo system. The purpose is to carry clean seawater ballast without contamination by oil.
>
> **Tonnage Applicability (MARPOL 73/78 Annex I, Reg. 18):**
> - **Crude oil tankers of 20,000 DWT and above**
> - **Product carriers of 30,000 DWT and above**

### Question 2: Capacity and Purpose
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | CO2, Understand Level]**

**Q:** State **three design requirements** that a Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT) must satisfy under MARPOL Reg. 18.

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Sufficient Capacity:** SBT must have capacity to enable the ship to navigate safely on a ballast voyage **without using cargo tanks** for ballast purposes.
> 
> 2. **Minimum Forward Draft:** SBT must be able to maintain a minimum forward draft of **$T_{fwd,min} = 2.00 + 0.02L$** meters (where $L$ is the rule length in meters).
> 
> 3. **Physical Segregation:** SBT must be **structurally separated** from cargo oil, fuel oil, and slop systems, with **no permanent cross-connections**; bilge wells must be small and easily cleanable.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** SBT with Double Hull (DH) or Crude Oil Washing (COW). SBT is a **ballast system**; DH is a **structural** arrangement; COW is a **cleaning system**. They are *complementary*, not interchangeable.
> 2. **Numerical mistakes** in SBT capacity questions usually come from:
>    - Forgetting to **subtract lightweight + stores** from required displacement.
>    - Using **wrong block coefficient** form (using $T$ where $T_{fwd,min}$ is required).
>    - Confusing **displacement (∆)** with **displaced volume (∇)**.
> 3. **Diagrams** in the exam should clearly show **physical separation** between cargo and ballast compartments — this is worth **1–2 marks** even without explanation.
> 4. **"Dedicated Clean Ballast"** in product carriers (CBT) is *different* from SBT — students often lose marks by using these terms interchangeably.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — SBT Capacity Design & Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | CO3, Apply / Analyze Level]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **MARPOL Annex I Regulation 18** requirements for the design and arrangement of Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT). Mention the applicable tonnage thresholds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A crude oil tanker has the following particulars:
- DWT = 120,000 tonnes
- Lightweight = 18,000 tonnes
- $L$ = 280 m, $B$ = 42 m
- $C_B$ = 0.83
- Stores = 3,000 tonnes
- Residual cargo + slop = 5,000 tonnes

Calculate the **minimum SBT capacity** required. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^{3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — MARPOL Reg. 18 Requirements (7 marks)

The design and arrangement of SBT under **MARPOL 73/78, Annex I, Regulation 18** requires the following:

**1. Tonnage Applicability (2 marks):**
- SBT is mandatory for **crude oil tankers ≥ 20,000 DWT** and **product carriers ≥ 30,000 DWT** built after the convention's effective dates (post-1982).

**2. Capacity Requirement (2 marks):**
- SBT must be of **sufficient capacity** to enable the vessel to navigate safely on a ballast voyage **without recourse to cargo tanks** for ballast water.
- The minimum forward draft requirement: $T_{fwd,min} = 2.00 + 0.02L$ (m).
- The vessel must be able to maintain satisfactory **draft and trim** under all expected ballast conditions.

**3. Piping and Equipment (2 marks):**
- SBT must have **dedicated pumps and piping** completely separate from the cargo oil transfer system.
- **No permanent cross-connections** are allowed between SBT and cargo oil, fuel oil, or slop systems. A **flanged spool piece** is permitted for emergency transfer but must normally be blanked off.
- Bilge wells in SBT must be **small, accessible, and easily cleanable** (no oil-trapping corners).

**4. Documentation (1 mark):**
- The **SBT capacity** must be recorded in the **International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate**, including the method used for capacity determination.

---

##### Part (b) — SBT Capacity Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- DWT = 120,000 t
- Lightweight = 18,000 t
- $L$ = 280 m, $B$ = 42 m
- $C_B$ = 0.83
- Stores = 3,000 t
- Residuals = 5,000 t
- $\rho_{sw}$ = 1.025 t/m³

**Step 1 — Minimum forward draft (1 mark):**

$$T_{fwd,min} = 2.00 + 0.02L = 2.00 + 0.02(280) = 7.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Required displaced volume (1.5 marks):**

$$\nabla_{min} = C_B \times L \times B \times T_{fwd,min}$$

$$\nabla_{min} = 0.83 \times 280 \times 42 \times 7.60 = 74{,}176.32 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Required displacement mass (1 mark):**

$$\Delta_{min} = \rho_{sw} \times \nabla_{min} = 1.025 \times 74{,}176.32 = 76{,}030.73 \text{ t}$$

**Step 4 — Weight on board without ballast (1 mark):**

$$W_{no\_ballast} = 18{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 26{,}000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 5 — Required ballast mass (1.5 marks):**

$$W_{SBT} = 76{,}030.73 - 26{,}000 = 50{,}030.73 \text{ t}$$

**Step 6 — Required SBT volume (1 mark):**

$$V_{SBT} = \dfrac{W_{SBT}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{50{,}030.73}{1.025} = 48{,}810.47 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Final Answer:**

> **Minimum SBT Capacity ≈ 48,810 m³ ≈ 50,031 tonnes**
>
> This is approximately **40.7 %** of the vessel's DWT, which is typical for a **Suezmax**-class crude oil tanker.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — SBT Integration with Pollution Control Systems

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | CO4, Analyze / Evaluate Level]**

**(a)** With the help of a **labeled schematic**, explain how the **Segregated Ballast Tank system** is integrated with the **Cargo Oil System** and **Crude Oil Washing (COW)** system in a modern double hull tanker. What is the role of SBT in the **segregation philosophy** of MARPOL Annex I? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of SBT. How does SBT compare with the older **"Oily Ballast"** system in terms of pollution risk, operational complexity, and cost? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Schematic & Integration (7 marks)

**Schematic (sketch expected, 3 marks):**

A simple cross-section / plan view should show:

```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|   SBT (Forepeak)   ||  SBT (Double Bottom)  ||  SBT (Wing)  |
|                    ||                       ||               |
|   C1   |   C2      ||   C3   |   C4         ||   C5          |
|   CARGO TANKS      ||   CARGO TANKS         ||   CARGO TANK  |
|                    ||                       ||               |
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
      \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\   OUTER HULL   \\\\\\\\\\\\\\
```

- **Cargo Tanks (C1–C5)** are in the **center** of the vessel.
- **SBT (Double Bottom + Wing Tanks)** form a **complete envelope** around the cargo tanks.
- **Cargo piping** runs through the cargo tank top (manifold).
- **Ballast piping** runs through SBT compartments to dedicated ballast pumps.
- **No pipe crosses the SBT–cargo boundary**.

**Integration Points (2 marks):**
- **SBT ↔ Sea Chest:** Dedicated sea chests feed ballast pumps that fill SBT.
- **SBT ↔ Overboard Discharge:** Ballast pumps discharge to sea via overboard valves.
- **SBT ↔ COW System:** SBT provides seawater for **top washing** of cargo tanks; COW system uses crude oil to clean the lower sections. SBT is **complementary** to COW, not a substitute.
- **SBT ↔ IOPP Certificate:** SBT capacities are documented in the IOPP Cert.

**Segregation Philosophy Role (2 marks):**
The MARPOL Annex I segregation philosophy is built on the principle of **"containment at source."** SBT embodies this by:
- Eliminating oil contamination of ballast water **structurally**, not by treatment.
- Allowing **parallel operations** (cargo handling + ballasting) without cross-contamination.
- Reducing dependence on mechanical/chemical treatment (OWS, bilge monitors).

---

##### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, Comparison (7 marks)

**Advantages of SBT (3 marks):**

1. **Zero Oil Contamination of Ballast:** SBT water is never in contact with cargo oil → no need for treatment.
2. **Regulatory Compliance:** Direct compliance with MARPOL Annex I Reg. 18.
3. **Operational Flexibility:** Allows parallel operations (loading + ballasting simultaneously).
4. **Pollution Prevention:** Eliminates a major historical source of operational pollution.
5. **Compatible with COW:** SBT + COW provides comprehensive pollution control.

**Limitations of SBT (2 marks):**

1. **Cargo Capacity Loss:** SBT occupies hull volume → reduces cargo carrying capacity.
2. **Increased Steel Weight:** SBT adds structural weight → reduces payload.
3. **Higher Initial Cost:** More complex piping and pumping system.
4. **Free Surface Effect:** Partially filled SBT reduces stability.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Oily Ballast (Old System) | SBT (Modern System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Oil contamination** | Yes — significant | None — segregated |
| **Discharge treatment** | Mandatory OWS + 15 ppm monitoring | None required |
| **Pollution risk** | High | Negligible |
| **Operational complexity** | Simple piping | More complex (dedicated pumps) |
| **Cargo capacity** | Maximum | Slightly reduced |
| **MARPOL compliance** | Non-compliant for newbuilds | Fully compliant |

> **Conclusion:** SBT is **environmentally superior** despite its slight cost and capacity penalties. Modern shipping has universally adopted SBT for tankers above MARPOL thresholds.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — SBT**

- ⭐ **Definition:** SBT = ballast tank **physically and operationally separated** from cargo oil system.
- ⭐ **MARPOL Reference:** Annex I, **Regulation 18**.
- ⭐ **Applicability:** Crude oil tankers ≥ **20,000 DWT**; Product carriers ≥ **30,000 DWT**.
- ⭐ **Key Formula (Minimum Forward Draft):** $T_{fwd,min} = 2.00 + 0.02L$ (m).
- ⭐ **Block Coefficient:** $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$.
- ⭐ **Displacement–Volume Relation:** $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \times \nabla$ where $\rho_{sw}$ = 1.025 t/m³.
- ⭐ **SBT Capacity Formula:** $V_{SBT} = \dfrac{\Delta_{min} - (LW + \text{Residuals} + \text{Stores})}{\rho_{sw}}$.
- ⭐ **No permanent cross-connections** between SBT and cargo systems.
- ⭐ **Dedicated pumps and piping** are required.
- ⭐ **IOPP Certificate** must record SBT capacity.
- ⭐ **Free Surface Effect (FSE):** Partial filling of SBT → reduces $GM$ → must be checked.
- ⭐ **SBT ≠ Double Hull (DH)** — SBT is a *ballast* concept; DH is *structural*.
- ⭐ **SBT + COW** are complementary, not alternatives.
- ⭐ **Typical SBT % DWT:** Aframax 28–35 %, VLCC 35–50 % (ratio increases with size).
- ⭐ **Seawater Density:** 1.025 t/m³ (1015 kPa, 4°C, 35 ‰ salinity).
- ⭐ **Bilge wells** in SBT must be **small, cleanable, and located** for easy maintenance.
- ⭐ **MARPOL Reg. 18.8** allows cargo tanks to be used as ballast in **exceptional circumstances** (e.g., emergency), but only after meeting strict conditions.
- ⭐ SBT **discharge** of clean ballast is permitted in most waters, subject to local regulations.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Segregated Ballast Tanks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oily Water Separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oily Water Separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** is a marine pollution control device installed in the bilge and machinery spaces of a seagoing vessel, designed to separate free and emulsified oil from wastewater prior to discharge overboard. Under **MARPOL 73/78, Annex I, Regulation 14**, the effluent from an OWS must not contain oil in excess of **15 parts per million (15 ppm)** by mass, ensuring compliance with international marine environmental protection standards set by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

The OWS operates on the principle of **gravitational separation** of immiscible fluids (oil and water) having different densities, augmented in modern designs by **coalescence** and **centrifugal** mechanisms to enhance separation efficiency of fine droplets (typically **< 50 μm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The OWS is a core component of the **pollution prevention machinery** sub-module. Examiners frequently ask about the 15 ppm IMO standard, the role of MARPOL Annex I, and the physical principles behind gravity separation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an OWS as a **"salad dressing restorer."** When you mix oil and vinegar (or oil and water) in a bottle and shake it, the mixture looks cloudy. If you let it sit still on the table for a few minutes, the oil floats to the top and water sinks to the bottom — because they are immiscible and have different densities. An OWS does exactly the same job, but:

- It is **far more precise** (achieves 15 ppm vs. visible separation).
- It works on **continuous flow**, not a static bottle.
- It uses **coalescer plates** to make tiny oil droplets stick together (grow bigger), so they rise faster.
- It uses the ship's **gravity** in a controlled settling tank to do the work.

In essence, the OWS is a "decanting machine" — it gives oil droplets time, space, and surface area to separate cleanly from the surrounding seawater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Memorize:** The IMO-mandated discharge limit is **15 ppm** of oil in water. This is **NOT** a tunable design choice — it is a hard regulatory threshold verified by the ship's **Oil Content Meter (OCM)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards in Use

| Constant / Standard | Value | Purpose |
|---------------------|-------|---------|
| MARPOL Effluent Limit | **15 ppm** | Maximum oil in discharged water |
| Density of Seawater | **ρ_w ≈ 1025 kg/m³** | Reference for buoyancy calculations |
| Density of Fuel Oil | **ρ_o ≈ 940–980 kg/m³** | Lighter than water → floats |
| Dynamic Viscosity of Water | **μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | Used in Stokes' Law |
| Gravitational Acceleration | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | Driving force for separation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle – The "Why" Behind Separation

Oil and water are **immiscible** (do not dissolve in each other) and have **different densities**. Oil is **lighter** (typically 850–980 kg/m³) than seawater (~1025 kg/m³). When a mixture is allowed to settle under gravity:

1. **Larger oil droplets** (diameter > 150 μm) rise rapidly to the surface — these are called **"free oil."**
2. **Medium droplets** (15–150 μm) rise more slowly and may require **lamella/coalescer plates** to merge.
3. **Fine emulsified droplets** (< 15 μm) do NOT separate by gravity alone — they require **centrifugal force**, **coalescers**, or **adsorption filters**.

The OWS design must handle the *worst case* — fine emulsified oil — which is why modern units combine **gravity + coalescence + centrifugal** stages.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Gravity Separation

- **Step 1 — Inlet Coalescence:** Bilge water enters through a strainer; first-stage coalescer plates cause fine droplets to merge into larger ones.
- **Step 2 — Gravity Settling Chamber:** Larger droplets rise under buoyancy; the chamber is sized to give sufficient **residence time**.
- **Step 3 — Second-Stage Coalescer:** A finer coalescer (often hydrophobic oleophilic material) traps residual oil.
- **Step 4 — Outlet Monitoring:** An **Oil Content Meter (OCM)** samples the water; if oil > 15 ppm, the **3-way valve** diverts the flow back to a **sludge/holding tank**.
- **Step 5 — Overboard Discharge:** When < 15 ppm is confirmed, water is pumped overboard via the **discharge port**.

## 2.3 Why Stokes' Law Governs the Process

For a small oil droplet rising in a viscous fluid (laminar flow, low Reynolds number), the terminal (constant) rise velocity is given by **Stokes' Law**:

$$v_t = \frac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \mu}$$

This equation is the **backbone** of every OWS design problem. It tells us:
- Velocity ∝ **droplet diameter squared** → larger drops separate much faster.
- Velocity ∝ **density difference** → more viscous oils separate slower.
- Velocity ∝ **1 / viscosity** → cold bilge water is harder to treat.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case | Units |
|---------|-----------|----------|-------|
| Terminal rise velocity (Stokes' Law) | $v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \mu}$ | Settling rate of oil droplets | m/s |
| Reynolds number for droplet | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Check laminar assumption | dimensionless |
| Minimum residence time | $t_{res} = \dfrac{H}{v_t}$ | Tank height H over velocity | s |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Throughput design | m³/s |
| Settling chamber cross-section | $A_s = \dfrac{Q}{v_t}$ | Required tank area | m² |
| Separation efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Performance metric | % |
| IMO compliance | $C_{out} \leq 15 \text{ ppm}$ | Regulatory threshold | ppm |
| Buoyancy force per drop | $F_b = \dfrac{\pi d^3 g (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{6}$ | Net upward force on drop | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes' Law** is valid only when **Re < 1** (laminar regime). For larger droplets, a drag-coefficient correction must be applied. In KTU problems, this is the standard assumption.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine Industry:** Every commercial ship must have an IMO-type-approved OWS (capacity typically 0.25–10 m³/h).
- **Offshore Platforms:** Similar units treat produced water before disposal.
- **Ports & Harbors:** Shore-based oily water reception facilities use the same physics.
- **Refineries:** Industrial OWS units pre-treat process wastewater.

The **15 ppm** discharge standard has eliminated millions of tonnes of oil pollution from the world's oceans since the 1980s.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Rise Velocity of an Oil Droplet (Stokes' Law)

We derive the classical expression for the terminal velocity of a spherical oil droplet rising through water under gravity.

### Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the droplet

Three forces act on a spherical oil droplet of diameter $d$ moving at velocity $v$ through water:

1. **Weight** (downward): $W = m \cdot g = \rho_o \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$
2. **Buoyancy** (upward): $F_b = \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$
3. **Drag force** (opposing motion, Stokes' drag for laminar flow): $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v$

### Step 2 — Apply equilibrium (terminal velocity condition)

At terminal velocity, the **net force = 0**, so buoyancy + drag = weight. Since buoyancy is greater (oil is lighter), the equation becomes:

$$\rho_w \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g - \rho_o \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g = 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v_t$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the LHS (factor out common terms)

Factor $\dfrac{\pi d^3 g}{6}$ on the left:

$$\frac{\pi d^3 g}{6} \left( \rho_w - \rho_o \right) = 3 \pi \mu d \cdot v_t$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $v_t$

Divide both sides by $3 \pi \mu d$:

$$v_t = \frac{\pi d^3 g (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{6 \cdot 3 \pi \mu d}$$

### Step 5 — Cancel common factors

Cancel $\pi$ and one $d$ from numerator and denominator:

$$v_t = \frac{d^2 g (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \mu}$$

### Step 6 — Final boxed result

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \frac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \, \mu}\,}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, the cornerstone equation for OWS design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Settling Chamber Area

### Step 1 — Continuity equation

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ through the OWS equals the product of cross-sectional area $A_s$ and the droplet's terminal rise velocity $v_t$:

$$Q = A_s \cdot v_t$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the required cross-sectional area

Rearranging for $A_s$:

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_t}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Stokes' expression for $v_t$

$$A_s = \frac{Q \cdot 18 \mu}{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_w - \rho_o)}$$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$\boxed{\,A_s = \frac{18 \, \mu \, Q}{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_w - \rho_o)}\,}$$

This tells designers **how large** the settling tank must be to capture droplets of a specified minimum diameter $d$ at a given flow rate $Q$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A bilge oily water separator processes $Q = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ of emulsified bilge water. The smallest oil droplets to be removed have diameter $d = 50 \text{ μm}$. Given:
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_o = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$

**Find:** (a) Terminal rise velocity, (b) Required settling chamber area, (c) Reynolds number to verify Stokes' Law validity.

### Part (a): Terminal rise velocity

Step 1 — Convert units consistently:
- $d = 50 \text{ μm} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$
- $Q = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{5}{3600} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

Step 2 — Compute density difference:
$$\rho_w - \rho_o = 1025 - 950 = 75 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Step 3 — Apply Stokes' Law:
$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (5.0 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times 75}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Step 4 — Evaluate numerator:
$$9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 75 = 1.839 \times 10^{-6}$$

Step 5 — Evaluate denominator:
$$18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.018$$

Step 6 — Final velocity:
$$v_t = \frac{1.839 \times 10^{-6}}{0.018} = 1.022 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.102 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Part (b): Settling chamber area

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_t} = \frac{1.389 \times 10^{-3}}{1.022 \times 10^{-4}} = 13.59 \text{ m}^2$$

### Part (c): Reynolds number check

$$Re = \frac{\rho_w \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu} = \frac{1025 \times 1.022 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = 5.24 \times 10^{-3} \ll 1$$

Since $Re \ll 1$, **Stokes' Law is valid** (laminar regime confirmed). ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Effect of Each Parameter on $v_t$

| Parameter | Effect on $v_t$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Droplet diameter $d$ ↑ | $v_t$ ↑ (∝ d²) | Coalescers drastically speed up separation |
| Density difference ↑ | $v_t$ ↑ | Heavy fuel oils (ρ ≈ 990) separate slowly |
| Viscosity μ ↑ | $v_t$ ↓ | Cold bilge water → less efficient |
| Gravity g ↑ (none on ship) | $v_t$ ↑ | Centrifugal force used instead |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Stage OWS

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of a typical marine OWS, showing the sequence of treatment stages and the regulatory feedback loop via the Oil Content Meter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet: Bilge Water from Ship] --> B[Strainer and Pre Filter]
    B --> C[Stage 1: First Coalescer Chamber]
    C --> D[Gravity Settling Tank]
    D --> E[Stage 2: Second Coalescer Element]
    E --> F[Oil Content Meter OCM]
    F --> G{OCM Reading less than 15 ppm}
    G -- Yes --> H[Overboard Discharge Valve OPEN]
    G -- No --> I[Three Way Valve Diverts to Sludge Tank]
    I --> J[Holding or Sludge Tank for Re Circulation]
    J --> C
    H --> K[Clean Water to Sea within IMO Limit]
```

## 4.2 Functional Process Flow Description

The diagram above maps the **complete control logic** of an IMO-compliant OWS system:

1. **Inlet Stage A → B:** Bilge water (a mixture of seawater, lube oil, and fuel oil leaks) enters through a coarse strainer to remove large debris.
2. **First Coalescence C:** Inertial impaction and droplet merging on oleophilic (oil-attracting) plates.
3. **Gravity Settling D:** Free oil rises to the surface and is skimmed to a sludge tank.
4. **Second Coalescence E:** Removes finer emulsified droplets that survived Stage 1.
5. **OCM Sampling F:** Real-time measurement of oil concentration in the treated water.
6. **Decision Gate G:** This is the **critical regulatory node** — the IMO 15 ppm test.
7. **Compliant Path H → K:** If oil < 15 ppm, overboard discharge is permitted.
8. **Non-Compliant Path I → J:** If oil > 15 ppm, the three-way valve recirculates the water back to the inlet, preventing any illegal discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The OCM-3-way-valve combination is the **enforcement mechanism** of MARPOL Annex I Regulation 14. Without it, the OWS would be merely a passive tank; with it, the system becomes a **self-policing** device that prevents accidental pollution even in cases of separator malfunction.

## 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oily Bilge Holding Tank] --> B[OWS Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Oily Water Separator]
    C --> D[OCM Oil Content Meter]
    D --> E{Decision Logic}
    E --> F[Sludge Tank Return Line]
    E --> G[Overboard Discharge Line]
    H[Sludge Tank] --> I[Incinerator or Shore Disposal]
    C --> H
```

The above shows the **integration** of the OWS with the ship's bilge system and sludge disposal chain, emphasizing that oil collected in the sludge tank must ultimately be incinerated onboard or discharged to a port reception facility.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the IMO regulation governing oily water discharge from ships. What is the maximum permissible oil concentration in the effluent?**

**Model Answer:**
Under **MARPOL 73/78, Annex I, Regulation 14**, the discharge of oil or oily mixtures from the machinery spaces of an oil tanker or any other ship of 400 GT and above is prohibited unless the oil content of the effluent does not exceed **15 parts per million (15 ppm)** by mass. The discharge must also occur more than 12 nautical miles from the nearest land, with the ship underway. The concentration is verified continuously by an **Oil Content Meter (OCM)**. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is the density of oil lower than that of seawater, and how does this enable separation in an OWS?**

**Model Answer:**
Oil has a **lower density** (typically 850–980 kg/m³) compared to seawater (~1025 kg/m³) because hydrocarbon molecules are non-polar and pack less densely than water molecules. This **density difference** creates a net **buoyant force** on every oil droplet suspended in water, causing it to rise under gravity. In an OWS, this rising motion is exploited by providing a **large quiescent settling tank** where oil accumulates at the top and is skimmed off, while clean water exits from the bottom — this is the basic principle of **gravity separation**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module-2 questions carry internal choice. The candidate must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a marine **Oily Water Separator (OWS)**. Discuss the role of the **Oil Content Meter (OCM)** and the **3-way valve** in ensuring MARPOL compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An OWS is to handle bilge water with a flow rate of $4 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ containing oil droplets of minimum diameter $60 \text{ μm}$. Using Stokes' Law, calculate the **terminal rise velocity** of the smallest droplet and the **minimum cross-sectional area** of the settling chamber required.
Given: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_o = 940 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The OWS consists of the following stages:

1. **Inlet strainer:** Removes large solid contaminants.
2. **First-stage coalescer:** Oleophilic plates cause fine oil droplets to merge into larger ones by impaction and adhesion.
3. **Gravity settling tank:** Provides residence time; oil rises to the top (skimmed off) and water exits from the bottom.
4. **Second-stage coalescer:** Catches finer emulsified droplets.
5. **Oil Content Meter (OCM):** A continuous online sensor that measures oil concentration in ppm using UV fluorescence or light scattering.
6. **Three-way valve:** A pneumatically or electrically actuated valve at the OWS outlet.

**Role of OCM and 3-way valve in MARPOL compliance:** The OCM continuously monitors the treated water. If the oil content exceeds **15 ppm**, the 3-way valve is automatically actuated to **divert** the flow back to the bilge holding tank, **preventing overboard discharge** of non-compliant effluent. This real-time feedback makes the OWS a self-regulating pollution control device and satisfies MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 14. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Unit conversions:**
- $d = 60 \text{ μm} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$
- $Q = 4 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{4}{3600} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 2 — Density difference:**
$$\rho_w - \rho_o = 1025 - 940 = 85 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' Law for terminal rise velocity:**
$$v_t = \frac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \, \mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (6.0 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times 85}{18 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**
$$9.81 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 85 = 3.002 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation:**
$$18 \times 1.1 \times 10^{-3} = 1.98 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 6 — Terminal velocity:**
$$v_t = \frac{3.002 \times 10^{-6}}{1.98 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.516 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Cross-sectional area from continuity:**
$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_t} = \frac{1.111 \times 10^{-3}}{1.516 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.33 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answers:**
- Terminal rise velocity $v_t = 1.516 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.152 \text{ mm/s}$
- Settling chamber area $A_s = 7.33 \text{ m}^2$ **(7 marks)**

---

### 📗 Question B (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** List and explain the **different stages of treatment** in a modern marine OWS. State the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bilge OWS handles $10 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ of oily water. The oil droplets have a density of $960 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and a minimum diameter of $40 \text{ μm}$. Determine the **settling time** required for these droplets in a tank of effective height 1.5 m.
Given: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The modern marine OWS has the following treatment stages:

1. **Inlet Strainer / Pre-Filter:** Removes large particulates, rags, and debris to protect downstream coalescers. **(1 mark)**
2. **First-Stage Coalescer:** Droplet merging using oleophilic (oil-attracting) plates; converts fine emulsions into larger drops. **(1 mark)**
3. **Gravity Settling Chamber:** Primary separation zone where oil rises and is skimmed from the top; water exits below. **(1 mark)**
4. **Second-Stage Coalescer:** Removes finer droplets that escape the first stage; uses finer oleophilic media. **(1 mark)**
5. **Oil Content Meter (OCM):** Continuous ppm monitor; triggers 3-way valve if > 15 ppm. **(1 mark)**
6. **3-Way Diverter Valve:** Routes non-compliant water back to the bilge holding tank; routes compliant water overboard. **(1 mark)**
7. **Sludge/Collection Tank:** Receives separated oil for incineration or shore disposal. **(1 mark)**

**Total: 7 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Unit conversions:**
- $d = 40 \text{ μm} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$
- $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Density difference:**
$$\rho_w - \rho_o = 1025 - 960 = 65 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply Stokes' Law:**
$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (4.0 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times 65}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**
$$9.81 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 65 = 1.020 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Terminal velocity:**
$$v_t = \frac{1.020 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} = 5.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Settling time:**
$$t_{res} = \frac{H}{v_t} = \frac{1.5}{5.67 \times 10^{-5}} = 26{,}455 \text{ s} \approx 7.35 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Answer:** Required settling time ≈ **26,455 s (≈ 7.35 hours)**. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ to $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or μm to m. This single error cascades and produces wrong final answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Density Difference Sign (–1 mark):** Writing $(\rho_o - \rho_w)$ instead of $(\rho_w - \rho_o)$ gives a *negative* velocity. Examiners deduct for this immediately.
> 3. **Not Verifying Reynolds Number (–1 mark):** Stokes' Law is only valid for $Re < 1$. If the problem says "very fine droplets" or gives a small diameter, you must confirm $Re < 1$ for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing OCM with Oil Discharge Monitor:** They are different systems; OCM is for OWS, ODME is for oil tankers. Using the wrong term costs **½ to 1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping a Diagram in Part A of Question B (–2 to 3 marks):** KTU examiners expect a *neat labelled diagram* of the OWS for full marks in theory questions.
> 6. **Forgetting to State the 15 ppm Limit:** This is the most-tested number. Omitting it loses **1 mark** instantly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **IMO MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 14** → **15 ppm** maximum oil in OWS effluent.
- ✅ **OWS** = Oily Water Separator; uses **gravity + coalescence** as primary mechanisms.
- ✅ **OCM** = Oil Content Meter (continuous monitor); **ODME** = Oil Discharge Monitoring Equipment (tankers only).
- ✅ **3-way valve** automatically diverts non-compliant water back to the bilge/sludge tank.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law** is the foundational equation: $v_t = \dfrac{g \, d^2 \, (\rho_w - \rho_o)}{18 \, \mu}$.
- ✅ Terminal velocity is **proportional to d²** → coalescers dramatically increase separation rate.
- ✅ Stokes' Law valid only when **Re < 1** (laminar flow around droplet).
- ✅ **Free oil** > 150 μm; **emulsified oil** < 50 μm; both handled in modern 2-stage OWS.
- ✅ **Centrifugal OWS** uses high g-forces for finer emulsions; **gravity OWS** simpler but needs larger tanks.
- ✅ Sludge from OWS must be **incinerated onboard** or **discharged to a port reception facility**.
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex V** covers garbage; **Annex IV** covers sewage — these are NOT OWS rules.
- ✅ All ships ≥ 400 GT must have an **IMO-type-approved OWS**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ballast Water Management Convention (BWMC)** is a globally binding international treaty adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** on **13 February 2004** under the auspices of the International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments. The Convention entered into force internationally on **8 September 2017**. Its primary objective is to prevent, minimize, and ultimately eliminate the transfer of harmful aquatic organisms and pathogens via the ballast water of ships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST412 – Module 2):**
> The BWM Convention is the cornerstone of marine pollution prevention in this module. Students must know the **D-1 (Ballast Water Exchange) Standard**, the **D-2 (Ballast Water Performance) Standard**, the **BWM Plan**, the **Ballast Water Record Book**, and the **type-approval framework for BWMS (Ballast Water Management Systems)**.

The Convention operates on a **two-tier regulatory architecture** known as the **D-1 Standard** and the **D-2 Standard**:

| Standard | Code | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| Ballast Water Exchange | **Regulation D-1** | A **tank-based volumetric/flow-based exchange** standard requiring ≥ 95 % volumetric exchange. |
| Ballast Water Performance | **Regulation D-2** | A **water-quality-based** standard specifying maximum permissible concentrations of organisms and indicators. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulation B-3** essentially states that all ships must conduct ballast water management that **at least meets the D-1 standard**, and from the date of compliance specified in the schedule, they must meet the **D-2 standard** (or an alternative of equivalent rigor).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are moving from one city to another and you carry drinking water in sealed bottles to avoid drinking contaminated tap water in your new city. That is logical for humans — but consider this: a giant cargo ship sailing from a tropical port loaded with **4,000 – 80,000 tonnes of seawater inside its bottom tanks** to balance the vessel (since it arrived empty, it must submerge itself to be safe at sea). The water it pumps in is full of tiny marine creatures, eggs, larvae, viruses, and bacteria from port A. When the ship reaches port B and offloads cargo, it pumps out this foreign water to lighten itself — releasing potentially **invasive alien species** into a new ecosystem. This is known as **biological pollution**, and it is the second-largest cause of species extinction worldwide.

The BWM Convention is essentially the **"international passport & quarantine"** rule for ships' ballast tanks, telling captains either to **swap the water mid-ocean** (D-1) or to **install on-board treatment systems** (BWMS) that kill/remove organisms to safe limits (D-2).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Numerical Standards (Regulation D-2) — must be memorized for KTU exams:**
> - Organisms ≥ 50 μm in minimum dimension: **< 10 viable organisms per cubic metre**
> - Organisms < 50 μm and ≥ 10 μm: **< 10 viable organisms per millilitre**
> - Indicator microbes — *Vibrio cholerae* (O1 and O139): **< 1 colony-forming unit (CFU) per 100 mL**
> - Indicator microbes — *E. coli*: **< 250 CFU per 100 mL**
> - Indicator microbes — Intestinal Enterococci: **< 100 CFU per 100 mL**

These standards apply at **ballast water discharge** points.

## 1.4 Why Ships Need Ballast (Engineering Context)

A ship without cargo is structurally unstable — it is too light at the top and can capsize, and the propeller may not be fully submerged. **Ballast water** is the universal solution:

- It lowers the centre of gravity
- Provides trim and stability
- Compensates for cargo weight changes
- Reduces structural stress (shear forces and bending moments)

A typical large bulk carrier may carry **30,000 – 100,000 tonnes** of ballast water on any given voyage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why the Two-Stage D-1/D-2 Approach?

The IMO adopted a **phased, risk-managed** approach because in 2004 the technology to universally treat ballast water to D-2 standards was neither mature nor affordable. Hence, the Convention allows a transitional period where **D-1 (Ballast Water Exchange — BWE)** acts as a stop-gap. BWE works on the simple ecological logic that **open-ocean organisms cannot survive in coastal waters** (and vice-versa) due to differences in salinity, temperature, and light. By exchanging ≥ 95 % of coastal water with mid-ocean water, the survival of coastal invasive species in the discharge port is statistically reduced by ~99 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D-1 Standard Logic (Sequential Ballast Water Exchange):**
> 1. Pump in ballast water at port A (coastal).
> 2. Sail into open ocean (depth ≥ 200 nautical miles from nearest land AND ≥ 50 m depth).
> 3. Pump in replacement mid-ocean water from bottom to top (sequential) — OR empty and refill (flow-through, 3× tank volume).
> 4. Achieve ≥ 95 % volumetric exchange of original coastal water.
> 5. Discharge safely at port B with predominantly open-ocean biota.

## 2.2 Theoretical D-2 Pathway

The D-2 pathway requires an **on-board BWMS (Ballast Water Management System)** that integrates two stages of treatment during ballast **uptake** or **discharge**:

- **Mechanical / Physical Treatment**: Filtration (typically 50 μm screens, cyclones, hydrocyclones), hydrocyclone separation, and back-flushing to remove macro-organisms and suspended solids.
- **Chemical / Physico-Chemical Treatment**: UV irradiation, electrochlorination (using side-stream electrolysis producing hypochlorite from seawater), chemical biocides, deoxygenation, cavitation, advanced oxidation, heat treatment.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Electrochlorination dominates the market (≈ 60 % of type-approved systems):**
> Seawater contains ~ 1.9 % chloride ions (≈ 19,000 ppm). When passed through an electrolytic cell, **Cl⁻ → Cl₂ → HOCl/OCl⁻ (active chlorine species)**. This is a *reagent that the ship carries for free* — a closed-loop oxidizer ideal for marine service.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value separators to preserve markdown table integrity. The variables used are: $\rho$ = seawater density, $V$ = volume, $m$ = mass, $T$ = time, $Q$ = flow rate, $C$ = concentration.

| # | Formula / Parameter | LaTeX Form | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Mass of ballast water | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | tonnes (1 t = 1 m³ of seawater) | Total ship ballast mass estimation |
| 2 | Seawater density (standard) | $\rho_{sw} \approx \mathbf{1.025\ t/m^3}$ | t/m³ | Default value for KTU problems |
| 3 | Volumetric exchange efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{exchanged}}{V_{tank}} \times 100$ | % | KTU D-1 compliance: must be $\geq \mathbf{95\ \%}$ |
| 4 | Sequential exchange pumping rate | $Q_{seq} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{T_{fill}}$ | m³/h | Determines duration of BWE |
| 5 | Required pumped volume (flow-through) | $V_{req} \geq 3 \cdot V_{tank}$ | m³ | IMO B-4 flow-through method |
| 6 | D-2 organism limit (large) | $N_{50} < \mathbf{10\ org/m^3}$ | org/m³ | KTU high-yield constant |
| 7 | D-2 organism limit (small) | $N_{10-50} < \mathbf{10\ org/mL}$ | org/mL | KTU high-yield constant |
| 8 | Treatment throughput | $Q_{BWMS} = \dfrac{V_{ballast}}{T_{treatment}}$ | m³/h | BWMS sizing |
| 9 | Log-reduction | $LR = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$ | dimensionless | UV dose, chlorination efficacy |
| 10 | Active chlorine dose | $D_{Cl} = C_{Cl} \cdot T_{contact}$ | mg·min/L | Disinfection kinetics (CT concept) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BWMS market is projected to exceed **USD 200 billion cumulatively** by 2030 across global fleets. Engineers design:
- Filtration units (50 μm) sized to $Q_{BWMS}$ up to **1,000 – 4,000 m³/h** for VLCCs
- UV reactors with dose calibrated to UV transmittance of port water
- TRO (Total Residual Oxidant) sensors and neutralizers (mandatory pre-discharge)
- **Power demand** is critical: a large VLCC BWMS may draw **150 – 350 kW** continuously during ballasting.

The D-2 standard also integrates with **port state control** and **port reception facilities** for sediment disposal from BWMS backflush streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Ballast Water Required for Trim Correction

**Given:** A bulk carrier of Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) = 60,000 t unloads 30,000 t of cargo at Port B. The vessel's lightship weight = 12,000 t. The vessel's required minimum draft for safe ocean passage = 12.5 m. Current draft at port B (light) = 7.2 m. Port B seawater density $\rho = 1.025\ t/m^3$. The ship needs to be re-ballasted to reach the safe draft.

**Step 1 — Identify the missing mass:**
The ship must gain a certain mass of water to sink from draft $T_1 = 7.2$ m to $T_2 = 12.5$ m. The corresponding additional displacement mass is found from the ship's **TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion)** — a hydrostatic constant from the ship's stability booklet.

For a typical Panamax bulk carrier, **TPC ≈ 50 t/cm**. Therefore the additional displacement needed is:

$$
\Delta m = TPC \times (T_2 - T_1) \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Convert draft difference to centimetres:**

$$
\Delta m = 50 \times (12.5 - 7.2) \times 100 = 50 \times 5.3 \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\Delta m = 50 \times 530 = \mathbf{26{,}500\ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent volume of ballast water:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho} = \dfrac{26{,}500}{1.025}
$$

$$
V = \mathbf{25{,}853.66\ m^3}
$$

This explains why a 60,000 DWT vessel often needs **≥ 25,000 m³ of ballast** — and why BWM is a colossal engineering problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: D-1 Sequential Exchange Efficiency

**The IMO flow-through method requires a pumped volume $V_{pump} \geq 3 \cdot V_{tank}$ to achieve ≥ 95 % exchange.**

Derivation via exponential decay (assuming perfect mixing during inflow):

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the dilution rate (1/h), and at any time $t$:

$$
V_{pump}(t) = Q \cdot t
$$

For a constant inflow rate $Q$ into a tank of volume $V_{tank}$:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{Q}{V_{tank}}
$$

Hence:

$$
\dfrac{C(t)}{C_0} = e^{-V_{pump}(t)/V_{tank}}
$$

**Setting $\dfrac{C(t)}{C_0} = 5\ \% = 0.05$ (i.e. 95 % exchange):**

$$
0.05 = e^{-V_{pump}/V_{tank}}
$$

$$
\ln(0.05) = -\dfrac{V_{pump}}{V_{tank}}
$$

$$
V_{pump} = -\ln(0.05) \cdot V_{tank} = 2.9957 \cdot V_{tank} \approx 3 \cdot V_{tank}
$$

This mathematically proves the IMO 3 × rule for ≥ 95 % exchange efficiency.

## 3.3 Derivation: D-2 Log-Reduction for UV Treatment

**Given:** A BWMS UV chamber receives ballast water with influent organism concentration $C_{in} = 5 \times 10^4$ org/mL. The D-2 limit is $C_{out,D2} = 10$ org/mL. Compute required **log-reduction**.

$$
LR = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out,D2}}\right)
$$

$$
LR = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 10^4}{10}\right) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^3)
$$

$$
LR = \log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10^3) = 0.699 + 3 = \mathbf{3.699}
$$

This means a **4-log UV dose** is required, which corresponds (per KTU reference values) to approximately **40 mJ/cm² fluence** at typical UV transmittance of 90 %.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Treatment Technologies

| Technology | Active Agent | D-2 Capable? | Power Draw | Risk Factor |
|------------|-------------|--------------|------------|-------------|
| UV irradiation | 254 nm photons | Yes (when sized) | High (250 kW) | Lamp ageing, turbidity interference |
| Electrochlorination (side-stream) | HOCl / OCl⁻ | Yes | Medium (150 kW) | TRO toxicity, DBP formation |
| Ozone injection | O₃ | Yes | Medium | Off-gas toxicity |
| Deoxygenation | N₂ stripping | Yes | Low | Anaerobic corrosion of tanks |
| Heat treatment | ΔT > 50 °C | Yes (limited) | Very high | Energy cost prohibitive |
| Filtration only | 50 μm screen | No (Stage 1 only) | Low | Cannot meet D-2 alone |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Ballast Water Management — System-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Arrives At Loading Port] --> B[Deballast Pumps Discharge Prior Ballast]
    B --> C{Sediment In Tank?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Dispose Sediment At Port Reception Facility]
    C -- No --> E[Cargo Loading Begins]
    E --> F[Vessel Departs In Ballast Condition]
    F --> G{BWM Method Selected}
    G -- D-1 Method --> H[Sequential Or Flow Through Exchange At 200 NM Zone]
    G -- D-2 Method --> I[BWMS Operational During Uptake And Discharge]
    H --> J[Ballast Water Record Book Entry]
    I --> K[Filtration Stage 50 Micron]
    K --> L[Disinfection Stage UV Or Electrochlorination]
    L --> M[TRO Neutralization Pre Discharge]
    M --> J
    J --> N[Port State Control Inspection On Arrival]
    N --> O[Discharge Compliant Ballast At Next Port]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Compliance Decision Tree for Ship Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Ship Or Existing Ship?] --> B{Year Of Keel Lay}
    B -- 2017 IOPP Renewal Or After --> C[D-2 Mandatory From Delivery]
    B -- Before 2017 --> D{Tank Capacity In m3}
    D -- Greater Than 5000 --> E[D-2 By 2022]
    D -- 1500 To 5000 --> F[D-2 By 2024]
    D -- Less Than 1500 --> G[D-2 By 2024]
    C --> H[Install Type Approved BWMS]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Conduct BWM Plan And Record Book Training]
    I --> J[Continuous Compliance Monitoring]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: BWM Convention Adoption Lifecycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Diplomatic Conference 2004] --> B[Adoption Of Convention]
    B --> C[Ratification By Flag States]
    C --> D{Threshold 30 States 35 Percent Tonnage Met}
    D -- No --> E[Awaiting Entry Into Force]
    D -- Yes --> F[Entry Into Force 8 Sept 2017]
    F --> G[Implementation Schedule Begins]
    G --> H[D-1 Transitional Phase]
    H --> I[D-2 Mandatory Compliance By Phase Out Year]
    I --> J[Port State Control Enforcement]
    J --> K[Continuous Review By MEPC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** Each Mermaid block above illustrates a different facet — the **process flow** of BWM during a voyage, the **ship-type decision tree** for compliance timeline (per IMO B-3 schedule), and the **policy lifecycle** of the Convention from adoption to enforcement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the two numerical concentration limits prescribed under Regulation D-2 of the BWM Convention for organisms greater than 50 μm and for indicator microbes (any two).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Regulation D-2 of the BWM Convention specifies the following water-quality limits for ballast water discharge:

- For viable organisms **≥ 50 μm in minimum dimension**: less than **10 organisms per cubic metre** of ballast water.
- For viable organisms **between 10 μm and 50 μm**: less than **10 organisms per millilitre** of ballast water.
- For indicator microbes:
  - *Vibrio cholerae* (O1 and O139): **< 1 CFU per 100 mL**
  - *Escherichia coli*: **< 250 CFU per 100 mL**
  - Intestinal Enterococci: **< 100 CFU per 100 mL**

(Mentioning any two correctly is sufficient for full marks.)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Define "Ballast Water Exchange" as per Regulation D-1 of the BWM Convention. What is the minimum volumetric exchange efficiency required?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ballast Water Exchange (BWE) is the process of replacing coastal/port ballast water with open-ocean water to minimize the survival of coastal organisms when discharged at the next port. It is performed either by the **sequential method** (top-up replacement in tanks) or the **flow-through method** (pumping three tank volumes of ocean water through). The minimum required volumetric exchange efficiency is **95 %**, and the exchange zone is defined as at least **200 nautical miles from the nearest land** in water of depth **≥ 50 m**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B in PENST412 carries **14 marks** with internal choice. The standard split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** per question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Q, Adapted from Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*(a) Discuss in detail the genesis, objectives, and structure of the IMO Ballast Water Management Convention, 2004. (7 marks)*

*(b) A bulk carrier of 50,000 DWT deballasts 18,000 tonnes at Port A. Subsequently, it loads 12,000 tonnes of cargo and must re-ballast to a safe sailing draft. Seawater density at the loading port is 1.024 t/m³. If the ship's stability booklet specifies a TPC of 48 t/cm and the additional draft needed is 3.5 m, compute: (i) the additional displacement mass required, (ii) the volume of ballast water to be pumped in, and (iii) the minimum volume of ocean water to be pumped under the D-1 flow-through method. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Genesis, Objectives, Structure (7 marks)**

1. **Genesis:** Increasing reports of invasive species transfer (e.g., *Dreissena polymorpha* zebra mussel, *Mnemiopsis leidyi* comb jelly) — the 1992 UNCED Rio Earth Summit, 1997 IMO Assembly Resolution A.868(20), leading to the **2004 Diplomatic Conference** at IMO London.
2. **Objectives:** To prevent, minimize, and ultimately eliminate the risks to the marine environment, human health, and economic resources arising from the transfer of Harmful Aquatic Organisms and Pathogens (HAOP) via ships' ballast water and sediments.
3. **Structure of Convention:**
   - **Article 1** — Definitions
   - **Article 2** — General Obligations
   - **Article 3** — Application
   - **Article 4** — Control of Transfer of HAOP
   - **Article 5** — Sediment Management
   - **Article 6** — Technical Assistance and Co-operation
   - **Annex — Regulations**:
     - Section A — General Provisions (Regulations A-1 to A-5)
     - Section B — Management and Control Requirements (Regulations B-1 to B-6)
     - Section C — Special Requirements (Regulations C-1 to C-5)
     - Section D — Standards (D-1 Exchange, D-2 Performance)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Given:
- $DWT = 50{,}000$ t
- Deballast mass = 18,000 t
- Cargo loaded = 12,000 t
- Seawater density $\rho = 1.024$ t/m³
- $TPC = 48$ t/cm
- Additional draft $\Delta T = 3.5$ m = **350 cm**

**(i) Additional displacement mass required (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta m = TPC \times \Delta T = 48 \times 350
$$

$$
\Delta m = \mathbf{16{,}800\ tonnes}
$$

**(ii) Volume of ballast water to be pumped in (2.5 marks):**

$$
V = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho} = \dfrac{16{,}800}{1.024}
$$

$$
V = \mathbf{16{,}406.25\ m^3}
$$

**(iii) Minimum volume for D-1 flow-through (2.5 marks):**

$$
V_{pump,min} = 3 \times V = 3 \times 16{,}406.25
$$

$$
V_{pump,min} = \mathbf{49{,}218.75\ m^3}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **DWT** with **lightweight** — DWT is the variable cargo capacity, not the ship's empty mass.
> - The **TPC must be multiplied by draft in centimetres, not metres** — a 3.5 m draft is 350 cm. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> - The factor **3 ×** is from the exponential decay derivation, not an arbitrary IMO rule. Mentioning the derivation earns bonus credit.
> - Always carry units in intermediate steps; missing units = 0.5 mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

*(a) Explain the D-2 Performance Standard in detail. List the indicator microbes and their concentration limits, and discuss any two BWMS technologies (mechanical + chemical) used to achieve compliance. (7 marks)*

*(b) A ship-board UV reactor receives ballast water with influent viable organism concentration of $2 \times 10^5$ organisms per mL. The D-2 compliance limit is 10 organisms per mL. The UV transmittance at 254 nm is 88 %, and the manufacturer's dose-response curve states that a UV dose of 60 mJ/cm² achieves a 4-log reduction at this transmittance. Compute: (i) the required log-reduction, and (ii) whether the available UV dose is sufficient. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — D-2 Standard, Indicators, BWMS (7 marks)**

D-2 is the **performance-based** discharge standard specifying maximum permissible concentrations of viable organisms and indicator microbes. Indicator microbes include:
- *Vibrio cholerae* (O1 and O139): < 1 CFU/100 mL
- *E. coli*: < 250 CFU/100 mL
- Intestinal Enterococci: < 100 CFU/100 mL

**Two BWMS technologies:**

1. **Mechanical Stage — Filtration (3 marks):**
   Self-cleaning 50-μm back-flushable filters remove plankton, larvae, and large suspended solids. Reduces downstream biological loading. Located in the uptake line. Back-flushed sediment is collected and disposed at port reception facilities.

2. **Chemical Stage — Electrochlorination (3 marks):**
   Side-stream electrolysis of seawater produces hypochlorous acid (HOCl) which is dosed into the main ballast line. Contact time of ~ 24 hours achieves required log-reduction. Pre-discharge neutralization with sodium bisulphite reduces Total Residual Oxidant (TRO) to < 0.1 mg/L to protect port waters.

(Alternative: UV, ozone, deoxygenation may be discussed.)

**Part (b) — UV Reactor Calculation (7 marks)**

Given: $C_{in} = 2 \times 10^5$ org/mL, $C_{out,D2} = 10$ org/mL, UV dose available = 60 mJ/cm², dose for 4-log = 60 mJ/cm².

**(i) Required log-reduction (3 marks):**

$$
LR_{req} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{in}}{C_{out,D2}}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{10}\right)
$$

$$
LR_{req} = \log_{10}(2 \times 10^4) = \log_{10}(2) + 4
$$

$$
LR_{req} = 0.301 + 4 = \mathbf{4.301}
$$

**(ii) Compliance check (4 marks):**

The available UV dose of 60 mJ/cm² delivers a 4-log reduction (per manufacturer curve at UVT 88 %).

$$
LR_{avail} = 4.00 < LR_{req} = 4.301
$$

The available dose is **NOT sufficient**. Required additional dose (linear interpolation assumed):

$$
D_{req} = 60 \times \dfrac{4.301}{4.000} = \mathbf{64.52\ mJ/cm^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The UV system is under-sized by ~ 7.5 %, and must be either upgraded (additional lamps, higher reactor power) or supplemented with chemical treatment (e.g., low-dose electrochlorination).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do not confuse the 4-log **influent-vs-discharge ratio** with influent density units — note that D-2 uses *org/mL* for small organisms and *org/m³* for large ones.
> - Many students fail to state the **UVT (UV Transmittance)** dependency of dose-response — losing 1 mark in KTU valuation.
> - A common error is assuming the 4-log and the requirement 4.301 are "approximately equal" — the answer must explicitly compute and compare.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the BWM Convention (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**

- The **BWM Convention** was adopted on **13 February 2004** and entered into force on **8 September 2017**.
- It operates on the **D-1 (Exchange) and D-2 (Performance) standards**.
- **D-1** requires ≥ **95 % volumetric exchange** of ballast water, ideally at **≥ 200 NM from nearest land** and in **depth ≥ 50 m**.
- **D-2** is a numerical water-quality standard applied at the point of **discharge**.
- D-2 limits: **< 10 org/m³ (≥ 50 μm)**, **< 10 org/mL (10–50 μm)**, **< 1 CFU/100 mL (V. cholerae)**, **< 250 CFU/100 mL (E. coli)**, **< 100 CFU/100 mL (Intestinal Enterococci)**.
- The IMO **3 × V_tank** flow-through exchange rule derives from the exponential decay equation $0.05 = e^{-V_{pump}/V_{tank}}$.
- Typical **BWMS** combines **mechanical (50 μm filtration)** + **disinfection (UV or electrochlorination)** stages.
- **TRO neutralization** before discharge is mandatory to protect port waters.
- Each ship must carry a **BWM Plan**, a **Ballast Water Record Book**, and a valid **BWMS Type Approval Certificate** (per IMO G8 Guidelines).
- Compliance schedule per **Reg B-3** is tied to **IOPP renewal year and ballast tank capacity** — new ships comply from delivery; existing ships by 2022–2024.
- Seawater density **$\rho \approx 1.025$ t/m³** is the standard KTU assumption.
- **TPC × Δdraft (in cm)** gives the required ballast mass; divide by $\rho$ for volume.
- **Log-reduction** is the universal measure of BWMS disinfection efficacy.
- Sediment from BWMS and tank cleaning must be disposed at **port reception facilities** — never at sea.
- The BWM Convention applies to **all ships flying the flag of a Party State**, regardless of where they operate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Recovery Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Recovery Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Recovery Systems** are a class of engineered mechanical, chemical, and physical units designed to **separate, collect, and retrieve spilled oil or oily mixtures from the marine environment**, restoring the spilled pollutant to a usable form while minimizing secondary environmental damage.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, recovery systems are broadly classified as the **third tier of the marine pollution control hierarchy** (after prevention and containment). They are characterized by:

- **Recovery Efficiency ($\eta_R$)** — the volumetric ratio of oil recovered to oil spilled
- **Throughput (Q)** — the volumetric rate of oil–water mixture handled, typically measured in **m³/hr** or **bbl/hr**
- **Oil-to-Water Ratio (OWR)** — the purity indicator of the recovered stream
- **Freeboard & Wave Tolerance** — operational limits in sea state conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** KTU/NEP 2020 module targets require students to understand at least **five major categories** of recovery systems along with their governing principles, advantages, and field-deployment limitations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Juice Spill"

Imagine you spill a glass of oil-thick juice on a kitchen counter. What is the most natural recovery sequence?

1. **Boom it** — grab a paper towel ring to **contain** the spreading juice (this is a *containment boom*, not a recovery device).
2. **Skim it** — gently slide a flat spoon across the surface to scoop the floating layer (this is a *weir skimmer*).
3. **Suck it up** — use a syringe or wet-vacuum to pull the rest (this is a *vacuum / suction skimmer*).
4. **Wipe it** — press a paper towel that *loves* the oil more than water (this is an *oleophilic sorbent*).
5. **Burn the residue** — if a thin film remains, ignite it carefully (this is *in-situ burning*).

Each of these "kitchen tools" maps directly to a real marine recovery system class. Just as you would never use a wet-vacuum to scoop a wide spill, an engineer must match the **sea state**, **oil viscosity**, and **spill volume** to the appropriate recovery technology.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The topic of "Types of Recovery Systems" is a **high-yield topic** because it:

- Bridges **fluid mechanics** (weir flow, viscous flow), **process equipment design** (pumps, separators), and **environmental engineering** (regulatory limits).
- Frequently appears in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition/classification question.
- Often features as a **Part B 14-mark question** with comparative analysis between skimmer types.
- Tests **CO (Course Outcome) mapping** typically to CO3 and CO4 of PENST412.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a question asks "compare X and Y," always structure the answer with a **comparative table** — KTU examiners award full marks only when both **merits and demerits** are explicitly listed.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Marine Recovery Systems

Marine recovery systems are classified along three orthogonal axes:

#### A. By Operating Principle
1. **Mechanical Recovery (Skimmers)** — physical extraction using moving parts
2. **Non-Mechanical Recovery** — sorption, in-situ burning, chemical dispersion
3. **Hybrid Systems** — combination of containment + recovery (e.g., Transrec 350, *Vikoma*)

#### B. By Deployment Platform
- **Vessel-mounted skimmers** (high throughput, 100–1000 m³/hr)
- **Shore-based skimmers** (limited by pumping distance)
- **Self-propelled recovery vessels** (e.g., *A Whale* class)
- **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROV)** for deep-sea recovery

#### C. By Phase Targeted
- **Surface oil recovery** (>90% of all field operations)
- **Sub-surface / submerged oil recovery** (post-sinking spills)

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Weir Skimmers
- **Principle:** Oil flows over a low dam (weir) into a collection sump; water is returned by gravity.
- **Governing Physics:** Sharp-crested weir flow equation.
- **Limitation:** Performance collapses when oil viscosity exceeds **~1000 cSt** (heavy fuel oils) or in waves > **0.6 m Hs**.
- **Best For:** Light crude, diesel, thin sheens in calm seas.

#### 2.2.2 Oleophilic Skimmers
- **Principle:** Adhesion of oil to a moving oleophilic (oil-attracting) surface, then mechanical scraping.
- **Subtypes:**
  - **Disc Skimmer** — rotating discs (0.3–3 m diameter) lift oil by adhesion
  - **Drum Skimmer** — large rotating drum (1–3 m), 40–60% partially submerged
  - **Brush Skimmer** — rotating brushes with oleophilic fibers (most versatile)
  - **Belt Skimmer** — endless oleophilic belt looped over rollers
  - **Rope Mop Skimmer** — continuous oleophilic rope (best for thick, weathered oils)
- **Governing Physics:** Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ for oil-wet surface; recovery rate ∝ **surface speed × wetted area × oil film thickness**.

#### 2.2.3 Vacuum / Suction Skimmers
- **Principle:** Vacuum pumps draw oil through a nozzle inserted at the oil–water interface.
- **Governing Physics:** Bernoulli's equation applied across the nozzle.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle debris; susceptible to emulsification loss.
- **Best For:** Calm seas, thick emulsions, confined spaces (e.g., in-vessel tanks).

#### 2.2.4 Sorbents
- **Principle:** Absorption (uptake into bulk) or adsorption (surface retention) by porous materials.
- **Types:**
  - **Natural Organic** — straw, peat, cotton, sawdust (low cost, biodegradable)
  - **Natural Mineral** — vermiculite, perlite, sand
  - **Synthetic** — polypropylene fibers, polyurethane foams (high capacity, **10–25× own weight**)
- **Metric:** **Sorption Capacity** = (mass of oil absorbed) / (mass of sorbent), dimensionless ratio.

#### 2.2.5 In-Situ Burning
- **Principle:** Controlled ignition of fresh oil slicks; removes **>90%** of oil *in situ*.
- **Requirements:** Oil thickness **≥ 2–3 mm**, calm winds, ignition with **helitorch or fire-resistant boom**.
- **Limitation:** Smoke plumes (particulate and CO₂), residue (sinking), regulatory bans in many ports.
- **Efficiency:** Defined as $\eta_{burn} = (m_{burned}/m_{spilled}) \times 100\%$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_R = \dfrac{V_{recovered}}{V_{spilled}} \times 100\%$ | Recovery Efficiency | % |
| 2 | $Q_{weir} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{3/2}$ | Weir skimmer discharge (sharp-crested) | m³/s |
| 3 | $Q = A_{eff} \cdot v \cdot \rho_{oil}$ | Mass throughput of oleophilic skimmer | kg/s |
| 4 | $\text{OWR} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{oil}}{\dot{V}_{oil} + \dot{V}_{water}}$ | Oil-to-Water Ratio (purity) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{m_{absorbed}}{m_{sorbent}}$ | Sorbent capacity (sorption coefficient) | kg/kg |
| 6 | $\eta_{burn} = 1 - e^{-k \cdot t}$ | In-situ burn decay (pseudo-first order) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{vac} = \rho g h_{suction} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Vacuum pump head requirement | Pa |
| 8 | $\text{Throughput Ratio} = \dfrac{Q_{skimmer}}{Q_{peak\,spill}}$ | Sizing adequacy | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Power Number} = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$ | Skimmer pump power scaling (affinity law) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Adhesion Force} \propto \gamma_{ow} \cdot \cos\theta$ | Oleophilic adhesion (Young's relation) | N/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention:** $\gamma_{ow}$ = oil–water interfacial tension (N/m), $\theta$ = contact angle. Always use **\vert** in LaTeX, never the bare pipe `|`, to avoid markdown table corruption.

| **Offshore Oil & Gas** | Rig blowout recovery, FPSO bilge treatment |
| **Ports & Harbours** | Ship-to-ship transfer spills, bunker fuel leaks |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Weir Skimmer Throughput Equation

**Starting from Torricelli's Law** (ideal fluid velocity through a sharp-crested weir):

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

**Step 1 — Volumetric discharge per unit width:**
The velocity $v$ acts across the elemental head $h$ above the weir crest. For a small strip of height $dh$ at depth $h$:

$$dQ = L \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \cdot dh$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to the head H above the weir crest:**

$$Q = L \int_{0}^{H} \sqrt{2 g h}\, dh$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$Q = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2}\, dh = L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the discharge coefficient $C_d$ (accounts for real-fluid effects, contraction, viscosity):**

$$Q_{weir} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $C_d$?** Theoretical analysis assumes ideal flow, but viscous effects, surface tension, weir geometry, and approach velocity all reduce actual flow. $C_d$ is typically **0.60 – 0.66** for sharp-crested rectangular weirs with full contraction.

**Step 5 — Engineering check (numerical substitution):**
For $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $H = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $C_d = 0.62$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$:

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.62 \cdot 2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81} \cdot (0.05)^{3/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.43\,\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$(0.05)^{3/2} = 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{0.05} = 0.05 \cdot 0.2236 = 0.01118\,\text{m}^{3/2}$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \cdot 2 \cdot 4.43 \cdot 0.01118 = 0.0409\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 147\,\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

This matches field data for typical weir skimmers operating at 5 cm head.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Oleophilic Skimmer Throughput

**Physical Model:** A rotating disc of diameter $D$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). At any instant, a fraction $\beta$ of the disc surface is wetted by oil.

**Step 1 — Wetted surface area:**

$$A_{wetted} = \beta \cdot \pi D^2$$

**Step 2 — Linear surface speed at disc rim:**

$$v_{rim} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = \pi D N$$

where $N$ is rotational speed in rev/s.

**Step 3 — Oil film thickness retained on surface (empirical, Berridge correlation):**

$$\delta_{film} = k_{\delta} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_{oil} \cdot v_{rim}}{g \cdot \Delta\rho}}$$

where $k_{\delta}$ is a geometry constant (typically 0.05–0.10 for discs).

**Step 4 — Volumetric pickup rate per disc:**

$$Q_{disc} = A_{wetted} \cdot v_{rim} \cdot \delta_{film} = \beta \pi D^2 \cdot \pi D N \cdot k_{\delta} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_{oil} \pi D N}{g \Delta\rho}}$$

**Step 5 — Compact form:**

$$Q_{disc} = \beta \pi^2 k_{\delta} D^3 N \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \mu_{oil} D N}{g \Delta\rho}}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Recovery rate scales roughly as **$D^{3.5} N^{1.5}$** — doubling the disc diameter more than **doubles** the throughput. This is why modern disc skimmers use diameters up to **3 m**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Recovery Efficiency for a Multi-Skimmer Operation

**Scenario:** $n$ skimmers operate in parallel over time $T$. Each skimmer $i$ has instantaneous throughput $Q_i(t)$.

**Step 1 — Total volume recovered:**

$$V_{recovered} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{0}^{T} Q_i(t) \cdot \eta_{i,local}(t)\, dt$$

where $\eta_{i,local}$ is local recovery efficiency (accounts for oil–water separation efficiency at that point).

**Step 2 — Spilled volume (mass balance, assuming first-order evaporation loss $k_e$):**

$$V_{spilled}(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t} - V_{recovered}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Define overall system recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta_R = \dfrac{V_{recovered}}{V_0} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Example calculation:**
- Initial spill $V_0 = 5000\,\text{m}^3$
- Three skimmers, each recovers $1200\,\text{m}^3$ in 24 hours
- $V_{recovered} = 3 \times 1200 = 3600\,\text{m}^3$

$$\eta_R = \dfrac{3600}{5000} \times 100\% = 72\%$$

This **72%** is realistic for a real-world sea operation where waves, viscosity changes, and skimming limits cap efficiency.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Flowchart (Numerical Logic)

| Parameter | Threshold | Recommended System |
|---|---|---|
| Oil viscosity < 50 cSt | Light, fresh crude | Weir, disc, drum |
| 50 – 1000 cSt | Medium crude | Brush, belt |
| > 1000 cSt | Heavy / weathered | Rope mop, vacuum with heating |
| Sea state > 0.7 m Hs | Rough | Rope mop, vessel-mounted |
| Spill < 10 m³ | Small | Sorbents, in-situ burn |
| Spill > 1000 m³ | Large | Multi-skimmer vessel systems |
| Oil thickness > 3 mm | Thick slick | In-situ burn (if permitted) |
| Near sensitive coastline | Critical | Vacuum + sorbent backup |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Complete Skimmer Sizing Problem

> **Problem:** A tanker spills 8000 m³ of light crude (viscosity 25 cSt) in the Arabian Sea. Sea state is 0.4 m significant wave height. Design a weir skimmer system to achieve at least 70% recovery in 48 hours.

**Step 1 — Required recovery volume:**

$$V_{target} = 0.70 \times 8000 = 5600\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required time-averaged throughput:**

$$\bar{Q} = \dfrac{5600}{48} = 116.7\,\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Apply weir equation with $C_d = 0.62$, $H = 0.04\,\text{m}$, $L = 2\,\text{m}$:**

$$Q_{single} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.62 \cdot 2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81} \cdot (0.04)^{3/2}$$

$$Q_{single} = 0.4133 \cdot 2 \cdot 4.43 \cdot 0.008 = 0.0293\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 105.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Number of skimmers required (with 10% redundancy):**

$$N = \lceil 1.1 \times \dfrac{116.7}{105.5} \rceil = \lceil 1.22 \rceil = 2 \text{ skimmers}$$

**Step 5 — Final design:** **Two weir skimmers**, each with $L = 2\,\text{m}$ weir, $H = 0.04\,\text{m}$ head, deployed in a parallel configuration with a containment boom upstream.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PENST412]**
*Define the term "Recovery Efficiency" as applied to marine oil spill recovery systems. State the standard units and a typical field-realistic value.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Recovery efficiency ($\eta_R$) is the ratio of the volume of oil successfully recovered to the total volume originally spilled, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_R = \dfrac{V_{recovered}}{V_{spilled}} \times 100\%$$

- **Units:** Dimensionless, expressed as **%**.
- **Typical field value:** For a well-organized multi-skimmer response, $\eta_R$ ranges from **60% to 90%**, with **70–75%** being the global historical average (ITOPF data).
- **Limiting factors:** sea state, oil viscosity, weather window, response time, equipment availability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Formula (1 mark), Typical range with limitation (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Short Question]**
*List any four types of oleophilic skimmers and state the principle on which they operate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Oleophilic skimmers operate on the principle of **selective adhesion of oil to an oil-wet (oleophilic) surface** that moves through the oil–water interface, lifts a film of oil, and mechanically scrapes it into a collection sump. The governing physics is Young's relation, where the oil–solid contact angle $\theta$ is **less than 90°**, favouring oil wetting.

**Four subtypes:**

1. **Disc Skimmer** — vertical rotating discs lift oil as they emerge from water.
2. **Drum Skimmer** — large horizontal rotating drum, partially submerged.
3. **Brush Skimmer** — rotating cylindrical brushes with oleophilic fibres; the most versatile type.
4. **Belt Skimmer** — endless inclined oleophilic belt looped over pulleys.
5. *(Bonus: Rope Mop Skimmer)* — continuous oleophilic rope pulled over pulleys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Principle (1 mark), Four types (1 mark), Brief description (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Weir Skimmer** and a **Drum Skimmer**. Compare their suitability for recovering (i) light crude and (ii) heavy fuel oil. (7 marks)

**(b)** A weir skimmer has a weir length of **3 m** and operates with a head of **0.06 m** above the weir crest. Taking $C_d = 0.62$ and $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, calculate the volumetric throughput in **m³/hr**. If the spilled volume is 4500 m³, how many such skimmers (operating 18 hours/day for 2 days) are needed to achieve 60% recovery? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle & Comparison (7 marks):**

*Weir Skimmer:*
- A floating weir is positioned at the oil–water interface.
- The weir acts as a sharp-crested dam; oil overflows into a collection sump.
- The sump drains to a transfer pump; water, being heavier, stays below the weir.
- The collected oil is pumped to a storage tank.

*Drum Skimmer:*
- A large rotating drum (1–3 m diameter) rotates slowly such that **40–60% of the drum is submerged**.
- The drum surface is coated with oleophilic material (e.g., stainless steel with rough finish or polymer).
- Oil adheres to the drum as it rotates out of the water; scraper blades remove the oil film into a collection trough.

**Comparative Table (for full marks):**

| Parameter | Weir Skimmer | Drum Skimmer |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Gravity overflow | Oleophilic adhesion |
| Viscosity suitability | Light oil (1–1000 cSt) | Medium–Heavy (50–50,000 cSt) |
| Wave tolerance | Low (≤ 0.3 m) | Moderate (≤ 0.5 m) |
| Light crude recovery | Excellent | Good |
| Heavy fuel oil recovery | Poor (clogs, emulsion) | Good with heated drum |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium |
| Maintenance | Simple | Moderate |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Volumetric throughput of one weir skimmer:**

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.62 \cdot 3 \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81} \cdot (0.06)^{3/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.43\,\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$(0.06)^{3/2} = 0.06 \cdot \sqrt{0.06} = 0.06 \cdot 0.2449 = 0.01470\,\text{m}^{3/2}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot 0.62 \cdot 3 \cdot 4.43 \cdot 0.01470 = 0.4133 \cdot 3 \cdot 4.43 \cdot 0.01470$$

$$Q = 0.0807\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 290.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 2 — Total operating hours:**

$$t_{op} = 2\,\text{days} \times 18\,\text{hr/day} = 36\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Volume recovered by one skimmer in 36 hours:**

$$V_{one} = 290.5 \times 36 = 10{,}458\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Target volume to be recovered (60% of 4500 m³):**

$$V_{target} = 0.60 \times 4500 = 2700\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Number of skimmers required:**
