$$N = \dfrac{V_{target}}{V_{one}} = \dfrac{2700}{10{,}458} = 0.258$$

Since we cannot have a fractional skimmer and accounting for 10% operational redundancy:

$$N = \lceil 0.258 \times 1.10 \rceil = \lceil 0.284 \rceil = 1\,\text{skimmer}$$

**Conclusion:** **A single weir skimmer** is sufficient to meet the 60% recovery target under ideal conditions. However, KTU best practice recommends deploying **2 skimmers** for redundancy and to compensate for sea-state losses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose **1–2 marks** in the numerical part by (i) forgetting to convert seconds to hours, (ii) omitting the 10% redundancy factor, and (iii) not explicitly stating the assumption of "ideal sea state." Always state assumptions clearly.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice, Module 3, KTU 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the principle, working, and field applications of **In-Situ Burning** as a marine oil spill recovery method. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a labelled diagram, describe the working of a **Rope Mop Skimmer**. Justify why it is considered the most versatile skimmer for weathered crude in rough seas. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) In-Situ Burning (7 marks):**

*Definition:* In-situ burning (ISB) is the controlled ignition and combustion of an oil slick *in place* on the sea surface, converting the liquid pollutant into combustion gases and a small solid/liquid residue.

*Working Principle:*
1. Verify oil thickness **≥ 2–3 mm** (thinner films cannot sustain combustion due to heat losses).
2. Confirm weather window: wind **< 20 knots**, wave height **< 0.5 m**, no rain.
3. Deploy a **fire-resistant boom** (e.g., Nova Scotia's *Fire Boom*) to thicken the slick to **5–10 mm**.
4. Ignite using a **helitorch** (helicopter-deployed) or a hand-held *Ignite* device.
5. Combustion front propagates at **0.05–0.2 m/s**, consuming 90–99% of the oil.

*Field Applications:*
- Arctic spills (where skimmers freeze).
- Open-ocean spills where response time is critical.
- Remote locations (lower logistics cost).
- The *Deepwater Horizon* (2010) response burned over **400 in-situ burns** removing **~30% of the spilled oil**.

*Advantages:*
- Extremely fast removal (**1000s of m³/hr**).
- Low capital cost; minimal infrastructure.
- No secondary waste (most oil is oxidized).
- Effective on emulsions and heavy crudes.

*Limitations:*
- **Smoke plumes** (CO₂, SO₂, particulates) cause air-quality concerns.
- **Residue** (3–10%) may sink, causing seabed contamination.
- Requires **regulatory approval** (often denied near populated coasts).
- **Restart risk** if wind shifts bring unburned oil ashore.

**(b) Rope Mop Skimmer (7 marks):**

*Construction:* A continuous loop of oleophilic rope (typically nylon or polypropylene) passes over a top driving pulley and a bottom pulley submerged in the oil–water interface.

*Working:*
1. Rope rotates slowly (0.5–2 m/s surface speed).
2. Oil adheres selectively to the oleophilic fibres.
3. Rope passes through a **scraper/wringer** at the top pulley, where oil is squeezed into a collection trough.
4. Recovered oil flows by gravity to a transfer pump.
5. Cleaned rope returns to the water.

*Justification for Versatility in Rough Seas & Weathered Crude:*
- **High oil viscosity tolerance:** The rope's fibrous structure can hold films of **1000–200,000 cSt** oil because oil is mechanically trapped between fibres, not just surface-adhered.
- **Sea state tolerance:** Operating depth is easily adjusted; the rope can follow wave heave up to **1.0 m Hs**.
- **Self-cleaning:** The wringer ensures continuous operation without fouling.
- **Low surface speed required:** Reduces re-entrainment losses in waves.
- **Debris tolerance:** Rope can be replaced quickly if damaged by debris.
- **Field-proven:** Used in the *Sea Empress* (1996), *Prestige* (2002), and *MV Wakashio* (2020) spills.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked "explain in-situ burning," students often **forget to mention the minimum oil thickness criterion (2–3 mm)**. This single missing point costs 1 mark consistently. Also, do not confuse "burn efficiency" with "recovery efficiency" — burn efficiency refers only to oil actually ignited and consumed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every Exam**

- **Recovery system definition:** Engineered units to separate, collect, and retrieve spilled oil; classified as **mechanical, non-mechanical, hybrid**.
- **Five major mechanical skimmer types:** Weir, Disc, Drum, Brush, Belt, plus Rope Mop.
- **Three non-mechanical methods:** Sorbents, In-situ burning, Chemical dispersants.
- **Weir equation (most-tested formula):** $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$; $C_d \approx 0.60 - 0.66$.
- **Recovery efficiency formula:** $\eta_R = V_{recovered}/V_{spilled} \times 100\%$; typical field value **60–90%**.
- **Oil-to-Water Ratio (OWR):** Purity indicator; higher OWR ⇒ better separation; OWR > 0.8 is refinery-grade.
- **Sorbent capacity:** $C = m_{absorbed}/m_{sorbent}$; polypropylene sorbents achieve **10–25× their weight**.
- **In-situ burn requirement:** Oil thickness **≥ 2–3 mm**, calm weather, fire-resistant boom, regulatory clearance.
- **Best skimmer for heavy weathered crude in rough seas:** **Rope Mop Skimmer**.
- **Best skimmer for calm seas and light crude:** **Weir Skimmer** (lowest cost, simplest design).
- **Best all-rounder for medium viscosities:** **Brush Skimmer**.
- **Young's relation for oleophilic adhesion:** $\gamma_{ow} \cos \theta > 0$ for oil-wet surfaces.
- **Most-tested historical spills:** *Exxon Valdez* (1989), *Deepwater Horizon* (2010), *MV Wakashio* (2020).
- **Always include in answers:** (i) Principle, (ii) Construction, (iii) Working, (iv) Viscosity range, (v) Sea-state limit, (vi) Advantages, (vii) Limitations, (viii) One field example.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 PENST412):** This topic primarily satisfies **CO3** (Apply recovery technologies) and **CO4** (Evaluate equipment selection).
- **Bloom's Level:** Part A → *Remember/Understand*; Part B → *Apply/Analyse/Evaluate*.
- **Examiner's Heuristic:** Use a **comparative table** for any "compare X and Y" question; full marks require **both merits and demerits**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skimming Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Skimming Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Skimming Systems — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **Skimming System** is a mechanical/oil-recovery assembly used during marine oil-spill response operations to physically separate and collect floating oil (or oily emulsions) from the water surface. The system typically comprises three sub-units operating in series: a **skimmer head** (the intake device that captures the oil layer), a **transfer/pumping mechanism** (vacuum, suction, or positive-displacement pump), and a **temporary storage reservoir** (dracones, bladders, or barge tanks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Skimming is classified under **Mechanical Recovery** of oil spills and is rated by the *ITOPF* (International Tanker Owners Pollution Federation) and *IPIECA* as the **primary in-situ recovery technique** for thick, emulsified, or weathered oil slicks where the slick thickness $h_{oil} > 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (SI):**
> - Oil layer thickness for skimmer operation: $h_{oil} \geq 1 \text{ mm}$ (rule of thumb = $1\,\mu\text{m} = 1 \text{ ppm}$ coverage on smooth water)
> - API gravity of recoverable oil: $\geq 10°\text{API}$ (heavier oils → use weir/drum; lighter oils → use oleophilic belt)
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Density of typical crude oil:** $\rho_o = 870\text{–}950 \text{ kg/m}^3$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Spatula"

Imagine a pot of boiling chicken soup. A layer of golden fat floats on the surface. You have **three ways** to remove it:

| Kitchen Analogy | Marine Equivalent | Physics Used |
|---|---|---|
| Tilt the pot and let fat run over the rim | **Weir Skimmer** | Gravity overflow over adjustable weir plate |
| Press a paper towel to the surface — fat sticks | **Oleophilic Skimmer** (drum, disc, brush, belt) | Surface adhesion (oleophilic attraction) |
| Suck the top layer with a straw held just below the surface | **Suction/Vacuum Skimmer** | Pressure differential (Bernoulli) |

The skimmer is essentially the marine engineer's "spatula" — but engineered to handle **thousands of tonnes/hour**, in **3-metre waves**, with **up to 80% water-in-oil emulsion content**.

## 1.3 Why Skimming? The Operational Window

> [!NOTE]
> Skimming is preferred when: (a) the slick is too thick for chemical dispersants, (b) the spill is near shore/in ports, and (c) the oil has not yet emulsified beyond a water content of $\sim 80\%$.

- **Limitation:** Skimmer performance degrades sharply when **emulsification exceeds 50%** ("mousse" formation), because the viscosity can rise from $\mu_o \approx 10 \text{ cP}$ to $\mu_{em} \approx 100{,}000 \text{ cP}$.
- **Threshold:** Most skimmers operate efficiently at $T \leq 1 \text{ m}$ wave height and $V_w \leq 1 \text{ knot}$ surface current.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Layered Physics

The floating oil layer sits atop the seawater due to density differential. The skimmer exploits one of **three physical principles**:

1. **Gravity Overflow (Weir Skimmers):** The weir crest is set slightly below the oil–water interface; oil flows over by gravity.
2. **Adhesion (Oleophilic Skimmers):** Rotating discs/drums/belts made of oleophilic polymer preferentially attract oil, which is then scraped off into a sump.
3. **Suction (Vacuum/Pneumatic Skimmers):** A pump creates a low-pressure region; oil is drawn in through a floating intake.

## 2.2 Skimmer Performance Metrics — The Four Ratios

| # | Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Recovery Rate** | $Q_r = A_{intake} \cdot v_{intake}$ | Volume of oil–water mixture collected per unit time (m³/h) |
| 2 | **Oil Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\dot{m}_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of recovered fluid that is actually oil |
| 3 | **Throughput / Capacity** | $Q_t = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g \Delta h}$ | Maximum volumetric flow (weir) using discharge coefficient $C_d$ |
| 4 | **Recovery Effectiveness** | $E_{rec} = \eta_{oil} \cdot \dfrac{Q_r}{Q_{max}}$ | Combined efficiency accounting for both purity and rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constant:** For weir skimmers, the **standard discharge coefficient** is empirically:
> $$C_d = 0.60 \text{–} 0.65 \quad (\text{sharp-crested weir over thin oil layer})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Symbol | Definition | Standard Unit | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{oil}$ | Oil slick thickness on water | m | Operationally $h_{oil} \geq 10^{-4}$ m |
| $h_w$ | Weir crest height (submerged) | m | Tunable; set $= h_{oil}$ |
| $\rho_o$ | Oil density | kg/m³ | Crude ≈ 870–950 |
| $\rho_w$ | Seawater density | kg/m³ | $1.025 \times 10^3$ |
| $\Delta \rho$ | Density difference $\rho_w - \rho_o$ | kg/m³ | Drives gravity separation |
| $\mu_o$ | Oil dynamic viscosity | Pa·s | $0.01 \text{–}0.1$ for fresh crude |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | m/s² | $9.81$ |
| $C_d$ | Discharge coefficient | dimensionless | $0.60 \text{–}0.65$ weir |
| $B$ | Weir width | m | Design parameter |
| $V_{boat}$ | Towing speed of skimmer vessel | m/s | Typical $0.5 \text{–} 1.5$ m/s |
| $\eta$ | Skimmer efficiency | % | Often $50 \text{–} 90\%$ in field |
| $Q_r$ | Volumetric recovery rate | m³/s or m³/h | Output target |
| $P$ | Pump power | W or kW | $P = \Delta p \cdot Q / \eta_{pump}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil spill response vessels** (OSRVs) deploy weir skimmers rated up to $Q_r = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ in $H_s = 1.5 \text{ m}$ seas.
- **Offshore platform bund skimmers** recover leaked crude at rates $\leq 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$.
- **Vortex / Hydrodynamic skimmers** (e.g., the *Maelstrom* type) achieve $\eta_{oil} \approx 95\%$ by inducing a swirling flow that separates oil centripetally.
- Skimming is the **only** mechanical method capable of recovering **uncontaminated** oil (recyclable back to refinery) — dispersants destroy this option.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weir Skimmer Recovery Rate (Torricelli + Discharge Coefficient)

**Starting principle:** Oil overflows a sharp-crested weir under gravity, with the driving head equal to the oil layer thickness above the crest.

**Step 1 — Identify the driving head.**
The oil sits at height $h_{oil}$ above the weir crest. The effective hydrostatic head is therefore:
$$h_{eff} = h_{oil} - h_w$$
where $h_w$ is the height of the weir crest below the oil surface. For optimal operation, the operator sets $h_w \approx 0.5 h_{oil}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Torricelli's law for ideal efflux velocity.**
The theoretical exit velocity of oil through the weir lip (analogous to a tank drain) is:
$$v_{th} = \sqrt{2 g h_{eff}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the empirical discharge coefficient.**
Real fluids suffer vena-contracta losses, viscous effects, and surface tension. The actual average velocity is:
$$v_{act} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g h_{eff}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by weir cross-sectional area to obtain volumetric flow rate.**
The flow cross-section is $A = B \cdot h_{eff}$, where $B$ is the width of the weir opening.
$$Q_r = A \cdot v_{act} = B \cdot h_{eff} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g h_{eff}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to standard KTU exam form.**

$$ \boxed{\, Q_r = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (h_{eff})^{3/2} \,} $$

> This is identical in form to the **Rehbock weir equation** used in hydraulic engineering — a classic KTU cross-topic link.

**Step 6 — Worked numerical evaluation.**

Given: $C_d = 0.62$, $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$, $h_{oil} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $h_w = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$h_{eff} = 0.05 - 0.02 = 0.03 \text{ m}$$

$$v_{act} = 0.62 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.03} = 0.62 \cdot \sqrt{0.5886} = 0.62 \times 0.7672 = 0.4757 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A = 2.0 \times 0.03 = 0.06 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_r = 0.06 \times 0.4757 = 0.02854 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 102.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Result:** $\;Q_r \approx 102.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ — typical mid-range weir skimmer output.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skimmer Efficiency Under Wave Action (Schmidt-Fortescue Approximation)

**Physical setup:** When the skimmer is deployed in waves, the oil layer thickness fluctuates with the wave elevation. We model the *effective recovery* as the time-averaged recovery.

**Step 1 — Define instantaneous oil thickness.**
Assuming small-amplitude Airy waves:
$$h_{oil}(t) = \bar{h}_{oil} + a_w \cos(\omega t)$$

where $a_w = H_s/2$ is the wave amplitude and $\omega$ is the angular frequency.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the recovery equation.**
$$Q_r(t) = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (h_{eff}(t))^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Time-average over one wave period $T$.**
For small $a_w \ll \bar{h}_{oil}$, expand via binomial series $(1+x)^{3/2} \approx 1 + \tfrac{3}{2}x + \tfrac{3}{8}x^2$ with $x = a_w \cos(\omega t)/\bar{h}_{oil}$:

$$ \langle h_{eff}^{3/2} \rangle = \bar{h}_{eff}^{3/2} \left[ 1 + \frac{3}{8}\left(\frac{a_w}{\bar{h}_{eff}}\right)^2 \right] $$

(The linear term vanishes because $\langle \cos \omega t\rangle = 0$.)

**Step 4 — Define wave-correction factor.**
$$\phi_{wave} = 1 + \frac{3}{8}\left(\frac{H_s/2}{\bar{h}_{eff}}\right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Final wave-corrected recovery rate.**

$$ \boxed{\, \langle Q_r \rangle = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \bar{h}_{eff}^{3/2} \cdot \phi_{wave} \,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even small waves **enhance** mean recovery slightly (because the $3/2$-power is convex), but in **breaking/steep waves** ($H_s/\lambda > 1/7$) the skimmer is **air-bound** and recovery collapses to zero.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pump Power Required for Skimmer Transfer

**Step 1 — Required pressure rise.** The pump must overcome: (a) hydrostatic head from recovery point to storage, (b) friction losses, and (c) nozzle/suction losses.
$$\Delta p = \rho_{mix} \cdot g \cdot H_{static} + \Delta p_{friction} + \Delta p_{nozzle}$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic power.**
$$P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_r$$

**Step 3 — Brake (shaft) power accounting for pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$.**

$$ \boxed{\, P_{shaft} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot Q_r}{\eta_{pump}} \,} $$

Typically $\eta_{pump} = 0.5 \text{–}0.7$ for positive-displacement oil skimmer pumps.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
Given: $Q_r = 0.0285 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H_{static} = 4 \text{ m}$, $\rho_{mix} = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{pump} = 0.6$, friction head $= 1 \text{ m}$:

$$\Delta p = 920 \times 9.81 \times 5 = 45{,}126 \text{ Pa} \approx 45.1 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{45{,}126 \times 0.0285}{0.6} = 2143 \text{ W} \approx 2.14 \text{ kW}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recovery Efficiency from Mass Balance

Let $M_{oil}$ = mass of oil entering skimmer per unit time, $M_{water}$ = mass of water co-ingested.

$$\eta_{oil} = \frac{M_{oil}}{M_{oil} + M_{water}} \times 100\%$$

For an **oleophilic drum skimmer** with adhesion efficiency $\alpha$ (dimensionless, 0.7–0.9 for fresh oil on a clean polymer drum of diameter $D$, rotating at $N$ rpm):

$$\eta_{oil} \approx \alpha \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot \pi D N t_{contact}}\right)$$

where $k$ is an empirical adhesion-rate constant and $t_{contact}$ is the drum–oil contact time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Skimming System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oil Slick on Sea Surface] --> B{Skimmer Head Selection}
    B -->|Gravity| C[Weir Skimmer Head]
    B -->|Adhesion| D[Oleophilic Drum/Disc/Belt]
    B -->|Suction| E[Vacuum/Pneumatic Head]
    B -->|Vortex| F[Hydrodynamic Vortex Head]
    C --> G[Primary Manifold and Suction Hose]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Positive Displacement Oil Pump]
    H --> I[Booster Pump Stage]
    I --> J[Temporary Storage - Dracone/Barge]
    J --> K[Segregator - Oil Water Separation]
    K --> L[Recovered Oil to Refinery]
    K --> M[Discharge Water overboard]
    J --> N[Sensor - Flow Rate, Density, Water Content]
    N --> O[SCADA Control Unit on OSRV]
    O --> P[Operator Display and Telemetry]
```

## 4.2 Skimmer Type Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Oil Spill Detected]) --> Q1{Oil Thickness?}
    Q1 -->|1 to 5 mm| R1[Weir Skimmer]
    Q1 -->|5 to 50 mm| R2[Oleophilic Drum or Disc]
    Q1 -->|Greater than 50 mm| R3[Suction or Vortex]
    Q1 -->|Emulsified Mousse| R4[Heated Oleophilic Belt]
    Q1 -->|Debris Laden| R5[Brush Skimmer with Debris Guard]
    R1 --> End[Deploy Skimming Vessel]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
    R5 --> End
    Q1 -->|Less than 0.1 mm| Skip[Use Dispersants or In-situ Burning]
    Skip --> End2[Document and Monitor]
```

## 4.3 Oleophilic Drum Skimmer — Internal Operating Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Oil-Water Mixture Approaches] --> P[Drum Rotates Clockwise]
    P --> Q[Oil Adheres to Oleophilic Polymer Surface]
    Q --> R[Scraper Blade Removes Oil at Top]
    R --> S[Oil Flows into Collection Sump]
    S --> T[Pump Transfers to Storage]
    P --> U[Water Drains Back Over Drum Bottom]
    U --> V[Returns to Sea]
    S --> W[Slotted Baffles to Remove Large Debris]
    W --> O
```

## 4.4 Weir Skimmer — Cross-Sectional Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sea[Sea Surface Level] --> Layer[Oil Layer Thickness h_oil]
    Layer --> Crest[Adjustable Weir Crest - Submerged to Depth h_w]
    Crest --> Lip[Sharp Crested Weir Lip]
    Lip --> Overflow[Oil Overflows at Velocity v_act]
    Overflow --> Manifold[Collection Manifold at Vessel Bow]
    Manifold --> Pump[Transfer Pump]
    Sea2[Water Below Weir] --> Underflow[Water Re-enters Sea Below Lip]
```

## 4.5 Wave-Recovery Coupling Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Wave Height H_s] --> Coeff[Correction Factor phi_wave]
    Heff[Mean Effective Head h_eff] --> Coeff
    Coeff --> Qmean[Time Averaged Recovery Rate Q_r]
    Cd[Discharge Coefficient C_d] --> Qmean
    B[Weir Width B] --> Qmean
    Qmean --> Pout[Power Required P_shaft]
    eta[Pump Efficiency eta_pump] --> Pout
    Hrho[Mixture Density rho_mix] --> Pout
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a weir skimmer and state the formula for its oil recovery rate.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A weir skimmer is a gravity-based oil recovery device in which the floating oil layer flows over an adjustable submerged weir (crest) into a collection sump, from where a pump transfers it to storage.
>
> The oil recovery rate is given by:
> $$Q_r = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (h_{eff})^{3/2}$$
>
> where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient (≈ 0.60–0.65), $B$ is the weir width, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $h_{eff} = h_{oil} - h_w$ is the effective head.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation of terms)*

---

### **Q2. List any four factors affecting the efficiency of a skimming system.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Oil layer thickness** $h_{oil}$ — recovery improves with thicker slicks.
2. **Oil viscosity** $\mu_o$ — high viscosity reduces inflow rate.
3. **Sea state / wave height** $H_s$ — choppy seas air-bound the skimmer.
4. **Degree of emulsification** (water content) — mousse ($> 50\%$ water) sharply reduces efficiency.
5. *(Bonus)* Skimmer vessel towing speed and discharge coefficient.

*(3 marks: 4 × 0.75 marks for each correctly identified factor)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of an **oleophilic drum skimmer**. Derive an expression for its recovery rate and discuss the effect of drum rotational speed on oil-recovery efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**

An oleophilic drum skimmer consists of a horizontal rotating cylinder whose outer surface is made of an **oil-attracting (oleophilic) polymer** (e.g., modified polyethylene or polyurethane). The drum is partially submerged at the waterline. As it rotates, oil adheres selectively to the drum surface while water runs off. A stationary scraper blade at the top of the drum detaches the oil film, which then flows into a collection sump and is pumped to a storage tank.

> **Schematic (refer Section 4.3).**

**Derivation of Recovery Rate:**

Let:
- $D$ = drum diameter (m)
- $L$ = drum length (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $h_{sub}$ = submergence depth (m)
- $v_{surface} = \pi D N$ = peripheral surface velocity (m/s)
- $\alpha$ = adhesion efficiency (0.7–0.9)
- $\rho_{oil}$ = oil density

**Step 1 — Oil pickup area per revolution.**
The drum contacts the oil over an arc length of approximately $L \cdot \theta_{sub}$ where $\theta_{sub} = \arccos\!\left(1 - 2 h_{sub}/D\right)$.

**Step 2 — Oil mass picked per revolution.**
The oil film thickness on the drum (empirical Schulze model):
$$\delta_{film} = K \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_{oil} \cdot v_{surface}}{\rho_{oil} \cdot g}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Recovery rate.**
$$Q_r = L \cdot \theta_{sub} \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \alpha \cdot \delta_{film}$$

Substituting $\delta_{film}$:

$$ \boxed{\, Q_r = L \cdot \theta_{sub} \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \alpha \cdot K \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_{oil} \cdot \pi D N}{\rho_{oil} \cdot g}\right)^{1/2} \,} $$

**Step 4 — Effect of $N$.**
- $Q_r \propto N^{3/2}$ — so faster rotation **increases** throughput.
- **BUT** above a critical peripheral speed $v_{crit} \approx 1 \text{ m/s}$, the oil film becomes unstable and water is entrained, **decreasing** $\eta_{oil}$.
- **Optimum:** $v_{surface} \approx 0.5 \text{–} 1.0 \text{ m/s}$ for fresh crude; lower for weathered/emulsified oil.

**(7 marks: 2 marks principle + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks discussion of N effect)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vessel-mounted weir skimmer of crest width $B = 3 \text{ m}$ operates in an oil slick of uniform thickness $h_{oil} = 60 \text{ mm}$. The weir is set with crest $h_w = 15 \text{ mm}$ below the oil surface. Take $C_d = 0.63$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Calculate: (i) the oil recovery rate in m³/h, and (ii) the hydraulic power required if the recovered fluid ($\rho_{mix} = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$) is pumped through a static head of $5 \text{ m}$ and friction head of $1.2 \text{ m}$ with pump efficiency $\eta_{pump} = 0.65$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Recovery rate:**

$$h_{eff} = h_{oil} - h_w = 60 - 15 = 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m}$$

$$Q_r = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot (h_{eff})^{3/2}$$

$$Q_r = 0.63 \times 3.0 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.045)^{3/2}$$

Step-by-step:
- $\sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$
- $(0.045)^{3/2} = (0.045) \times \sqrt{0.045} = 0.045 \times 0.2121 = 0.009546$
- $Q_r = 0.63 \times 3.0 \times 4.4294 \times 0.009546$
- $Q_r = 0.63 \times 3.0 \times 0.04228 = 0.0799 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

Converting: $Q_r = 0.0799 \times 3600 = 287.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$

> **Result:** $Q_r \approx 287.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$  ← *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Pump shaft power:**

$$H_{total} = 5 + 1.2 = 6.2 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta p = \rho_{mix} \cdot g \cdot H_{total} = 920 \times 9.81 \times 6.2 = 55{,}952.4 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot Q_r}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{55{,}952.4 \times 0.0799}{0.65}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{4470.6}{0.65} = 6878 \text{ W} \approx 6.88 \text{ kW}$$

> **Result:** $P_{shaft} \approx 6.88 \text{ kW}$  ← *(4 marks: 1 mark setup + 1 mark pressure + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark final answer)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the working principles, advantages, and limitations of **Weir**, **Oleophilic (drum)**, and **Suction (vacuum) skimmers**. State one engineering application for each.

**Model Answer — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Weir Skimmer | Oleophilic Drum | Suction/Vacuum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Gravity overflow | Adhesion to polymer | Pressure differential |
| **Best Oil Thickness** | 1–5 mm | 5–50 mm | $>50$ mm (incl. mousse) |
| **Recovery Rate $Q_r$** | High for thin layers | Moderate–High | High for thick/emulsified |
| **Oil Purity $\eta_{oil}$** | 80–95% (thin slicks) | 90–98% (excellent) | 50–80% (water co-ingested) |
| **Sea State Limit** | $H_s \leq 0.5$ m | $H_s \leq 1.0$ m | $H_s \leq 1.5$ m |
| **Emulsified Oil Handling** | Poor | Moderate | Good |
| **Power Required** | Low | Low–Medium | High (vacuum pump) |
| **Application** | Port/harbour clean-up | Open-sea slicks, calm–moderate seas | Heavy crude, emulsified mousse |

> *(7 marks: 2 marks for principle + 3 marks for comparison rows + 2 marks for application examples)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a complete skimming system** on an Oil Spill Response Vessel (OSRV), listing the function of each sub-system.

**Model Answer (refer to Section 4.1 for full mermaid block):**

The complete skimming system on an OSRV consists of the following sub-systems in series:

1. **Skimmer Head** — physical intake device (weir, drum, or vacuum) that captures oil from the water surface.
2. **Primary Manifold and Suction Hose** — channels the oil–water mixture from the head to the pump.
3. **Positive-Displacement Oil Pump** — provides the suction to lift oil from sea-level to deck-level storage.
4. **Booster Pump Stage** — increases pressure for transfer over longer distances to the storage barge.
5. **Temporary Storage** — dracones, inflatable bladders, or deck tanks on the OSRV.
6. **Oil–Water Segregator** — decanter or hydrocyclone to separate free water before offloading.
7. **Sensors and SCADA** — flow meters, density probes, water-in-oil sensors, and a control unit for remote operation.
8. **Offloading System** — to transfer recovered oil to shuttle tankers or barges for transport to refineries.

> **Functional Flow:** Sea Surface → Skimmer Head → Manifold → Pump → Booster → Storage → Segregator → Offloading.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark block diagram + 6 marks for sub-system explanation — allocate 0.75–1 mark per sub-system)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Skimming System Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3600** when converting m³/s → m³/h. This single error costs **1–2 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Wrong exponent in weir equation:** students often write $(h_{eff})^{1/2}$ instead of $(h_{eff})^{3/2}$ — losing **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing units:** using $h_{eff}$ in mm without converting to m in the formula. Always convert to SI (metres) before substitution.
> 4. **Confusing $\eta_{oil}$ and $E_{rec}$:** purity efficiency vs. overall recovery effectiveness. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch:** in weir/drum skimmer questions, the absence of a labelled diagram typically costs **1–2 marks** even if the derivation is correct.
> 6. **Omitting the discharge coefficient explanation:** $C_d$ is empirical, not unity — students writing $C_d = 1$ lose a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Skimming Systems**

- **Skimmer = mechanical oil recovery** for slicks of thickness $h_{oil} \geq 0.1 \text{ mm}$.
- **Three core types:** Weir (gravity), Oleophilic (adhesion), Suction (vacuum).
- **Weir recovery formula (KTU must-know):** $Q_r = C_d \cdot B \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h_{eff}^{3/2}$
- **Standard $C_d$ for sharp-crested weir over oil:** $0.60 \text{–} 0.65$.
- **Oil density:** $870\text{–}950 \text{ kg/m}^3$; **seawater:** $1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Oleophilic drum:** $Q_r \propto N^{3/2}$ (peripheral speed); optimum $v_{surface} \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Sea-state limit:** most skimmers fail at $H_s > 2 \text{ m}$.
- **Pump power:** $P_{shaft} = \Delta p \cdot Q_r / \eta_{pump}$.
- **Skimmer components (8):** Head → Manifold → Pump → Booster → Storage → Segregator → SCADA → Offloading.
- **Field efficiency:** $\eta_{oil} = 50\text{–} 95\%$ depending on emulsification.
- **Wave-correction factor:** $\phi_{wave} = 1 + \tfrac{3}{8}\left(\tfrac{H_s/2}{\bar{h}_{eff}}\right)^2$.
- **Emulsified "mousse"** ($\mu > 10^4$ cP) defeats weir skimmers; use **heated belt** or **vortex** skimmers instead.
- **ITOPF/IPIECA rank skimming** as the **most preferred in-situ recovery method** for recoverable oil.
- **Conversion check:** $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3600 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 264.17 \text{ US gal/s}$.
- **SI unit reminders:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
- **Dispersants vs. Skimmers:** dispersants sacrifice oil; skimmers **preserve and recover** it — a key KTU exam point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Storage System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Oil Storage System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Oil Storage System (KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **Oil Storage System** in a marine engineering context refers to the integrated arrangement of tanks, piping, valves, pumps, monitoring instrumentation, and safety devices used for the safe containment, transfer, measurement, and handling of petroleum oils (crude oil, fuel oil, lubricating oil, sludge, and bilge water) on board a vessel or at a marine terminal, designed in compliance with **MARPOL 73/78 Annex I**, **SOLAS**, and **IBC/IGC Code** regulations to prevent operational and accidental pollution of the marine environment. The system encompasses: 1. **Cargo oil storage tanks** (for tankers) 2. **Bunker fuel oil storage tanks** (for propulsion) 3. **Lubricating oil storage tanks** (for engine lubrication) 4. **Sludge and bilge holding tanks** (waste collection) 5. **Slop tanks** (for oily water separation and decanting) 6. **Segregated ballast tanks (SBT)** and **Clean ballast tanks** 7. Associated **piping networks**, **pumping systems**, and **gauging devices** ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The "Water Bottle System of a Ship":** > Imagine a giant floating city (the ship) that drinks thousands of liters of fuel every day, transports thousands of tons of oil as cargo, and constantly produces oily waste. The **Oil Storage System** is essentially the "stomach," "bloodstream," and "kidney" of this floating city all rolled into one: > - **Tanks = Stomach**: Store the oil (food/energy) safely without leaking. > - **Pipes = Bloodstream**: Carry the oil to where it is needed (engine, cargo pumps, etc.). > - **Pumps = Heart**: Push the oil through the system. > - **Filters/Separators = Kidneys**: Clean the oil and separate water from it. > - **Sensors & Alarms = Nervous System**: Continuously monitor for leaks, overflow, or pressure problems. Just as a human body cannot function if its stomach leaks, a ship cannot operate if its oil tanks leak — and the consequences at sea are far worse than a kitchen spill, because spilled oil can devastate entire coastlines. ## 1.3 Key Standards and Regulatory Framework | Standard | Governing Body | Relevance | |---|---|---| | **MARPOL Annex I** | IMO | Oil pollution prevention rules | | **SOLAS Chapter II-1** | IMO | Tank construction & stability | | **IACS Unified Requirements** | IACS | Classification society rules | | **ISGOTT** | OCIMF/ICS | International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers | | **OCIMF MEG** | OCIMF | Mooring, Equipment, and Guidelines | ## 1.4 Classification of Marine Oil Storage Systems Marine oil storage can be broadly classified as: 1. **Onboard Ship Storage** - Cargo tanks (Crude carriers, Product carriers, Chemical tankers) - Bunker tanks (HFO, DO, LO storage) - Bilge and sludge tanks - Slop tanks 2. **Onshore/Offshore Storage** - Storage terminals - FPSO storage tanks - Subsea storage systems (rare) > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember the **three pollution prevention principles** built into the design of any oil storage system: > 1. **Containment** (prevent leak to sea) > 2. **Segregation** (separate oil from water) > 3. **Recovery** (collect any spilled oil for treatment) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Oil Storage Tanks on Ships ### 2.1.1 Cargo Oil Tanks Cargo oil tanks are the primary oil-carrying compartments of an oil tanker. They are designed to maximize capacity while ensuring structural integrity and pollution safety. | Type | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **Centerline tanks** | Large rectangular tanks along the ship's centerline | Crude carriers | | **Wing tanks (Side tanks)** | Tanks on port and starboard sides | All tankers | | **Slop tanks** | Dedicated for oil-water emulsion | MARPOL requirement | | **Cofferdams** | Empty isolating spaces between oil tanks and accommodation/machinery | Safety & segregation | | **Double hull tanks** | Tanks protected by double skin and double bottom | Mandatory for new tankers post-MARPOL | ### 2.1.2 Bunker Fuel Oil Tanks Bunker tanks store Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO), Diesel Oil (DO), and Marine Gas Oil (MGO) for main propulsion and auxiliary engines. - Usually located in **double bottom**, **engine room side**, or **forepeak** regions. - Must be designed with **double-wall protection** or cofferdams to prevent leakage into sea. ### 2.1.3 Lubricating Oil Tanks Located near the engine room, these tanks store various grades of LO used for: - Main engine cylinder lubrication - System oil for turbochargers - Gearbox lubrication ### 2.1.4 Sludge and Bilge Tanks - **Sludge tank**: Collects oil residue from purifiers, separators, and tank cleaning. - **Bilge tank**: Collects oily water from engine room and other compartments. ### 2.1.5 Slop Tank A dedicated tank required by **MARPOL Annex I Regulation 29** for collection of oil-water mixtures from cargo tank washing. Used in the **Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System (ODMCS)**. ## 2.2 Design Considerations for Oil Storage Tanks > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Five Pillars of Oil Storage Tank Design:** > 1. **Structural Integrity** — Must withstand hydrostatic pressure, sloshing forces, and ship motions. > 2. **Corrosion Resistance** — Coatings, cathodic protection, and material selection. > 3. **Pollution Prevention** — Double hull, drip trays, high-level alarms. > 4. **Operational Efficiency** — Easy loading, discharge, cleaning, and gauging. > 5. **Regulatory Compliance** — MARPOL, SOLAS, Class rules. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. | # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Tank Volume (Cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | Volume of vertical cylindrical tank | $m^{3}$ | | 2 | Tank Volume (Rectangular) | $V = L \times B \times H$ | Volume of rectangular/square tank | $m^{3}$ | | 3 | Tank Volume (Horizontal Cylinder) | $V = L \left[ r^{2} \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{r-h}{r}\right) - (r-h)\sqrt{2rh - h^{2}} \right]$ | Partially filled horizontal cylinder | $m^{3}$ | | 4 | Mass of Oil Stored | $m = \rho \times V$ | Mass = Density × Volume | tonnes | | 5 | Filling Ratio | $FR = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{tank}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of tank filled | \% | | 6 | Ullage | $U = H - h_{oil}$ | Empty space above oil level | $m$ | | 7 | Sloshing Natural Frequency | $f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi g \tanh(\pi h / L)}{L}}$ | Natural frequency of liquid sloshing | Hz | | 8 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pressure at depth h | Pa | | 9 | Discharge Flow Rate | $Q = A \times v$ | Volumetric flow = Area × Velocity | $m^{3}/s$ | | 10 | Pump Power | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power for pumping | W | | 11 | Reynolds Number (Oil flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime indicator | dimensionless | | 12 | Effective Oil Discharge Limit | $EOD = \dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{total\,discharge}} \leq 30\,ppm$ | MARPOL compliance | $ppm$ | | 13 | Tank Stress (Hoop) | $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{P r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical tank | Pa | | 14 | Tank Stress (Longitudinal) | $\sigma_{L} = \dfrac{P r}{2t}$ | Longitudinal stress | Pa | | 15 | Oil-Water Separator Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Separation efficiency | \% | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | Use of Oil Storage System | |---|---| | **Oil Tankers** | Primary cargo containment, double-hull design | | **FPSO Vessels** | Offshore production storage and offloading | | **Power Plant Ships** | Fuel storage for floating power generation | | **Naval Auxiliaries** | Bunker and LO for fleet replenishment | | **Port Terminals** | Bulk storage and transfer to/from vessels | | **Pollution Response** | Recovery of spilled oil using storage barges | ## 2.5 MARPOL Annex I Key Regulations for Oil Storage > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know MARPOL Annex I Regulations (KTU Exam Favorite):** > - **Reg. 14** — Oil filtering equipment and discharge limits (15 ppm legacy / 5 ppm special areas) > - **Reg. 18** — Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) for crude carriers > - **Reg. 19** — Dedicated Clean Ballast Tanks (CBT) for product carriers > - **Reg. 20** — Double hull and double bottom requirements > - **Reg. 23** — Slop tanks and ODMCS > - **Reg. 24** — Transfer of oil cargo between oil tankers at sea > - **Reg. 25** — Subdivision and stability (damaged stability) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Permissible Oil Loading (Volume) The maximum safe loading volume of a tank is determined by the **filling ratio (FR)** which accounts for thermal expansion of oil. > [!NOTE] > **Concept:** Oil expands when heated. If a tank is filled 100%, expansion will cause overflow and pollution. Hence a safety margin (ullage) must be maintained. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the **ullage** (empty space above the oil): $$U = H_{tank} - h_{oil}$$ **Step 2:** Define the **filling ratio** (the fraction of tank occupied by oil): $$FR = \frac{h_{oil}}{H_{tank}}$$ **Step 3:** Rearranging for oil height: $$h_{oil} = FR \times H_{tank}$$ **Step 4:** For a cylindrical tank, the **maximum allowable volume** of oil: $$V_{max} = \pi r^{2} \times h_{oil} = \pi r^{2} \times FR \times H_{tank}$$ **Step 5:** If oil has a **coefficient of thermal expansion** $\beta$, the temperature rise $\Delta T$ produces a volume change: $$\Delta V = V_{0} \times \beta \times \Delta T$$ **Step 6:** The required **ullage volume** to accommodate this expansion: $$V_{ullage} = V_{0} \times \beta \times \Delta T$$ **Step 7:** The required **filling ratio** to prevent overflow: $$FR = \frac{V_{0}}{V_{0} + V_{ullage}} = \frac{1}{1 + \beta \Delta T}$$ For typical crude oil: $\beta \approx 0.0007$ per °C, $\Delta T \approx 15$°C (loading at 15°C, voyage at 30°C). **Numerical Example:** A cylindrical cargo tank has radius $r = 5\,m$ and height $H = 10\,m$. Find the maximum safe loading volume of crude oil given that oil is loaded at 15°C and may reach 30°C during voyage. ($\beta = 0.0007\,/^{\circ}C$) **Solution:** Step 1 — Temperature rise: $$\Delta T = 30 - 15 = 15\,^{\circ}C$$ Step 2 — Volume expansion fraction: $$\beta \Delta T = 0.0007 \times 15 = 0.0105$$ Step 3 — Filling ratio: $$FR = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0105} = \frac{1}{1.0105} \approx 0.9896$$ Step 4 — Maximum oil height: $$h_{oil} = 0.9896 \times 10 = 9.896\,m$$ Step 5 — Maximum oil volume: $$V_{max} = \pi \times 5^{2} \times 9.896 \approx 777.1\,m^{3}$$ Step 6 — Mass of oil (if $\rho = 850\,kg/m^{3}$): $$m_{oil} = 850 \times 777.1 \approx 660{,}535\,kg \approx 660.5\,tonnes$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sloshing Natural Frequency in a Partially Filled Tank > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Sloshing is a critical phenomenon in oil tankers. Resonance between ship motion and tank sloshing can cause structural damage and oil spills. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider a rectangular tank of length $L$ with liquid depth $h$. The natural frequency of the first sloshing mode in the longitudinal direction is derived from the linear wave theory. The dispersion relation for surface gravity waves in a finite-depth container is: $$\omega^{2} = g k \tanh(kh)$$ **Step 2:** For a rectangular tank of length $L$, the boundary condition at the walls gives: $$k = \frac{\pi}{L}$$ This is because the wave must have a node (zero velocity) at each wall. **Step 3:** Substituting $k = \pi/L$ into the dispersion relation: $$\omega_{n}^{2} = g \cdot \frac{\pi}{L} \cdot \tanh\left(\frac{\pi h}{L}\right)$$ **Step 4:** The natural frequency in Hz: $$f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\pi g}{L}\tanh\left(\frac{\pi h}{L}\right)}$$ **Step 5:** Two limiting cases: - **Case A: Shallow liquid** ($h \ll L$): $\tanh(\pi h / L) \approx \pi h / L$ $$f_{n} \approx \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{g h}$$ - **Case B: Deep liquid** ($h \gg L$): $\tanh(\pi h / L) \approx 1$ $$f_{n} \approx \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$ ### Numerical Example A rectangular oil tank has length $L = 20\,m$ and the oil depth during voyage is $h = 8\,m$. Find the natural sloshing frequency. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute the argument of tanh: $$\frac{\pi h}{L} = \frac{\pi \times 8}{20} = 1.2566$$ Step 2 — Evaluate tanh: $$\tanh(1.2566) \approx 0.8521$$ Step 3 — Compute $g/L$: $$\frac{g}{L} = \frac{9.81}{20} = 0.4905$$ Step 4 — Compute inside the square root: $$\frac{\pi g}{L} \times 0.8521 = \pi \times 0.4905 \times 0.8521 = 1.3128$$ Step 5 — Take square root and divide by $2\pi$: $$f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1.3128} = \frac{1.1457}{6.2832} \approx 0.1824\,Hz$$ Step 6 — Natural period: $$T_{n} = \frac{1}{f_{n}} \approx 5.48\,s$$ This frequency is critical: if the ship's natural roll or pitch period is close to 5.48 s, severe resonance can occur. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Oil-Water Separation Efficiency The performance of a settling tank (slop tank) follows the **Stokes' Law** for droplet rise velocity. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** A small oil droplet of radius $r_d$ rising through water experiences three forces: - **Buoyancy force** (upward): $F_{B} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{d}^{3} \rho_{w} g$ - **Weight** (downward): $F_{W} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{d}^{3} \rho_{o} g$ - **Drag force** (opposing motion): $F_{D} = 6 \pi \mu r_{d} v_{t}$ **Step 2:** At terminal velocity, net force = 0: $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{d}^{3} (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) g = 6 \pi \mu r_{d} v_{t}$$ **Step 3:** Solving for terminal (rise) velocity — the **Stokes velocity**: $$v_{t} = \frac{2 r_{d}^{2} g (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o})}{9 \mu}$$ **Step 4:** The required retention time in a tank of height $H$: $$t_{ret} = \frac{H}{v_{t}} = \frac{9 \mu H}{2 r_{d}^{2} g (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o})}$$ **Step 5:** The separation efficiency for a given residence time $t_{r}$: $$\eta = 1 - e^{-t_{r}/t_{ret}}$$ ### Numerical Example An oil-water separator has height $H = 3\,m$. Oil droplets of diameter $d = 50\,\mu m$ must be separated. Given: - $\rho_{o} = 850\,kg/m^{3}$ - $\rho_{w} = 1025\,kg/m^{3}$ - $\mu_{w} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,Pa\cdot s$ Find the minimum residence time for 90% separation. **Solution:** Step 1 — Droplet radius: $$r_{d} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,m$$ Step 2 — Terminal velocity: $$v_{t} = \frac{2 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times 9.81 \times (1025 - 850)}{9 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ Step 3 — Numerator: $$2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 9.81 \times 175 = 2.146 \times 10^{-6}$$ Step 4 — Denominator: $$9 \times 10^{-3} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$$ Step 5 — Terminal velocity: $$v_{t} = \frac{2.146 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{-4}\,m/s$$ Step 6 — Retention time: $$t_{ret} = \frac{3}{2.38 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 12{,}605\,s \approx 3.5\,hours$$ Step 7 — For 90% efficiency, $t_{r} = -\ln(0.1) \times t_{ret} = 2.303 \times 12{,}605 \approx 29{,}030\,s \approx 8.06\,hours$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Storage System Components | Component | Primary Function | Pollution Control Role | Failure Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | **Double Hull** | Structural + pollution barrier | Prevents oil leak in collision/grounding | Catastrophic spill | | **Cofferdam** | Air/empty isolating space | Prevents oil reaching hot machinery | Fire/explosion | | **Slop Tank** | Collects oil-water emulsion | Enables ODMCS operation | Illegal discharge | | **ODMCS** | Monitors oil content in discharge | Ensures <15 ppm compliance | MARPOL violation | | **IACS Sounding** | Measures oil level/ullage | Prevents overflow | Spill on deck | | **Inert Gas System** | Maintains O₂ < 8% in tanks | Prevents explosion in empty tanks | Tank fire | | **Crude Oil Washing (COW)** | Cleans cargo tanks with crude | Reduces slop generation | Excessive sludge | | **High-Level Alarm** | Warns at 95% filling | Prevents overflow | Oil spill | | **Overfill Protection** | Auto-shutoff at 98% filling | Final defense against overflow | Major spill | | **Drip Tray** | Catches minor leaks | Local containment | Small spill | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Marine Oil Storage System — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HULL["HULL STRUCTURE"] DH["DOUBLE HULL LAYER"] DB["DOUBLE BOTTOM"] end subgraph CARGO["CARGO OIL STORAGE"] CT1["CENTERLINE TANK 1"] CT2["CENTERLINE TANK 2"] WT_P["WING TANK PORT"] WT_S["WING TANK STARBOARD"] SBT["SEGREGATED BALLAST TANK"] SLT["SLOP TANK"] COF1["COFFERDAM FORE"] COF2["COFFERDAM AFT"] end subgraph BUNKER["BUNKER STORAGE"] HFO["HEAVY FUEL OIL TANK"] DO["DIESEL OIL TANK"] MGO["MARINE GAS OIL TANK"] LO["LUBRICATING OIL TANK"] end subgraph WASTE["WASTE STORAGE"] ST["SLUDGE TANK"] BT["BILGE TANK"] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL & MONITORING"] ODMCS["ODMCS UNIT"] HLA["HIGH LEVEL ALARM"] OFP["OVERFILL PROTECTION"] IGS["INERT GAS SYSTEM"] COW["CRUDE OIL WASHING"] end subgraph TRANSFER["TRANSFER SYSTEM"] CP["CARGO PUMPS"] BP["BUNKER PUMPS"] MAN["MANIFOLD / VALVES"] PIPE["PIPING NETWORK"] end DH --> CT1 DH --> WT_P DH --> WT_S DB --> CT1 DB --> CT2 DB --> HFO CT1 --> CP CT2 --> CP WT_P --> CP WT_S --> CP HFO --> BP DO --> BP MGO --> BP LO --> BP CP --> MAN BP --> PIPE MAN --> PIPE CT1 --> SLT WT_P --> SLT SLT --> ODMCS HFO --> IGS DO --> IGS CT1 --> COW CT2 --> COW HFO --> ST DO --> ST BT --> ODMCS CT1 --> HLA CT1 --> OFP WT_P --> HLA COF1 --> CT1 COF2 --> CT2 ``` ## 4.2 Oil Flow Hierarchy on a Crude Oil Tanker ```mermaid flowchart LR A["LOADING MANIFOLD
AT TERMINAL"] --> B["CARGO PIPELINE"] B --> C["MANIFOLD VALVE
AND STRAINER"] C --> D["DISTRIBUTION
HEADER"] D --> E1["CENTERLINE TANK 1"] D --> E2["CENTERLINE TANK 2"] D --> E3["WING TANK PORT"] D --> E4["WING TANK STARBOARD"] D --> E5["SLOP TANK"] E1 --> F["CARGO PUMP ROOM"] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F E5 --> F F --> G["DISCHARGE MANIFOLD"] G --> H["SHORE TANK
AT TERMINAL"] F --> I["ODMCS MONITOR"] I --> J["15 PPM LIMIT CHECK"] J --> K{"COMPLIANT?"} K -->|YES| H K -->|NO| L["RECIRCULATE TO SLOP TANK"] ``` ## 4.3 Tank Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT["MARINE OIL STORAGE"] --> Q1{"CARGO OR BUNKER?"} Q1 -->|CARGO| CARGO_PATH["CARGO TANK SYSTEM"] Q1 -->|BUNKER| BUNKER_PATH["BUNKER TANK SYSTEM"] CARGO_PATH --> Q2{"CRUDE OR PRODUCT?"} Q2 -->|CRUDE| SBT_REQ["SBT REQUIRED
REG 18"] Q2 -->|PRODUCT| CBT_REQ["CBT POSSIBLE
REG 19"] SBT_REQ --> DH_REQ["DOUBLE HULL
REG 20"] CBT_REQ --> DH_REQ DH_REQ --> ODMCS_REQ["ODMCS REQUIRED
REG 23"] ODMCS_REQ --> COW_OPT["COW OPTIONAL"] BUNKER_PATH --> Q3{"WHICH FUEL?"} Q3 -->|HFO| HFO_T["HFO SERVICE TANK
AND SETTLING TANK"] Q3 -->|DO| DO_T["DO SERVICE TANK"] Q3 -->|LO| LO_T["LO STORAGE TANK
NEAR ENGINE ROOM"] HFO_T --> PURIF["PURIFIER / SEPARATOR"] DO_T --> PURIF LO_T --> LOP["LO PUMPS TO ENGINE"] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Double-Hull Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TOP["DECK LEVEL"] DECK["WEATHER DECK"] end subgraph INNER["INNER SHELL - CARGO TANK ZONE"] CT["CARGO TANK INTERIOR
OIL PRODUCT"] end subgraph PROTECT["PROTECTIVE SPACE"] DS["DOUBLE SIDE
BALLAST VOID"] DB2["DOUBLE BOTTOM
BALLAST VOID"] COFF["COFFERDAM
AIR SPACE"] end subgraph OUTER["OUTER SHELL"] HULL_OUT["SHIP HULL PLATING
EXPOSED TO SEA"] end subgraph BOTTOM["LOWEST LEVEL"] SEA["SEA WATER"] end DECK --> CT CT --> DS CT --> DB2 CT --> COFF DS --> HULL_OUT DB2 --> HULL_OUT COFF --> HULL_OUT HULL_OUT --> SEA ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define an Oil Storage System as used in marine engineering and list its main components.** **Model Answer:** An **Oil Storage System** on a ship is the complete arrangement of tanks, piping, pumps, valves, and monitoring equipment used to safely store, transfer, and handle petroleum products (cargo oil, bunker fuel, lubricating oil) on board a vessel in compliance with **MARPOL Annex I** and **SOLAS** regulations. **Main Components:** 1. Cargo oil tanks (centerline and wing tanks) 2. Bunker fuel oil tanks (HFO, DO, MGO storage) 3. Lubricating oil tanks 4. Slop tank 5. Sludge and bilge holding tanks 6. Cofferdams 7. Piping network, pumps, manifolds, and valves 8. Monitoring systems (ODMCS, high-level alarms, gauging) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is the purpose of a slop tank in an oil tanker? Mention the relevant MARPOL regulation.** **Model Answer:** A **slop tank** is a dedicated tank used to collect: - Oil-water emulsions generated during **cargo tank washing** - Dirty ballast water containing oil residues - Oil residues from cargo line draining It enables the operation of the **Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System (ODMCS)**, which allows the oil to be separated from water by gravity settling, ensuring the discharged water contains less than **15 ppm** of oil (or 5 ppm in special areas). **Relevant Regulation:** **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 23** — Slop tanks and ODMCS requirements. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Module-level questions. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both options below carry **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** Explain the **MARPOL Annex I regulations** related to oil storage and pollution prevention on tankers, with specific reference to: - Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) - Double Hull requirements - Slop tanks and ODMCS *(7 marks)* **(b)** A cylindrical oil tank on a tanker has a diameter of **10 m** and height of **12 m**. The oil is loaded at **20°C** and may reach **35°C** during voyage. The coefficient of thermal expansion of the oil is $\beta = 0.0008\,/^{\circ}C$. Calculate: 1. The maximum filling ratio 2. The maximum safe loading volume 3. The ullage at maximum loading *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) MARPOL Annex I Regulations:** - **Regulation 18 — Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT):** Crude oil tankers of 20,000 DWT and above must have SBT to provide ballast capacity without using cargo tanks, thereby reducing the risk of oil pollution during ballasting/deballasting. - **Regulation 20 — Double Hull and Double Bottom:** Oil tankers delivered after July 1996 must have a double hull of minimum 2 m width (side) and 2 m height (bottom) to protect cargo tanks from impact damage in case of grounding or collision. - **Regulation 23 — Slop Tanks and ODMCS:** Every oil tanker of 150 GT and above must have at least **two slop tanks** with sufficient capacity and an **Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System** to ensure that the oil content in any discharged water does not exceed **15 ppm** (or 5 ppm in special areas). **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 10\,m$, so radius $r = 5\,m$ - Height $H = 12\,m$ - $\beta = 0.0008\,/^{\circ}C$ - $\Delta T = 35 - 20 = 15\,^{\circ}C$ **Step 1:** Maximum filling ratio: $$FR_{max} = \frac{1}{1 + \beta \Delta T} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0008 \times 15} = \frac{1}{1.012}$$ $$FR_{max} = 0.9881 \approx 98.81\%$$ **Step 2:** Maximum oil height: $$h_{oil} = FR_{max} \times H = 0.9881 \times 12 = 11.857\,m$$ **Step 3:** Maximum safe volume: $$V_{max} = \pi r^{2} h_{oil} = \pi \times 5^{2} \times 11.857$$ $$V_{max} = \pi \times 25 \times 11.857 = 931.0\,m^{3}$$ **Step 4:** Ullage at maximum loading: $$U = H - h_{oil} = 12 - 11.857 = 0.143\,m \approx 14.3\,cm$$ **Results:** $FR = 98.81\%$, $V_{max} \approx 931\,m^{3}$, $U \approx 0.143\,m$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **components and working of an Oil Discharge Monitoring and Control System (ODMCS)** as required by MARPOL Annex I. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A rectangular cargo tank has length $L = 25\,m$, breadth $B = 15\,m$, and the oil depth during voyage is $h = 10\,m$. Calculate: 1. The natural sloshing frequency in the longitudinal direction 2. The corresponding natural period 3. Comment on the design implication if the ship's roll period is 6 seconds. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) ODMCS Working:** The **ODMCS** continuously monitors the oil content in any discharge from the slop tank to the sea. Main components: 1. **Sampling probe** — extracts water from the discharge line 2. **Oil content meter** — measures oil concentration (typically using UV fluorescence or light scattering) 3. **Flow meter** — measures the discharge flow rate 4. **Control unit** — calculates total oil discharged; starts/stops the pump 5. **Recording device** — logs all discharge data for port state inspection 6. **Overboard valve** — automatically closes if oil content exceeds the limit **Working:** When oily water from the slop tank is pumped overboard, the sampling probe draws a small stream through the oil content meter. If the oil concentration exceeds 15 ppm (or 5 ppm in special areas), the system automatically shuts the overboard valve and recirculates the flow back to the slop tank. All data is recorded for inspection by port state authorities. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $L = 25\,m$, $h = 10\,m$, $g = 9.81\,m/s^{2}$ **Step 1:** Compute $\pi h / L$: $$\frac{\pi h}{L} = \frac{\pi \times 10}{25} = 1.2566$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate $\tanh(1.2566)$: $$\tanh(1.2566) \approx 0.8521$$ **Step 3:** Compute $\pi g / L$: $$\frac{\pi g}{L} = \frac{\pi \times 9.81}{25} = 1.2327$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by tanh: $$1.2327 \times 0.8521 = 1.0503$$ **Step 5:** Natural frequency: $$f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1.0503} = \frac{1.0248}{6.2832} = 0.1631\,Hz$$ **Step 6:** Natural period: $$T_{n} = \frac{1}{f_{n}} = \frac{1}{0.1631} \approx 6.13\,s$$ **Step 3 — Design Implication:** The ship's roll period (6 s) is **very close to the sloshing natural period (6.13 s)**. The difference is only about 2.2%. This means the ship is operating in a **resonance-prone condition**: - Sloshing forces will be amplified significantly. - This may cause **structural damage** to the tank bulkheads. - It may also cause **excessive ullage movement**, creating safety and stability concerns. - **Recommendation:** Baffles should be installed inside the tank, or the loading plan should be adjusted to change the oil depth so that the natural period moves away from the roll period. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing SBT (Reg. 18) with CBT (Reg. 19):** SBT is for *crude carriers*; CBT is for *product carriers*. Many students interchange them. > 2. **Wrong MARPOL limits:** Writing "30 ppm" instead of "15 ppm" or "5 ppm in special areas." This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Forgetting to mention ullage calculation in filling ratio problems:** Examiners expect to see the ullage value, not just the volume. > 4. **In sloshing problems, missing the comparison with ship's roll/pitch period:** Always interpret the result in terms of resonance — this fetches the final 1–2 marks. > 5. **Not labeling the Mermaid/sketch components** in Part B (a) questions: Always name each component on the diagram. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Oil Storage System:** - [ ] Oil storage system = tanks + piping + pumps + monitoring + safety - [ ] Two main categories: **cargo tanks** and **bunker tanks** - [ ] Cargo tanks include: **centerline tanks, wing tanks, slop tanks, cofferdams** - [ ] Bunker tanks include: **HFO, DO, MGO, LO tanks** - [ ] **MARPOL Annex I** is the primary regulatory framework - [ ] Key regulations: **Reg. 14, 18, 19, 20, 23, 25** - [ ] **SBT** → Crude carriers (Reg. 18) - [ ] **CBT** → Product carriers (Reg. 19) - [ ] **Double hull** → Mandatory for new tankers (Reg. 20) - [ ] **Slop tank** → Required for ODMCS (Reg. 23) - [ ] **ODMCS limit** → 15 ppm (5 ppm in special areas) - [ ] **Filling ratio formula:** $FR = 1 / (1 + \beta \Delta T)$ - [ ] **Sloshing frequency:** $f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\pi g \tanh(\pi h / L)}{L}}$ - [ ] **Avoid resonance** between sloshing period and ship roll/pitch period - [ ] **Stokes' Law** governs oil-water separation in slop tanks - [ ] **ISGOTT** and **OCIMF** are key industry guidelines - [ ] Pollution prevention principles: **Containment, Segregation, Recovery**

topics.title: Treatment of Pollutant After Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Treatment of Pollutant After Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Treatment of Pollutant After Recovery** is defined as the systematic sequence of physical, chemical, biological, and thermal processes applied to the recovered pollutant stream (oily water, plastic debris, chemical cargo residues, sewage sludge, segregated garbage, etc.) to **reduce the concentration and hazard quotient** of the contaminant to a level compliant with **MARPOL 73/78 Annexes**, **IMO Resolutions**, and **flag-state / port-state discharge regulations**, before final disposal, recycling, or overboard release.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the treatment phase forms the **fourth pillar** of the marine pollution management hierarchy:

$$
\text{Marine Pollution Management Chain} = \text{Prevention} \rightarrow \text{Containment} \rightarrow \text{Recovery} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Treatment}} \rightarrow \text{Disposal / Reuse}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, PENST412):**
> The *treatment* step is **NOT** the same as *disposal*. Treatment aims to *transform* the pollutant into a less harmful, reusable, or compliant form. Disposal is the *final* terminal step (e.g., incineration ash to land reception facility).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you spill cooking oil on the kitchen floor. You first **mop it up** (that's *recovery*). But the cloth and the dirty water in the bucket are still contaminated. You then:

- Pour the oily water through a sieve (removes chunks — *coarse filtration*),
- Let it sit in a jug (oil floats up — *gravity separation*),
- Pass it through a coffee filter (removes fine droplets — *coalescence / fine filtration*),
- Test it with a sensor (verifies it's clean — *monitoring*).

That entire chain is **"treatment of pollutant after recovery."** On a ship, the pollutant is industrial-scale (thousands of ppm of oil, tons of plastic, cubic meters of sewage), but the logic is identical: **separate → concentrate → polish → verify → discharge responsibly.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Governing Limits Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Oil-in-water discharge limit (MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 34): **≤ 15 ppm** (parts per million) in the continuous overboard stream.
> - Sewage discharge (MARPOL Annex IV): **≤ 100 CFU/100 mL** of faecal coliforms after treatment.
> - Plastic garbage (MARPOL Annex V): **Zero discharge** — all plastics must be recovered and disposed ashore.

---

### 1.3 Scope of Treatment Under Module 3

| # | Recovered Pollutant Stream | Typical Source | Treatment Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oily bilge / ballast / tank-wash water | Engine room, cargo tanks | Oil content **≤ 15 ppm** |
| 2 | Recovered crude / fuel oil (slops) | Tank cleaning, bunker spills | Separate water, recover oil for reuse |
| 3 | Plastic debris (macro & micro) | Accidental spills, fishing gear | Sort, shred, pelletize, landfill or recycle |
| 4 | Hazardous chemical residues | Tanker incidents, cargo spills | Neutralize, solidify, hand to port reception |
| 5 | Sewage & grey water | Accommodation blocks | Biological oxidation, disinfection |
| 6 | Air pollutants (SOx, NOx, PM) | Main & auxiliary engines | Scrubbing, SCR, DPF |

> [!NOTE]
> **State-of-the-Art Metric:** Modern oily water treatment systems are rated to produce effluent of **< 5 ppm** in real conditions to maintain a **safety margin** below the regulatory 15 ppm ceiling — this is a common KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Treatment Philosophy

Marine pollutant treatment is universally organized into a **three-stage architecture**. This logic appears in nearly every KTU question paper.

**Stage 1 — Primary (Mechanical / Physical) Treatment**
- Removes bulk suspended solids, free oil (droplet diameter $\gt 150 \, \mu m$).
- Equipment: Coarse strainers, plate coalescers, gravity separators, hydrocyclones.
- Goal: Reduce oil content from **~1000–10 000 ppm → ~100–200 ppm**.

**Stage 2 — Secondary (Chemical / Physico-chemical) Treatment**
- Destabilizes emulsified oil, breaks oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions, adsorbs dissolved organics.
- Equipment: Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF), chemical dosing skids (coagulant + flocculant), activated carbon adsorbers.
- Goal: Reduce oil content from **~100–200 ppm → ~15–30 ppm**.

**Stage 3 — Tertiary (Polishing) Treatment**
- Final polishing, polishing filtration, biological oxidation, disinfection.
- Equipment: 5 µm / 10 µm cartridge filters, UV units, chlorination, membrane bioreactors (MBR).
- Goal: Reduce oil content to **≤ 5 ppm** (well below MARPOL 15 ppm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three stages?** A single-stage system cannot simultaneously handle (i) free oil (large, fast-settling) and (ii) emulsified oil (small, slow, chemically stabilized). Each stage targets a specific droplet-size class, governed by **Stokes' Law**.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Treatment Step

| Step | Why It Is Necessary | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Coarse straining | Protects downstream pumps & plates from ragging | Pump cavitation, separator flooding |
| Gravity separation | Free oil (low density, ρ ≈ 850–950 kg/m³) floats naturally on seawater (ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³) | Overload of secondary stage |
| Coalescence | Small droplets collide and merge on angled plates → larger drops → faster rise | Emulsified oil passes through |
| Hydrocyclone | Centrifugal field enhances separation 50–500× gravity | Bulky tanks, slow throughput |
| DAF | Micro-bubbles attach to oil droplets (10–50 µm) → float to surface | Residual oil in effluent |
| Carbon filtration | Adsorbs trace dissolved hydrocarbons & surfactants | Failed 15 ppm compliance |
| 15 ppm monitor | IMO-mandated self-check before overboard discharge | Illegal discharge, port-state penalties |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KRUST NOTE:** All equations below are tested numerically or conceptually in KTU 2024 ESE papers. Memorize the *form*, units, and the **physical meaning** of each variable.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{s} = \dfrac{d^{2} \, g \, (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o})}{18 \, \mu}$ | **Stokes' Law** — terminal rise velocity of an oil droplet in quiescent water. Foundation of gravity separator design. | m/s |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{w} \, v_{s} \, d}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** — confirms laminar regime ($Re \lt 1$) for Stokes' Law validity. | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | **Removal / Separation efficiency** of any treatment unit. | % |
| 4 | $C_{mix} = \dfrac{Q_{1}C_{1} + Q_{2}C_{2}}{Q_{1} + Q_{2}}$ | **Concentration after mixing** two streams. Mass-balance check. | ppm or mg/L |
| 5 | $\Sigma = \dfrac{V_{c} \, \omega^{2}}{g \, \ln \left( \dfrac{2r_{2}}{r_{1} + r_{2}} \right)}$ | **Sigma (Σ) factor** of a disc-stack centrifuge — equivalent settling area. | m² |
| 6 | $Q = A \cdot v_{s}$ | **Volumetric throughput** of a gravity separator with surface area *A*. | m³/s |
| 7 | $A_{req} = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{v_{s}}$ | **Required separator area** for a given peak flow. | m² |
| 8 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_{i} - \Delta T_{o}}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_{i}}{\Delta T_{o}} \right)}$ | Log-Mean Temperature Difference (for incinerator / heat-exchanger design). | °C or K |
| 9 | $BOD_{5} = \dfrac{(D_{1} - D_{2}) - (B_{1} - B_{2}) \times f}{P}$ | **Standard 5-day BOD** (biochemical oxygen demand) of sewage. | mg/L |
| 10 | $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-k \tau}$ | **First-order reaction** (UV disinfection, biological decay). | ppm |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Stokes' Law is valid only when** $Re \lt 1$. For oil droplets larger than ~100 µm in water, the actual rise velocity is better predicted by a Reynolds-number correction (e.g., the **Schiller-Naumann correlation**). This is a frequent KTU "trick" question.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Treatment Stage

- **Compliance Engineering:** Allows the vessel to legally discharge treated effluent in any MARPOL signatory port.
- **Resource Recovery:** Recovered oil from the sludge tank is re-bunkered or sold — direct economic gain.
- **Environmental Stewardship:** Brings the shipping industry in line with **UN SDG 14 (Life Below Water)**.
- **Ship Operation:** A well-designed treatment train reduces port-reception-facility (PRF) costs by **60–80%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Modern OWS (Oily Water Separator) systems like the **Alfa Laval PUREBilge**, **Wärtsilä Hamworthy**, and **Oceanic Systems** integrate all three stages in a single skid, with automated back-flushing, real-time 15 ppm monitoring, and IoT-based logging for port-state inspection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Law (Foundation of Gravity Separation)

We start from the force balance on a single spherical oil droplet rising through quiescent seawater under gravity.

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces acting on the droplet:**

- Buoyancy (upward): $F_{b} = \rho_{w} \, g \, V_{d}$
- Gravity (downward): $F_{g} = \rho_{o} \, g \, V_{d}$
- Viscous drag (downward, opposing motion): $F_{d} = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d \, v_{s}$ (Stokes drag for laminar flow)

where:
- $V_{d} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$ is the volume of a spherical droplet of diameter $d$.
- $\rho_{w}$ = density of seawater, $\rho_{o}$ = density of oil.
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of seawater, $v_{s}$ = terminal rise velocity.

**Step 2 — Net accelerating force:**

$$
F_{net} = F_{b} - F_{g} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) \, g \, V_{d} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) \, g \, \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}
$$

**Step 3 — At terminal velocity, $F_{net} = F_{d}$ (zero acceleration):**

$$
(\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) \, g \, \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d \, v_{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\pi$ from both sides and solve for $v_{s}$:**

$$
\frac{(\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) \, g \, d^{3}}{6} = 3 \, \mu \, d \, v_{s}
$$

$$
v_{s} = \frac{(\rho_{w} - \rho_{o}) \, g \, d^{2}}{18 \, \mu}
$$

This is **Stokes' Law** — the cornerstone equation of every gravity-based OWS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Implication:** Because $v_{s} \propto d^{2}$, **doubling the droplet diameter quadruples the rise velocity.** This is why **coalescer plates** are so effective — they merge small drops into larger ones.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Surface Area of a Gravity Separator

A horizontal-plate gravity separator (or a ship's sludge tank operating as a gravity tank) is sized by its **plan area**, not volume.

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow relationship:**

$$
Q = A \cdot v_{s}
$$

A droplet of diameter $d$ will reach the surface (and be skimmed) only if it has time to rise through depth $H$ during its residence time.

**Step 2 — Residence time in the tank:**

$$
\tau = \frac{V_{tank}}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot H}{Q}
$$

**Step 3 — Required rise time for the smallest droplet to be removed ($d_{min}$):**

$$
t_{rise} = \frac{H}{v_{s}(d_{min})}
$$

**Step 4 — For 100% capture of $d_{min}$, set $\tau = t_{rise}$:**

$$
\frac{A \cdot H}{Q} = \frac{H}{v_{s}(d_{min})}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $A$:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{v_{s}(d_{min})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the **MARPOL-mandated 15 ppm OWS** uses a **coalescer** with a $d_{min}$ target of **15 µm** — small enough to drive $v_{s}$ to a few mm/s, but the large plate area compensates by giving long residence time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Separation Efficiency of a Two-Stage Treatment Train

**Step 1 — Stage 1 (Coalescer) efficiency:** $\eta_{1}$

**Step 2 — Stage 2 (DAF) efficiency:** $\eta_{2}$

**Step 3 — Concentration after Stage 1:**

$$
C_{1} = C_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta_{1})
$$

**Step 4 — Concentration after Stage 2:**

$$
C_{out} = C_{1} \cdot (1 - \eta_{2}) = C_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta_{1})(1 - \eta_{2})
$$

**Step 5 — Overall efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{1})(1 - \eta_{2})
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** If $C_{in} = 5000$ ppm, $\eta_{1} = 0.90$, $\eta_{2} = 0.85$:

$$
C_{out} = 5000 \times (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85) = 5000 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ ppm}
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - \frac{75}{5000} = 0.985 = 98.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with two high-efficiency stages, we land at 75 ppm — *still above 15 ppm*. **This is why a third polishing stage is mandatory on every IMO-certified OWS.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sludge Mass Balance

**Step 1 — Total pollutant mass entering the treatment train:**

$$
M_{in} = Q \cdot C_{in} \cdot t \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

**Step 2 — Mass recovered as oil (re-bunkerable):**

$$
M_{oil} = M_{in} \cdot \eta_{recovery} \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

**Step 3 — Mass discharged in compliant effluent (≤ 15 ppm):**

$$
M_{out} = Q \cdot C_{out} \cdot t \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

**Step 4 — Mass transferred to sludge tank / incinerator:**

$$
M_{sludge} = M_{in} - M_{oil} - M_{out}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of sludge (assuming density $\rho_{sludge} \approx 950$ kg/m³):**

$$
V_{sludge} = \frac{M_{sludge}}{\rho_{sludge}} \quad [\text{m}^{3}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real ship operating tip:** The sludge tank must be sized to hold **at least 30 days** of sludge generation, otherwise the vessel cannot complete a typical voyage between port reception facilities. This is part of the **IOPP Certificate** survey requirements.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Treatment Method Selection

| Pollutant | Property | Best Primary Treatment | Best Secondary | Best Polishing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free oil ($d \gt 150 \, \mu m$) | Floats readily | Gravity / API separator | Coalescer plate | Cartridge filter |
| Emulsified oil ($d = 10\text{–}50 \, \mu m$) | Stabilized by surfactants | Chemical demulsifier | DAF | Activated carbon |
| Dissolved hydrocarbons | Molecular | Adsorption onto PAC | Biological reactor | UV oxidation |
| Plastic pieces (macro) | Solid, non-wettable | Manual sorting | Shredder | Pelletizer |
| Microplastics | Solid, $< 5 \, mm$ | Coagulation | Membrane (UF) | UV disinfection |
| Sewage | Organic, biological | Screening | Biological (MBR / SBR) | Chlorination / UV |
| Chemical residues (acid/alkali) | Reactive | Neutralization tank | Precipitation | Solidification (cement kiln dust) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Oily Water Treatment System (OWS) on a Ship

This is the **single most important block diagram** for the KTU 2024 syllabus — it is tested as a "draw and label" question in nearly every Dec/May paper.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OILY BILGE WATER INLET] --> B[COARSE STRAINER 5 MM]
    B --> C[OIL CONTENT MONITOR AT INLET]
    C --> D[GRAVITY SEPARATOR TANK]
    D --> E[INCLINED PLATE COALESCER PACK]
    E --> F[HYDROCYCLONE BANK]
    F --> G[DAF DISSOLVED AIR FLOTATION UNIT]
    G --> H[CHEMICAL DEMULSIFIER DOSING SKID]
    H --> I[ACTIVATED CARBON POLISHER]
    I --> J[15 PPM COMPLIANCE MONITOR]
    J --> K{OVERBOARD DISCHARGE BELOW WATERLINE
    J --> L[RECIRCULATE BACK TO INLET NON COMPLIANT]
    D --> M[SLUDGE COLLECTION HOPPER]
    F --> M
    G --> M
    M --> N[SLUDGE TANK]
    N --> O[INCINERATOR OR PORT RECEPTION FACILITY]
    style K fill:lightgreen
    style O fill:lightcoral
    style J fill:gold
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The compliance monitor (gold) is the **decision node**. If the stream exceeds 15 ppm, it is automatically **recirculated** (red) — the overboard valve remains shut. This is mandated by **IMO Res. MEPC.107(49)**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Plastic Waste Treatment After Recovery (MARPOL Annex V)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[RECOVERED PLASTIC WASTE DUMPED ON DECK] --> B[MANUAL SORTING STATION]
    B --> C[SHREDDER UNIT]
    C --> D[WASHING TANK WITH FRESH WATER]
    D --> E[DRYING CENTRIFUGE]
    E --> F[EXTRUDER PELLETIZER]
    F --> G[STORAGE SILO FOR RECYCLED PELLETS]
    B --> H[CONTAMINATED OR NON RECYCLABLE FRACTION]
    H --> I[COMPACTOR]
    I --> J[ONBOARD INCINERATOR]
    J --> K[ASH DISPOSAL TO PORT RECEPTION FACILITY]
    J --> L[FLUE GAS SCRUBBER AND MONITORING UNIT]
    style G fill:lightgreen
    style K fill:lightcoral
    style L fill:gold
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Plastic recycling aboard ship is **economically marginal**. Most vessels still prefer to **compact and store** plastics for shore disposal. In-voyage pelletization is only cost-effective on large cruise ships or research vessels with shore-discharge constraints.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Treatment Decision Logic for a Pollutant Stream

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[RECOVERED POLLUTANT STREAM] --> Q{PHYSICAL STATE}
    Q --> R[LIQUID]
    Q --> S[ SOLID]
    Q --> T[ GAS]
    R --> R1{OIL CONTENT}
    R1 --> R2[OILY WATER TRAIN OWS]
    R1 --> R3[CHEMICAL OR SEWAGE TRAIN]
    S --> S1{PLASTIC OR HAZARDOUS}
    S1 --> S2[SHRED AND PELLETIZE]
    S1 --> S3[STABILIZE AND SOLIDIFY]
    T --> T1[POLLUTANT TYPE]
    T1 --> T2[SOX SCRUBBER]
    T1 --> T3[NOX SCR UNIT]
    T1 --> T4[PARTICULATE DPF FILTER]
    R2 --> Z[COMPLIANT DISCHARGE OR DISPOSAL]
    R3 --> Z
    S2 --> Z
    S3 --> Z
    T2 --> Z
    T3 --> Z
    T4 --> Z
    style Z fill:lightgreen
    style Q fill:gold
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The examiner often gives a *scenario* (e.g., "A product tanker has just completed tank washing using crude oil washing. The washings are recovered in the slop tank. Outline the treatment train.") and asks for a flow diagram. This decision tree is the *template* you should reproduce on paper.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the three stages of marine pollutant treatment after recovery, and state the **primary objective** of each stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Primary (Mechanical/Physical) Treatment:** Removes bulk suspended solids and free oil (droplet diameter $\gt 150 \, \mu m$) using strainers, gravity separators, and plate coalescers. *(1 mark)*
2. **Secondary (Chemical/Physico-chemical) Treatment:** Destabilizes emulsified oil and adsorbs dissolved organics using DAF, chemical dosing, and activated carbon. *(1 mark)*
3. **Tertiary (Polishing) Treatment:** Final polishing to bring oil content $\le 5$ ppm using cartridge filters, UV, or membrane bioreactors. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term **"Separation Efficiency"** in the context of marine oily water treatment, and state the IMO regulatory limit for overboard discharge.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Separation efficiency $\eta$ of a treatment unit is the fractional reduction in pollutant concentration achieved, expressed as: $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$. *(2 marks)*
- **IMO Regulatory Limit:** As per **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 34**, the oil content of the effluent continuously discharged overboard must not exceed **15 parts per million (15 ppm)**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
