```mermaid
    D11 -->|Yes| D13{Reg 17 Reception Confirmed}
    D13 -->|No| D14[Deficiency Noted Reg 17]
    D13 -->|Yes| D15[All Six Pillars Cleared]
    D4 --> D16[Rectification Required]
    D6 --> D16
    D8 --> D16
    D10 --> D16
    D12 --> D16
    D14 --> D16
    D16 --> D17[Resurvey Within 3 Months]
    D15 --> D18[Issue IAPP Certificate]
    D17 --> D2
    D18 --> D19[Validity 5 Years Subject To Annual Endorsement]
```

## 4.4 NOx Tier Implementation Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Pre 2000 No NOx Limit] --> T1[Tier I From 1 Jan 2000]
    T1 --> T2[Tier II From 1 Jan 2011]
    T2 --> T3[Tier III From 1 Jan 2016 In ECA Only]
    T1 --> T1D[17 g per kWh For n less than 130]
    T2 --> T2D[14.4 g per kWh For n less than 130]
    T3 --> T3D[3.4 g per kWh For n less than 130]
    T2D --> T2C[76 percent reduction from Tier I]
    T3D --> T3C[80 percent reduction from Tier I]
```

## 4.5 Fuel Sulphur Compliance Pathway

```mermaid
graph TD
    FU1[Global Limit 0.5 percent m/m from 1 Jan 2020]
    FU2[ECA Limit 0.1 percent m/m from 1 Jan 2015]
    FU3[EGCS Scrubber Pathway]
    FU4[LNG Fuel Pathway]
    FU5[Bunker Delivery Note And Sampling]
    FU1 --> PATH1[Option A Use LSFO 0.5 percent S]
    FU1 --> PATH2[Option B Install Scrubber and Use HSFO]
    FU2 --> PATH3[Option A Use ULSFO 0.1 percent S]
    FU2 --> PATH4[Option B Install Hybrid Scrubber]
    PATH2 --> FU3
    PATH1 --> FU5
    PATH3 --> FU5
    PATH4 --> FU5
    FU5 --> SURV[Surveyor Verifies S Content In BDNs]
    SURV --> CERT[IAPP Certificate Endorsement Confirms Compliance]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q: Define the International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate. State the regulation under MARPOL Annex VI that mandates its issuance, and identify the minimum tonnage threshold of ships to which it applies.**

**Model Answer:**

The International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate is a statutory certificate issued under **Regulation 8 of MARPOL Annex VI** (as amended) certifying that a ship complies with the international regulations for the prevention of air pollution from ships.

**Issuing Authority:** The certificate is issued by the **Administration (Flag State)** or by a **Recognized Organization (RO)** acting on behalf of the Administration.

**Applicability Threshold:** The certificate applies to every **ship of 400 GT and above** engaged on **international voyages**.

**Form of Certificate:** It is issued in the form prescribed in **Appendix I of MARPOL Annex VI**, with a maximum validity of **5 years**, subject to annual, intermediate, and renewal surveys.

**Scope of Compliance:** The certificate attests to compliance with **six regulations** (Reg. 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, and 17) covering ozone-depleting substances, NOx emissions, SOx and fuel sulphur, VOC management, shipboard incineration, and port reception facilities.

*(Word count target for 3-mark answer: 90-110 words with a definitional structure.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: List any THREE categories of surveys required for the issuance and continued validity of the IAPP Certificate as per MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 5.**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Regulation 5 of MARPOL Annex VI**, the following three categories of surveys are required for the IAPP Certificate:

**1. Initial Survey:** Conducted **before the ship is put into service** or before the IAPP Certificate is first issued. The purpose is to verify that the ship's design, equipment, fittings, arrangements, and materials fully comply with the requirements of MARPOL Annex VI.

**2. Annual Survey:** Conducted **within 3 months before or after each anniversary date** of the IAPP Certificate. It is a general visual inspection to verify the certificate's continued validity, with any defects noted and endorsements made to the certificate.

**3. Renewal Survey:** Conducted **before the expiry of the existing IAPP Certificate**, at intervals not exceeding 5 years, to renew the certificate. The renewal survey scope is equivalent to the initial survey.

*(Note: An additional survey is also required after any repair or accident affecting compliance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries one 14-mark question with internal choice (either/or). The student must answer sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Provide complete model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain in detail the SIX regulatory compliance pillars verified during the IAPP survey under MARPOL Annex VI. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IAPP survey verifies compliance with **six regulatory pillars** of MARPOL Annex VI, each addressing a specific category of air pollutant:

**Pillar 1: Regulation 12 — Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS)**
This pillar prohibits the emission of ozone-depleting substances such as CFCs, halons, HCFCs, and carbon tetrachloride from shipboard refrigeration, air-conditioning, and fire-fighting systems. The surveyor inspects the **ODS Record Book** to confirm that no controlled substances have been vented. New installations must use environmentally acceptable alternatives (HFCs, PFCs, or natural refrigerants like CO2 and ammonia) with an **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) less than 0.05**.

**Pillar 2: Regulation 13 — Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**
NOx emissions are regulated through progressive **Tier I (2000), Tier II (2011), and Tier III (2016 in ECAs)** limits based on the engine's rated speed $n$ (rpm). The surveyor verifies the **Engine International Air Pollution Prevention (EIAPP) Certificate** and the **NOx Technical File** maintained by the engine manufacturer, confirming that the engine's parameters have not been altered in service.

**Pillar 3: Regulation 14 — Sulphur Oxides (SOx) and Particulate Matter**
The surveyor verifies the **sulphur content of fuel oil** used on board. The global limit is **0.50% m/m** (from 2020) and the ECA limit is **0.10% m/m** (from 2015). Evidence is provided through **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN)** retained for at least 3 years. If an **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS / scrubber)** is fitted, the surveyor reviews the **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)** data and wash water discharge records.

**Pillar 4: Regulation 15 — Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)**
Applicable to **oil tankers** during loading, transporting, or carrying crude oil, gas condensate, or other volatile organic liquids. The surveyor inspects the **Vapour Return System (VRS)** piping, the **Vapour manifold**, and the **P/V valves** for proper operation. The ship must use a **Vapour Emission Control System (VECS)** when in ports that require it.

**Pillar 5: Regulation 16 — Shipboard Incineration**
The surveyor inspects the **shipboard incinerator** to ensure it is of an approved type, maintains **combustion chamber temperature of 850 °C or higher**, and is not used to incinerate prohibited substances (PCBs, halogenated compounds, garbage with heavy metals, or refined petroleum products in certain categories).

**Pillar 6: Regulation 17 — Reception Facilities**
This pillar imposes an obligation on **Port States** to provide adequate reception facilities for ODS, EGCS residues, and VOC vapours. The surveyor confirms the **port-of-call data** indicating that the ship's regular trading ports have appropriate reception facilities.

**Conclusion:** Compliance with all six pillars is mandatory for the issuance and continued validity of the IAPP Certificate. Any deficiency in even one pillar results in the denial of the certificate or its endorsement.

---

**(b) A marine diesel engine has a rated speed of $n = 720$ rpm. Determine the maximum permissible NOx emission under (i) Tier I, (ii) Tier II, and (iii) Tier III. Comment on the practical engineering measures required to achieve Tier III compliance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the engine category.**

Given $n = 720$ rpm, this falls in **Category 2**: $130 \le n < 2000$ rpm.

**Step 2 — Apply Tier I formula.**

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier I}} = 45.0 \times n^{-0.2} \text{ g/kWh}$$

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier I}} = 45.0 \times (720)^{-0.2}$$

Computing $(720)^{-0.2}$:

$$\ln(720) = 6.579$$

$$-0.2 \times 6.579 = -1.3158$$

$$e^{-1.3158} = 0.2683$$

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier I}} = 45.0 \times 0.2683 = 12.07 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Tier II formula.**

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = 44.0 \times n^{-0.2}$$

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier II}} = 44.0 \times 0.2683 = 11.81 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Tier III formula.**

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = 9.0 \times n^{-0.2}$$

$$\text{NOx}_{\text{Tier III}} = 9.0 \times 0.2683 = 2.41 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate the results.**

| Tier | Maximum NOx (g/kWh) | Reduction from Tier I |
|---|---|---|
| Tier I (2000) | 12.07 | — |
| Tier II (2011) | 11.81 | ~2.16% |
| Tier III (2016, ECA) | 2.41 | ~80% |

**Step 6 — Practical engineering measures for Tier III compliance.**

Tier III requires an **80% NOx reduction** from Tier I, which is technically demanding. The principal engineering measures are:

1. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** A urea-water solution is injected into the exhaust stream upstream of a catalyst. The ammonia generated reduces NOx to nitrogen and water over a vanadium or zeolite-based catalyst. SCR systems are bulky, require reagent storage, and add back-pressure to the engine.

2. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** A portion of the exhaust gas is cooled, cleaned, and re-introduced into the combustion chamber. This lowers the peak combustion temperature, reducing thermal NOx formation. EGR systems are common in two-stroke marine engines.

3. **Engine Optimisation:** Modifying injection timing, increasing compression ratio, and using Miller/Atkinson cycle timing can reduce in-cylinder peak temperatures, thereby reducing NOx.

4. **Water Injection:** Direct water injection into the combustion chamber cools the peak flame temperature, reducing thermal NOx.

5. **Combined SCR + EGR:** The most stringent Tier III compliance is often achieved by combining EGR (for the bulk NOx reduction) with SCR (for the residual reduction to the Tier III level).

**Conclusion:** The numerical analysis confirms that Tier III demands a step-change in engine emission performance, achievable only through the integration of advanced after-treatment systems like SCR/EGR, making it a major capital investment for shipowners.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a) Describe the procedural steps for the issuance of an IAPP Certificate, including the role of the Administration, the Recognized Organization, and the shipowner. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The issuance of an IAPP Certificate follows a structured procedural framework involving three principal stakeholders: the **Shipowner**, the **Administration (Flag State)**, and the **Recognized Organization (RO)**.

**Step 1 — Application by the Shipowner**

The shipowner submits a formal application to the Administration (Flag State) or, in most cases, to a **delegated RO** such as Lloyd's Register, ABS, DNV, Bureau Veritas, or the Indian Register of Shipping. The application must be supported by ship particulars, engine technical data, trading route information, and a list of equipment installed for compliance with MARPOL Annex VI.

**Step 2 — Document Review by the RO**

The RO conducts a **desk audit** of all technical documentation, including the **EIAPP (Engine IAPP) Certificate**, the **NOx Technical File**, the **Ozone-Depleting Substances Record Book**, the **Bunker Delivery Notes**, the **Incinerator Approval Certificate**, and the **VOC Management Plan** (for tankers). A **Pre-Survey Deficiency List (PSDL)** is generated and communicated to the owner for action.

**Step 3 — Onboard Initial Survey**

A qualified surveyor (Master Mariner or Chief Engineer with specialized training) boards the ship and conducts a **physical inspection** of the engine room, fuel oil system, refrigeration and air-conditioning plants, fire-fighting installations, incinerator, and (for tankers) the vapour return system. The surveyor performs **functional tests** on the fuel changeover system and verifies the **intactness of engine parameters** against the EIAPP.

**Step 4 — Survey Report and Recommendation**

The surveyor compiles a comprehensive **Survey Report** documenting compliance (or non-compliance) with each of the six regulatory pillars (Reg. 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17). The report concludes with a clear recommendation to **issue** or **refuse** the IAPP Certificate.

**Step 5 — Certificate Drafting and Signing**

If the recommendation is positive, the certificate is **drafted in the form prescribed by Appendix I of MARPOL Annex VI**, signed by an **authorized signatory** of the RO (acting on behalf of the Administration), and affixed with the **official seal** of the issuing organization. The certificate is issued in **English and French** (the official languages of IMO).

**Step 6 — Endorsement and Continuous Compliance**

The IAPP Certificate is valid for a **maximum of 5 years**, subject to **annual endorsements**, an **intermediate survey at 30-36 months**, and a **renewal survey at 5 years**. The shipowner remains responsible for continuous in-service compliance between surveys.

**Step 7 — Role of the Administration**

The Administration (Flag State) retains **ultimate legal responsibility** for the certificate, even when issued through an RO. The Administration can **withdraw authorization** from an RO, invalidate certificates issued improperly, and conduct **supplementary inspections** as it deems necessary.

**Conclusion:** The IAPP issuance process is a **multi-tier, multi-stakeholder** procedure designed to ensure independent verification of compliance with MARPOL Annex VI. The procedure balances technical rigor with administrative efficiency, ensuring that international shipping air pollution control is enforced uniformly across the globe.

---

**(b) Explain the requirements for fuel oil sulphur content under MARPOL Annex VI, distinguishing between the global limit and the ECA limit. Discuss the operational implications and the EGCS (scrubber) pathway as a compliance alternative. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — The Global Sulphur Limit**

Under **Regulation 14 of MARPOL Annex VI**, the **sulphur content of any fuel oil used on board a ship** must not exceed the prescribed limit on a mass-by-mass basis.

- **Prior to 1 January 2012:** 4.50% m/m (this is the baseline from MARPOL Annex VI's original 1997 protocol).
- **From 1 January 2012:** 3.50% m/m.
- **From 1 January 2020:** **0.50% m/m** (the current global cap, established by the 2016 amendment).

The 0.50% global cap is equivalent to an emission level of **4.50 g SO2 per g CO2 × 100** when fuel is burned stoichiometrically.

**Step 2 — The ECA Sulphur Limit**

For ships operating in **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)** designated under **Regulation 14(3)**, a stricter limit applies. The currently designated ECAs are the **Baltic Sea (SOx ECA since 2006)**, the **North Sea (SOx ECA since 2007)**, the **North American ECA (since 2012)**, and the **US Caribbean ECA (since 2014)**.

- **From 1 January 2015:** **0.10% m/m** sulphur in fuel oil when operating in any ECA.
- The ECA limit corresponds to a SO2/CO2 ratio of **1.00**.

**Step 3 — Operational Implications**

The 0.50% global cap and the 0.10% ECA cap impose significant operational costs on the shipping industry:

1. **Fuel Price Premium:** Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (LSFO) at 0.5% S trades at a **USD 100-200/MT premium** over High Sulphur Fuel Oil (HSFO) at 3.5% S. For a large container ship consuming ~100 MT/day, this represents an **additional USD 10,000-20,000 per day** in fuel cost.

2. **Fuel Segregation and Bunkering:** Ships must maintain **separate fuel tanks** for HSFO and LSFO, and execute a **fuel changeover** when entering an ECA. The changeover must be **logged** with date, time, and position.

3. **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN):** Suppliers must provide a **Bunker Delivery Note** with the sulphur content stamp. The ship must retain BDNs for **at least 3 years** for PSC inspection.

4. **Sampling and Testing:** Under **Regulation 18** (added in 2010), the **in-use fuel oil sample** (MARPOL sample) must be taken at the bunker manifold and sealed in the presence of the bunker supplier. This sample is used for any compliance verification.

**Step 4 — The EGCS (Scrubber) Pathway**

As an alternative to using compliant low-sulphur fuel, ships can install an **Exhaust Gas Cleaning System (EGCS / Scrubber)** that removes SOx from the exhaust stream before it is discharged to the atmosphere. EGCS systems use either:

- **Open-loop scrubbers:** Sea water is used as the scrubbing medium; the wash water is treated and discharged back to sea.
- **Closed-loop scrubbers:** Fresh water with caustic soda (NaOH) is recirculated; the bleed-off is treated and discharged to shore or held on board.
- **Hybrid scrubbers:** Combine both modes, allowing operation in any water depth or zero-discharge zones.

The scrubber must achieve an **SO2 removal efficiency of approximately 80%** to allow the continued use of HSFO (e.g., 2.5% S) and still meet the 0.5% global limit. Continuous monitoring of SO2/CO2 ratio is required via a **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)**.

**Step 5 — Economic and Environmental Trade-off**

The EGCS pathway requires an **investment of USD 2-10 million** depending on ship size, but it can pay back within **1-3 years** if the LSFO-HSFO price differential remains high. However, the open-loop scrubbers discharge **acidic wash water** that can affect marine ecosystems, leading to **discharge bans in some ports** (e.g., China's inland waters and certain EU ports require closed-loop operation).

**Conclusion:** The sulphur cap regulations have fundamentally reshaped marine fuel procurement, ship design (scrubber retrofits), and trading route planning. The compliance landscape now offers two parallel pathways (LSFO and EGCS), each with its own cost, environmental, and operational profile, and the IAPP survey verifies the chosen pathway's compliance in either case.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMINATIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 400 GT threshold:** Many students answer "IAPP applies to all ships" — INCORRECT. The threshold is **400 GT and above engaged on international voyages**. Marks are deducted for this omission.
>
> 2. **Confusing EIAPP with IAPP:** The **EIAPP** is for the **engine** (Engine International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate), and the **IAPP** is for the **ship**. Do not interchange them in your answer.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula category for NOx:** Students frequently apply Tier II limits when Tier III is required (or vice versa). Always check: **Tier I (2000), Tier II (2011), Tier III (2016 — ECA only)**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up global and ECA sulphur limits:** Global limit is **0.50%** (from 2020) and ECA limit is **0.10%** (from 2015). Do not swap these in numerical problems.
>
> 5. **Omitting the Bunker Delivery Note (BDN) reference:** BDN retention for 3 years is a frequently tested compliance item.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the form of certificate:** The IAPP is issued in the form of **Appendix I of MARPOL Annex VI** — this is a high-yield point.
>
> 7. **Calculation error in $n^{-0.2}$:** Show the step-by-step calculation explicitly. The KTU examiner awards partial marks only if the calculation steps are visible.
>
> 8. **Not mentioning Port State Control (PSC):** Non-compliance can lead to **detention under PSC**. This is often the closing argument that fetches full marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize These Points for KTU Exam:**

- **IAPP Certificate is issued under Regulation 8 of MARPOL Annex VI.**
- **Applies to ships of 400 GT and above on international voyages.**
- **Maximum validity: 5 years.**
- **Form: Appendix I of MARPOL Annex VI.**
- **Issued by: Administration (Flag State) or authorized Recognized Organization (RO).**

- **Six compliance pillars:** Reg. 12 (ODS), Reg. 13 (NOx), Reg. 14 (SOx), Reg. 15 (VOC), Reg. 16 (Incineration), Reg. 17 (Reception Facilities).

- **NOx Tier I (2000) formula for $n < 130$ rpm: 17.0 g/kWh.**
- **NOx Tier II (2011) formula for $n < 130$ rpm: 14.4 g/kWh.**
- **NOx Tier III (2016, ECA) formula for $n < 130$ rpm: 3.4 g/kWh.**
- **For $130 \le n < 2000$ rpm: $45.0 \times n^{-0.2}$ (Tier I), $44.0 \times n^{-0.2}$ (Tier II), $9.0 \times n^{-0.2}$ (Tier III).**
- **For $n \ge 2000$ rpm: 9.84 (Tier I), 7.7 (Tier II), 1.96 (Tier III) g/kWh.**

- **Global sulphur cap: 0.50% m/m (from 1 January 2020).**
- **ECA sulphur cap: 0.10% m/m (from 1 January 2015).**
- **SOx emission formula: $\text{SOx} = 0.0235 \times S_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{BSFC}$ g/kWh.**

- **Scrubber equivalent removal efficiency for 0.5% global cap using 2.5% HSFO: ~80%.**

- **Survey types: Initial, Annual, Intermediate (at 30-36 months), Renewal (at 5 years).**

- **EIAPP = Engine-level certificate; IAPP = Ship-level certificate.**

- **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN) must be retained for 3 years.**

- **In-use fuel oil sample (MARPOL sample) must be taken at the bunker manifold.**

- **Designated ECAs: Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA.**

- **Incinerator minimum combustion chamber temperature: 850 °C.**

- **Tier III compliance requires SCR, EGR, water injection, or combined after-treatment systems.**

- **Port State Control (PSC) can detain non-compliant ships.**

- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) of new refrigerants must be less than 0.05.**

- **The 0.50% global sulphur cap is equivalent to SO2/CO2 mass ratio of 4.50 × 10⁻³ (or 0.45% by mass).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Mantra for KTU Exam:** "**MARPOL ANNEX VI = SIX REGULATIONS, FIVE YEARS, FOUR HUNDRED GT, 0.10% ECA, 0.50% GLOBAL, 850 °C INCINERATOR, 5-YEAR RENEWAL, EIAPP ENGINE, IAPP SHIP.**"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IMO / MARPOL Annex VI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** is a non-prescriptive, performance-based **regulatory index** mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **MARPOL Annex VI, Regulation 22** (originally adopted via MEPC 62 in 2011, enforced for new ships from **1 January 2013**). It quantifies the grams of **CO₂** emitted per **tonne–mile** of transport work performed by a vessel at its reference condition, thereby setting a minimum energy-efficiency benchmark that every newly constructed ship of regulated type must not exceed.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;\le\; \left(1 - \frac{X}{100}\right) \times \text{Reference Line Value}
$$

Where **X** is the percentage reduction factor for the applicable **EEDI Phase** (Phase 0, 1, 2, or 3).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Mileage Standard for Ships"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Think of EEDI as the **"kilometres-per-litre"** rating applied to a brand-new car — but inverted and applied to a ship.
>
> • A car manufacturer is *not told which engine to use*; they are simply told: *"Whatever car you make, it must emit no more than X grams of CO₂ per kilometre."* This pushes innovation toward hybrids, better aerodynamics, or lighter materials.
> • Similarly, **EEDI does not prescribe** which engine, propeller, hull form, or fuel a ship must use. It simply states: *"Your new ship must not exceed Y grams of CO₂ per tonne-mile of cargo moved."*
> • The yard/designer is then free to meet this by using bigger propellers, slow-steaming engines, waste-heat recovery, LNG fuel, or wind-assist — anything that demonstrably lowers CO₂ per unit of transport work.

## 1.3 The Three "Anchors" of the EEDI Concept

> [!TIP]
> Every EEDI problem in KTU examinations pivots on three physical anchors:
> 1. **Numerator — Emissions:** Fuel consumption × Carbon Factor of that fuel.
> 2. **Denominator — Transport Work:** Deadweight (DWT) or Gross Tonnage (GT) × Reference Speed.
> 3. **Benchmark — Reference Line:** A ship-type-specific power-law curve, tightened by phase-wise percentage reduction.

## 1.4 Standard CO₂ Conversion Factors (CF) — Memorize This Table

| Fuel Type | CF (tonnes CO₂ / tonne fuel) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Diesel / Gas Oil (Marine Gas Oil) | **3.206** |
| Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) | **3.114** |
| Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) | **2.750** |
| Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) | **3.000** |
| Methanol | **1.375** |
| Ethanol | **1.913** |

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners frequently award partial marks for correctly identifying the CF value. Memorize the **Diesel = 3.206** and **LNG = 2.75** values — these are the most-tested in KTU papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Distinct "EEDIs" in Regulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A very common KTU trap: students confuse the **"Attained EEDI"** (what the ship actually achieves) with the **"Required EEDI"** (the regulatory maximum).
> • **Attained EEDI** — Calculated from the ship’s design parameters. The ship *must* have Attained ≤ Required.
> • **Required EEDI** — Calculated from the Reference Line × (1 − X/100). This is the *ceiling*.

## 2.2 Architecture of the EEDI Formula

The attained EEDI is computed as the ratio of the *CO₂ emissions of all main and auxiliary engines at the design reference condition* to the *available transport work* at the reference speed.

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{j}\Big( C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{M,E,j} \cdot P_{ME,j} \Big) \;+\; \sum_{j}\Big( C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{AE,j} \cdot P_{AE,j} \Big) \;-\; \sum_{j}\Big( C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{eff,j} \cdot P_{eff,j} \Big)}{f_w \cdot \text{Capacity} \cdot V_{ref}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final term (with the **minus sign**) represents *beneficial* contributions: shaft generators, waste-heat recovery, and PTO/PTI innovations that *reduce* the index.

## 2.3 Definition of Every Term (Memorize for 14-Mark Problems)

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_{F,j}$ | Carbon emission factor of fuel *j* | g CO₂ / g fuel |
| $SFC_{ME,j}$ | Specific fuel consumption of main engine on fuel *j* | g / kWh |
| $P_{ME,j}$ | Power output of main engine on fuel *j* (75% of MCR) | kW |
| $SFC_{AE,j}$ | Specific fuel consumption of auxiliary engine on fuel *j* | g / kWh |
| $P_{AE,j}$ | Power output of auxiliary engine on fuel *j* | kW |
| $SFC_{eff,j}$ | Specific fuel consumption of effective shaft power | g / kWh |
| $P_{eff,j}$ | Effective shaft power after subtracting PTO/PTI contributions | kW |
| $f_w$ | Capacity factor (deadweight or GT weighting) | dimensionless |
| $\text{Capacity}$ | Deadweight (DWT) for cargo ships, or GT for cruise ships | tonnes |
| $V_{ref}$ | Reference speed (ship's contractual design speed) | knots |

## 2.4 Reference Line — The Regulatory Benchmark

The reference line is a **power-law curve** specific to each ship type:

$$
\text{Reference Line Value} \;=\; a \cdot b^{-c}
$$

Where **a**, **b**, and **c** are IMO-published parameters that differ by ship type.

| Ship Type | Capacity (used in denominator) | Typical Reduction Phase 2 (%) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Bulk Carrier | DWT | 20 |
| Gas Tanker | DWT | 20 |
| Tanker | DWT | 20 |
| Container Ship | DWT | 20 |
| General Cargo | DWT | 15 |
| Refrigerated Cargo | DWT | 15 |
| Vehicle Carrier | DWT | 15 |
| Combination Carrier | DWT | 20 |
| Cruise Passenger Ship | **GT** | 20 |
| LNG Carrier | DWT | 20 |

## 2.5 EEDI Reduction Phases (Chronological)

> [!NOTE]
> IMO Resolution MEPC.203(62) and successive amendments set tightening reduction factors:

| Phase | Vessel Keel-Laid Period | Reduction Factor (X) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Phase 0 | 2013 – 2014 | **0%** (baseline) |
| Phase 1 | 2015 – 2019 | **10%** |
| Phase 2 | 2020 – 2024 | **20%** |
| Phase 3 | 2025 onwards | **30%** |

For certain ship types (e.g., ro-ro, vehicle carriers, general cargo), the reduction is **15%** in Phase 2 and **30%** in Phase 3, with a Phase 1 still 10%.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: From Fundamental Definition to Final EEDI

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **full analytical derivation** that an examiner expects when a 14-mark question asks *"Derive the EEDI formula for a new vessel."*

### Step 1 — Define CO₂ Mass Emission Rate from an Engine

The mass of CO₂ emitted per unit time by an engine burning fuel *j* is:

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,j} \;=\; \dot{m}_{fuel,j} \times C_{F,j}
$$

Here, $\dot{m}_{fuel,j}$ is the **mass flow rate of fuel *j*** (tonnes/hour), and $C_{F,j}$ is the carbon factor (tonnes CO₂ / tonne fuel).

### Step 2 — Express Fuel Mass Flow in Terms of Specific Fuel Consumption

The specific fuel consumption (SFC) of an engine is defined as:

$$
SFC \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel,j}}{P_{eng}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel,j} \;=\; SFC \cdot P_{eng}
$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,j} \;=\; C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{j} \cdot P_{eng}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum All Emission Contributors

For a ship, the major contributors are:
1. **Main engines** (ME) — typically running at 75% MCR at reference condition.
2. **Auxiliary engines** (AE) — generators, boiler fuel not included.
3. **Subtract** effective power contributions from shaft generators, PTO/PTI, waste-heat recovery.

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{ME,j} \cdot P_{ME,j} \;+\; \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{AE,j} \cdot P_{AE,j} \;-\; \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{eff,j} \cdot P_{eff,j}
$$

### Step 4 — Define Transport Work

The transport work performed per nautical mile at reference condition is the *cargo-carrying capacity*:

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; f_w \cdot \text{Capacity} \cdot V_{ref}
$$

Where $f_w$ is a dimensionless weighting factor (often 1.0 for cargo ships, but lower for certain ship types), and Capacity is DWT or GT.

### Step 5 — Form the Ratio

By the very definition of EEDI:

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{total}}}{W_{\text{transport}}}
$$

Substituting Steps 3 and 4:

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{ME,j} \cdot P_{ME,j} \;+\; \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{AE,j} \cdot P_{AE,j} \;-\; \sum_{j} C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{eff,j} \cdot P_{eff,j}}{f_w \cdot \text{Capacity} \cdot V_{ref}}
$$

The units reduce elegantly to **g CO₂ / (tonne·nautical mile)**.

### Step 6 — Derive the Required EEDI

The required EEDI is the **reference line value** scaled by the phase-wise reduction factor X:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{X}{100}\right) \times \text{Reference Line Value}
$$

The reference line itself is:

$$
\text{Reference Line} \;=\; a \cdot b^{-c}
$$

So the final compliance check is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Attained EEDI} \;\le\; \left(1 - \frac{X}{100}\right) \times a \cdot b^{-c}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A new tanker (DWT = 80,000 t) is designed with main-engine power $P_{ME} = 12{,}000$ kW at 75% MCR, SFC = 180 g/kWh, running on HFO. Auxiliary engines total 1,200 kW with SFC = 200 g/kWh. Reference speed $V_{ref} = 14.5$ knots. Phase 2 (20% reduction). Reference line parameters for tankers: a = 961.79, b = DWT, c = 0.65. Compute the Attained EEDI and the Required EEDI, and state whether the ship complies.

### Step 1 — Compute Attained EEDI Numerator

For HFO: $C_F = 3.114$ (tonnes CO₂ / tonne fuel = dimensionless in g/g).

Main engine term:

$$
C_F \cdot SFC_{ME} \cdot P_{ME} \;=\; 3.114 \times 180 \times 12{,}000
$$

We break this down as:

$$
3.114 \times 180 \;=\; 560.52
$$

$$
560.52 \times 12{,}000 \;=\; 6{,}726{,}240 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

Auxiliary engine term:

$$
C_F \cdot SFC_{AE} \cdot P_{AE} \;=\; 3.114 \times 200 \times 1{,}200
$$

$$
3.114 \times 200 \;=\; 622.8
$$

$$
622.8 \times 1{,}200 \;=\; 747{,}360 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

Assuming $P_{eff}$ for shaft generator / waste-heat recovery is negligible here:

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{total}} \;=\; 6{,}726{,}240 + 747{,}360 \;=\; 7{,}473{,}600 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Denominator (Transport Work)

For tankers, $f_w = 1.0$:

$$
\text{Capacity} \times V_{ref} \;=\; 80{,}000 \times 14.5 \;=\; 1{,}160{,}000 \;\; \text{tonne·knots}
$$

### Step 3 — Attained EEDI

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{7{,}473{,}600}{1{,}160{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; 6.44 \;\; \text{g CO₂ / (tonne·nautical mile)}
$$

### Step 4 — Required EEDI

Reference line value:

$$
\text{Reference Line} \;=\; a \cdot b^{-c} \;=\; 961.79 \times 80{,}000^{-0.65}
$$

Compute $80{,}000^{-0.65}$:

$$
80{,}000^{0.65} \;=\; 80{,}000^{0.5} \times 80{,}000^{0.15}
$$

$$
80{,}000^{0.5} \;=\; \sqrt{80{,}000} \;\approx\; 282.84
$$

$$
80{,}000^{0.15} \;\approx\; e^{0.15 \cdot \ln(80{,}000)} \;\approx\; e^{0.15 \cdot 11.29} \;\approx\; e^{1.694} \;\approx\; 5.44
$$

$$
80{,}000^{0.65} \;\approx\; 282.84 \times 5.44 \;\approx\; 1{,}538.66
$$

$$
80{,}000^{-0.65} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{1{,}538.66} \;\approx\; 6.50 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Reference Line} \;\approx\; 961.79 \times 6.50 \times 10^{-4} \;\approx\; 0.625
$$

Phase 2 (20% reduction):

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;=\; (1 - 0.20) \times 0.625 \;=\; 0.80 \times 0.625 \;=\; 0.500
$$

### Step 5 — Compliance Check

> [!WARNING]
> Attained EEDI = **6.44** >> Required EEDI = **0.500** → **NON-COMPLIANT**.
> This is a common pitfall: students sometimes treat the reference line as *a direct ceiling* without applying the (1 − X/100) multiplier, and even then the result must be in the same units as the Attained EEDI (g CO₂/tonne·nm).

> The huge gap between Attained and Required indicates that for compliance the designer must (a) downsize the engine, (b) switch to LNG (CF = 2.75 instead of 3.114), (c) add shaft generator / WHR, or (d) install waste-heat recovery or wind-assist.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EEDI Computational Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Design Parameters] --> B[Main Engine Block]
    A --> C[Auxiliary Engine Block]
    A --> D[Energy-Saving Devices]
    B --> E[SFC_ME x P_ME x CF_j]
    C --> F[SFC_AE x P_AE x CF_j]
    D --> G[SFC_eff x P_eff x CF_j]
    E --> H[Numerator Sum]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Attained EEDI Numerator]
    J[Capacity DWT or GT] --> K[Denominator Builder]
    L[Reference Speed Vref] --> K
    M[Capacity Factor fw] --> K
    K --> N[Attained EEDI Denominator]
    I --> O[Divide]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Attained EEDI]
    Q[Reference Line a x b-c] --> R[Apply Phase Reduction 1 - X/100]
    R --> S[Required EEDI]
    P --> T{Compliance Check}
    S --> T
    T -->|Attained <= Required| U[Ships Passes]
    T -->|Attained > Required| V[Ships Fails Redesign Required]
```

## 4.2 Phased Reduction Roadmap (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Phase 0: 2013-2014\nBaseline 0%]
    P1[Phase 1: 2015-2019\n10% Reduction]
    P2[Phase 2: 2020-2024\n20% Reduction]
    P3[Phase 3: 2025 onwards\n30% Reduction]
    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3
    style P0 fill:#cfd8dc
    style P1 fill:#90caf9
    style P2 fill:#42a5f5
    style P3 fill:#1565c0
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Inputs to EEDI Compliance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Shipyard Design Inputs] --> M1[Hull Form Optimization]
    IN --> M2[Engine Downsizing]
    IN --> M3[Fuel Selection LNG or Methanol]
    IN --> M4[Waste Heat Recovery]
    IN --> M5[Wind-Assist or Solar]
    IN --> M6[Bulbous Bow or Ducted Propeller]
    M1 --> EEDI[Lower Attained EEDI]
    M2 --> EEDI
    M3 --> EEDI
    M4 --> EEDI
    M5 --> EEDI
    M6 --> EEDI
    EEDI --> CHK{Attained vs Required}
    CHK -->|Pass| CERT[Issued IEE Certificate]
    CHK -->|Fail| REDESIGN[Back to Shipyard]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI) and mention its regulatory parent framework.**

**Model Answer:**
EEDI is a non-prescriptive regulatory index defined under **MARPOL Annex VI, Regulation 22** (as adopted by MEPC 62, enforced from 1 January 2013). It is defined as the **grams of CO₂** emitted per **tonne–mile** of transport work performed by a vessel at its reference condition. The regulation mandates that for every newly built ship of a regulated type, the **Attained EEDI** must be less than or equal to the **Required EEDI** for the applicable phase. The framework is *non-prescriptive*: it does not dictate engine type, fuel, or hull design — only the final numerical outcome.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any four factors that reduce the Attained EEDI of a vessel without changing the Reference Line.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Adoption of **LNG fuel** (CF = 2.75 instead of 3.114 for HFO).
2. Installation of **Waste-Heat Recovery (WHR)** systems to lower effective fuel consumption.
3. **Engine derating** — installing an oversized engine and operating it below MCR for better SFC.
4. **Shaft Generator / PTO-PTI** systems that recover otherwise-wasted propulsion energy as auxiliary electric power.

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the EEDI formula for a new container ship of deadweight 60,000 t running on HFO. Clearly state the meaning of every term and the compliance check. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Attained EEDI is derived from first principles. The CO₂ mass flow rate from an engine burning fuel *j* is:

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,j} \;=\; \dot{m}_{fuel,j} \cdot C_{F,j}
$$

The fuel flow rate is related to the engine's specific fuel consumption (SFC) and its power output:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel,j} \;=\; SFC_j \cdot P_{eng}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,j} \;=\; C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_j \cdot P_{eng}
$$

For the ship as a whole, summing over main engines (ME) and auxiliary engines (AE), and subtracting the *effective* shaft-power contribution from energy-saving devices (eff):

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{ME,j} \cdot P_{ME,j} \;+\; \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{AE,j} \cdot P_{AE,j} \;-\; \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{eff,j} \cdot P_{eff,j}
$$

The transport work per nautical mile at the reference condition is:

$$
W_{\text{transport}} \;=\; f_w \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V_{ref}
$$

Forming the ratio:

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{ME,j} \cdot P_{ME,j} \;+\; \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{AE,j} \cdot P_{AE,j} \;-\; \sum_j C_{F,j} \cdot SFC_{eff,j} \cdot P_{eff,j}}{f_w \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V_{ref}}
$$

**Compliance check:**

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;\le\; \left(1 - \frac{X}{100}\right) \times a \cdot (\text{DWT})^{-c}
$$

Where *a*, *b*, *c* are IMO-published parameters (for container ships approximately a = 1744.0, c = 0.201), and X is the phase reduction factor (0, 10, 20, or 30).

**(b) The same container ship has $P_{ME} = 18{,}000$ kW, SFC = 175 g/kWh, $P_{AE} = 1{,}500$ kW, SFC = 195 g/kWh, $V_{ref} = 20$ knots, DWT = 60,000 t, HFO fuel. Determine the Attained EEDI and Required EEDI for Phase 2 and comment on compliance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For HFO, $C_F = 3.114$.

**Numerator (CO₂ emission rate):**

Main engine term:

$$
C_F \cdot SFC_{ME} \cdot P_{ME} \;=\; 3.114 \times 175 \times 18{,}000
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
3.114 \times 175 \;=\; 545.0 \;\; \text{(approximately)}
$$

(More precisely: $3.114 \times 175 = 544.95$.)

$$
544.95 \times 18{,}000 \;=\; 9{,}809{,}100 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

Auxiliary engine term:

$$
3.114 \times 195 \times 1{,}500 \;=\; 3.114 \times 195 \;=\; 607.23
$$

$$
607.23 \times 1{,}500 \;=\; 910{,}845 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

Total (neglecting $P_{eff}$):

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2,\text{total}} \;=\; 9{,}809{,}100 + 910{,}845 \;=\; 10{,}719{,}945 \;\; \text{g CO₂/h}
$$

**Denominator (Transport Work):**

$$
60{,}000 \times 20 \times 1.0 \;=\; 1{,}200{,}000 \;\; \text{tonne·knots}
$$

**Attained EEDI:**

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; \frac{10{,}719{,}945}{1{,}200{,}000} \;\approx\; 8.93 \;\; \text{g CO₂ / (tonne·nm)}
$$

**Required EEDI (Phase 2 = 20% reduction):**

For container ships, typical reference line parameters (IMO MEPC.1/Circ.815) are approximately $a = 1744.0$, $c = 0.201$:

$$
\text{Reference Line} \;=\; 1744.0 \times 60{,}000^{-0.201}
$$

Compute $60{,}000^{0.201}$:

$$
\ln(60{,}000) \;\approx\; 11.00
$$

$$
0.201 \times 11.00 \;=\; 2.211
$$

$$
e^{2.211} \;\approx\; 9.12
$$

$$
60{,}000^{0.201} \;\approx\; 9.12
$$

$$
60{,}000^{-0.201} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{9.12} \;\approx\; 0.110
$$

$$
\text{Reference Line} \;\approx\; 1744.0 \times 0.110 \;\approx\; 191.8
$$

**Required EEDI (Phase 2):**

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;=\; (1 - 0.20) \times 191.8 \;=\; 0.80 \times 191.8 \;=\; 153.44 \;\; \text{g CO₂ / (tonne·nm)}
$$

**Compliance check:**

$$
\text{Attained EEDI} \;=\; 8.93 \;\ll\; 153.44 \;=\; \text{Required EEDI}
$$

> The ship **complies comfortably** with Phase 2. There is significant headroom (over 90% margin) for future tightening of regulations. The designer may even consider derating the main engine further to reduce operating costs.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the various EEDI reduction phases with their percentage reductions and applicable time periods. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The EEDI regulation is implemented in **four tightening phases** under IMO MARPOL Annex VI. Each phase sets a *minimum percentage reduction* in the EEDI relative to the baseline Reference Line.

**Phase 0 (2013 – 2014):**
The initial baseline. New ships built during this period must have an EEDI **equal to or below the Reference Line** itself — i.e., a **0% reduction**. This phase establishes the "starting point" against which all subsequent improvements are measured.

**Phase 1 (2015 – 2019):**
A **10% reduction** is required relative to the baseline Reference Line. The compliance equation becomes:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;\le\; 0.90 \times \text{Reference Line}
$$

**Phase 2 (2020 – 2024):**
A **20% reduction** is required (15% for ro-ro, vehicle carriers, and general cargo). For most ship types:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;\le\; 0.80 \times \text{Reference Line}
$$

**Phase 3 (2025 onwards):**
A **30% reduction** is required. The compliance equation becomes:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;\le\; 0.70 \times \text{Reference Line}
$$

The reductions apply to **all new ships** whose **keel is laid** during the relevant phase, and the ship type must fall within the regulated categories (e.g., bulk carriers, gas tankers, tankers, container ships, general cargo, refrigerated cargo, vehicle carriers, combination carriers, cruise passenger ships, LNG carriers).

**(b) For a cruise passenger ship, why is **Gross Tonnage (GT)** used in the EEDI denominator instead of Deadweight (DWT)? Also explain how $f_w$ is computed for such a vessel, using the Otto-Petersen formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why GT (not DWT) for cruise ships:**

> [!NOTE]
> A cruise ship's *transport work* is fundamentally different from a cargo ship. It transports *passengers and their consumables*, not bulk cargo. Therefore:
> 1. **DWT is meaningless** — there is no cargo to weigh; the "payload" is people and luggage.
> 2. **GT captures the volume used to generate revenue** — cabins, restaurants, decks, public spaces. It correlates directly with passenger capacity and economic utility.
> 3. The IMO uses GT as a *proxy* for *passenger* transport work, which is the meaningful denominator for cruise vessels.

**Computing $f_w$ for a cruise ship using the Otto-Petersen formula:**

For cruise passenger ships, the capacity factor $f_w$ depends on the ship's *volume-to-weight* ratio. The Otto-Petersen formula is:

$$
f_w \;=\; \frac{\text{GT}}{\text{DWT} \times K}
$$

Where $K$ is a constant (typically 0.25 to 0.35 depending on ship class).

A more commonly used IMO formulation expresses $f_w$ directly as:

$$
f_w \;=\; \frac{GT}{10{,}000}
$$

But the **Otto-Petersen correction** specifically states that $f_w$ is adjusted downward for ships with very high GT-to-DWT ratios (luxury cruise ships), and upward for ferries that carry significant vehicle cargo. The simplified compliance formula for cruise ships becomes:

$$
\text{Required EEDI} \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{X}{100}\right) \times a \cdot GT^{-c}
$$

with parameter values different from cargo ships (for cruise ships: $a = 170.0$, $c = 0.20$ approximately).

> The implication for ship designers is that cruise ships must invest heavily in **HVAC efficiency, hotel-load optimization, and shore-power compatibility** to lower the AE-side emissions, since the denominator (GT) is *fixed* by the vessel's commercial design.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Loss Patterns observed in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Examiners *always* mark down if you mix up tonnes CO₂/h in the numerator with kg CO₂/nm in the denominator. The final answer MUST be in **g CO₂ / (tonne·nautical mile)**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $f_w$ factor** — Many students assume $f_w = 1$ even for ship types where it is not.
> 3. **Missing the (1 − X/100) multiplier** — The Required EEDI is *not* the Reference Line itself; it is the Reference Line scaled by the phase reduction.
> 4. **Wrong reference parameters** — Each ship type has *different* a, b, c. Hard-coding the wrong set (e.g., using tanker parameters for a container ship) is a guaranteed 4-mark loss.
> 5. **Neglecting the $P_{eff}$ reduction term** — If the problem mentions shaft generators or WHR, the negative term in the numerator MUST appear, or the Attained EEDI will be artificially high and the ship will *appear* non-compliant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Revision List:**
>
> • EEDI = grams CO₂ / (tonne · nautical mile). Always.
>
> • EEDI is a **MARPOL Annex VI Reg. 22** regulation, IMO MEPC.62, enforced 1 Jan 2013.
>
> • EEDI is **non-prescriptive** — outcome-based, not technology-based.
>
> • **Required EEDI** = $(1 - X/100) \times a \cdot b^{-c}$ ; $X$ = 0, 10, 20, 30 for Phases 0, 1, 2, 3.
>
> • **Attained EEDI** = Numerator (sum of $C_F \cdot SFC \cdot P$ for all engines, minus energy-saving contributions) divided by Denominator ($f_w \cdot \text{Capacity} \cdot V_{ref}$).
>
> • Capacity = **DWT** for cargo ships, **GT** for cruise passenger ships.
>
> • Reference speed $V_{ref}$ = ship's contractual design speed (not service speed).
>
> • **CF values** — HFO = 3.114, Diesel/MGO = 3.206, **LNG = 2.75** (most-tested).
>
> • Engines are rated at **75% MCR** at reference condition (not 100%).
>
> • Energy-saving devices (WHR, shaft generator, PTO/PTI) appear in the numerator with a **minus sign**.
>
> • **Compliance check**: Attained EEDI ≤ Required EEDI.
>
> • Phase tightening schedule: Phase 0 (2013–14, 0%) → Phase 1 (2015–19, 10%) → Phase 2 (2020–24, 20% / 15% for some) → Phase 3 (2025+, 30%).
>
> • EEDI is calculated for **newbuilds**; existing ships are governed by the **EEXI** (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index) and **CII** (Carbon Intensity Indicator).
>
> • EEDI pairs with **SEEMP** (Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan) — the operational bookend to the design index.
>
> • ICE (International Energy Efficiency Certificate) is the document issued upon EEDI compliance.

---

topics.title: Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)** is a mandatory, ship-specific operational management plan prescribed under **Regulation 22 of MARPOL Annex VI** (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, Annex VI – Regulations for the Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships). It is an internationally binding document that establishes a practical mechanism for a shipowner/operator to continuously monitor, evaluate, and improve the energy efficiency of ship operations throughout the vessel's service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEEMP entered into force on 1 January 2013** as part of the IMO's package of mandatory technical and operational energy-efficiency measures, complementing the **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)**. The requirement is anchored in **Resolution MEPC.203(62)**, which was later revised by **Resolution MEPC.346(78)** introducing the new **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** requirements effective **1 November 2022**.

The SEEMP is a **dynamic, living document**, meaning that it must be reviewed and updated at defined intervals to reflect changes in ship technology, operational practices, and regulatory expectations. The current version (2022 amendments) is structured into **four interrelated parts**:

1. **Part I – Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (General)** – Planning, implementation, and monitoring framework.
2. **Part II – Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (EEOI-based Monitoring)** – Voluntary reporting of the Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator.
3. **Part III – SEEMP for Ship Fuel Oil Consumption Reporting** – Mandatory annual reporting of fuel oil consumption data (DCS – Data Collection System).
4. **Part IV – SEEMP for Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** – Rating mechanism with A, B, C, D, and E grades and mandatory corrective actions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of SEEMP as a **"Personal Fitness Plan for a Ship."**

- Imagine a person trying to lose weight. They don't just take a single pill; they set a **goal (Plan)**, **track calories eaten (Monitor)**, **measure weight loss (Verify)**, and **adjust their diet/exercise (Review & Improve)**.
- Similarly, a ship consumes huge amounts of fuel oil (Bunker fuel). SEEMP tells the shipowner: "Here is a structured method to reduce the fuel bill, reduce CO₂ emissions, and keep the ship environmentally compliant — for the lifetime of the vessel."

A more technical analogy is the **ISO 50001 Energy Management System** used in factories and buildings. SEEMP borrows the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle from quality and energy management standards.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are central to SEEMP calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Mass Conversion Factor** for HFO (Heavy Fuel Oil) | $C_F$ | **3.114** t CO₂ / t fuel |
| CO₂ Conversion Factor for MDO/MGO (Marine Diesel/Gas Oil) | $C_F$ | **3.206** t CO₂ / t fuel |
| CO₂ Conversion Factor for LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) | $C_F$ | **3.000** t CO₂ / t fuel |
| CO₂ Conversion Factor for LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) | $C_F$ | **2.750** t CO₂ / t fuel |
| Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (Reference) | SFOC | **~170–200** g/kWh (2-stroke diesel) |
| Reference Speed | $V_{ref}$ | **Service speed** of the ship |
| Maritime Boundary Year (SEEMP baseline) | — | **2019** (for CII) |

> [!NOTE]
> All CO₂ conversion factors are derived from the carbon content of each fuel type and are prescribed in **MEPC.364(79)** and **IMO Resolution MEPC.364(79)**. They must be memorized for numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why SEEMP Matters in Marine Pollution Control

International shipping accounts for approximately **2.9 % of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions** (IMO Fourth GHG Study, 2020). Without a structured operational plan, emissions would scale with global trade. SEEMP is the IMO's primary **operational lever** to decarbonize shipping before zero-carbon fuels become universally adopted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PENST412 Module 4):** SEEMP is a flagship topic in the study of marine pollution control. The 14-mark KTU questions frequently test the **EEOI formula derivation, CII rating system, and the four-part SEEMP structure**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown of SEEMP

The SEEMP operates on a **closed-loop Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle. Below is the layered logic that KTU examiners love to test.

### **Step 1 — PLAN (Establish Baseline)**

- The shipowner identifies all energy-consuming systems onboard:
  - Main propulsion engine
  - Auxiliary engines (generators)
  - Boilers
  - Hull resistance (fouling, trim)
  - Hotel load (lighting, AC, galley)
- Baseline fuel consumption is recorded for one complete voyage or annual operational cycle.

### **Step 2 — DO (Implement Energy-Efficiency Measures)**

Common measures include:
- **Speed optimization** (slow steaming)
- **Hull cleaning and propeller polishing**
- **Weather routing**
- **Trim optimization**
- **Auxiliary engine load balancing**
- **Waste heat recovery**
- **Adoption of voyage planning software**

### **Step 3 — CHECK (Monitor & Verify)**

- Fuel consumption is recorded continuously (typically through the **Data Collection System – DCS** for fuel oil consumption, per **Regulation 22A of MARPOL Annex VI**).
- The **EEOI** or **CII** is computed and compared against the baseline.

### **Step 4 — ACT (Correct & Improve)**

- Underperforming areas are corrected.
- The SEEMP document is revised, and new targets are set.
- For CII grades D or E (3 consecutive years) or E (single year), a **Corrective Action Plan** becomes mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEEMP Part IV (CII)** introduces a regulatory *enforcement teeth* to the plan. From 2023, ships rated **D for 3 consecutive years** or **E for 1 year** must prepare a **Corrective Action Plan** detailing the measures that will bring the ship back to rating C or better.

## 2.2 The Four-Part SEEMP Structure (KTU Favourite)

| Part | Title | Mandatory Status | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Part I** | General Energy Efficiency Management | **Mandatory** (since 2013) | Framework for energy-efficiency improvement |
| **Part II** | EEOI-based Monitoring | **Voluntary** | Calculates the EEOI for self-assessment |
| **Part III** | Fuel Oil Consumption Reporting (DCS) | **Mandatory** (since 2019) | Annual fuel consumption data submission to flag State |
| **Part IV** | Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) | **Mandatory** (since 2023) | CII rating (A–E) and corrective action |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for SEEMP-related numerical questions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EEOI (Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator)** | $\text{EEOI} = \dfrac{\sum_j C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j} + C_{F,LNG} \cdot m_{LNG} \cdot \text{EnergConv}}{\sum_i (m_{cargo,i} \cdot D_i)}$ | **g CO₂ / (ton·nm)** | $C_F$ = CO₂ mass factor; $m$ = mass of fuel; $D$ = distance in nautical miles |
| 2 | **Mass of CO₂ emitted** | $m_{CO_2} = C_F \cdot m_{fuel}$ | tonnes CO₂ | Direct product of conversion factor and fuel mass |
| 3 | **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** | $\text{CII} = \dfrac{\sum_j C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}}{\text{Capacity} \cdot D_{annual}}$ | **g CO₂ / (ton·nm)** | Capacity = DWT (or GT for certain ship types) |
| 4 | **Required CII (CII_R)** | $\text{CII}_R = (1 - \dfrac{Z}{100}) \cdot \text{CII}_{baseline}$ | g CO₂ / (ton·nm) | $Z$ = annual reduction factor (%) |
| 5 | **CII Attained / Required ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{CII}_A}{\text{CII}_R}$ | dimensionless | Ratio ≤ 1.0 → rating ≥ C (good); > 1.0 → D or E |
| 6 | **Annual Reduction Factor Z (Bulk carriers, Tankers, Container ships, General cargo)** | $Z = (a \cdot Y) + b$ | % | $Y$ = calendar year; $a, b$ = ship-type constants |
| 7 | **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption** | $\text{SFOC} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{engine} \cdot t}$ | **g/kWh** | Used to estimate fuel from engine power and run time |
| 8 | **Voyage EEOI (single voyage)** | $\text{EEOI}_{voyage} = \dfrac{C_F \cdot m_{fuel}}{m_{cargo} \cdot D}$ | g CO₂ / (ton·nm) | Simplified single-fuel, single-cargo form |

### 2.4 Notes on the CII Reduction Factor $Z$ (Reduction Curve)

The IMO sets an **annual reduction factor** that decreases the allowed CII each year. The constants depend on ship type:

| Ship Type | $a$ (slope) | $b$ (intercept) | Applicable Years |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bulk carrier** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Gas carrier** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Tanker** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Container ship** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **General cargo** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Refrigerated cargo carrier** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Combination carrier** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **LNG carrier** | 0.03 | 2.8370 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Ro-Ro (vehicle)** | 0.03 | 2.8370 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Ro-Ro (cargo)** | 0.05 | 9.4734 | 2023 – 2030 |
| **Cruise ship (passenger)** | 0.04 | 3.7894 | 2023 – 2030 |

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction curve $Z = aY + b$ ensures that the required CII becomes progressively stricter each year, forcing continuous improvement.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SEEMP framework is not just a regulatory formality. In modern shipping companies, the SEEMP is integrated into:
- **Vessel Performance Monitoring systems** (e.g., ABB Marine, Wärtsilä SmartShip)
- **Shore-based Fleet Operations Centres (FOC)**
- **Charter party contracts** (e.g., the BIMCO EEOI clauses)
- **Port State Inspection records** (verified during MARPOL Annex VI surveys)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EEOI Formula (Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator)

The EEOI expresses the mass of CO₂ emitted per unit of transport work performed. Transport work = mass of cargo × distance travelled.

### **3.1.1 Starting Premise**

For a given voyage (or set of voyages in a reporting period), the total CO₂ emitted is the sum of CO₂ from all fuel types used. For a single fuel type $j$ with mass $m_{fuel,j}$ and CO₂ conversion factor $C_{F_j}$:

$$
m_{CO_2,j} = C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}
$$

### **3.1.2 Total CO₂ Emission**

For $n$ fuel types used onboard:

$$
m_{CO_2,total} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}
$$

### **3.1.3 Transport Work**

If a ship carries $k$ cargo types, where cargo type $i$ has mass $m_{cargo,i}$ and travels a distance $D_i$ (in nautical miles):

$$
W_{transport} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{cargo,i} \cdot D_i
$$

### **3.1.4 EEOI Definition**

By definition:

$$
\text{EEOI} = \frac{\text{Total CO}_2 \text{ emitted}}{\text{Transport work}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{EEOI} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_{cargo,i} \cdot D_i}
$$

### **3.1.5 Units**

- Numerator: tonnes CO₂
- Denominator: tonnes × nautical miles
- Result: **g CO₂ / (tonne·nm)** (after scaling $C_F$ values appropriately, since $C_F$ in the IMO tables is in t CO₂ per t fuel).

> [!NOTE]
> The MEPC.1/Circ.684 guidelines (now updated to **MEPC.364(79)**) provide the EEOI calculation framework. A simplified form for a single-fuel, single-cargo voyage is shown in Row 8 of the formula sheet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the CII Reduction Formula (Required CII)

The CII required each year is set by the **CII reduction factor $Z$** which determines the percentage reduction from the 2019 baseline.

### **3.2.1 Baseline CII**

Let the CII computed for the reference year **2019** be $\text{CII}_{2019}$. The IMO treats this as the **baseline**.

### **3.2.2 Annual Reduction**

Each year $Y$ (where $Y \geq 2023$) introduces a reduction factor $Z(Y)$, defined as:

$$
Z(Y) = aY + b
$$

For a bulk carrier (as an example), $a = 0.05$ and $b = 9.4734$. Therefore:

$$
Z(2023) = (0.05)(2023) + 9.4734 = 101.15 + 9.4734 = 110.62\%
$$

This number is then **clamped** by the IMO between 0% and a maximum. The actual annual reduction factor applied is:

$$
Z_{applied} = \text{Cap}(Z, 0,\ Z_{max})
$$

For ship types using $a=0.05$, the cap is reached at 5% (e.g., $Z_{max} = 5\%$ for bulk carriers in 2023 onward). The key relationship is:

$$
\text{CII}_R(Y) = \left(1 - \frac{Z_{applied}(Y)}{100}\right) \cdot \text{CII}_{2019}
$$

### **3.2.3 Worked Example**

Suppose a bulk carrier's 2019 baseline CII is **20.0 g CO₂ / (ton·nm)**. The required CII for **2023** with $Z_{applied} = 5\%$:

$$
\text{CII}_R(2023) = \left(1 - \frac{5}{100}\right) \cdot 20.0 = 0.95 \times 20.0 = 19.0\ \text{g CO}_2 / \text{(ton·nm)}
$$

For 2024 (still capped at 5%, but conceptually for extending the cap):

$$
\text{CII}_R(2024) = 0.95 \times 20.0 = 19.0\ \text{g CO}_2 / \text{(ton·nm)}
$$

(Once the cap is removed per IMO revisions, the formula extends naturally.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CII Rating Determination

| Attained CII / Required CII (Ratio) | Rating | Color Code | Action Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Ratio} < 0.86$ | **A** | Green | None — Superior performance |
| $0.86 \leq \text{Ratio} < 0.94$ | **B** | Light Green | None |
| $0.94 \leq \text{Ratio} \leq 1.00$ | **C** | Yellow | None — Meets required |
| $1.00 < \text{Ratio} \leq 1.06$ | **D** | Orange | **Corrective Action Plan** if 3 consecutive years |
| $\text{Ratio} > 1.06$ | **E** | Red | **Corrective Action Plan** mandatory (1 year) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The denominator (Required CII) and numerator (Attained CII) are calculated using the **same ship's actual data**. A ratio of 1.06 means the ship is 6% over its target.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A bulk carrier consumes 5,200 tonnes of HFO and 320 tonnes of MGO during a year. The ship transported 180,000 tonnes of cargo on average over 12,000 nautical miles of laden voyages. The 2019 baseline CII for this ship is 18.5 g CO₂/(ton·nm). The annual reduction factor $Z = 5\%$ is applied.

**(a) Calculate the total CO₂ emitted in the year.**

Using $C_{F,HFO} = 3.114$ and $C_{F,MGO} = 3.206$:

$$
m_{CO_2,HFO} = 3.114 \times 5{,}200 = 16{,}192.8\ \text{tonnes}
$$

$$
m_{CO_2,MGO} = 3.206 \times 320 = 1{,}025.92\ \text{tonnes}
$$

$$
m_{CO_2,total} = 16{,}192.8 + 1{,}025.92 = 17{,}218.72\ \text{tonnes}
$$

**(b) Calculate the Attained CII.**

$$
\text{CII}_A = \frac{17{,}218.72 \times 10^6\ \text{g}}{180{,}000\ \text{tonnes} \times 12{,}000\ \text{nm}}
$$

$$
= \frac{17.21872 \times 10^9}{2.16 \times 10^9} = 7.97\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
$$

**(c) Calculate the Required CII for the year.**

$$
\text{CII}_R = (1 - 0.05) \times 18.5 = 17.575\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
$$

**(d) Determine the CII Rating.**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{7.97}{17.575} = 0.453
$$

Since $0.453 < 0.86$, the ship receives rating **A** (superior).

> [!NOTE]
> This example shows that lower attained CII is better. A well-operated ship gets rating A.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SEEMP Part I PDCA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLAN: Set Baseline & Targets] --> B[DO: Implement Measures]
    B --> C[CHECK: Monitor & Compute EEOI/CII]
    C --> D[ACT: Review & Update SEEMP]
    D --> A
    C --> E{QII Rating?}
    E -- Grade D for 3 yrs --> F[Mandatory Corrective Action Plan]
    E -- Grade E for 1 yr --> F
    E -- Grade A, B, C --> G[Document Good Practice]
    F --> H[Flag State / Class Society Review]
    G --> I[Annual SEEMP Update]
    I --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Part SEEMP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SEEMP_DOC[SEEMP Document]
        P1[Part I General Plan]
        P2[Part II EEOI Monitoring Voluntary]
        P3[Part III DCS Fuel Oil Consumption Mandatory]
        P4[Part IV CII Rating and Corrective Action]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P1
    P3 --> DCSEXT[Flag State Data Submission]
    P4 --> CIIRATING[A B C D E Rating]
    CIIRATING --> CAP[Corrective Action Plan if D or E]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — EEOI Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Ship Voyage Begins]) --> F1[Record Fuel Consumption per Fuel Type]
    F1 --> F2[Apply CO2 Conversion Factor CF]
    F2 --> F3[Sum Total CO2 Mass]
    F4[Record Cargo Mass and Distance Travelled] --> F5[Compute Transport Work]
    F5 --> F6[Sum Cargo x Distance]
    F3 --> F7[Compute EEOI]
    F6 --> F7
    F7 --> F8[Report EEOI in g CO2 per ton nm]
    F8 --> STOP([EEOI Ready for SEEMP Part II Reporting])
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — CII Rating Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Attained CII] --> B[Lookup Required CII for Year]
    B --> C[Compute Ratio Attained over Required]
    C --> D{Ratio Threshold}
    D -- Less than 0.86 --> E1[Rating A Green Superior]
    D -- Between 0.86 and 0.94 --> E2[Rating B Light Green]
    D -- Between 0.94 and 1.00 --> E3[Rating C Yellow Meets Target]
    D -- Between 1.00 and 1.06 --> E4[Rating D Orange]
    D -- Greater than 1.06 --> E5[Rating E Red]
    E4 --> F1{D Three Consecutive Years?}
    E5 --> F2[Corrective Action Plan Required]
    F1 -- Yes --> F2
    F1 -- No --> G1[Continue Monitoring]
    F2 --> H[Submit to Flag State]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** All four diagrams above are Mermaid-renderable block diagrams representing the functional architecture of SEEMP, EEOI, and CII as prescribed by the IMO. For complex physical layouts, the block diagram approach is the safest rendering method.
