# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define SEEMP. List its four parts as per MARPOL Annex VI.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)** is a mandatory operational plan required under **Regulation 22 of MARPOL Annex VI** that establishes a mechanism for a ship to continuously improve its energy efficiency.

The four parts are:
1. **Part I** – General Energy Efficiency Management Plan (PDCA framework)
2. **Part II** – EEOI-based Monitoring (voluntary operational indicator)
3. **Part III** – Fuel Oil Consumption Reporting (DCS – mandatory annual reporting)
4. **Part IV** – Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) rating and corrective action mechanism

*(3 marks – 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for regulation reference + 1 mark for listing the four parts.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is EEOI? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

**EEOI (Energy Efficiency Operational Indicator)** is an operational indicator that expresses the mass of CO₂ emitted per unit of transport work (tonne-nautical mile).

$$
\text{EEOI} = \frac{\sum_{j} C_{F_j} \cdot m_{fuel,j}}{\sum_{i} m_{cargo,i} \cdot D_i}\ \left[\frac{\text{g CO}_2}{\text{ton·nm}}\right]
$$

Where:
- $C_{F_j}$ = CO₂ mass conversion factor of fuel $j$
- $m_{fuel,j}$ = mass of fuel $j$ consumed
- $m_{cargo,i}$ = mass of cargo $i$ carried
- $D_i$ = distance in nautical miles

*(3 marks – 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the formula + 1 mark for unit explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **four-part structure of SEEMP** under MARPOL Annex VI. Explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied in Part I. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship consumes 4,500 t of HFO and 280 t of MGO in a year. It carries 150,000 t of cargo over 10,000 nautical miles. Calculate the **EEOI** of the ship. Given $C_{F,HFO} = 3.114$ and $C_{F,MGO} = 3.206$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The SEEMP is a structured plan under **MARPOL Annex VI, Reg. 22** with four parts:

- **Part I – General Plan:** Provides the planning framework. The owner sets a baseline of fuel consumption, identifies improvement measures (slow steaming, hull cleaning, weather routing), and assigns responsibilities. The PDCA cycle works as:
  - **Plan:** Identify energy-consuming systems and set improvement targets.
  - **Do:** Implement the energy-saving measures.
  - **Check:** Monitor fuel consumption and verify effectiveness.
  - **Act:** Review outcomes, refine the plan, and continue the cycle annually.

- **Part II – EEOI Monitoring:** A voluntary self-assessment tool where the operator calculates the EEOI to benchmark performance.

- **Part III – Fuel Oil Consumption Reporting (DCS):** Mandatory annual reporting to the flag State under **Regulation 22A** from 2019 onwards.

- **Part IV – Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII):** Introduced in 2022 amendments, mandatory from 2023. The ship is rated A–E annually; corrective action is required for sustained D or any E.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

Total CO₂ from HFO:
$$
m_{CO_2,HFO} = 3.114 \times 4{,}500 = 14{,}013\ \text{tonnes}
$$

Total CO₂ from MGO:
$$
m_{CO_2,MGO} = 3.206 \times 280 = 897.68\ \text{tonnes}
$$

Total CO₂ emitted:
$$
m_{CO_2,total} = 14{,}013 + 897.68 = 14{,}910.68\ \text{tonnes}
$$

Transport work:
$$
W = 150{,}000 \times 10{,}000 = 1.5 \times 10^9\ \text{ton·nm}
$$

EEOI:
$$
\text{EEOI} = \frac{14{,}910.68 \times 10^6\ \text{g}}{1.5 \times 10^9\ \text{ton·nm}} = 9.94\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
$$

*(7 marks — 1 mark for fuel split, 2 marks for CO₂ calculation, 1 mark for transport work, 2 marks for EEOI formula and unit conversion, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **CII rating system** under SEEMP Part IV. List the **five rating grades** and their corresponding corrective action requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier's 2019 baseline CII is **17.8 g CO₂/(ton·nm)**. Calculate the **Required CII for 2024** and **2025** assuming the annual reduction factor is 5% and remains constant. If the attained CII for 2024 is **17.0 g CO₂/(ton·nm)**, determine the **rating** of the ship. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** rates a ship's operational carbon intensity on a five-grade scale. The rating compares the **Attained CII** with the **Required CII** for the calendar year.

| Ratio $\dfrac{\text{CII}_A}{\text{CII}_R}$ | Rating | Color | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $< 0.86$ | **A** | Green | None |
| $0.86 \leq \text{Ratio} < 0.94$ | **B** | Light Green | None |
| $0.94 \leq \text{Ratio} \leq 1.00$ | **C** | Yellow | None |
| $1.00 < \text{Ratio} \leq 1.06$ | **D** | Orange | Corrective Action Plan if **3 consecutive years** |
| $> 1.06$ | **E** | Red | **Corrective Action Plan** mandatory (any year) |

The rating is computed annually using the ship's actual fuel consumption and transport work from the previous calendar year. The Required CII is updated each year through the reduction factor $Z$, leading to progressively tighter targets.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

For 2024 (with $Z = 5\%$ applied to 2019 baseline):
$$
\text{CII}_R(2024) = (1 - 0.05) \times 17.8 = 0.95 \times 17.8 = 16.91\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
$$

For 2025 (additional 5% reduction, compounded linearly per the formula):
$$
\text{CII}_R(2025) = (1 - 0.05) \times 17.8 = 16.91\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
$$

> Note: The simplified exam version assumes the cap holds; for a more rigorous answer where reduction accumulates:
> $$
> \text{CII}_R(2025) = 17.8 \times (0.95)^2 = 17.8 \times 0.9025 = 16.06\ \text{g CO}_2/\text{(ton·nm)}
> $$

Rating determination for 2024:
$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{17.0}{16.91} = 1.0053
$$

Since $1.00 < 1.0053 \leq 1.06$, the ship receives rating **D** (Orange). Since this is the first consecutive year at D, **no corrective action plan is yet required**, but the shipowner must monitor closely.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for the rating table, 2 marks for the two CII_R calculations, 2 marks for the ratio calculation, 1 mark for the final rating and action.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** when computing EEOI/CII — the numerator is in g, not tonnes. Multiply tonnes of CO₂ by $10^6$.
> 2. **Mixing EEDI and SEEMP** in definitions. EEDI is a *design-time* index; SEEMP is an *operational* plan.
> 3. **Using $C_F = 3.0$ (approximate)** instead of the exact values **3.114** (HFO) and **3.206** (MGO). Examiners deduct marks for approximate values.
> 4. **Confusing transport work units** — students often write ton·nm instead of tonne·nm.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the year-specific reduction factor** in CII problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SEEMP** = Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan; **mandatory under MARPOL Annex VI, Reg. 22**.
- SEEMP has **4 parts**: General Plan, EEOI Monitoring, DCS Fuel Reporting, CII Rating.
- **PDCA cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) is the backbone of SEEMP Part I.
- **EEOI** = $\dfrac{\sum C_F \cdot m_{fuel}}{\sum m_{cargo} \cdot D}$, in **g CO₂/(ton·nm)**.
- **CII** = $\dfrac{\sum C_F \cdot m_{fuel}}{\text{Capacity} \cdot D_{annual}}$.
- **CII Rating grades:** A (<0.86), B (0.86–0.94), C (0.94–1.00), D (1.00–1.06), E (>1.06).
- **Corrective Action Plan** required if **3 consecutive D ratings** or **any E rating**.
- **CO₂ conversion factors (must memorize):**
  - HFO: **3.114** t CO₂/t fuel
  - MDO/MGO: **3.206** t CO₂/t fuel
  - LPG: **3.000** t CO₂/t fuel
  - LNG: **2.750** t CO₂/t fuel
- **EEDI vs SEEMP:** EEDI is a *technical design index*; SEEMP is an *operational management tool*. They complement each other.
- **Baseline year for CII reduction:** **2019**.
- **CII reduction factor** for most ship types: $Z = 0.05Y + 9.4734$ (clamped at 5% early years).
- **DCS** (Data Collection System) under **Reg. 22A** mandates annual fuel oil consumption reporting from **2019**.
- **SEEMP Part II (EEOI)** is **voluntary** — students often mark it as mandatory (common mistake).
- **SEEMP Part IV (CII)** became **mandatory from 1 January 2023**.
- **Resolution references** for the examiner: **MEPC.203(62)** (original SEEMP), **MEPC.346(78)** (2022 amendments with CII), **MEPC.364(79)** (CII guidelines).
- **CII rating is annual**, computed by **3 March of the following year**, and is valid until that date next year.
- **Ship operators** must retain the SEEMP on board for the **entire operational life** of the ship and produce it during **port state control inspections**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Survey and Issuance of International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificates (ISPP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST412 - MARINE POLLUTION, CONTROL AND RECOVERY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Survey and Issuance of International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificates (ISPP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP Certificate)** is a statutory document issued under the provisions of **MARPOL 73/78, Annex IV** — *Regulations for the Prevention of Pollution by Sewage from Ships*. The certificate provides prima facie evidence that a ship, its equipment, systems, fittings, arrangements, and material comply fully with the requirements of Annex IV, and that the ship has been subjected to the required surveys as stipulated under **Regulation 4 of Annex IV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme (Course Outcome mapping), this topic directly satisfies **CO4** — *"Illustrate the survey procedures and certification requirements of MARPOL Annex IV, V, and VI"*, mapped to **Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine that every commercial vehicle on the road must pass an annual "Pollution Fitness Test" before it can legally operate. The vehicle inspector checks the exhaust system, fuel leakage, noise emissions, etc., and issues a *Pollution Under Control (PUC) Certificate*. If the vehicle passes, it gets a certificate with a validity period; if not, it cannot run on public roads.

In the maritime world, this is exactly what the **ISPP Certificate** does — but for **sewage** (the human waste and greywater generated aboard a ship). A ship cannot legally sail internationally without a valid ISPP Certificate. Just as the PUC sticker is pasted on your car's windshield, the ISPP Certificate must be kept on board and made available for inspection at any port.

### 1.3 Key Terms & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

| Term | Definition / Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| **ISPP Certificate** | Statutory certificate of compliance with MARPOL Annex IV |
| **Annex IV** | MARPOL Annex on *Prevention of Pollution by Sewage from Ships* |
| **Regulation 4** | Surveys under MARPOL Annex IV |
| **Sewage** | (a) drainage & other waste from toilets/urinals; (b) drainage from medical premises; (c) drainage from spaces containing living animals; or other waste waters mixed with above |
| **Standard Discharge Rate** | **rate of discharge** must not exceed the approved rate, generally based on **commutating speed of ≥ 4 knots** |
| **Holding Tank Capacity** | Calculated at **≥ 4 days** sewage generation based on persons on board |
| **Validity Period** | **5 years maximum** from date of issue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact:** MARPOL Annex IV entered into force on **27 September 2003**, making it the last of the six MARPOL Annexes to become binding internationally. The ISPP Certificate provisions were phased in, and became mandatory for all ships ≥ **400 GT** and ships certified to carry **> 15 persons**.

### 1.4 Applicability Scope

The ISPP requirement applies to:

- All ships of **400 gross tonnage and above**, engaged in international voyages.
- Ships of **less than 400 GT** that are certified to carry **more than 15 persons**.

Ships excluded from the requirement include warships, naval auxiliaries, and ships owned/operated by governments used only for non-commercial service.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Surveys Under MARPOL Annex IV — Structured Logic

The survey regime under **Regulation 4 of Annex IV** is structured around **five (5) types of surveys**:

#### A. Initial Survey (Regulation 4(a))

Performed **before the ship is put into service** or **before the ISPP Certificate is first issued**. The survey verifies:

- Compliance with **Regulation 9** (sewage systems design)
- Compliance with **Regulation 10** (standard discharge connections)
- Compliance with **Regulation 11** (discharge of sewage — comminuting/disinfecting system and rate of discharge)

#### B. Renewal Survey (Regulation 4(b))

Conducted **every 5 years** (within a 3-month window before the certificate expiry date). Essentially a repeat of the initial survey, ensuring the ship remains compliant throughout its operational life.

#### C. Annual / Intermediate Survey (Regulation 4(c))

Conducted **within 3 months before or after each anniversary date** of the certificate. Less extensive than renewal, but verifies:

- General condition of sewage treatment plant
- Holding tanks integrity (leak test / pressure test)
- Piping and overboard discharge valves
- Commutator, screening device operational status

#### D. Additional Survey (Regulation 4(d))

Conducted after any **substantial repair or renewal** of the sewage system, treatment plant, or holding tanks. The survey is limited to verifying continued compliance of the repaired equipment.

#### E. Inspection (Port State Control)

Conducted at any time by **Port State Control Officers (PSCOs)** to verify validity of the certificate and overall compliance.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Standard / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sewage Holding Tank Capacity | $C_{tank} = K \times N_{crew} \times D_{storage}$ | $C_{tank}$ in $\text{m}^3$; $N_{crew}$ = number of persons on board; $D_{storage}$ = days of storage (≥ 4 days) | Minimum holding volume to retain sewage between port discharges |
| Per Capita Sewage Generation Rate | $Q_{gen} = 65 \, \text{L/person/day}$ (industry rule of thumb) | $\text{L} \cdot \text{person}^{-1} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$ | Used in $C_{tank}$ calculation |
| Discharge Flow Rate Limit (treatment plant) | $Q_{discharge} \leq Q_{max}$ | $\text{L/h}$ or $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ | Approved Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) design check |
| Comminuting System Discharge Speed Condition | $V_{ship} \geq 4 \, \text{knots}$ (for comminuted & disinfected discharge) | $\text{knots}$ | Regulatory discharge speed threshold |
| Fecal Coliform Limit (discharge standard) | $C_{FC} \leq 1000 \, \text{CFU}/100\text{mL}$ (geometric mean) | $\text{CFU}$ = Colony Forming Units | Effluent quality for treated sewage |
| Suspended Solids Limit (discharge standard) | $SS \leq 35 \, \text{mg/L}$ (IMO MEPC guideline) | $\text{mg/L}$ | Effluent quality verification |
| IMO Standard Discharge Connection Diameter | $D_{conn} = 100 \, \text{mm}$ nominal flange | $\text{mm}$ | Standard shore connection flange per IMO Resolution A.785 |
| Certificate Validity Period | $T_{valid} \leq 5 \, \text{years}$ | $\text{years}$ | Maximum duration after which renewal survey is mandatory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** IMO Resolutions and the MARPOL Annex IV itself do not provide explicit numerical formulas for holding tank sizing — the **4-day storage rule** is derived from **Resolution MEPC.227(64)** (the revised Sewage Treatment Plant standard) and is a widely accepted industry interpretation aligned with KTU examiner expectations.

### 2.3 Why and How — Engineering Logic

The **why** behind the ISPP survey regime is straightforward:

- Sewage discharged untreated in coastal waters causes **biological oxygen demand (BOD) pollution**, **bacterial contamination**, **nutrient loading (eutrophication)**, and **public health hazards**.
- The **how** is through a layered compliance structure:
  1. **Equipment Standardization** — Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs), holding tanks, standard discharge connections, pipelines, and valves.
  2. **Operational Restriction** — Sewage discharge prohibited within **4 nautical miles** of the nearest land (unless using approved STP); distance increases for untreated discharge.
  3. **Documentation & Certification** — ISPP Certificate is the legal evidence of compliance.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architects & Ship Designers** use these formulas during initial ship design to size sewage piping, holding tanks, and STP compartments.
- **Marine Surveyors & Classifiers (IACS societies)** rely on these formulae for survey planning.
- **Port State Control Officers** use them for compliance verification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Holding Tank Capacity

The minimum holding tank capacity is derived from the worst-case scenario where the ship must retain **all generated sewage** between successive port discharges. The 4-day rule is the regulatory minimum storage period based on IMO guidance.

**Given:**
- Number of persons on board: $N_{crew}$
- Daily sewage generation rate per person: $Q_{gen}$
- Required storage days: $D_{storage}$

**Step 1:** Daily sewage volume generated by all persons on board.

$$
V_{daily} = Q_{gen} \times N_{crew}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply the per-person generation rate by the number of persons to obtain the total daily volume in litres.

**Step 2:** Total sewage volume to be stored over the storage duration.

$$
V_{storage} = V_{daily} \times D_{storage} = Q_{gen} \times N_{crew} \times D_{storage}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply the daily volume by the number of storage days. This represents the total sewage volume that must be accommodated in the holding tank.

**Step 3:** Convert litres to cubic metres (since tanks are sized in m³).

$$
C_{tank} = \frac{V_{storage}}{1000}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 m³ = 1000 L. Dividing by 1000 converts litres to cubic metres.

**Step 4:** Combine into a single compact equation.

$$
\boxed{C_{tank} = \frac{Q_{gen} \times N_{crew} \times D_{storage}}{1000}}
$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the final design equation used by naval architects for holding tank sizing.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cruise ship carries **2,500 persons** (crew + passengers). The flag State regulation requires holding tank capacity for at least **4 days**. Per-capita generation rate is **65 L/person/day**. Compute the required holding tank capacity.

**Step 1:** Daily sewage generation volume.

$$
V_{daily} = 65 \times 2500 = 162{,}500 \, \text{L/day}
$$

**Step 2:** Total sewage volume to be stored over 4 days.

$$
V_{storage} = 162{,}500 \times 4 = 650{,}000 \, \text{L}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to cubic metres.

$$
C_{tank} = \frac{650{,}000}{1000} = 650 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Engineering check — is the capacity sufficient for route profile?

A 4-day storage corresponds to a typical trans-oceanic voyage leg between two major ports. If the actual leg is longer, $D_{storage}$ must be increased proportionally. So the final design equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{storage, required} &= \frac{V_{storage, available}}{V_{daily}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{C_{tank} \times 1000}{Q_{gen} \times N_{crew}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Discharge Speed Constraint

The 4-knot speed condition arises from hydrodynamics — at low speeds, the vessel's wake does not provide sufficient mixing of the discharged comminuted sewage. Below 4 knots, the discharge plume may concentrate near the hull.

**Step 1:** Define the condition.

$$
V_{ship} \geq V_{min} = 4 \, \text{knots}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert knots to metres per second for engineering use.

$$
V_{min, m/s} = 4 \times 0.5144 = 2.058 \, \text{m/s}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s. Multiply by 4 to obtain the speed in SI units.

**Step 3:** Therefore the discharge pump flow rate $Q_{discharge}$ must be sized such that the discharge completes while the ship is moving at or above this minimum speed.

$$
t_{discharge} \leq \frac{D_{voyage}}{V_{min}}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The discharge duration must be less than the time taken to traverse the discharge zone at minimum speed.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Discharge Zones vs. Discharge Type

| Discharge Type | Distance from Nearest Land | Treatment Required | Speed Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Treated sewage (approved STP) | ≥ 4 nautical miles | Yes — effluent quality within IMO standards | Any speed |
| Comminuted & disinfected sewage | ≥ 4 nautical miles | Comminuted to ≤ 25 mm + disinfection | ≥ 4 knots |
| Untreated sewage (holding tank) | ≥ 12 nautical miles | None | Ship proceeding en route at ≥ 4 knots |
| Holding tank discharge | Any | Holding tank designed for retention | At shore reception facility |

> [!NOTE]
> The above zones are derived from **MARPOL Annex IV Regulation 11.1.1** and are the most-tested item in KTU module exams.

### 3.5 Survey Checklist — Analytical Breakdown

| Survey Item | Verification Action | Compliance Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) capacity | Type approval certificate, operating parameters | Regulation 9 |
| Holding tank integrity | Visual, hydrostatic/pressure test, capacity confirmation | Regulation 9 |
| Pipelines & valves | Visual inspection, operational test | Regulation 9 |
| Standard discharge connection | Flange dimensions 100 mm, material grade | Regulation 10 |
| Sewage Discharge Rate | ≤ approved rate from type approval | Regulation 11.1.1 |
| Distance-based discharge log | Master to maintain Sewage Discharge Record Book | Regulation 12 |
| Effluent Quality (Treated) | Fecal coliform ≤ 1000 CFU/100 mL, SS ≤ 35 mg/L | MEPC.227(64) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: ISPP Survey & Issuance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Owner / Operator Applies to Flag State or RO] --> B[Initial Survey Scheduled]
    B --> C{Inspection Points}
    C --> D[Sewage Treatment Plant Check]
    C --> E[Holding Tank Check]
    C --> F[Piping and Overboard Valve Check]
    C --> G[Standard Discharge Connection Check]
    D --> H{All Items Satisfactory?}
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[Issue ISPP Certificate]
    H -->|No| J[Rectification Required]
    J --> C
    I --> K[Certificate Valid for 5 Years]
    K --> L[Annual Survey Every Year]
    L --> M{Continued Compliance?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Annual Survey Endorsement]
    M -->|No| J
    K --> O[Renewal Survey before 5 Years]
    O --> P{Continued Compliance?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Reissue ISPP Certificate]
    P -->|No| J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Sewage Discharge Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sewage to be Discharged] --> T{Discharge Type?}
    T -->|Treated by STP| U[Distance from Nearest Land?]
    T -->|Comminuted and Disinfected| V[Distance and Speed Check]
    T -->|Untreated from Holding Tank| W[Distance Check]
    U --> U1[At least 4 nautical miles from land]
    U1 --> U2[Discharge Permitted at any speed]
    V --> V1[At least 4 nautical miles from land]
    V1 --> V2[Ship speed at least 4 knots]
    V2 --> V3[Discharge Permitted]
    W --> W1[At least 12 nautical miles from land]
    W1 --> W2[Ship speed at least 4 knots and proceeding en route]
    W2 --> W3[Discharge Permitted]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ISPP Survey Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Flag State Authority]
        A2[Recognized Organization - RO]
        A3[Ship Owner Application]
    end

    subgraph SURVEY[Survey Execution Stage]
        B1[Surveyor Inspection Team]
        B2[Equipment and System Checks]
        B3[Piping and Tank Verification]
        B4[Effluent Quality Testing]
    end

    subgraph REVIEW[Review and Approval Stage]
        C1[Compliance Evaluation]
        C2[Non-Conformity Reporting]
        C3[Corrective Action Verification]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Certification Output Stage]
        D1[ISPP Certificate Drafted]
        D2[Survey Report Filed]
        D3[Certificate Issued and Endorsed]
        D4[Sewage Record Book Maintained Onboard]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the International Sewage Pollution Prevention (ISPP) Certificate and state its period of validity.

**Model Answer:**

The International Sewage Pollution Prevention Certificate (ISPP Certificate) is a statutory document issued under **MARPOL 73/78, Annex IV, Regulation 4**, certifying that a ship and its sewage-related equipment and arrangements comply with the requirements of Annex IV. It is issued by the **Flag State Administration** or its **Recognized Organization (RO)** following an initial survey.

**Period of Validity:** The ISPP Certificate is valid for a maximum period of **five (5) years** from the date of issue, subject to satisfactory annual/intermediate and renewal surveys.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for issuing authority, 1 mark for validity period.)*

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the minimum distance from the nearest land within which discharge of sewage is permitted for (a) treated sewage, and (b) untreated sewage.

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Treated sewage (using approved Sewage Treatment Plant):** Discharge is permitted when the ship is at least **4 nautical miles** from the nearest land.
- **(b) Untreated sewage (from holding tank):** Discharge is permitted when the ship is at least **12 nautical miles** from the nearest land, with the ship proceeding en route at not less than **4 knots**.

*(Examiner's note: 1.5 marks for each sub-part with correct distance and speed.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the **Annex IV** distance limits with **Annex V** (Garbage) limits. Memorize: **Annex IV = 4 nm (treated), 12 nm (untreated)**. Also, students lose marks by omitting the **4-knot speed** condition — always state both distance AND speed.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the different types of surveys required under MARPOL Annex IV for the issue and maintenance of the ISPP Certificate. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A container ship has 30 crew members. Calculate the minimum holding tank capacity required to retain sewage for 4 days at sea, given the per-capita sewage generation rate as 65 L/person/day. State the unit clearly and explain each step. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The ISPP Certificate is supported by **five distinct survey types** under Regulation 4 of MARPOL Annex IV:

**1. Initial Survey (Regulation 4(a)):**
Conducted before the ship is placed in service, or before the certificate is first issued. The surveyor inspects the sewage treatment plant, holding tanks, pipelines, overboard valves, and the standard shore discharge connection to ensure compliance with Regulations 9, 10, and 11.

**2. Renewal Survey (Regulation 4(b)):**
Conducted every five years, within a 3-month window before expiry of the existing certificate. The scope mirrors the initial survey, ensuring the ship's sewage system remains compliant throughout its service life.

**3. Annual / Intermediate Survey (Regulation 4(c)):**
Conducted annually within 3 months before or after the certificate anniversary. Scope is less intensive — focuses on operational integrity of the STP, holding tank leak tests, piping condition, and valve operations.

**4. Additional Survey (Regulation 4(d)):**
Conducted after substantial repair, alteration, or replacement of any sewage system component. Survey scope is limited to verifying continued compliance of the modified equipment.

**5. Port State Inspection:**
Conducted by Port State Control Officers in any port of call. PSC officers verify validity of the ISPP Certificate and may conduct operational checks; deficiencies may lead to detention.

*(Examiner's note: 1.4 marks per survey type — full marks for type, scope, and regulatory reference.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**

$$
N_{crew} = 30, \quad Q_{gen} = 65 \, \text{L/person/day}, \quad D_{storage} = 4 \, \text{days}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily sewage generation volume:**

$$
V_{daily} = Q_{gen} \times N_{crew} = 65 \times 30 = 1950 \, \text{L/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total sewage volume for 4 days:**

$$
V_{storage} = V_{daily} \times D_{storage} = 1950 \times 4 = 7800 \, \text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert litres to cubic metres:**

$$
C_{tank} = \frac{7800}{1000} = 7.8 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Answer:** The minimum holding tank capacity required is **7.8 m³**.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for stepwise calculation, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for final numerical answer.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Alternate]**

**(a) Explain the engineering requirements for the standard discharge connection and the sewage holding tank as per MARPOL Annex IV. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A chemical tanker has a sewage holding tank of 12 m³ capacity. If the per-capita generation rate is 65 L/person/day, and the ship stores sewage for 3 days, find the maximum number of persons that the tank can serve. Also comment on whether this capacity satisfies the IMO 4-day minimum storage rule. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Standard Discharge Connection (Regulation 10):**
- The discharge connection must be of a standard **nominal flange diameter of 100 mm** with a **flange outer diameter of approximately 215 mm** and a **flange bolt circle of approximately 170 mm** with **4 bolt holes of 18 mm diameter**.
- Material should be **steel or equivalent material** with a **flat face**.
- The connection should be designed for a **working pressure of 6 bar**.
- A **quick-closing coupling or stub pipe** arrangement is recommended.

**Sewage Holding Tank Requirements (Regulation 9):**
- The tank must have a **sounding pipe** to allow level measurement.
- It must be equipped with a **dedicated pump** capable of emptying the tank at an acceptable rate.
- Tank must be constructed of **corrosion-resistant material** suitable for sewage retention.
- Capacity must be sufficient to **store all sewage generated between port discharges**.
- The tank must have **air vents and overflow arrangements** to prevent over-pressurization.
- A **discharge connection** compatible with shore reception facility must be provided.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**

$$
C_{tank} = 12 \, \text{m}^3 = 12{,}000 \, \text{L}, \quad Q_{gen} = 65 \, \text{L/person/day}, \quad D_{storage} = 3 \, \text{days}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the holding tank equation and solve for $N_{crew}$:**

$$
C_{tank} = \frac{Q_{gen} \times N_{crew} \times D_{storage}}{1000}
$$

$$
12{,}000 = \frac{65 \times N_{crew} \times 3}{1} \quad (\text{since } C_{tank} \text{ in L})
$$

Wait — re-derivation. Since $C_{tank}$ is already in litres:

$$
12{,}000 = 65 \times N_{crew} \times 3
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
12{,}000 = 195 \times N_{crew}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_{crew}$:**

$$
N_{crew} = \frac{12{,}000}{195} \approx 61.5
$$

Since the number of persons must be an integer and cannot exceed the tank capacity, the maximum number of persons the tank can serve is **61 persons**.

**Step 5 — Comment on IMO 4-day rule:**

For the same 61 persons at the given generation rate, the 4-day storage requirement would demand:

$$
V_{4-day} = 65 \times 61 \times 4 = 15{,}860 \, \text{L} = 15.86 \, \text{m}^3
$$

Since the available capacity is only **12 m³**, the tank **does NOT satisfy** the IMO 4-day minimum storage rule. A larger tank (≥ **16 m³**) would be required for full IMO compliance.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for proper formula inversion, 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for unit consistency, 1 mark for the IMO comment.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **ISPP Certificate** is governed by **MARPOL Annex IV**, Regulation 4, and applies to ships **≥ 400 GT** or ships carrying **> 15 persons** on international voyages.
- **Five survey types**: Initial, Renewal, Annual, Additional, Port State Control.
- **Certificate validity** = **5 years maximum**.
- **Discharge zones**: Treated sewage at **≥ 4 nm**; untreated at **≥ 12 nm**; both require ship speed of **≥ 4 knots** (and the ship must be proceeding en route for untreated discharge).
- **Standard discharge connection**: **100 mm nominal flange, 6 bar working pressure** — per IMO Resolution A.785.
- **Holding tank sizing formula**: $C_{tank} = (Q_{gen} \times N_{crew} \times D_{storage}) / 1000$
- **IMO 4-day storage rule**: $D_{storage} \geq 4$ days as a minimum retention benchmark.
- **Effluent quality limits**: Fecal coliform **≤ 1000 CFU/100 mL**, Suspended solids **≤ 35 mg/L** — per **MEPC.227(64)**.
- **Surveyor Authority**: Issue by **Flag State Administration** or **Recognized Organization (RO)** authorized by the Flag State.
- **Master's responsibility**: Maintain the **Sewage Discharge Record Book** and ensure **port reception facility** is used when the discharge zone restrictions cannot be met.
- **Frequently confused**: Annex IV sewage rules vs. Annex V garbage rules — keep them separate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Examiner Tip:** For any 14-mark question on ISPP, always structure your answer as: **(1) Regulation reference, (2) Survey type and scope, (3) Equipment/system inspected, (4) Validity and renewal, (5) Real-world engineering significance**. This five-fold structure aligns with KTU 2024 scheme marking rubrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
